The sermon's last word is that hearing Jesus without doing his words is suicidal folly, while hearing and doing builds a life that can withstand any storm.
meta.theme: The Ethics of the Kingdom — Radical Love, Reversals of Fortune, and the Two Foundations
meta.keyWordOriginal: ἀγαπᾶτε (agapate)
meta.keyWordMeaning: Love — the imperative of agapaō, the distinctively Christian love of deliberate will and self-giving action, not primarily an emotion. In v.27: 'But I say unto you which hear, Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you' (agapate tous echthrous hymōn). This is the most radical ethical command in the chapter and arguably in all of Scripture. Agapaō in Greek literature did not carry the specific Christian sense before the NT; the LXX and NT transformed it into a word for the highest love — uncaused, unconditional, self-giving. To agapan enemies is to will their highest good, to act for their benefit regardless of whether they reciprocate. It mirrors the love of the Father who sends rain on the just and unjust (Matthew 5:45) and who 'so loved the world' (John 3:16) while we were yet sinners (Romans 5:8). The command is grounded not in sentiment but in the character of God himself: 'be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful' (v.36).
outline: Sabbath Controversies (vv.1-11): On the second sabbath after the first his disciples plucked ears of corn and ate rubbing them in their hands. The Pharisees asked — Why do ye that which is not lawful to do on the sabbath? Jesus said — Have ye not read what David did eating the shewbread which is not lawful but for the priests? The Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath. On another sabbath he entered the synagogue and taught and a man was there whose right hand was withered. The scribes and Pharisees watched to see whether he would heal on the sabbath. He said to the man — Stretch forth thy hand. And he did and his hand was restored. They were filled with madness and communed what they might do to Jesus

The Choice of the Twelve (vv.12-16): In those days he went out to a mountain to pray and continued all night in prayer to God. When it was day he called his disciples and chose twelve whom he also named apostles: Simon Peter his brother Andrew James and John Philip and Bartholomew Matthew and Thomas James the son of Alphaeus and Simon called Zelotes and Judas the brother of James and Judas Iscariot which also was the traitor

The Sermon on the Plain — Beatitudes and Woes (vv.17-26): He stood in the plain and a great multitude of people from Judaea and Jerusalem and the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon came to hear him and to be healed. He lifted up his eyes on his disciples and said — Blessed be ye poor for yours is the kingdom of God. Blessed are ye that hunger now for ye shall be filled. Blessed are ye that weep now for ye shall laugh. Blessed are ye when men hate you for the Son of man's sake. But woe unto you that are rich for ye have received your consolation. Woe unto you that are full now for ye shall hunger. Woe unto you that laugh now for ye shall mourn. Woe unto you when all men speak well of you

Love of Enemies (vv.27-36): But I say unto you which hear — Love your enemies do good to them which hate you bless them that curse you pray for them which despitefully use you. To him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other. Give to every man that asketh and of him that taketh away thy goods ask them not again. As ye would that men should do to you do ye also to them likewise. If ye love them which love you what thank have ye? Love ye your enemies and do good and lend hoping for nothing again and your reward shall be great and ye shall be the children of the Highest for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil. Be ye therefore merciful as your Father also is merciful

On Judging and Bearing Fruit (vv.37-49): Judge not and ye shall not be judged. Condemn not and ye shall not be condemned. Forgive and ye shall be forgiven. Give and it shall be given unto you. Can the blind lead the blind? Why beholdest thou the mote in thy brother's eye but perceivest not the beam in thine own? A good tree bringeth not forth corrupt fruit. Every tree is known by his own fruit. Why call ye me Lord Lord and do not the things which I say? He that cometh to me and heareth my sayings and doeth them is like a man which built a house and digged deep and laid the foundation on a rock. But he that heareth and doeth not is like a man that built a house upon the earth without a foundation against which the stream did beat vehemently and immediately it fell and the ruin of that house was great

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass on the second sabbath after the first, that he went through the corn fields; and his disciples plucked the ears of corn, and did eat, rubbing them in their hands.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν σαββάτῳ δευτεροπρώτῳ διαπορεύεσθαι αὐτὸν διὰ σπορίμων καὶ ἔτιλλον οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ τοὺς στάχυας καὶ ἤσθιον ψώχοντες ταῖς χερσίν
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en sabbatō deuteroproōtō diaporeuasthai auton dia sporimōn kai etillon hoi mathētai autou tous stachyas kai ēsthion psōchontes tais chersin
verses.study_note: 'Second sabbath after the first' (sabbatō deuteroproōtō — second-first sabbath; a technical term debated by scholars: possibly the second Sabbath after Passover in the 50-day count to Pentecost, or the first Sabbath of the second month in the Jewish calendar). The disciples' action — plucking heads of grain and rubbing them in their hands — was explicitly permitted by Mosaic law (Deuteronomy 23:25: 'thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand; but thou shalt not move a sickle into thy neighbour's standing corn'). The Pharisaic objection is not to eating the grain but to the rubbing (considered a form of 'threshing') on the Sabbath — an example of rabbinic fence-building around the original commandment.

verses.kjv_text: And certain of the Pharisees said unto them, Why do ye that which is not lawful to do on the sabbath days?
verses.original_text: τινὲς δὲ τῶν Φαρισαίων εἶπαν αὐτοῖς Τί ποιεῖτε ὃ οὐκ ἔξεστιν ποιεῖν ἐν τοῖς σάββασιν
verses.transliteration: tines de tōn Pharisaiōn eipan autois Ti poieite ho ouk exestin poiein en tois sabbasin
verses.study_note: 'Which is not lawful' (ho ouk exestin — which it is not permitted; ouk exestin = it is not lawful, permissible). The Pharisees' complaint is based on their oral tradition (the Mishnah categorised 39 principal classes of work forbidden on the Sabbath; 'threshing' was one of them; rubbing grain with the hands was classified as a form of threshing). The Pharisees had surrounded the Sabbath commandment with hundreds of specific regulations in an attempt to protect it — but in doing so, had obscured its original purpose: a day of rest and delight in God's goodness. Jesus will address not the specific regulation but the underlying theological question: what is the Sabbath for?

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answering them said, Have ye not read so much as this, what David did, when himself was an hungred, and they which were with him;
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς πρὸς αὐτοὺς εἶπεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς Οὐδὲ τοῦτο ἀνέγνωτε ὃ ἐποίησεν Δαυὶδ ὅτε ἐπείνασεν αὐτὸς καὶ οἱ μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis pros autous eipen ho Iēsous Oude touto anegnōte ho epoiēsen Dauid hote epeinasen autos kai hoi met autou
verses.study_note: 'Have ye not read this?' (Oude touto anegnōte — did you not even read this?). Jesus does not respond to the Pharisaic tradition but to Scripture — specifically 1 Samuel 21:1-6 (David at Nob). The argument from Scripture takes precedence over the argument from tradition. 'What David did, when himself was an hungred' — the principle Jesus invokes: urgent human need can override ritual regulations when these conflict. David ate the showbread (the bread of the Presence) reserved exclusively for priests — yet he was not condemned for it because human necessity took precedence. The point: if David could eat the consecrated showbread under necessity, Jesus's disciples can rub grain on the Sabbath under necessity (the context of the journey).

verses.kjv_text: How he went into the house of God, and did take and eat the shewbread, and gave also to them that were with him; which it is not lawful to eat but for the priests alone?
verses.original_text: ὡς εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τοὺς ἄρτους τῆς προθέσεως λαβὼν ἔφαγεν καὶ ἔδωκεν τοῖς μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ οὓς οὐκ ἔξεστιν φαγεῖν εἰ μὴ μόνους τοὺς ἱερεῖς
verses.transliteration: hōs eisēlthen eis ton oikon tou theou kai tous artous tēs protheseōs labōn ephagen kai edōken tois met autou hous ouk exestin phagein ei mē monous tous hiereis
verses.study_note: 'The shewbread' (artous tēs protheseōs — the bread of the setting forth/Presence; the twelve loaves set out on the table in the Holy Place each Sabbath, replaced weekly, Leviticus 24:5-9; Exodus 25:30). Only priests could eat this bread — yet David ate it and gave it to his men. The action was irregular (technically against Levitical law) but excused by the combination of David's royal authority, the urgency of his need, and the principle that ritual law serves human welfare, not the other way around. The deeper point Jesus makes: if the Messiah-prefiguring David can make such a claim on religious ritual, how much more can the Messiah himself determine the proper use of his own institution — the Sabbath?

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, That the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔλεγεν αὐτοῖς ὅτι Κύριός ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου καὶ τοῦ σαββάτου
verses.transliteration: kai elegen autois hoti Kyrios estin ho huios tou anthrōpou kai tou sabbatou
verses.study_note: 'The Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath' (Kyrios estin ho huios tou anthrōpou kai tou sabbatou — the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath). The climactic claim. The Sabbath was instituted by God (Genesis 2:2-3; Exodus 20:8-11); only God can be Lord of the Sabbath. By claiming Sabbath lordship, Jesus claims divine authority. The argument is conclusive: the disciples pluck grain under the authority of the Son of Man, who is Lord of the Sabbath; since the Lord of the institution cannot violate his own institution, the disciples cannot violate the Sabbath by doing what their Lord permits. Mark 2:27-28 adds the further point: 'The sabbath was made for man, and not man for the sabbath' — the Sabbath is a gift, not a burden.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass also on another sabbath, that he entered into the synagogue and taught: and there was a man whose right hand was withered.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ καὶ ἐν ἑτέρῳ σαββάτῳ εἰσελθεῖν αὐτὸν εἰς τὴν συναγωγὴν καὶ διδάσκειν καὶ ἦν ἐκεῖ ἄνθρωπος καὶ ἡ χεὶρ αὐτοῦ ἡ δεξιὰ ἦν ξηρά
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de kai en heterō sabbatō eiselthein auton eis tēn synagōgēn kai didaskein kai ēn ekei anthrōpos kai hē cheir autou hē dexia ēn xēra
verses.study_note: 'Another sabbath' — a second Sabbath controversy. 'Right hand' (dexia — the right hand; Luke alone specifies 'right hand'; as a physician he would note the functional significance of which hand was affected). 'Withered' (xēra — dried up, withered; xēros = dry; the hand has lost its normal moisture, circulation, and function). The man's withered right hand would prevent him from working (manual labour), worshipping (ritual ablutions), and participating fully in social life. The Sabbath synagogue context is again the setting: Jesus's Sabbath teaching ministry repeatedly encounters the question of Sabbath healing, forcing the definitional question: what does God intend for the Sabbath day?

verses.kjv_text: And the scribes and Pharisees watched him, whether he would heal on the sabbath day; that they might find an accusation against him.
verses.original_text: παρετηροῦντο δὲ αὐτὸν οἱ γραμματεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι εἰ ἐν τῷ σαββάτῳ θεραπεύει ἵνα εὕρωσιν κατηγορίαν αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: paretēronto de auton hoi grammateis kai hoi Pharisaioi ei en tō sabbatō therapeuei hina heurōsin katēgorian autou
verses.study_note: 'Watched him' (paretēronto — they were watching closely, observing carefully; paratēreō = to watch alongside, to spy on; imperfect: continuous surveillance). 'That they might find an accusation' (hina heurōsin katēgorian autou — so that they might find a charge against him; katēgoria = accusation, the root of our word 'category' via legal categorisation). The observers have shifted from enquirers to adversaries: they are not watching to learn but to catch. The man with the withered hand is in their eyes not a suffering human being but a potential legal test case. The dehumanisation of the sick man by the Pharisees' legal focus is in sharp contrast to Jesus's seeing 'a man' (v.6).

verses.kjv_text: But he knew their thoughts, and said to the man which had the withered hand, Rise up, and stand forth in the midst. And he arose and stood forth.
verses.original_text: αὐτὸς δὲ ᾔδει τοὺς διαλογισμοὺς αὐτῶν εἶπεν δὲ τῷ ἀνδρὶ τῷ ξηρὰν ἔχοντι τὴν χεῖρα Ἔγειρε καὶ στῆθι εἰς τὸ μέσον ὁ δὲ ἀναστὰς ἔστη
verses.transliteration: autos de ēdei tous dialogismous autōn eipen de tō andri tō xēran echonti tēn cheira Egeire kai stēthi eis to meson ho de anastas estē
verses.study_note: 'He knew their thoughts' (ēdei tous dialogismous autōn — he knew their inner reasonings; perfect of oida = fully knew). Jesus's divine omniscience (cf. 5:22) knows the adversarial intent of the watchers. Rather than acting quietly to avoid conflict, Jesus does the opposite: he commands the man with the withered hand to stand in the middle (eis to meson) — in full public view, directly before the observers. Jesus will not heal in a corner to placate his critics. The man stands; the stage is set; the Pharisees will observe both the healing and the reasoning that accompanies it.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus unto them, I will ask you one thing; Is it lawful on the sabbath days to do good, or to do evil? to save life, or to destroy it?
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς πρὸς αὐτούς Ἐπερωτήσω ὑμᾶς τί ἔξεστιν τῷ σαββάτῳ ἀγαθοποιῆσαι ἢ κακοποιῆσαι ψυχὴν σῶσαι ἢ ἀπολέσαι
verses.transliteration: eipen de ho Iēsous pros autous Eperōtēsō hymas ti exestin tō sabbatō agathopoiēsai ē kakopoiēsai psychēn sōsai ē apolesai
verses.study_note: 'Is it lawful on the sabbath days to do good, or to do evil? to save life, or to destroy it?' — Jesus reduces the Sabbath question to its essence with a brilliant dilemma. The options are: do good or do evil; save life or destroy it. Not healing the man who can be healed is not neutral — it is, on the Sabbath, a choice to 'do evil' and 'destroy life.' The Pharisees' tradition had created a binary (Sabbath observance vs. breaking the Sabbath) that Jesus exposes as a false binary: the real question is between good and evil, life and death. Inaction in the face of suffering is not Sabbath observance but Sabbath abuse. The Sabbath is for saving, not for destroying.

verses.kjv_text: And looking round about upon them all, he said unto the man, Stretch forth thy hand. And he did so: and his hand was restored whole as the other.
verses.original_text: καὶ περιβλεψάμενος πάντας αὐτοὺς εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ἔκτεινον τὴν χεῖρά σου ὁ δὲ ἐποίησεν καὶ ἀπεκατεστάθη ἡ χεὶρ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai periblepsamenos pantas autous eipen autō Ekteinon tēn cheira sou ho de epoiēsen kai apekatesatathē hē cheir autou
verses.study_note: 'Looking round about upon them all' (periblepsamenos pantas autous — looking around at all of them; the searching gaze of Jesus that takes in every observer before the act). 'Stretch forth thy hand' (Ekteinon tēn cheira sou — stretch out your hand). The command to stretch forth a withered hand is itself a test of faith: the withered hand cannot naturally be stretched. Obedience to the command is the act of faith that releases the healing. 'His hand was restored whole as the other' (apekatesatathē hē cheir autou — his hand was completely restored; apokathistēmi = to restore to the original condition; 'as the other' = perfectly normal, matching the healthy hand). Complete restoration, publicly visible, before all the observers.

verses.kjv_text: And they were filled with madness; and communed one with another what they might do to Jesus.
verses.original_text: αὐτοὶ δὲ ἐπλήσθησαν ἀνοίας καὶ διελάλουν πρὸς ἀλλήλους τί ἂν ποιήσαιεν τῷ Ἰησοῦ
verses.transliteration: autoi de eplēsthēsan anoias kai dielaloun pros allēlous ti an poiēsaien tō Iēsou
verses.study_note: 'Filled with madness' (eplēsthēsan anoias — they were filled with senselessness/rage; anoia = mindlessness, folly, fury; from a-nous = without mind/sense). The healing of a suffering man on the Sabbath produces murderous fury among the observers — one of the most chilling verses in the Gospel. The irony is devastating: while Jesus is 'saving life' (v.9), his critics are plotting 'to destroy' (v.9) his. 'Communed one with another what they might do to Jesus' (dielaloun...ti an poiēsaien — they kept discussing what they might do to Jesus; optative mood of deliberate planning). This is the first explicit plotting against Jesus's life in Luke. The healing of the man with the withered hand marks the point of no return in the Pharisaic opposition.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass in those days, that he went out into a mountain to pray, and continued all night in prayer to God.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις ταύταις ἐξελθεῖν αὐτὸν εἰς τὸ ὄρος προσεύξασθαι καὶ ἦν διανυκτερεύων ἐν τῇ προσευχῇ τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tais hēmerais tautais exelthein auton eis to oros proseuxasthai kai ēn dianyktereūōn en tē proseuchē tou theou
verses.study_note: 'Continued all night in prayer to God' (ēn dianyktereuōn en tē proseuchē — was spending the whole night in prayer; dianyktereuō = to pass the night through, to keep vigil all night; a hapax legomenon — only here in the NT; en tē proseuchē tou theou = in the prayer to/of God). Luke places an all-night prayer vigil immediately before the appointment of the Twelve — the single most important institutional decision of Jesus's ministry. Every major decision in Luke is preceded by prayer (3:21; 5:16; 9:18, 28-29; 22:41-44; 23:34, 46). The twelve apostles are chosen not through wisdom, strategic calculation, or democratic selection, but through a whole night of seeking God's will. The appointment of the Twelve comes from above, through prayer.

verses.kjv_text: And when it was day, he called unto him his disciples: and of them he chose twelve, whom also he named apostles;
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅτε ἐγένετο ἡμέρα προσεφώνησεν τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐκλεξάμενος ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν δώδεκα οὓς καὶ ἀποστόλους ὠνόμασεν
verses.transliteration: kai hote egeneto hēmera prosephōnēsen tous mathētas autou kai eklexamenos ap autōn dōdeka hous kai apostolous ōnomasen
verses.study_note: 'He chose twelve, whom also he named apostles' (eklexamenos dōdeka hous kai apostolous ōnomasen — having chosen twelve whom he also named apostles; eklegomai = to choose out, to select; apostellō = to send). 'Apostles' (apostolous — sent ones; the ones commissioned and sent with authority). The twelve mirrors the twelve tribes of Israel — a deliberate symbolic reconstitution of Israel around Jesus as the new centre. Jesus does not merely have disciples (learners); he constitutes apostles (sent ones) who carry his authority to the world (cf. 10:16: 'He that heareth you heareth me'). The appointment 'after a night of prayer' is Luke's marker that this is a divinely guided selection, not a human one.

verses.kjv_text: Simon, (whom he also named Peter,) and Andrew his brother, James and John, Philip and Bartholomew,
verses.original_text: Σίμωνα ὃν καὶ ὠνόμασεν Πέτρον καὶ Ἀνδρέαν τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ καὶ Ἰάκωβον καὶ Ἰωάννην καὶ Φίλιππον καὶ Βαρθολομαῖον
verses.transliteration: Simōna hon kai ōnomasen Petron kai Andrean ton adelphon autou kai Iakōbon kai Iōannēn kai Philippon kai Bartholomaion
verses.study_note: 'Simon, whom he also named Peter' (Simōna hon kai ōnomasen Petron — Simon whom he also named Peter; Petros = rock/stone; Aramaic Kēphā = Cephas). The renaming of Simon as Peter is a prophetic act: Jesus sees not what he is but what he will become (John 1:42). Andrew (Heb. Aner = manly; a Greek name, suggesting a Hellenised family; cf. John 1:40-41 — Andrew introduces Peter to Jesus). James and John (sons of Zebedee; cf. 5:10): 'sons of thunder' (Boanerges, Mark 3:17). Philip (Greek name; from Bethsaida, John 1:44; brings Nathanael/Bartholomew to Jesus, John 1:45-51). Bartholomew (Bar-Tolmai = son of Tolmai; traditionally identified with Nathanael).

verses.kjv_text: Matthew and Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon called Zelotes,
verses.original_text: καὶ Μαθθαῖον καὶ Θωμᾶν καὶ Ἰάκωβον Ἁλφαίου καὶ Σίμωνα τὸν καλούμενον Ζηλωτήν
verses.transliteration: kai Matthaion kai Thōman kai Iakōbon Halphaiou kai Simōna ton kaloumenon Zēlōtēn
verses.study_note: 'Matthew' (Matthaion — gift of God; the tax collector Levi of 5:27). 'Thomas' (Thōman — twin; Aramaic Tōmā; Greek Didymus, twin, John 11:16). 'James the son of Alphaeus' (Iakōbon Halphaiou — the other James, not the son of Zebedee). 'Simon called Zelotes' (Simōna ton kaloumenon Zēlōtēn — Simon the called Zealot; Zēlōtēs = one who is zealous, a member of the Zealot party). The Zealots were a Jewish nationalist movement committed to violent resistance against Roman occupation. Simon the Zealot and Matthew the tax collector (Roman collaborator) are in the same apostolic group — a living demonstration of the kingdom's power to unite political opposites in a higher allegiance.

verses.kjv_text: And Judas the brother of James, and Judas Iscariot, which also was the traitor.
verses.original_text: καὶ Ἰούδαν Ἰακώβου καὶ Ἰούδαν Ἰσκαριὼθ ὃς ἐγένετο προδότης
verses.transliteration: kai Ioudan Iakōbou kai Ioudan Iskariōth hos egeneto prodotēs
verses.study_note: 'Judas Iscariot, which also was the traitor' (Ioudan Iskariōth hos egeneto prodotēs — Judas Iscariot who became a traitor; prodotēs = betrayer, traitor; proditōr in Latin). Luke includes the dark note immediately: even among the Twelve, chosen after prayer, there is one who will betray. 'Iscariot' — possibly Ish-Karioth (man from Kerioth, a town in Judah; the only non-Galilean among the Twelve), or possibly ish-shaqar (man of falsehood). 'Who became a traitor' (hos egeneto prodotēs — who became, not was always; suggesting a process of hardening and choice). The inclusion of Judas in the Twelve is not a divine mistake but fulfils the Scriptures (Psalm 41:9; John 13:18; 17:12; Acts 1:16-20).

verses.kjv_text: And he came down with them, and stood in the plain, and the company of his disciples, and a great multitude of people out of all Judaea and Jerusalem, and from the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon, which came to hear him, and to be healed of their diseases;
verses.original_text: καὶ καταβὰς μετ᾽ αὐτῶν ἔστη ἐπὶ τόπου πεδινοῦ καὶ ὄχλος πολὺς μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ καὶ πλῆθος πολὺ τοῦ λαοῦ ἀπὸ πάσης τῆς Ἰουδαίας καὶ Ἱερουσαλὴμ καὶ τῆς παραλίου Τύρου καὶ Σιδῶνος οἳ ἦλθον ἀκοῦσαι αὐτοῦ καὶ ἰαθῆναι ἀπὸ τῶν νόσων αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: kai katabas met autōn estē epi topou pedinou kai ochlos polys mathētōn autou kai plēthos poly tou laou apo pasēs tēs Ioudaias kai Hierousalēm kai tēs paralou Tyrou kai Sidōnos hoi ēlthan akousai autou kai iasthēnai apo tōn nosōn autōn
verses.study_note: 'Stood in the plain' (estē epi topou pedinou — he stood on a level place/plain; pedinos = flat, level). The 'Sermon on the Plain' (as distinct from Matthew's 'Sermon on the Mount') is addressed to a broad crowd that includes not just disciples but the general public. 'From the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon' — Gentile territory; the universal reach of Jesus's ministry is already present. 'To hear him, and to be healed' — the double motivation: the word and the works draw the crowd. The sermon follows this double ministry: Jesus teaches the crowd that has come both to hear and to be healed. The Beatitudes address those who know their need; the Woes address those who think they have all they need.

verses.kjv_text: And they that were vexed with unclean spirits: and they were healed.
verses.original_text: καὶ οἱ ἐνοχλούμενοι ἀπὸ πνευμάτων ἀκαθάρτων ἐθεραπεύοντο
verses.transliteration: kai hoi enochloumenoi apo pneumatōn akathartōn etherapeuonto
verses.study_note: 'Vexed with unclean spirits' (enochloumenoi apo pneumatōn akathartōn — being troubled/tormented by unclean spirits; enochleō = to trouble within, to crowd against, to disturb). Luke distinguishes between the physically sick (v.17 — nosōn) and those tormented by unclean spirits. The healing ministry addresses both: physical disease and demonic oppression. 'They were healed' (etherapeuonto — were being healed; imperfect passive: a continuous, ongoing healing ministry as the crowd pressed upon Jesus). The comprehensive scope of Jesus's healing anticipates the mission of the church: the disciples will be sent out with authority over unclean spirits and to heal diseases (9:1-2).

verses.kjv_text: And the whole multitude sought to touch him: for there went virtue out of him, and healed them all.
verses.original_text: καὶ πᾶς ὁ ὄχλος ἐζήτει ἅπτεσθαι αὐτοῦ ὅτι δύναμις παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ ἐξήρχετο καὶ ἰᾶτο πάντας
verses.transliteration: kai pas ho ochlos ezētei haptesthai autou hoti dynamis par autou exērcheto kai iato pantas
verses.study_note: 'Virtue went out of him' (dynamis par autou exērcheto — power kept going out from him; dynamis = miraculous power, energy; imperfect: a continuous going out of power). 'Healed them all' (iato pantas — healed all of them). The scene is remarkable: the entire crowd seeking physical contact with Jesus, and a continuous stream of healing power going out from him to meet every need. This is not the power of a healer who is depleted by ministry but the inexhaustible power of the divine Son, channelled through human flesh. Luke's medical interest is evident in the careful language: dynamis (power/energy), iasthai (to heal), therapeuō (to treat, cure). The healing is both supernatural in origin and total in scope.

verses.kjv_text: And he lifted up his eyes on his disciples, and said, Blessed be ye poor: for yours is the kingdom of God.
verses.original_text: Καὶ αὐτὸς ἐπάρας τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ εἰς τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ ἔλεγεν Μακάριοι οἱ πτωχοί ὅτι ὑμετέρα ἐστὶν ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: Kai autos eparas tous ophthalmous autou eis tous mathētas autou elegen Makarioi hoi ptōchoi hoti hymetera estin hē basileia tou theou
verses.study_note: 'Blessed' (Makarioi — happy, blessed, fortunate; the beatitudes formula; makarios = the state of divine favour and inner joy that transcends circumstances). Luke's Beatitudes (vv.20-23) are addressed in the second person ('ye') and are more concrete than Matthew's (which are third person and spiritualised: 'poor in spirit,' 'hunger and thirst after righteousness'). 'Ye poor' (hoi ptōchoi — the literally destitute, the economically marginalised; ptōchos = one who crouches or cowers, the utterly destitute). Luke's version speaks directly to the economically poor. 'Yours is the kingdom of God' (hymetera estin hē basileia tou theou — the kingdom of God is yours; present tense: already). The kingdom is now the possession of those who have nothing to depend on but God.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are ye that hunger now: for ye shall be filled. Blessed are ye that weep now: for ye shall laugh.
verses.original_text: μακάριοι οἱ πεινῶντες νῦν ὅτι χορτασθήσεσθε μακάριοι οἱ κλαίοντες νῦν ὅτι γελάσετε
verses.transliteration: makarioi hoi peinōntes nyn hoti chortasthēsesthe makarioi hoi klaiontes nyn hoti gelasete
verses.study_note: 'Blessed are ye that hunger now' (hoi peinōntes nyn — those who are hungry now; peinaō = to be hungry, to crave food). Luke's beatitude of hunger is physical; Matthew's is spiritual ('hunger and thirst after righteousness'). Both dimensions are true: Jesus is concerned with physical hunger (cf. the feeding of the five thousand; 1:53: 'He hath filled the hungry with good things') and spiritual hunger. 'Ye shall be filled' (chortasthēsesthe — you will be satisfied, fed to fullness; chortazō = to feed to satiety, to fill completely; used of feeding animals). 'Blessed are ye that weep now' — present grief will be transformed to eschatological joy. 'Ye shall laugh' (gelasete — you will laugh; the only appearance of gelaō [laugh] in a Beatitude context in the NT). The reversal is total: mourning becomes laughter, hunger becomes fullness.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are ye, when men shall hate you, and when they shall separate you from their company, and shall reproach you, and cast out your name as evil, for the Son of man's sake.
verses.original_text: μακάριοί ἐστε ὅταν μισήσωσιν ὑμᾶς οἱ ἄνθρωποι καὶ ὅταν ἀφορίσωσιν ὑμᾶς καὶ ὀνειδίσωσιν καὶ ἐκβάλωσιν τὸ ὄνομα ὑμῶν ὡς πονηρὸν ἕνεκα τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου
verses.transliteration: makarioi este hotan misēsōsin hymas hoi anthrōpoi kai hotan aphorisōsin hymas kai oneidisōsin kai ekbalōsin to onoma hymōn hōs ponēron heneka tou huiou tou anthrōpou
verses.study_note: 'When men shall hate you, and...separate you...reproach you, and cast out your name as evil' — the fourth beatitude lists a progressive intensification of social rejection: hatred, separation (excommunication from the synagogue; aphorisōsin = to mark off, separate, ostracise), reproach (oneidizō = to upbraid, insult), and defamation (casting out your name as evil — treating your very name as something shameful to be expelled). 'For the Son of man's sake' (heneka tou huiou tou anthrōpou) — the crucial qualifier. The beatitude does not bless all suffering but specifically suffering for Jesus's sake. Persecution that has no christological reason is not blessed; persecution that comes from explicit identification with the Son of Man is the ground of the blessing.

verses.kjv_text: Rejoice ye in that day, and leap for joy: for, behold, your reward is great in heaven: for in the like manner did their fathers unto the prophets.
verses.original_text: χάρητε ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ καὶ σκιρτήσατε ἰδοὺ γὰρ ὁ μισθὸς ὑμῶν πολὺς ἐν τῷ οὐρανῷ κατὰ τὰ αὐτὰ γὰρ ἐποίουν τοῖς προφήταις οἱ πατέρες αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: charēte en ekeinē tē hēmera kai skirtēsate idou gar ho misthos hymōn polys en tō ouranō kata ta auta gar epoioun tois prophētais hoi pateres autōn
verses.study_note: 'Rejoice ye in that day, and leap for joy' (charēte kai skirtēsate — rejoice and leap/skip; skirtaō = to leap, spring, frisk; the word used of John leaping in the womb, 1:41, 44). The command to leap for joy in the face of persecution is not cruel irony but eschatological reality: the persecuted disciple stands in the succession of the OT prophets and is assured of great heavenly reward. 'Your reward is great in heaven' (ho misthos hymōn polys en tō ouranō — your reward is great/much in heaven; misthos = wages, reward, payment; polys = great, much). The reward is not vague but assured: heaven's currency is vastly more valuable than earth's honours. 'Their fathers unto the prophets' — the parallels between the treatment of OT prophets and Jesus's disciples confirms both the disciples' authenticity and the persecutors' ancestry in Israel's history of rejecting its prophets (cf. Acts 7:52).

verses.kjv_text: But woe unto you that are rich! for ye have received your consolation.
verses.original_text: πλὴν οὐαὶ ὑμῖν τοῖς πλουσίοις ὅτι ἀπέχετε τὴν παράκλησιν ὑμῶν
verses.transliteration: plēn ouai hymin tois plousiois hoti apechete tēn paraklēsin hymōn
verses.study_note: 'Woe unto you that are rich' (Ouai hymin tois plousiois — woe to you the rich ones). Luke's four Woes correspond to his four Beatitudes — a symmetrical structure. 'Woe' (ouai — a cry of grief, distress, lamentation; not a curse but an expression of prophetic grief over the one addressed). 'Ye have received your consolation' (apechete tēn paraklēsin hymōn — you have received/hold your consolation in full; apechō = to have in full, as in a receipt; used in papyri for 'paid in full'). The rich have received their comfort here and now — in their wealth, their social standing, their earthly security. Having received their portion here, they have nothing to receive in the kingdom. Not all wealth is condemned (cf. Luke 19:2-10 — Zacchaeus is wealthy); the issue is wealth as the terminus of life's comfort rather than as a stewardship.

verses.kjv_text: Woe unto you that are full! for ye shall hunger. Woe unto you that laugh now! for ye shall mourn and weep.
verses.original_text: οὐαὶ ὑμῖν οἱ ἐμπεπλησμένοι νῦν ὅτι πεινάσετε οὐαὶ οἱ γελῶντες νῦν ὅτι πενθήσετε καὶ κλαύσετε
verses.transliteration: ouai hymin hoi empeplēsmenoi nyn hoti peinasete ouai hoi gelōntes nyn hoti penthēsete kai klausete
verses.study_note: 'Full...ye shall hunger' — the second woe precisely reverses the second beatitude. 'Ye that are full' (hoi empeplēsmenoi — those who have been filled to satiety; perfect passive: thoroughly satisfied in the present). The danger of satisfaction is the cessation of hunger — including spiritual hunger. The complacency of the full is the opposite of the blessed hunger of the needy. 'Ye that laugh now' (hoi gelōntes nyn — those who laugh now; the laughter of the worldly comfortable, those for whom life's pleasures are complete). This laughter is not the joy of the persecuted (v.23, skirtaō) but the laughter of comfortable self-satisfaction. 'Ye shall mourn and weep' (penthēsete kai klausete — you will grieve and cry; pentheō = to grieve, mourn; klaiō = to weep). The eschatological reversal operates in both directions.

verses.kjv_text: Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you! for so did their fathers to the false prophets.
verses.original_text: οὐαὶ ὅταν καλῶς ὑμᾶς εἴπωσιν πάντες οἱ ἄνθρωποι κατὰ τὰ αὐτὰ γὰρ ἐποίουν τοῖς ψευδοπροφήταις οἱ πατέρες αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: ouai hotan kalōs hymas eipōsin pantes hoi anthrōpoi kata ta auta gar epoioun tois pseudoprophētais hoi pateres autōn
verses.study_note: 'When all men shall speak well of you' (hotan kalōs hymas eipōsin pantes hoi anthrōpoi — when all people speak well/beautifully of you). The fourth woe is the most counterintuitive: universal human praise is a danger sign rather than a mark of divine favour. 'Their fathers to the false prophets' — the contrast with v.23 is sharp. True prophets were persecuted; false prophets were praised. Universal popularity in a world at enmity with God signals accommodation to worldly values rather than faithful proclamation of the kingdom. The disciple who is praised by everyone has likely compromised the message that the Beatitude promises will cause persecution. Faithful proclamation inevitably creates conflict with those who prefer comfortable lies.

verses.kjv_text: But I say unto you which hear, Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you,
verses.original_text: Ἀλλὰ ὑμῖν λέγω τοῖς ἀκούουσιν ἀγαπᾶτε τοὺς ἐχθροὺς ὑμῶν καλῶς ποιεῖτε τοῖς μισοῦσιν ὑμᾶς
verses.transliteration: Alla hymin legō tois akouousin agapate tous echthrous hymōn kalōs poieite tois misousin hymas
verses.study_note: 'But I say unto you which hear' (Alla hymin legō tois akouousin — but to you I say who are hearing). The sermon now addresses those who are genuinely listening — not all present will hear in this sense. 'Love your enemies' (agapate tous echthrous hymōn — love your enemies; agapaō = the deliberate act of love as self-giving commitment, not primarily emotion; echthrous = hostile enemies, those who are actively adversarial). The command is absolute and without qualification: not 'love your enemies when they show signs of repentance' or 'love them in your heart while avoiding contact.' Love is expressed in action: 'do good to them which hate you' (kalōs poieite tois misousin — do what is good/beautiful to those who hate you). The love is active, other-directed, and specific.

verses.kjv_text: Bless them that curse you, and pray for them which despitefully use you.
verses.original_text: εὐλογεῖτε τοὺς καταρωμένους ὑμᾶς προσεύχεσθε περὶ τῶν ἐπηρεαζόντων ὑμᾶς
verses.transliteration: eulogite tous katarōmenous hymas proseuchesthe peri tōn epēreazontōn hymas
verses.study_note: 'Bless them that curse you' (eulogeite tous katarōmenous — bless those who curse you; eulogeō = to speak well of, to bless; kataraomai = to curse). The antithetical response: to each hostile action, a corresponding positive act. Cursing → blessing. Hatred → good deeds. 'Pray for them which despitefully use you' (proseuchesthe peri tōn epēreazontōn — pray concerning those who treat you abusively; epēreazō = to threaten, mistreat, to treat with contumely). Prayer for one's abusers is perhaps the most difficult command in this entire section — and the most transformative. It is impossible to pray earnestly for someone and continue to harbour hatred toward them. Prayer for enemies is both obedience and the means of grace that makes enemy-love possible.

verses.kjv_text: And unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other; and him that taketh away thy cloke forbid not to take thy coat also.
verses.original_text: τῷ τύπτοντί σε ἐπὶ τὴν σιαγόνα πάρεχε καὶ τὴν ἄλλην καὶ ἀπὸ τοῦ αἴροντός σου τὸ ἱμάτιον καὶ τὸν χιτῶνα μὴ κωλύσῃς
verses.transliteration: tō typtonti se epi tēn siagona pareche kai tēn allēn kai apo tou airontos sou to himation kai ton chitōna mē kōlysēs
verses.study_note: 'Unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other' (tō typtonti se epi tēn siagona — to the one striking you on the cheek; siagon = cheek/jaw; pareche kai tēn allēn — present also the other). The blow on the cheek was in the ancient world primarily an act of humiliation rather than violence (an insult with the back of the hand was a common formal dishonour). Jesus's command is to refuse the honour-shame cycle of retaliation: instead of demanding equal insult, offer the other cheek — disarming the cycle by refusing to play by its rules. 'Him that taketh away thy cloke' — the outer garment (himation); 'forbid not...thy coat' (chitōna — the inner garment). Give even more than is taken.

verses.kjv_text: Give to every man that asketh of thee; and of him that taketh away thy goods ask them not again.
verses.original_text: παντὶ τῷ αἰτοῦντί σε δίδου καὶ ἀπὸ τοῦ αἴροντος τὰ σά μὴ ἀπαίτει
verses.transliteration: panti tō aitounti se didou kai apo tou airontos ta sa mē apaitei
verses.study_note: 'Give to every man that asketh of thee' (panti tō aitounti se didou — give to everyone who asks you; panti = everyone without distinction; didou = keep giving, present imperative: ongoing, habitual giving). The command to give is directed at the disciple's response to any request — generous giving without prior evaluation of the requester's worthiness. 'Of him that taketh away thy goods ask them not again' (apo tou airontos ta sa mē apaitei — from the one taking your things, do not demand back). The disciple does not claim rights over material possessions to the point of retaliation or legal action. This is not an absolute prohibition of all legal recourse but a statement of the kingdom's non-possessive orientation toward material goods. Life in the kingdom is not organised around protecting property.

verses.kjv_text: And as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise.
verses.original_text: καὶ καθὼς θέλετε ἵνα ποιῶσιν ὑμῖν οἱ ἄνθρωποι ποιεῖτε αὐτοῖς ὁμοίως
verses.transliteration: kai kathōs thelete hina poiōsin hymin hoi anthrōpoi poieite autois homoiōs
verses.study_note: 'As ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise' — the Golden Rule (so called since the 17th century; also found in negative form in Tobit 4:15, Hillel, Confucius, and other traditions, but uniquely here in positive, active form: 'do to them' not merely 'do not do to them'). The positive form creates a more demanding standard: it is not enough to avoid harming others; one must actively seek their good, in the way one would wish to be actively sought out for good. The Rule is set in the context of enemy love (vv.27-30): it applies not just to friendly interactions but specifically to hostile or adversarial relationships. What would you want your enemy to do to you if they were obeying God? Do that to them.

verses.kjv_text: For if ye love them which love you, what thank have ye? for sinners also love those that love them.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἰ ἀγαπᾶτε τοὺς ἀγαπῶντας ὑμᾶς ποία ὑμῖν χάρις ἐστίν καὶ γὰρ οἱ ἁμαρτωλοὶ τοὺς ἀγαπῶντας αὐτοὺς ἀγαπῶσιν
verses.transliteration: kai ei agapate tous agapōntas hymas poia hymin charis estin kai gar hoi hamartōloi tous agapōntas autous agapōsin
verses.study_note: 'What thank have ye?' (poia hymin charis estin — what grace/credit is it for you?; charis = grace, favour, gratitude; here 'credit'). Loving those who love you is natural reciprocity — it requires no divine grace and produces no distinctive witness. 'Sinners also love those that love them' — even those who make no claim to religious virtue love their own circle. The standard for kingdom love is not the standard of the street but the standard of the Father (v.36). Three times (vv.32, 33, 34) Jesus applies the same argument: if your love/goodness/lending is only directed at those who can reciprocate, it is no different from ordinary human behaviour, which requires no divine enablement and produces no kingdom witness.

verses.kjv_text: And if ye do good to them which do good to you, what thank have ye? for sinners also do even the same.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐὰν ἀγαθοποιῆτε τοὺς ἀγαθοποιοῦντας ὑμᾶς ποία ὑμῖν χάρις ἐστίν καὶ γὰρ οἱ ἁμαρτωλοὶ τὸ αὐτὸ ποιοῦσιν
verses.transliteration: kai ean agathopoiēte tous agathopoiountas hymas poia hymin charis estin kai gar hoi hamartōloi to auto poiousin
verses.study_note: The second application of the same argument: reciprocal goodness (doing good to those who do good to you) is the standard of even 'sinners' (hamartōloi — those who make no claim to righteousness). The word 'sinners' here is used sociologically, not theologically — it refers to the people the Pharisees designated as publicly disreputable. Even they practise mutual benefit within their own networks. Kingdom ethics are not measured against the standard of the worst but against the standard of the Father. The Christian call is to exceed ordinary human reciprocity with the unconditional, initiative-taking love that mirrors God's own character.

verses.kjv_text: And if ye lend to them of whom ye hope to receive, what thank have ye? for sinners also lend to sinners, to receive as much again.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐὰν δανίζητε παρ᾽ ὧν ἐλπίζετε λαβεῖν ποία ὑμῖν χάρις ἐστίν καὶ γὰρ ἁμαρτωλοὶ ἁμαρτωλοῖς δανίζουσιν ἵνα ἀπολάβωσιν τὰ ἴσα
verses.transliteration: kai ean danizēte par hōn elpizete labein poia hymin charis estin kai gar hamartōloi hamartōlois danizousin hina apolabosin ta isa
verses.study_note: The third application: lending with expectation of return is commercial calculation, not grace. In the ancient world, lending to the wealthy with expectation of reciprocal favour was the foundation of social networks. Jesus asks for a different kind of lending: without the expectation of getting back what was lent (v.35). The pagan system of reciprocal lending maintained social hierarchies; the kingdom system creates new communities of grace across social strata. The test of kingdom generosity is whether it extends beyond the network of those who can reciprocate — whether it reaches the poor, the excluded, the ones who cannot pay back.

verses.kjv_text: But love ye your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again: and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be the children of the Highest: for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil.
verses.original_text: πλὴν ἀγαπᾶτε τοὺς ἐχθροὺς ὑμῶν καὶ ἀγαθοποιεῖτε καὶ δανίζετε μηδὲν ἀπελπίζοντες καὶ ἔσται ὁ μισθὸς ὑμῶν πολύς καὶ ἔσεσθε υἱοὶ ὑψίστου ὅτι αὐτὸς χρηστός ἐστιν ἐπὶ τοὺς ἀχαρίστους καὶ πονηρούς
verses.transliteration: plēn agapate tous echthrous hymōn kai agathopoieite kai danizete mēden apelpizontes kai estai ho misthos hymōn polys kai esesthe huioi hypsistou hoti autos chrēstos estin epi tous acharistous kai ponērous
verses.study_note: 'Lend, hoping for nothing again' (danizete mēden apelpizontes — lend, despairing of nothing in return; or: without expecting anything back; apelpizō = to give up hope of return). 'Your reward shall be great' (ho misthos hymōn polys — your reward is great; the reward is not the human reciprocation foregone but God's own eschatological compensation). 'Ye shall be the children of the Highest' (esesthe huioi hypsistou — you will be sons of the Most High). Sons resemble their Father — acting as God acts creates the resemblance that identifies us as his children. 'He is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil' (autos chrēstos estin epi tous acharistous kai ponērous — he himself is kind toward the ungrateful and evil ones). God's love is not conditional on the recipient's gratitude or goodness. Our enemy-love is to mirror this divine pattern.

verses.kjv_text: Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful.
verses.original_text: Γίνεσθε οὖν οἰκτίρμονες καθὼς καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ὑμῶν οἰκτίρμων ἐστίν
verses.transliteration: Ginesthe oun oiktirmones kathōs kai ho patēr hymōn oiktirmōn estin
verses.study_note: 'Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful' (Ginesthe oiktirmones kathōs kai ho patēr hymōn oiktirmōn estin — be becoming merciful as also your Father is merciful; ginesthe = become, a process; oiktirmōn = compassionate, full of tender mercy; oiktirmos = tender compassion, bowels of mercy; the Hebrew racham, from which rachamim, the womb-like love of a mother for her child). Matthew's parallel (5:48) says 'Be ye therefore perfect, as your Father...is perfect'; Luke says 'merciful,' capturing the specific aspect of divine perfection being called for in the context of enemy love. The foundation of the entire ethical teaching is theocentric and theomimetic: become what God is, do what God does. The motivation for enemy love is not pragmatic benefit but imitation of the Father.

verses.kjv_text: Judge not, and ye shall not be judged: condemn not, and ye shall not be condemned: forgive, and ye shall be forgiven:
verses.original_text: Καὶ μὴ κρίνετε καὶ οὐ μὴ κριθῆτε καὶ μὴ καταδικάζετε καὶ οὐ μὴ καταδικασθῆτε ἀπολύετε καὶ ἀπολυθήσεσθε
verses.transliteration: Kai mē krinete kai ou mē krithēte kai mē katadikazete kai ou mē katadikasthēte apolyete kai apolythēsesthe
verses.study_note: 'Judge not' (mē krinete — do not judge; the present imperative: stop judging, do not make it your practice). The prohibition is not against all discernment or evaluation (v.39-42 require evaluation of one's own state) but against the hypocritical, censorious judging of others that ignores one's own failures. 'Ye shall not be judged' (ou mē krithēte — the double negative of emphatic denial: you absolutely will not be judged). The divine principle of correspondence: the standard you apply to others becomes the standard applied to you. 'Condemn not' (mē katadikazete — do not condemn; katadikazō = to pronounce guilty, to sentence; a stronger word than krinō). 'Forgive' (apolyete — release, let go; apolyo = to release from obligation or penalty). The three commands form a progression: refrain from judging, refrain from condemning, actively forgive.

verses.kjv_text: Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom. For with the same measure that ye mete withal it shall be measured to you again.
verses.original_text: δίδοτε καὶ δοθήσεται ὑμῖν μέτρον καλὸν πεπιεσμένον σεσαλευμένον ὑπερεκχυνόμενον δώσουσιν εἰς τὸν κόλπον ὑμῶν τῷ γὰρ αὐτῷ μέτρῳ ᾧ μετρεῖτε ἀντιμετρηθήσεται ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: didote kai dothēsetai hymin metron kalon pepiesmenon sesalemenon hyperekchynomenon dōsousin eis ton kolpon hymōn tō gar autō metrō hō metreite antimetrēthēsetai hymin
verses.study_note: 'Give, and it shall be given unto you' (didote kai dothēsetai hymin — keep giving, and it will be given to you). The positive counterpart to vv.37: just as withholding leads to withholding, giving leads to receiving. 'Good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over' (metron kalon pepiesmenon sesalemenon hyperekchynomenon — a good/beautiful measure, having been pressed down, having been shaken, overflowing). The imagery is of a grain merchant who fills the measure, presses it down to remove air pockets, shakes it to settle the grain, and then piles it up until it overflows. God gives with extravagant generosity beyond what is owed. 'The same measure' (tō autō metrō — the same measure; the measure of generosity is returned in kind from God). The principle of correspondence operates in both directions.

verses.kjv_text: And he spake a parable unto them, Can the blind lead the blind? shall they not both fall into the ditch?
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ παραβολὴν αὐτοῖς Μήτι δύναται τυφλὸς τυφλὸν ὁδηγεῖν οὐχὶ ἀμφότεροι εἰς βόθυνον πεσοῦνται
verses.transliteration: Eipen de parabolēn autois Mēti dynatai typhlos typhlon hodēgein ouchi amphoteroi eis bothynon pesountai
verses.study_note: 'Can the blind lead the blind?' (Mēti dynatai typhlos typhlon hodēgein — can a blind person guide a blind person?; mēti = surely not? — expecting a negative answer). 'Both fall into the ditch' (amphoteroi eis bothynon pesountai — both will fall into a pit). The parable of the blind leading the blind warns against following teachers or leaders who are themselves spiritually blind. In context (after the warnings about judging), it addresses the danger of the Pharisees guiding the people: their moral blindness (inability to see their own failures) disqualifies them from guiding others. The application to the disciples is also present: one must have the log removed from one's own eye (v.42) before guiding others.

verses.kjv_text: The disciple is not above his master: but every one that is perfect shall be as his master.
verses.original_text: οὐκ ἔστιν μαθητὴς ὑπὲρ τὸν διδάσκαλον κατηρτισμένος δὲ πᾶς ἔσται ὡς ὁ διδάσκαλος αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: ouk estin mathētēs hyper ton didaskalon katērtismenos de pas estai hōs ho didaskalos autou
verses.study_note: 'The disciple is not above his master' (ouk estin mathētēs hyper ton didaskalon — a disciple is not above his teacher; meizon = greater, above; in Luke the context is: the disciple follows the teacher's lead, not exceeds it). 'Every one that is perfect shall be as his master' (katērtismenos de pas estai hōs ho didaskalos autou — one who has been fully trained will be as his teacher; katartizō = to fit together, prepare completely, restore; the word used in Ephesians 4:12 for 'perfecting of the saints'). The goal of discipleship is not to exceed the teacher but to become like him — full conformity to the teacher's character and practice. Applied to following Jesus: the goal is Christlikeness, not spiritual self-improvement beyond Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother's eye, but perceivest not the beam that is in thine own eye?
verses.original_text: τί δὲ βλέπεις τὸ κάρφος τὸ ἐν τῷ ὀφθαλμῷ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ σου τὴν δὲ δοκὸν τὴν ἐν τῷ ἰδίῳ ὀφθαλμῷ οὐ κατανοεῖς
verses.transliteration: ti de blepeis to karphos to en tō ophthalmō tou adelphou sou tēn de dokon tēn en tō idiō ophthalmō ou katanöeis
verses.study_note: 'The mote...the beam' (karphos...dokon — a tiny chip/speck of wood...a house-beam; karphos = a dry stalk, fragment, tiny piece of wood; dokos = a load-bearing wooden beam). The contrast is comic in scale: a wood chip vs. a wooden roof beam. The hypocrisy of criticising a small fault in a brother while carrying a massive fault of one's own is both absurd and universal. 'Beholdest' (blepeis — you see, notice; kata-noeis = you do not thoroughly notice or consider). The different verbs indicate: you spot (blepeis) the tiny speck but you do not carefully consider (katanoeō = to consider thoroughly, to contemplate) the enormous beam. Self-deception is the consistent obstacle to both authentic self-knowledge and genuine service to others.

verses.kjv_text: Either how canst thou say to thy brother, Brother, let me pull out the mote that is in thine eye, when thou thyself beholdest not the beam that is in thine own eye? Thou hypocrite, cast out first the beam out of thine own eye, and then shalt thou see clearly to pull out the mote that is in thy brother's eye.
verses.original_text: πῶς δύνασαι λέγειν τῷ ἀδελφῷ σου Ἀδελφέ ἄφες ἐκβάλω τὸ κάρφος τὸ ἐν τῷ ὀφθαλμῷ σου αὐτὸς τὴν ἐν τῷ ὀφθαλμῷ σου δοκὸν οὐ βλέπων ὑποκριτά ἔκβαλε πρῶτον τὴν δοκὸν ἐκ τοῦ ὀφθαλμοῦ σου καὶ τότε διαβλέψεις ἐκβαλεῖν τὸ κάρφος τὸ ἐν τῷ ὀφθαλμῷ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ σου
verses.transliteration: pōs dynasai legein tō adelphō sou Adelphe aphes ekbalō to karphos to en tō ophthalmō sou autos tēn en tō ophthalmō sou dokon ou blepōn hypokritā ekbale prōton tēn dokon ek tou ophthalmou sou kai tote diablepseis ekbalein to karphos to en tō ophthalmō tou adelphou sou
verses.study_note: 'Thou hypocrite' (hypokrita — actor, pretender; hypokritēs = one who answers from behind a mask, an actor playing a role; our word 'hypocrite'). The strong address is directed at the person who offers to remove the tiny speck from another's eye while unaware of the beam in their own. 'Cast out first the beam out of thine own eye' — the sequence is crucial: first, self-examination and self-correction; then, and only then, helping the brother. 'Then shalt thou see clearly' (tote diablepseis — then you will see through/clearly; diablepo = to see clearly, thoroughly). The mote-removal is not prohibited — helping a brother with his failings is legitimate — but only after one's own vision has been cleared. Self-knowledge is the prerequisite for genuinely helpful community.

verses.kjv_text: For a good tree bringeth not forth corrupt fruit; neither doth a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit.
verses.original_text: Οὐ γάρ ἐστιν δένδρον καλὸν ποιοῦν καρπὸν σαπρόν οὐδὲ πάλιν δένδρον σαπρὸν ποιοῦν καρπὸν καλόν
verses.transliteration: Ou gar estin dendron kalon poioun karpon sapron oude palin dendron sapron poioun karpon kalon
verses.study_note: 'A good tree bringeth not forth corrupt fruit' (dendron kalon...karpon sapron — a beautiful/good tree does not produce rotten/corrupt fruit). The tree-fruit parable (vv.43-45) shifts from the diagnosis of hypocrisy to the underlying principle: character determines conduct. 'Good' (kalon — beautiful, sound, excellent) vs. 'corrupt' (sapron — rotten, decayed; the word used for spoiled food). The consistency between tree and fruit is the point: a healthy tree consistently produces good fruit; a diseased tree cannot. This is not perfectionism but a statement about the consistent direction of a life — what one habitually produces reveals what one essentially is. The application follows in v.45: what comes out of the mouth reveals what is in the heart.

verses.kjv_text: For every tree is known by his own fruit. For of thorns men do not gather figs, nor of a bramble bush gather they grapes.
verses.original_text: ἕκαστον γὰρ δένδρον ἐκ τοῦ ἰδίου καρποῦ γινώσκεται οὐ γὰρ ἐξ ἀκανθῶν συλλέγουσιν σῦκα οὐδὲ ἐκ βάτου σταφυλὴν τρυγῶσιν
verses.transliteration: hekaston gar dendron ek tou idiou karpou ginōsketai ou gar ex akanthōn syllegousin syka oude ek batou staphylēn trygōsin
verses.study_note: 'Every tree is known by his own fruit' (hekaston dendron ek tou idiou karpou ginōsketai — each tree is known from its own fruit). The discernment criterion: not self-report or social reputation but observable fruit. 'Of thorns men do not gather figs, nor of a bramble bush gather they grapes' (akanthōn...syka; batou...staphylēn — thorns...figs; bramble...grapes). The botanical impossibility underlines the moral principle: a thornbush cannot produce figs by trying harder; it is constitutively unable. Similarly, a person of corrupt character (whether a teacher, leader, or ordinary disciple) cannot produce consistently good fruit without being constitutively transformed. Only grace produces genuine change of character, not merely behavioural improvement.

verses.kjv_text: A good man out of the good treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is good; and an evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is evil: for of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaketh.
verses.original_text: ὁ ἀγαθὸς ἄνθρωπος ἐκ τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ θησαυροῦ τῆς καρδίας αὐτοῦ προφέρει τὸ ἀγαθόν καὶ ὁ πονηρὸς ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ προφέρει τὸ πονηρόν ἐκ γὰρ περισσεύματος καρδίας λαλεῖ τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: ho agathos anthrōpos ek tou agathou thēsaurou tēs kardias autou propherei to agathon kai ho ponēros ek tou ponērou propherei to ponēron ek gar perisseumatos kardias lalei to stoma autou
verses.study_note: 'Out of the good treasure of his heart' (ek tou agathou thēsaurou tēs kardias — from the good treasury/storehouse of the heart; thēsauros = treasure, storehouse). The heart is the storehouse from which life's words and deeds are drawn. 'The abundance of the heart' (perisseumatos kardias — the overflow of the heart; perisseuō = to abound, overflow; what overflows from what is stored in the heart). The mouth speaks from heart-overflow — in moments of pressure, emotion, or spontaneity, what is genuinely stored in the heart emerges. You cannot produce good speech from a heart that has not been transformed (cf. Jeremiah 17:9-10: 'The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked: who can know it? I the LORD search the heart'). The sermon is calling for heart transformation, not merely behavioural compliance.

verses.kjv_text: And why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?
verses.original_text: Τί δέ με καλεῖτε Κύριε κύριε καὶ οὐ ποιεῖτε ἃ λέγω
verses.transliteration: Ti de me kaleite Kyrie kyrie kai ou poieite ha legō
verses.study_note: 'Why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?' (Ti de me kaleite Kyrie kyrie kai ou poieite ha legō — why do you keep calling me Lord Lord but not doing what I say?). The double 'Lord, Lord' (Kyrie kyrie — the repetition of the address emphasises the fervency and sincerity of the confession; cf. Matthew 7:21-23 where the same double address introduces those who performed miracles in Jesus's name but were rejected). Calling Jesus 'Lord' is not the problem — failing to do what the Lord commands is the problem. The disconnect between confession and obedience is the fundamental failure of false discipleship. Verbal acknowledgment of Jesus's lordship without practical submission to his teaching is both inconsistent and dangerous (v.49).

verses.kjv_text: Whosoever cometh to me, and heareth my sayings, and doeth them, I will shew you to whom he is like:
verses.original_text: πᾶς ὁ ἐρχόμενος πρός με καὶ ἀκούων μου τῶν λόγων καὶ ποιῶν αὐτούς ὑποδείξω ὑμῖν τίνι ἐστὶν ὅμοιος
verses.transliteration: pas ho erchomenos pros me kai akouōn mou tōn logōn kai poiōn autous hypodeixō hymin tini estin homoios
verses.study_note: 'Cometh to me, and heareth my sayings, and doeth them' — three-stage discipleship: coming (relationship), hearing (attention), doing (obedience). All three are necessary. Coming without hearing is formalism; hearing without doing is self-deception (James 1:22: 'be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving your own selves'); doing without coming is moralism. The complete disciple does all three. 'I will shew you to whom he is like' (hypodeixō hymin tini estin homoios — I will show you what he is comparable to; hypodeiknymi = to show, indicate, point out). Jesus introduces the parable of the two builders to make the comparison vivid and memorable.

verses.kjv_text: He is like a man which built an house, and digged deep, and laid the foundation on a rock: and when the flood arose, the stream beat vehemently upon that house, and could not shake it: for it was founded upon a rock.
verses.original_text: ὅμοιός ἐστιν ἀνθρώπῳ οἰκοδομοῦντι οἰκίαν ὃς ἔσκαψεν καὶ ἐβάθυνεν καὶ ἔθηκεν θεμέλιον ἐπὶ τὴν πέτραν πλημμύρης δὲ γενομένης προσέρρηξεν ὁ ποταμὸς τῇ οἰκίᾳ ἐκείνῃ καὶ οὐκ ἴσχυσεν σαλεῦσαι αὐτὴν διὰ τὸ καλῶς οἰκοδομῆσθαι αὐτήν
verses.transliteration: homoios estin anthrōpō oikodomonti oikian hos eskapsen kai ebathynen kai ethēken themelion epi tēn petran plēmmyrēs de genomenēs prosereēxen ho potamos tē oikia ekeinē kai ouk ischysen saleusei autēn dia to kalōs oikodomos esthai autēn
verses.study_note: 'Digged deep, and laid the foundation on a rock' (eskapsen kai ebathynen kai ethēken themelion epi tēn petran — he dug and went deep and placed a foundation on the rock). Luke's version of the parable is more specific than Matthew's about the digging: the builder works hard to find bedrock. 'When the flood arose' (plēmmyrēs de genomenēs — when a flooding came; plēmmyra = a flood, high water). 'The stream beat vehemently' (prosreexen ho potamos — the river crashed against). 'Could not shake it: for it was founded upon a rock' (ouk ischysen saleusei...dia to kalōs oikodomos esthai — was not able to shake it because it had been well built). The obedient disciple's life withstands crisis because it is built on the reality of Jesus's teaching, not merely on comfortable religious experience.

verses.kjv_text: But he that heareth, and doeth not, is like a man that without a foundation built an house upon the earth; against which the stream did beat vehemently, and immediately it fell; and the ruin of that house was great.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἀκούσας καὶ μὴ ποιήσας ὅμοιός ἐστιν ἀνθρώπῳ οἰκοδομήσαντι οἰκίαν ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν χωρὶς θεμελίου ᾗ προσέρρηξεν ὁ ποταμός καὶ εὐθὺς ἔπεσεν καὶ ἐγένετο τὸ ῥῆγμα τῆς οἰκίας ἐκείνης μέγα
verses.transliteration: ho de akousas kai mē poiēsas homoios estin anthrōpō oikodomeēsanti oikian epi tēn gēn chōris themelious hē prosereexen ho potamos kai euthys epesen kai egeneto to rhēgma tēs oikias ekeinēs mega
verses.study_note: 'Without a foundation built an house upon the earth' (chōris themelou — without a foundation; themelios = founding stone, foundation; chōris = apart from, without). The hearer who does not obey builds on the surface — no digging, no hard work, no bedrock. The house looks identical from the outside; the difference is invisible until the storm comes. 'The stream did beat vehemently, and immediately it fell' (prosreexen ho potamos kai euthys epesen — the river crashed and immediately it fell; euthys = immediately, at once). The collapse is sudden and total. 'The ruin of that house was great' (to rhēgma tēs oikias ekeinēs mega — the crack/collapse of that house was great; rhēgma = fracture, collapse). The sermon ends on a solemn note of warning: the stakes of hearing and not doing are catastrophic. The life built on Jesus's word withstands all storms; the life built on profession without obedience collapses utterly when tested.

meta.overview: Luke 7 presents a gallery of faith and compassion that reveals the identity of Jesus as the messianic Son of God. The chapter opens with the centurion of Capernaum — a Gentile soldier who demonstrates a quality of faith Jesus has not found even in Israel. His humble acknowledgment that a word from Jesus suffices reveals the unlimited authority of the Saviour over distance and disease. This Gentile's faith stands as an implicit rebuke to the unbelief of those who have had every privilege of Torah and temple.
meta.theme: The raising of the widow's son at Nain (vv.11-17) is Luke's only resurrection miracle unique to his Gospel, chosen to demonstrate that Jesus is the Lord of life as well as healer of disease. The compassion of Jesus is not merely emotional sympathy but divine action that overturns death itself. The crowd's response — "God hath visited his people" — echoes Zechariah's Benedictus and confirms the fulfilment of prophetic hope. Together the two miracles illustrate the twin categories of messianic work recited in verse 22: healing and resurrection.
meta.keyWordOriginal: The embassy from John the Baptist (vv.18-35) forms the theological centre of the chapter. John, imprisoned and hearing of the works of Christ, sends to ask whether Jesus is truly "he that should come." Jesus's answer is not a direct affirmation but a recital of messianic deeds drawn from Isaiah, each category corresponding to OT prophetic expectation. Blessed is the man who is not offended by the form in which the Messiah comes. Jesus then turns to the crowd and delivers the highest tribute ever paid to a human being: John is greater than any prophet, yet the least in the kingdom is greater still — not by personal merit but by participatory privilege.
meta.keyWordMeaning: The chapter closes with the most intimate scene of the entire chapter: a sinful woman who washes Jesus's feet with her tears, dries them with her hair, and anoints them with ointment. Simon the Pharisee's silent contempt provides the foil for Jesus's parable of two debtors. The key theological syllogism is powerful: she loved much because she was forgiven much. The forgiveness was not earned by the love — the love was the evidence that forgiveness had already occurred. The declaration "Thy faith hath saved thee; go in peace" closes the chapter with the same gospel offer that echoes through all of Luke.
outline: θεραπεύω (therapeuō)

verses.kjv_text: Now when he had ended all his sayings in the audience of the people, he entered into Capernaum.
verses.original_text: Ἐπειδὴ ἐπλήρωσεν πάντα τὰ ῥήματα αὐτοῦ εἰς τὰς ἀκοὰς τοῦ λαοῦ, εἰσῆλθεν εἰς Καφαρναούμ.
verses.transliteration: Epeidē eplērōsen panta ta rhēmata autou eis tas akoas tou laou, eisēlthen eis Kapharnaum.
verses.study_note: The transition from the Sermon on the Plain is smooth and immediate. "All his sayings" (panta ta rhēmata) signals that the discourse was complete, not interrupted. Capernaum was Jesus's ministry base (4:31), the city of headquarters where he would encounter the next test of his authority.

verses.kjv_text: And a certain centurion's servant, who was dear unto him, was sick, and ready to die.
verses.original_text: Ἑκατοντάρχου δέ τινος δοῦλος κακῶς ἔχων ἤμελλεν τελευτᾶν, ὃς ἦν αὐτῷ ἔντιμος.
verses.transliteration: Hekatontarchou de tinos doulos kakōs echōn ēmellen teleutān, hos ēn autō entimos.
verses.study_note: The centurion commanded 100 Roman soldiers. His servant (doulos, a slave) was "dear unto him" (entimos — highly valued, esteemed). The word is used elsewhere for precious stones, indicating genuine personal regard rather than mere economic loss. The illness is described as near-fatal, setting the stage for an urgent appeal.

verses.kjv_text: And when he heard of Jesus, he sent unto him the elders of the Jews, beseeching him that he would come and heal his servant.
verses.original_text: ἀκούσας δὲ περὶ τοῦ Ἰησοῦ, ἀπέστειλεν πρὸς αὐτὸν πρεσβυτέρους τῶν Ἰουδαίων, ἐρωτῶν αὐτὸν ὅπως ἐλθὼν διασώσῃ τὸν δοῦλον αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: akousas de peri tou Iēsou, apesteilen pros auton presbuterous tōn Ioudaiōn, erōtōn auton hopōs elthōn diasōsē ton doulon autou.
verses.study_note: The centurion's approach through Jewish elders shows cultural sensitivity and respect for Jewish customs. He does not presume to approach a Jewish rabbi directly. Faith here is not passive belief but active, resourceful pursuit of help. "Beseeching" (erōtōn) is the imperfect tense, suggesting persistent appeal.

verses.kjv_text: And when they came to Jesus, they besought him instantly, saying, That he was worthy for whom he should do this:
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ παραγενόμενοι πρὸς τὸν Ἰησοῦν παρεκάλουν αὐτὸν σπουδαίως, λέγοντες ὅτι Ἄξιός ἐστιν ᾧ παρέξῃ τοῦτο,
verses.transliteration: hoi de paragenomenoi pros ton Iēsoun parekaloun auton spoudaiōs, legontes hoti Axios estin hō parexē touto,
verses.study_note: The elders' intercession is earnest (spoudaiōs — urgently, diligently). They present the centurion's worthiness (axios) based on his deeds, which is the human evaluation. Jesus's response will reframe "worthiness" in terms of faith, not works. The elders argue from merit; the centurion will argue from unworthiness.

verses.kjv_text: For he loveth our nation, and he hath built us a synagogue.
verses.original_text: ἀγαπᾷ γὰρ τὸ ἔθνος ἡμῶν, καὶ τὴν συναγωγὴν αὐτὸς ᾠκοδόμησεν ἡμῖν.
verses.transliteration: agapa gar to ethnos hēmōn, kai tēn sunagōgēn autos ōkodomēsen hēmin.
verses.study_note: The centurion's synagogue-building demonstrates concrete benevolence. Such patronage was unusual and evidenced genuine love for the Jewish people, perhaps reflecting a God-fearer's attraction to Jewish monotheism. Luke, writing for Gentile readers, presents this Gentile as a model of generosity and faith.

verses.kjv_text: Then Jesus went with them. And when he was now not far from the house, the centurion sent friends to him, saying unto him, Lord, trouble not thyself: for I am not worthy that thou shouldest enter under my roof:
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς ἐπορεύετο σὺν αὐτοῖς. ἤδη δὲ αὐτοῦ οὐ μακρὰν ἀπέχοντος ἀπὸ τῆς οἰκίας, ἔπεμψεν φίλους ὁ ἑκατοντάρχης λέγων αὐτῷ, Κύριε, μὴ σκύλλου· οὐ γὰρ ἱκανός εἰμι ἵνα ὑπὸ τὴν στέγην μου εἰσέλθῃς·
verses.transliteration: ho de Iēsous eporeueto sun autois. ēdē de autou ou makran apechontos apo tēs oikias, epempsen philous ho hekatontarchēs legōn autō, Kurie, mē skulou; ou gar hikanos eimi hina hupo tēn stegēn mou eiselthēs;
verses.study_note: "Lord" (Kurie) acknowledges Jesus's authority. "I am not worthy" (hikanos — sufficient, fit) is the opposite of what the elders said. This humility is not false modesty but a profound recognition of the holiness of Jesus. The word hikanos is related to the Baptist's "not worthy to unloose his sandals" (3:16), linking the centurion's faith to John's Christology.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore neither thought I myself worthy to come unto thee: but say in a word, and my servant shall be healed.
verses.original_text: διὸ οὐδὲ ἐμαυτὸν ἠξίωσα πρὸς σέ ἐλθεῖν· ἀλλὰ εἰπὲ λόγῳ, καὶ ἰαθήτω ὁ παῖς μου.
verses.transliteration: dio oude emauton ēxiōsa pros se elthein; alla eipe logō, kai iathētō ho pais mou.
verses.study_note: "Say in a word" (eipe logō — say with/by a word) is the heart of this remarkable faith. The centurion does not ask Jesus to touch, pray, or perform a ritual — just to speak. This is creation-faith: trust that the word of Jesus has power equivalent to the word of God in Genesis. "My servant" here uses paida, possibly indicating a higher-status household servant or even a young boy.

verses.kjv_text: For I also am a man set under authority, having under me soldiers, and I say unto one, Go, and he goeth; and to another, Come, and he cometh; and to my servant, Do this, and he doeth it.
verses.original_text: καὶ γὰρ ἐγὼ ἄνθρωπός εἰμι ὑπὸ ἐξουσίαν τασσόμενος, ἔχων ὑπ' ἐμαυτὸν στρατιώτας, καὶ λέγω τούτῳ, Πορεύθητι, καὶ πορεύεται, καὶ ἄλλῳ, Ἔρχου, καὶ ἔρχεται, καὶ τῷ δούλῳ μου, Ποίησον τοῦτο, καὶ ποιεῖ.
verses.transliteration: kai gar egō anthrōpos eimi hupo exousian tassomenos, echōn hup' emauton stratiōtas, kai legō toutō, Poreuthēti, kai poreuetai, kai allō, Erchou, kai erchetai, kai tō doulō mou, Poiēson touto, kai poiei.
verses.study_note: The a fortiori argument is brilliant: if I — a mere man under authority — can command soldiers with a word and they obey, how much more can you — who possess supreme authority — command disease with a word? The centurion understands authority structures better than the religious leaders. Exousia (authority) is the key word; the same word was used of Jesus's teaching in 4:32.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus heard these things, he marvelled at him, and turned him about, and said unto the people that followed him, I say unto you, I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel.
verses.original_text: ἀκούσας δὲ ταῦτα ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐθαύμασεν αὐτόν, καὶ στραφεὶς τῷ ἀκολουθοῦντι αὐτῷ ὄχλῳ εἶπεν, Λέγω ὑμῖν, οὐδὲ ἐν τῷ Ἰσραὴλ τοσαύτην πίστιν εὗρον.
verses.transliteration: akousas de tauta ho Iēsous ethaumasen auton, kai strapheis tō akolouthounti autō ochlō eipen, Legō humin, oude en tō Israēl tosautēn pistin heuron.
verses.study_note: Jesus "marvelled" (ethaumasen) — one of only two instances in the Gospels where Jesus marvels (the other is Mark 6:6, at unbelief). Here he marvels at faith. The public declaration — "not in Israel" — is a stunning rebuke. A Gentile soldier surpasses the covenant people in faith. This anticipates the universal mission and the inclusion of Gentiles in Luke-Acts.

verses.kjv_text: And they that were sent, returning to the house, found the servant whole that had been sick.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὑποστρέψαντες οἱ πεμφθέντες εἰς τὸν οἶκον εὗρον τὸν δοῦλον ὑγιαίνοντα.
verses.transliteration: kai hupostrepsantes hoi pemphthenetes eis ton oikon heuron ton doulon hugiainonta.
verses.study_note: The healing is instantaneous and complete (hugiainonta — in a state of health, whole). Matthew's parallel (8:13) notes it happened "in that selfsame hour." Luke does not specify the moment but focuses on the discovery of wholeness. The faith of the centurion, not the physical presence of Jesus, was the channel through which healing came — demonstrating that faith, not proximity, is the operative factor.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass the day after, that he went into a city called Nain; and many of his disciples went with him, and much people.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῇ ἑξῆς ἐπορεύθη εἰς πόλιν καλουμένην Ναΐν· καὶ συνεπορεύοντο αὐτῷ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ ἱκανοὶ καὶ ὄχλος πολύς.
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en tē hexēs eporeuēthē eis polin kaloumenēn Nain; kai suneporeuonto autō hoi mathētai autou hikanoi kai ochlos polus.
verses.study_note: Nain is about 25 miles south of Capernaum in Galilee, near Shunem where Elisha raised the Shunammite's son (2 Kgs 4:8-37). The geographical echo is deliberate — Luke is about to present Jesus as greater than Elisha. The large company with Jesus creates a double procession at the city gate: the crowd of life meeting the procession of death.

verses.kjv_text: Now when he came nigh to the gate of the city, behold, there was a dead man carried out, the only son of his mother, and she was a widow: and much people of the city was with her.
verses.original_text: ὡς δὲ ἤγγισεν τῇ πύλῃ τῆς πόλεως, καὶ ἰδοὺ ἐξεκομίζετο τεθνηκὼς υἱὸς μονογενὴς τῇ μητρὶ αὐτοῦ, καὶ αὐτὴ ἦν χήρα, καὶ ὄχλος τῆς πόλεως ἱκανὸς ἦν σὺν αὐτῇ.
verses.transliteration: hōs de ēngisen tē pulē tēs poleōs, kai idou exekomizeto tethnēkōs huios monogenēs tē mētri autou, kai autē ēn chēra, kai ochlos tēs poleōs hikanos ēn sun autē.
verses.study_note: "Only son" (monogenēs) is the same word used of Isaac and of Jesus himself in John 3:16. The woman is a widow — already bereft of her husband, now losing her only son, her sole support and protection in an ancient world with no social safety net. Luke's repeated attention to widows (2:37, 4:25-26, 18:1-8, 21:2) reflects both his pastoral concern and Jesus's.

verses.kjv_text: And when the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her, and said unto her, Weep not.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδὼν αὐτὴν ὁ Κύριος ἐσπλαγχνίσθη ἐπ' αὐτῇ, καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῇ, Μὴ κλαῖε.
verses.transliteration: kai idōn autēn ho Kurios esplanchnisthē ep' autē, kai eipen autē, Mē klaie.
verses.study_note: Luke uses "the Lord" (Kurios) as a narrative title for Jesus — a post-resurrection perspective written back into the ministry account, affirming his divine identity. "Had compassion" (esplanchnisthē) is from splanchnon (bowels, the seat of deep emotion in Jewish thought). This is not sentiment but the visceral mercy of God encountering human suffering. No request was made — the compassion of Jesus moved first.

verses.kjv_text: And he came and touched the bier: and they that bare him stood still. And he said, Young man, I say unto thee, Arise.
verses.original_text: καὶ προσελθὼν ἥψατο τῆς σοροῦ, οἱ δὲ βαστάζοντες ἔστησαν, καὶ εἶπεν, Νεανίσκε, σοὶ λέγω, ἐγέρθητι.
verses.transliteration: kai proselthōn hēpsato tēs sorou, hoi de bastazontes estēsan, kai eipen, Neaniske, soi legō, egerthēti.
verses.study_note: Touching the bier (soros — funeral stretcher/coffin) rendered a person ceremonially unclean under the Law (Num 19:11,16). Jesus deliberately touches the untouchable — as with the leper (5:13) — not to be defiled but to cleanse and restore. "Young man, I say unto thee" (Neaniske, soi legō) echoes Elisha's "arise" to the Shunammite's son. But Elisha prayed; Jesus commands. The authority is direct and personal.

verses.kjv_text: And he that was dead sat up, and began to speak. And he delivered him to his mother.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀνεκάθισεν ὁ νεκρὸς καὶ ἤρξατο λαλεῖν· καὶ ἔδωκεν αὐτὸν τῇ μητρὶ αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai anekathisen ho nekros kai ērxato lalein; kai edōken auton tē mētri autou.
verses.study_note: "He that was dead sat up" (anekathisen ho nekros) — Luke the physician notes the physical action precisely. The use of nekros (dead one) while describing him sitting up creates a dramatic juxtaposition. "He delivered him to his mother" exactly echoes the LXX of 1 Kings 17:23, where Elijah restores the widow of Zarephath's son — a deliberate literary echo confirming Jesus as the fulfilment of prophetic ministry.

verses.kjv_text: And there came a fear on all: and they glorified God, saying, That a great prophet is risen up among us; and, That God hath visited his people.
verses.original_text: ἔλαβεν δὲ φόβος πάντας, καὶ ἐδόξαζον τὸν Θεόν, λέγοντες ὅτι Προφήτης μέγας ἠγέρθη ἐν ἡμῖν, καὶ ὅτι Ἐπεσκέψατο ὁ Θεὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: elaben de phobos pantas, kai edoxazon ton Theon, legontes hoti Prophētēs megas ēgerthē en hēmin, kai hoti Epeskepsato ho Theos ton laon autou.
verses.study_note: "God hath visited his people" (epeskepsato ho Theos ton laon autou) is verbatim from the LXX of Ruth 1:6 and echoes Zechariah's Benedictus (1:68: "he hath visited and redeemed his people"). The crowd identifies Jesus as a "great prophet," which is insufficient Christology but genuine recognition. Luke uses their response to confirm the theological meaning of the miracle: the eschatological visitation of God has begun.

verses.kjv_text: And this rumour of him went forth throughout all Judaea, and throughout all the region round about.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ὁ λόγος οὗτος ἐν ὅλῃ τῇ Ἰουδαίᾳ περὶ αὐτοῦ καὶ πάσῃ τῇ περιχώρῳ.
verses.transliteration: kai exēlthen ho logos houtos en holē tē Ioudaia peri autou kai pasē tē perichōrō.
verses.study_note: "Rumour" (logos — word, report) spread beyond Galilee into Judaea. Luke uses the geographic spread of the report to indicate the mounting of messianic expectation that sets the stage for John's question. The word logos here is ironic — it is the logos (Word) himself who is the content of the report that goes forth.

verses.kjv_text: And the disciples of John shewed him of all these things.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἀπήγγειλαν Ἰωάννῃ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ περὶ πάντων τούτων.
verses.transliteration: Kai apēngeilan Iōannē hoi mathētai autou peri pantōn toutōn.
verses.study_note: John the Baptist was imprisoned by Herod Antipas (3:19-20). His disciples maintained contact and reported the works of Jesus. The information "all these things" encompasses the two miracles just narrated plus the ministry of chapters 4-6. The report precipitates John's crucial question — not doubt but a desire for confirmed identification.

verses.kjv_text: And John calling unto him two of his disciples sent them to Jesus, saying, Art thou he that should come? or look we for another?
verses.original_text: καὶ προσκαλεσάμενος δύο τινὰς τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ ὁ Ἰωάννης ἔπεμψεν πρὸς τὸν Κύριον λέγων, Σὺ εἶ ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἢ ἄλλον προσδοκῶμεν;
