The Twelve receive what they do not possess in themselves, given for the purpose of ministry. The commission to overcome "all devils" (panta ta daimonia) and cure diseases mirrors Jesus's own mission statement (4:18; 7:22), constituting the Twelve as extensions of his own ministry.

verses.kjv_text: And he sent them to preach the kingdom of God, and to heal the sick.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπέστειλεν αὐτοὺς κηρύσσειν τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ Θεοῦ καὶ ἰᾶσθαι τοὺς ἀσθενεῖς,
verses.transliteration: kai apesteilen autous kērussein tēn basileian tou Theou kai iasthai tous astheneis,
verses.study_note: "Sent them" (apesteilen — apostled them; the verb form of apostolos). The Twelve are formally commissioned as apostles here. The double mandate — preaching the kingdom and healing the sick — insists that the gospel cannot be reduced to words alone. The kingdom comes in both proclamation and demonstration. The message and the miracle belong together as two aspects of one saving reality.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Take nothing for your journey, neither staves, nor scrip, neither bread, neither money; neither have two coats apiece.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς, Μηδὲν αἴρετε εἰς τὴν ὁδόν, μήτε ῥάβδον μήτε πήραν μήτε ἄρτον μήτε ἀργύριον, μήτε ἀνὰ δύο χιτῶνας ἔχειν.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen pros autous, Mēden airete eis tēn hodon, mēte rhabdon mēte pēran mēte arton mēte argurion, mēte ana duo chitōnas echein.
verses.study_note: The radical simplicity of the mission orders requires total dependence on God's provision through hospitality. No staff (rhabdon), no bag (pēran), no bread (arton), no money (argurion), no second tunic (chitōna). The stripping away of every security forces the missionaries to trust in the same God whose kingdom they are preaching. The mission's credibility depends on the missionaries' demonstrated faith in God's provision.

verses.kjv_text: And whatsoever house ye enter into, there abide, and thence depart.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἰς ἣν ἂν οἰκίαν εἰσέλθητε, ἐκεῖ μένετε καὶ ἐκεῖθεν ἐξέρχεσθε.
verses.transliteration: kai eis hēn an oikian eiselthēte, ekei menete kai ekeithen exerchesthe.
verses.study_note: "There abide" — stay in the first house that receives you; do not shop around for better hospitality. Stability of residence prevents any appearance of working for personal material gain. The single-household instruction also reflects trust in God's initial provision rather than continual seeking for improved conditions. The mission's credibility requires contentment and simplicity.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever will not receive you, when ye depart from that city, shake off the very dust from your feet for a testimony against them.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅσοι ἂν μὴ δέχωνται ὑμᾶς, ἐξερχόμενοι ἀπὸ τῆς πόλεως ἐκείνης καὶ τὸν κονιορτὸν ἀπὸ τῶν ποδῶν ὑμῶν ἀποτινάσσετε εἰς μαρτύριον ἐπ' αὐτούς.
verses.transliteration: kai hosoi an mē dechōntai humas, exerchomenoi apo tēs poleōs ekeinēs kai ton koniorton apo tōn podōn humōn apotinassete eis marturion ep' autous.
verses.study_note: "Shake off the very dust" — a Jewish gesture indicating that even the dust of a Gentile land was defiled. Applied here to Jewish cities that reject the gospel, it treats such rejection as comparable to Gentile uncleanness. "For a testimony against them" (eis marturion ep' autous) — the gesture is a formal witness that the offer was made and refused. The responsibility for the rejection rests with the rejectors.

verses.kjv_text: And they departed, and went through the towns, preaching the gospel, and healing every where.
verses.original_text: ἐξερχόμενοι δὲ διήρχοντο κατὰ τὰς κώμας εὐαγγελιζόμενοι καὶ θεραπεύοντες πανταχοῦ.
verses.transliteration: exerchomenoi de diērchonto kata tas kōmas euangelizomenoi kai therapeuontes pantachou.
verses.study_note: "Everywhere" (pantachou) — the mission was comprehensive. The two participles (euangelizomenoi — gospelizing; therapeuontes — healing) confirm that they carried out exactly what they were commissioned to do. The first mission of the Twelve is a success. Luke does not report any failure here; the disciples will fail later (v.40), but their first deployment is characterized by faithful obedience.

verses.kjv_text: Now Herod the tetrarch heard of all that was done by him: and he was perplexed, because that it was said of some, that John was risen from the dead;
verses.original_text: Ἤκουσεν δὲ Ἡρῴδης ὁ τετράρχης τὰ γινόμενα πάντα, καὶ διηπόρει διὰ τὸ λέγεσθαι ὑπό τινων ὅτι Ἰωάννης ἠγέρθη ἐκ νεκρῶν,
verses.transliteration: Ēkousen de Hērōdēs ho tetrarches ta ginomena panta, kai diēporei dia to legesthai hupo tinōn hoti Iōannēs ēgerthē ek nekrōn,
verses.study_note: Herod Antipas (tetrarch of Galilee and Perea) is confronted by reports of Jesus's ministry. His "perplexity" (diēporei — complete bewilderment) is not the response of a neutral observer but of a man tormented by guilt. He executed John (3:19-20) and fears John's resurrection. The three popular identifications — John risen, Elijah, an old prophet — all miss the truth but testify to the extraordinary impact of Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: And of some, that Elias had appeared; and of others, that one of the old prophets was risen again.
verses.original_text: ὑπό τινων δὲ ὅτι Ἡλίας ἐφάνη, ἄλλων δὲ ὅτι προφήτης τις τῶν ἀρχαίων ἀνέστη.
verses.transliteration: hupo tinōn de hoti Ēlias ephanē, allōn de hoti prophētēs tis tōn archaiōn anestē.
verses.study_note: The three popular identifications anticipate the options posed to the disciples in v.19. Elijah, expected to return before the Messiah (Mal 4:5), was a natural candidate. "One of the old prophets" reflected the hope for an eschatological prophetic figure (Deut 18:15). These identifications are not without honor — John was greater than any prophet (7:28) — but they are all inadequate for the one who is the Son of God.

verses.kjv_text: And Herod said, John have I beheaded: but who is this, of whom I hear such things? And he desired to see him.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ Ἡρῴδης, Ἰωάννην ἐγὼ ἀπεκεφάλισα· τίς δέ ἐστιν οὗτος περὶ οὗ ἀκούω τοιαῦτα; καὶ ἐζήτει ἰδεῖν αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: eipen de Hērōdēs, Iōannēn egō apekephalisa; tis de estin houtos peri hou akouō toiauta? kai ezētei idein auton.
verses.study_note: "John have I beheaded" — a casual reference to his own murderous act, revealing Herod's moral desensitization. "Who is this?" (tis de estin houtos) — the question that runs through chapters 7-9 and is about to receive its definitive answer in v.20. Herod's curiosity (he will finally see Jesus at the trial, 23:8-12) is idle curiosity, not genuine seeking. He "desired to see" a wonder-worker, not the Lord.

verses.kjv_text: And the apostles, when they were returned, told him all that they had done. And he took them, and went aside privately into a desert place belonging to the city called Bethsaida.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ὑποστρέψαντες οἱ ἀπόστολοι διηγήσαντο αὐτῷ ὅσα ἐποίησαν. καὶ παραλαβὼν αὐτοὺς ὑπεχώρησεν κατ' ἰδίαν εἰς πόλιν καλουμένην Βηθσαϊδά.
verses.transliteration: Kai hupostrepsantes hoi apostoloi diēgēsanto autō hosa epoiēsan. kai paralabōn autous hupechōrēsen kat' idian eis polin kaloumenēn Bēthsaida.
verses.study_note: The Twelve return and report their mission to Jesus. He takes them to a "desert place" (erēmon topon) near Bethsaida for rest and debriefing — a pattern of withdrawal after intense ministry. Bethsaida was Philip's tetrarchy, outside Herod Antipas's jurisdiction. The private retreat is interrupted by the crowds, which sets up the feeding miracle.

verses.kjv_text: And the people, when they knew it, followed him: and he received them, and spake unto them of the kingdom of God, and healed them that had need of healing.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ ὄχλοι γνόντες ἠκολούθησαν αὐτῷ· καὶ ἀποδεξάμενος αὐτοὺς ἐλάλει αὐτοῖς περὶ τῆς βασιλείας τοῦ Θεοῦ, καὶ τοὺς χρείαν ἔχοντας θεραπείας ἰᾶτο.
verses.transliteration: hoi de ochloi gnontes ēkolouthēsan autō; kai apodexamenos autous elalei autois peri tēs basileias tou Theou, kai tous chreian echontas therapeias iato.
verses.study_note: Jesus "received them" (apodexamenos — welcomed, accepted with grace). The retreat is interrupted but the hospitality is gracious, not resentful. He speaks of the kingdom and heals those who need healing — the same double mandate he gave the Twelve (v.2). The miracle that follows will reveal the messianic abundance of the kingdom in a way no lecture could.

verses.kjv_text: And when the day began to wear away, then came the twelve, and said unto him, Send the multitude away, that they may go into the towns and country round about, and lodge, and get victuals: for we are here in a desert place.
verses.original_text: Ἡ δὲ ἡμέρα ἤρξατο κλίνειν· προσελθόντες δὲ οἱ δώδεκα εἶπον αὐτῷ, Ἀπόλυσον τὸν ὄχλον, ἵνα πορευθέντες εἰς τὰς κύκλῳ κώμας καὶ ἀγροὺς καταλύσωσιν καὶ εὕρωσιν ἐπισιτισμόν· ὅτι ὧδε ἐν ἐρήμῳ τόπῳ ἐσμέν.
verses.transliteration: Hē de hēmera ērxato klinein; proselthontes de hoi dōdeka eipon autō, Apoluon ton ochlon, hina poreuthentes eis tas kuklō kōmas kai agrous katalusōsin kai heurōsin episitismon; hoti hōde en erēmō topō esmen.
verses.study_note: The disciples' practical concern is reasonable: it is late, remote, and the crowd has not eaten. Their solution — "send them away" — is the solution of limitation. They see the crowd as a problem to be dispersed, not a multitude to be fed. Jesus's counter-command ("give ye them to eat," v.13) reframes their perception entirely: the disciples themselves are the agent of God's provision for the crowd.

verses.kjv_text: But he said unto them, Give ye them to eat. And they said, We have no more but five loaves and two fishes; except we should go and buy meat for all this people.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς αὐτούς, Δότε αὐτοῖς ὑμεῖς φαγεῖν. οἱ δὲ εἶπον, Οὐκ εἰσὶν ἡμῖν πλεῖον ἢ ἄρτοι πέντε καὶ ἰχθύες δύο, εἰ μήτι πορευθέντες ἡμεῖς ἀγοράσωμεν εἰς πάντα τὸν λαὸν τοῦτον βρώματα.
verses.transliteration: eipen de pros autous, Dote autois humeis phagein. hoi de eipon, Ouk eisin hēmin pleion ē artoi pente kai ichthues duo, ei mēti poreuthentes hēmeis agorasōmen eis panta ton laon touton brōmata.
verses.study_note: "Give ye them to eat" (Dote autois humeis phagein) — the command places the responsibility on the disciples. They respond with an inventory of inadequacy: five loaves, two fish. The number is perfectly insufficient by any human calculation. But Jesus takes insufficient human resources, blesses them, and produces miraculous abundance. The disciples' inadequacy is the necessary condition for the demonstration of divine sufficiency.

verses.kjv_text: For they were about five thousand men. And he said to his disciples, Make them sit down by fifties in a company.
verses.original_text: ἦσαν γὰρ ὡσεὶ ἄνδρες πεντακισχίλιοι. εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ, Κατακλίνατε αὐτοὺς κλισίας ὡσεὶ ἀνὰ πεντήκοντα.
verses.transliteration: ēsan gar hōsei andres pentakischilioi. eipen de pros tous mathētas autou, Kataklinete autous klisias hōsei ana pentēkonta.
verses.study_note: "About five thousand men" (andres — males, not counting women and children, Matt 14:21, so the total crowd was considerably larger). The organization into groups of fifty recalls the organization of Israel in the wilderness (Exod 18:21-25) and the camps of Qumran, evoking Exodus typology: Jesus is providing manna for the new Israel in a new desert. The orderly seating also facilitates the efficient distribution of the miracle.

verses.kjv_text: And they did so, and made them all sit down.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐποίησαν οὕτως καὶ κατέκλιναν ἅπαντας.
verses.transliteration: kai epoiēsan houtōs kai kateklinan hapantas.
verses.study_note: The disciples' obedient action in making thousands sit down in the wilderness before any visible provision is itself an act of faith. They are acting on Jesus's word before the miracle occurs. This kind of obedient anticipation is the pattern of kingdom discipleship: act as though the provision is already given, because the word of Jesus is more reliable than visible evidence.

verses.kjv_text: Then he took the five loaves and the two fishes, and looking up to heaven, he blessed them, and brake, and gave to the disciples to set before the multitude.
verses.original_text: λαβὼν δὲ τοὺς πέντε ἄρτους καὶ τοὺς δύο ἰχθύας, ἀναβλέψας εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν, εὐλόγησεν αὐτοὺς καὶ κατέκλασεν, καὶ ἐδίδου τοῖς μαθηταῖς παρατιθέναι τῷ ὄχλῳ.
verses.transliteration: labōn de tous pente artous kai tous duo ichthuas, anablepsas eis ton ouranon, eulogēsen autous kai kateklasen, kai edidou tois mathētais paratithenai tō ochlō.
verses.study_note: Four eucharistic actions: took, blessed, broke, gave. These identical four actions appear at the Last Supper (22:19) and at Emmaus (24:30), deliberately linking the feeding miracle to the sacramental meal. Jesus does not multiply the bread privately then distribute it; he gives it to the disciples to distribute. They are the mediators of his provision — a model of apostolic ministry. The imperfect tense "was giving" (edidou) suggests continuous giving.

verses.kjv_text: And they did eat, and were all filled: and there was taken up of fragments that remained to them twelve baskets.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔφαγον καὶ ἐχορτάσθησαν πάντες, καὶ ἤρθη τὸ περισσεῦσαν αὐτοῖς κλασμάτων κόφινοι δώδεκα.
verses.transliteration: kai ephagon kai echortasthēsan pantes, kai ērthē to perisseusan autois klasmatōn kophinoi dōdeka.
verses.study_note: "Were all filled" (echortasthēsan — satisfied, fully satiated; from chortos, pasture/grass for animals, i.e., filled to contentment). The kingdom provides abundantly and without lack. "Twelve baskets" of fragments — twelve corresponding to the twelve tribes of Israel, symbolizing messianic provision for all God's people. The surplus is greater than the original supply; the miracle produced more than was needed, with enough left over to feed the twelve disciples themselves.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as he was alone praying, his disciples were with him: and he asked them, saying, Whom do the crowds say that I am?
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ εἶναι αὐτὸν προσευχόμενον κατὰ μόνας, συνῆσαν αὐτῷ οἱ μαθηταί, καὶ ἐπηρώτησεν αὐτοὺς λέγων, Τίνα με λέγουσιν οἱ ὄχλοι εἶναι;
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en tō einai auton proseuchomenon kata monas, sunēsan autō hoi mathētai, kai epērōtēsen autous legōn, Tina me legousin hoi ochloi einai?
verses.study_note: The confession scene is prefaced by prayer — uniquely in Luke. Jesus is praying alone (kata monas — in solitude) when the disciples join him, and out of that prayerful solitude the crucial question arises. Luke consistently links pivotal moments to Jesus's prayer life (3:21; 6:12; 9:28; 22:41). The question "Whom do the crowds say that I am?" moves from the popular (vv.7-8) to the personal (v.20).

verses.kjv_text: They answering said, John the Baptist; but some say, Elias; and others say, that one of the old prophets is risen again.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ ἀποκριθέντες εἶπον, Ἰωάννην τὸν Βαπτιστήν· ἄλλοι δὲ Ἠλίαν· ἄλλοι δὲ ὅτι προφήτης τις τῶν ἀρχαίων ἀνέστη.
verses.transliteration: hoi de apokrithentes eipon, Iōannēn ton Baptistēn; alloi de Ēlian; alloi de hoti prophētēs tis tōn archaiōn anestē.
verses.study_note: The three popular opinions recapitulate Herod's perplexity (vv.7-8). All three are honorable identifications — John the greatest of prophets, Elijah the expected forerunner, an ancient prophet risen. But all three are inadequate. They place Jesus in the prophetic category without recognizing his unique divine sonship. The crowds' perception, however admiring, falls short of saving knowledge.

verses.kjv_text: He said unto them, But whom say ye that I am? Peter answering said, The Christ of God.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτοῖς, Ὑμεῖς δὲ τίνα με λέγετε εἶναι; Πέτρος δὲ ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν, Τὸν Χριστὸν τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: eipen de autois, Humeis de tina me legete einai? Petros de apokritheis eipen, Ton Christon tou Theou.
verses.study_note: "But whom say ye?" (Humeis de — but you, emphatic pronoun) — the question to the disciples is personal, direct, and urgent. Peter's answer is "The Christ of God" (Ton Christon tou Theou) — Luke's distinctive form, more theocentric than Matthew's "Son of the living God." Christos is the Greek translation of Hebrew Mashiach — the anointed one. Peter's confession is the turning point of the Galilean ministry and the foundation on which Jesus builds his passion teaching.

verses.kjv_text: And he straitly charged them, and commanded them to tell no man that thing;
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἐπιτιμήσας αὐτοῖς παρήγγειλεν μηδενὶ λέγειν τοῦτο,
verses.transliteration: ho de epitimēsas autois parēngeilen mēdeni legein touto,
verses.study_note: The command to silence (the "messianic secret") follows the confession. The restraint is not denial but timing — the confession without the cross would be a misunderstanding. The crowds' messianic expectations were political and triumphal; announcement of messiahship without the passion would have ignited those expectations prematurely and misrepresentatively. The disciples must hear the passion prediction (v.22) before the confession can be proclaimed.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, The Son of man must suffer many things, and be rejected of the elders and chief priests and scribes, and be slain, and be raised the third day.
verses.original_text: εἰπὼν ὅτι Δεῖ τὸν Υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου πολλὰ παθεῖν καὶ ἀποδοκιμασθῆναι ἀπὸ τῶν πρεσβυτέρων καὶ ἀρχιερέων καὶ γραμματέων καὶ ἀποκτανθῆναι καὶ τῇ τρίτῃ ἡμέρᾳ ἐγερθῆναι.
verses.transliteration: eipōn hoti Dei ton Huion tou anthrōpou polla pathein kai apodokimasthēnai apo tōn presbuterōn kai archiereōn kai grammateōn kai apoktanthēnai kai tē tritē hēmera egerthēnai.
verses.study_note: "Must" (Dei — divine necessity). The first passion prediction contains five elements: (1) suffer many things; (2) be rejected (apodokimasthēnai — tested and found wanting, as a stone rejected by builders — Ps 118:22); (3) by the three constituent parts of the Sanhedrin (elders, chief priests, scribes); (4) be killed; (5) rise the third day. The full plan of redemption is announced in one sentence. The resurrection is not an afterthought but the goal.

verses.kjv_text: And he said to them all, If any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross daily, and follow me.
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν δὲ πρὸς πάντας, Εἴ τις θέλει ὀπίσω μου ἔρχεσθαι, ἀρνησάσθω ἑαυτόν, καὶ ἀράτω τὸν σταυρὸν αὐτοῦ καθ' ἡμέραν, καὶ ἀκολουθείτω μοι.
verses.transliteration: Elegen de pros pantas, Ei tis thelei opisō mou erchesthai, arnēsasthō heauton, kai aratō ton stauron autou kath' hēmeran, kai akoloutheitō moi.
verses.study_note: "To them all" — the conditions of discipleship are not for a spiritual elite but for all followers. "Deny himself" (arnēsasthō heauton) — renounce the self as the center and authority of one's life. "Take up his cross daily" — Luke uniquely adds "daily" (kath' hēmeran), making cross-bearing a continuous habitual practice, not merely a crisis decision. The cross is the posture of self-giving love, not merely physical martyrdom.

verses.kjv_text: For whosoever will save his life shall lose it: but whosoever will lose his life for my sake, the same shall save it.
verses.original_text: ὃς γὰρ ἂν θέλῃ τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ σῴζειν ἀπολέσει αὐτήν· ὃς δ' ἂν ἀπολέσῃ τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ ἕνεκεν ἐμοῦ, οὗτος σώσει αὐτήν.
verses.transliteration: hos gar an thelē tēn psuchēn autou sōzein apolesei autēn; hos d' an apolesē tēn psuchēn autou heneken emou, houtos sōsei autēn.
verses.study_note: The paradox of discipleship: the life saved by self-preservation is the life that is ultimately lost; the life surrendered for Christ's sake is the life ultimately saved. "Life" (psuchē) refers to the self, the whole person, the very thing one considers most precious. The verse describes the fundamental inversion of the kingdom: power through weakness, life through death, gain through surrender. This is the logic of the cross applied to every disciple.

verses.kjv_text: For what is a man advantaged, if he gain the whole world, and lose himself, or be cast away?
verses.original_text: τί γὰρ ὠφελεῖται ἄνθρωπος κερδήσας τὸν κόσμον ὅλον, ἑαυτὸν δὲ ἀπολέσας ἢ ζημιωθείς;
verses.transliteration: ti gar ōpheleitai anthrōpos kerdēsas ton kosmon holon, heauton de apolesas ē zēmiōtheis?
verses.study_note: A rhetorical question that illuminates ultimate values. The entire world's resources, power, and pleasures cannot compensate for the loss of the self that was made for God. "Lose himself" (heauton apolesas — to destroy oneself) and "be cast away" (zēmiōtheis — to forfeit, suffer damage/loss) describe two ways of soul-ruin. The devil offered Jesus the whole world (4:6); Jesus rejected it for the same reason this verse articulates.

verses.kjv_text: For whosoever shall be ashamed of me and of my words, of him shall the Son of man be ashamed, when he shall come in his own glory, and in his Father's, and of the holy angels.
verses.original_text: ὃς γὰρ ἂν ἐπαισχυνθῇ με καὶ τοὺς ἐμοὺς λόγους, τοῦτον ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐπαισχυνθήσεται, ὅταν ἔλθῃ ἐν τῇ δόξῃ αὐτοῦ καὶ τοῦ Πατρὸς καὶ τῶν ἁγίων ἀγγέλων.
verses.transliteration: hos gar an epaischunthē me kai tous emous logous, touton ho Huios tou anthrōpou epaischunthēsetai, hotan elthē en tē doxē autou kai tou Patros kai tōn hagiōn angelōn.
verses.study_note: "Ashamed of me and of my words" — shame is the social emotion that seeks approval from humans rather than from God. It fears the ridicule of the world more than the judgment of God. The consequence: the Son of Man will be "ashamed" of such a person at the Parousia. The threefold glory — his own, the Father's, and the angels' — contrasts with the humble, cross-bearing form of his present coming, confirming that the exalted Christ will vindicate those who were faithful in his humiliation.

verses.kjv_text: But I tell you of a truth, there be some standing here, which shall not taste of death, till they see the kingdom of God.
verses.original_text: λέγω δὲ ὑμῖν ἀληθῶς, εἰσίν τινες τῶν ὧδε ἑστηκότων οἳ οὐ μὴ γεύσωνται θανάτου ἕως ἂν ἴδωσιν τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: legō de humin alēthōs, eisin tines tōn hōde hestēkotōn hoi ou mē geusōntai thanatou heōs an idōsin tēn basileian tou Theou.
verses.study_note: "Some standing here" most naturally refers to Peter, James, and John who will witness the Transfiguration six days later — "the kingdom of God come with power" (Mark 9:1) is fulfilled in that preview of glory. Others have interpreted it as the resurrection appearances, Pentecost, or the destruction of Jerusalem. The Transfiguration interpretation (immediately following in all three Synoptics) is most contextually coherent.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass about an eight days after these sayings, he took Peter and John and James, and went up into a mountain to pray.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ μετὰ τοὺς λόγους τούτους ὡσεὶ ἡμέραι ὀκτὼ καὶ παραλαβὼν Πέτρον καὶ Ἰωάννην καὶ Ἰάκωβον ἀνέβη εἰς τὸ ὄρος προσεύξασθαι.
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de meta tous logous toutous hōsei hēmerai oktō kai paralabōn Petron kai Iōannēn kai Iakōbon anebē eis to oros proseuxasthai.
verses.study_note: Luke alone says "about eight days" (Matthew and Mark say "after six days" — Luke uses the inclusive reckoning). The mountain is not named; tradition identifies it as Mount Tabor or Hermon. Luke uniquely notes the purpose: "to pray" (proseuxasthai). The Transfiguration occurs in the context of prayer — another Lukan signature of prayer at pivotal moments (3:21; 6:12; 22:41).

verses.kjv_text: And as he prayed, the fashion of his countenance was altered, and his raiment was white and glistering.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ προσεύχεσθαι αὐτὸν τὸ εἶδος τοῦ προσώπου αὐτοῦ ἕτερον, καὶ ὁ ἱματισμὸς αὐτοῦ λευκὸς ἐξαστράπτων.
verses.transliteration: kai egeneto en tō proseuchesthai auton to eidos tou prosōpou autou heteron, kai ho himatismos autou leukos exastraptōn.
verses.study_note: "As he prayed" — the transformation occurs during prayer. Luke is unique in this detail, connecting the Transfiguration directly to the prayer. "The fashion of his countenance was altered" (heteron — different, other). "White and glistering" (exastraptōn — dazzling, blazing like lightning). This is the shekinah glory — the radiant presence of God made visible in Christ's body. The divine glory that was veiled in the Incarnation is momentarily unveiled.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, there talked with him two men, which were Moses and Elias:
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἄνδρες δύο συνελάλουν αὐτῷ, οἵτινες ἦσαν Μωϋσῆς καὶ Ἠλίας,
verses.transliteration: kai idou andres duo sunelaloun autō, hoitines ēsan Mōusēs kai Ēlias,
verses.study_note: Moses and Elijah represent the Law and the Prophets — the entire OT witness to Christ. Their appearance with Jesus confirms that he is the fulfilment of all Scripture. Moses (who died and whose body was mysterious — Deut 34:6) and Elijah (who was taken alive — 2 Kgs 2:11) represent both types of exit from this world, confirming that both resurrection and translation serve the same divine purpose.

verses.kjv_text: Who appeared in glory, and spake of his decease which he should accomplish at Jerusalem.
verses.original_text: οἳ ὀφθέντες ἐν δόξῃ ἔλεγον τὴν ἔξοδον αὐτοῦ ἣν ἤμελλεν πληροῦν ἐν Ἱερουσαλήμ.
verses.transliteration: hoi ophthentes en doxē elegon tēn exodon autou hēn ēmellen plēroun en Hierousalēm.
verses.study_note: "His decease" (exodon — Exodus). Luke uniquely reveals the subject of the heavenly conversation: the departure/exodus of Jesus to be accomplished in Jerusalem. The word exodos is profound: it echoes the great Exodus from Egypt, casting Jesus's death-resurrection as the new Exodus — the definitive liberation from the slavery of sin and death. Moses and Elijah discuss the very event the disciples struggle to accept (v.45): the cross is the great Exodus.

verses.kjv_text: But Peter and they that were with him were heavy with sleep: and when they were awake, they saw his glory, and the two men that stood with him.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Πέτρος καὶ οἱ σὺν αὐτῷ ἦσαν βεβαρημένοι ὕπνῳ· διαγρηγορήσαντες δὲ εἶδον τὴν δόξαν αὐτοῦ καὶ τοὺς δύο ἄνδρας τοὺς συνεστῶτας αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: ho de Petros kai hoi sun autō ēsan bebarēmenoi hupnō; diagrēgorēsantes de eidon tēn doxan autou kai tous duo andras tous sunestōtas autō.
verses.study_note: "Heavy with sleep" (bebarēmenoi hupnō — weighed down by sleep). The disciples were overwhelmed — by the hour, by the exertion of climbing, by the spiritual intensity of Jesus's prayer. "When they were awake" (diagrēgorēsantes — having stayed fully awake, roused themselves thoroughly). Luke notes they saw "his glory" (tēn doxan autou) — a witness to the divine nature of the Transfigured One. The same drowsiness will afflict them in Gethsemane (22:45-46), with tragic consequences.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as they departed from him, Peter said unto Jesus, Master, it is good for us to be here: and let us make three tabernacles; one for thee, and one for Moses, and one for Elias: not knowing what he said.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ διαχωρίζεσθαι αὐτοὺς ἀπ' αὐτοῦ εἶπεν ὁ Πέτρος πρὸς τὸν Ἰησοῦν, Ἐπιστάτα, καλόν ἐστιν ἡμᾶς ὧδε εἶναι· καὶ ποιήσωμεν σκηνὰς τρεῖς, μίαν σοι καὶ μίαν Μωϋσεῖ καὶ μίαν Ἠλίᾳ· μὴ εἰδὼς ὃ λέγει.
verses.transliteration: kai egeneto en tō diachōrizesthai autous ap' autou eipen ho Petros pros ton Iēsoun, Epistata, kalon estin hēmas hōde einai; kai poiēsōmen skēnas treis, mian soi kai mian Mōusei kai mian Ēlia; mē eidōs ho legei.
verses.study_note: "Three tabernacles" (skēnas — tents, booths). Peter wants to build structures to prolong the glorious experience and to honor the three figures equally. But Luke adds "not knowing what he said" — a gentle rebuke. To equate Jesus with Moses and Elijah in three equal booths is a category error. The divine voice (v.35) will correct it: Jesus alone is the Son; Moses and Elijah are servants. The Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkoth) background — the booths — is likely intended, pointing to the eschatological dwelling of God with his people.

verses.kjv_text: While he thus spake, there came a cloud, and overshadowed them: and they feared as they entered into the cloud.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα δὲ αὐτοῦ λέγοντος ἐγένετο νεφέλη καὶ ἐπεσκίαζεν αὐτούς· ἐφοβήθησαν δὲ ἐν τῷ εἰσελθεῖν αὐτοὺς εἰς τὴν νεφέλην.
verses.transliteration: tauta de autou legontes egeneto nephelē kai epeskiazen autous; ephobēthēsan de en tō eiselthein autous eis tēn nephelēn.
verses.study_note: "A cloud" (nephelē) — the cloud of the shekinah presence, the pillar of cloud that led Israel through the wilderness (Exod 40:34-38), the cloud that filled Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11). It is the divine presence made visible. "Overshadowed them" (epeskiazen — as the Spirit overshadowed Mary in 1:35). The disciples enter the cloud — they are immersed in the divine presence — and fear appropriately.

verses.kjv_text: And there came a voice out of the cloud, saying, This is my beloved Son: hear him.
verses.original_text: καὶ φωνὴ ἐγένετο ἐκ τῆς νεφέλης λέγουσα, Οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ Υἱός μου ὁ ἐκλελεγμένος· αὐτοῦ ἀκούετε.
verses.transliteration: kai phōnē egeneto ek tēs nephelēs legousa, Houtos estin ho Huios mou ho eklelegmenos; autou akouete.
verses.study_note: The divine voice from the cloud (as at the Baptism, 3:22) gives the definitive answer to the question of Jesus's identity. "My beloved Son" confirms the Baptism declaration. "Whom I have chosen" (eklelegmenos — elected, the chosen one) echoes Isaiah 42:1 (the Servant). "Hear him" (autou akouete) — the plural command to the three disciples, but applicable to all. It directly answers the Parable of the Sower's call: this is the one whose word must be heard and kept. The Father himself commands it.

verses.kjv_text: And when the voice was past, Jesus was found alone. And they kept it close, and told no man in those days any of those things which they had seen.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐν τῷ γενέσθαι τὴν φωνὴν εὑρέθη Ἰησοῦς μόνος. καὶ αὐτοὶ ἐσίγησαν καὶ οὐδενὶ ἀπήγγειλαν ἐν ἐκείναις ταῖς ἡμέραις οὐδὲν ὧν ἑώρακαν.
verses.transliteration: kai en tō genesthai tēn phōnēn heurethē Iēsous monos. kai autoi esigēsan kai oudeni apēngeilan en ekeinais tais hēmerais ouden hōn heōrakasin.
verses.study_note: "Jesus was found alone" — Moses and Elijah have vanished. Only Jesus remains. The law and the prophets have served their purpose as witnesses; the Son supersedes them. "They kept it close" — the command to silence (cf. v.21) is respected. They told no one "in those days" — implying they spoke of it after the resurrection (cf. Mark 9:9: "until the Son of Man be risen from the dead"). The Transfiguration makes full sense only in resurrection light.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that on the next day, when they were come down from the hill, much people met him.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ τῇ ἑξῆς ἡμέρᾳ κατελθόντων αὐτῶν ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄρους συνήντησεν αὐτῷ ὄχλος πολύς.
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de tē hexēs hēmera katelthontōn autōn apo tou orous sunēntēsen autō ochlos polus.
verses.study_note: The descent from the mountain of Transfiguration into the valley of human suffering creates a deliberate theological contrast. Moses descended from Sinai to find the people in idolatrous chaos (Exod 32). Jesus descends to find a desperate father and a powerless church. The glory on the mountain is not an escape from human need but the empowerment for engagement with it.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, a man of the company cried out, saying, Master, I beseech thee, look upon my son: for he is mine only child.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἀνὴρ ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄχλου ἐβόησεν λέγων, Διδάσκαλε, δέομαί σου, ἐπιβλέψαι ἐπὶ τὸν υἱόν μου, ὅτι μονογενής μοί ἐστιν·
verses.transliteration: kai idou anēr apo tou ochlou eboēsen legōn, Didaskale, deomai sou, epiblepsai epi ton huion mou, hoti monogenēs moi estin;
verses.study_note: "Only child" (monogenēs) — the third time in Luke 9 this word is used (7:12 for the widow's son; 8:42 for Jairus's daughter). Luke deliberately uses this word to prepare for its application to Jesus (John 3:16). The father's cry (eboēsen — shouted loudly) cuts through the crowd. His desperation and his focus on the uniqueness of the child amplifies the pathos and urgency.

verses.kjv_text: And, lo, a spirit taketh him, and he suddenly crieth out; and it teareth him that he foameth again, and bruising him hardly departeth from him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ πνεῦμα λαμβάνει αὐτόν, καὶ ἐξαίφνης κράζει, καὶ σπαράσσει αὐτὸν μετὰ ἀφροῦ, καὶ μόγις ἀποχωρεῖ ἀπ' αὐτοῦ συντρῖβον αὐτόν·
verses.transliteration: kai idou pneuma lambanei auton, kai exaiphnēs krazei, kai sparassei auton meta aphrou, kai mogis apochōrei ap' autou suntribon auton;
verses.study_note: The description of the demonic seizures is medically precise: sudden onset (exaiphnēs), crying out (krazei), convulsive shaking (sparassei — convulsion, tearing), foaming (aphrou), and extreme violence leaving the boy bruised and exhausted. Luke the physician observes these symptoms carefully. The spirit hardly departs (mogis apochōrei — barely goes away, implying it always returns). This is a chronic, entrenched possession resisting all human intervention.

verses.kjv_text: And I besought thy disciples to cast him out; and they could not.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐδεήθην τῶν μαθητῶν σου ἵνα ἐκβάλωσιν αὐτό, καὶ οὐκ ἠδυνήθησαν.
verses.transliteration: kai edeēthēn tōn mathētōn sou hina ekbalōsin auto, kai ouk ēdunēthēsan.
verses.study_note: "They could not" (ouk ēdunēthēsan) — the disciples who had just returned from a successful mission (v.6: healing everywhere) are now unable to cast out a single demon. Their failure is not a permanent state but a diagnostic one: it reveals prayerlessness and faithlessness (v.41; Mark 9:29: "this kind goeth not out but by prayer"). Spiritual power is not accumulated; it is moment-by-moment dependence on the Lord.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answering said, O faithless and perverse generation, how long shall I be with you, and suffer you? Bring thy son hither.
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν, Ὦ γενεὰ ἄπιστος καὶ διεστραμμένη, ἕως πότε ἔσομαι πρὸς ὑμᾶς καὶ ἀνέξομαι ὑμῶν; προσάγαγέ μου τὸν υἱόν σου.
verses.transliteration: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen, Ō genea apistos kai diestrammenē, heōs pote esomai pros humas kai anexomai humōn? prosagagē mou ton huion sou.
verses.study_note: "O faithless and perverse generation" — a lament echoing Moses's cry over Israel (Deut 32:5,20). The address appears to encompass the disciples, the crowd, and the era. "How long shall I be with you?" — the language of divine patience stretched to the limit, looking forward to the cross and departure that will transform this failing generation into the empowered church of Acts. "Bring thy son hither" — the command cuts through the situation and brings resolution.

verses.kjv_text: And as he was yet a coming, the devil threw him down, and tare him. And Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, and healed the child, and delivered him again to his father.
verses.original_text: ἔτι δὲ προσερχομένου αὐτοῦ ἔρριψεν αὐτὸν τὸ δαιμόνιον καὶ συνεσπάραξεν· ἐπετίμησεν δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς τῷ πνεύματι τῷ ἀκαθάρτῳ, καὶ ἰάσατο τὸν παῖδα καὶ ἀπέδωκεν αὐτὸν τῷ πατρὶ αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: eti de proserchomenou autou erripsen auton to daimonion kai sunespharaxen; epetimēsen de ho Iēsous tō pneumati tō akathartō, kai iasato ton paida kai apedōken auton tō patri autou.
verses.study_note: "The devil threw him down" in a final violent convulsion as the boy approached Jesus — a last act of demonic defiance before defeat. Jesus "rebuked the unclean spirit" (epetimēsen — the same verb used in 4:35,39 and 8:24) and healed the child. "Delivered him again to his father" — the same phrase as the Nain resurrection (7:15). The echo is deliberate: demonic deliverance and physical resurrection are both acts of the same life-giving Lord.

verses.kjv_text: And they were all amazed at the mighty power of God. But while they wondered every one at all things which Jesus did, he said unto his disciples,
verses.original_text: ἐξεπλήσσοντο δὲ πάντες ἐπὶ τῇ μεγαλειότητι τοῦ Θεοῦ. πάντων δὲ θαυμαζόντων ἐπὶ πᾶσιν οἷς ἐποίει, εἶπεν πρὸς τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ,
verses.transliteration: exeplēssonto de pantes epi tē megaleihotēti tou Theou. pantōn de thaumazontōn epi pasin hois epoiei, eipen pros tous mathētas autou,
verses.study_note: "Mighty power of God" (megaleihotēti tou Theou — the majesty, greatness of God). The crowd attributes the miracle to God — a correct response. But while they are still marveling, Jesus immediately pivots to address his disciples with the second passion prediction. The juxtaposition is deliberate: the crowd glorifies God for the miracle; Jesus uses the moment of maximum fame to announce his impending suffering. The pattern of power through humility continues.

verses.kjv_text: Let these sayings sink down into your ears: for the Son of man shall be delivered into the hands of men.
verses.original_text: Θέσθε ὑμεῖς εἰς τὰ ὦτα ὑμῶν τοὺς λόγους τούτους· ὁ γὰρ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου μέλλει παραδίδοσθαι εἰς χεῖρας ἀνθρώπων.
verses.transliteration: Thesthe humeis eis ta ōta humōn tous logous toutous; ho gar Huios tou anthrōpou mellei paradidosthai eis cheiras anthrōpōn.
verses.study_note: "Let these sayings sink down into your ears" (Thesthe eis ta ōta — set/place in your ears). The imperative is emphatic: make this a priority, store it carefully. "Shall be delivered" (paradidosthai — to be handed over, betrayed). The word is used throughout the passion narrative. The contrast between "Son of Man" and "hands of men" is deliberately ironic: the divine-human figure is delivered into the power of mere humans. This is the deepest paradox of the Incarnation.

verses.kjv_text: But they understood not this saying, and it was hid from them, that they perceived it not: and they feared to ask him of that saying.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ ἠγνόουν τὸ ῥῆμα τοῦτο, καὶ ἦν παρακεκαλυμμένον ἀπ' αὐτῶν ἵνα μὴ αἴσθωνται αὐτό, καὶ ἐφοβοῦντο ἐρωτῆσαι αὐτὸν περὶ τοῦ ῥήματος τούτου.
verses.transliteration: hoi de ēgnooun to rhēma touto, kai ēn parakalummenon ap' autōn hina mē aisthōntai auto, kai ephobunto erōtēsai auton peri tou rhēmatos toutou.
verses.study_note: "It was hid from them" (parakalummenon — veiled, covered over) — divine passivity: God conceal it from them until the proper time (cf. 18:34; 24:16,25,31,45). The disciples could not yet receive the full weight of the cross without losing all hope; the unveiling would come with the resurrection. "They feared to ask" — their incomprehension was mixed with reluctance to hear the answer. Sometimes we fear to understand what we sense will require too much of us.

verses.kjv_text: Then there arose a reasoning among them, which of them should be greatest.
verses.original_text: Εἰσῆλθεν δὲ διαλογισμὸς ἐν αὐτοῖς, τὸ τίς ἂν εἴη μείζων αὐτῶν.
verses.transliteration: Eisēlthen de dialogismos en autois, to tis an eiē meizōn autōn.
verses.study_note: "There arose a reasoning" (eisēlthen dialogismos — a debate, reasoning entered among them). In the context of Jesus's just-announced death, the disciples are arguing about greatness. The jarring irony is Luke's point: immediately after the announcement that Jesus would be handed over to death, the disciples competed for the top position. The passion is incomprehensible to them; the kingdom they are imagining is still a political hierarchy.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus, perceiving the thought of their heart, took a child, and set him by him,
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς εἰδὼς τὸν διαλογισμὸν τῆς καρδίας αὐτῶν, ἐπιλαβόμενος παιδίον ἔστησεν αὐτὸ παρ' ἑαυτῷ,
verses.transliteration: ho de Iēsous eidōs ton dialogismon tēs kardias autōn, epilabomenos paidion estēsen auto par' heautō,
verses.study_note: "Perceiving the thought of their heart" — Jesus knows unexpressed thoughts (5:22; 6:8; 7:39-40). He does not address the dispute verbally but with a visual parable: he places a child (paidion — a small child) beside himself. In the ancient world, children had no social status, no rights, and no earning capacity. A child was the quintessential non-person in terms of social importance.

verses.kjv_text: And said unto them, Whosoever shall receive this child in my name receiveth me: and whosoever shall receive me receiveth him that sent me: for he that is least among you all, the same shall be great.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς, Ὃς ἐὰν δέξηται τοῦτο τὸ παιδίον ἐπὶ τῷ ὀνόματί μου, ἐμὲ δέχεται· καὶ ὃς ἂν ἐμὲ δέξηται, δέχεται τὸν ἀποστείλαντά με· ὁ γὰρ μικρότερος ἐν πᾶσιν ὑμῖν ὑπάρχων, οὗτος ἔσται μέγας.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autois, Hos ean dexētai touto to paidion epi tō onomati mou, eme dechetai; kai hos an eme dexētai, dechetai ton aposteilanta me; ho gar mikroteros en pasin humin huparchōn, houtos estai megas.
verses.study_note: A three-stage chain of identification: to receive this child = to receive Jesus = to receive the Father who sent Jesus. Greatness in the kingdom is measured by willingness to receive and serve the powerless, the marginal, the least — "in my name" (service as a representative of Christ's character, not as a career strategy). "He that is least among you all, the same shall be great" — the absolute inversion of worldly status hierarchies.

verses.kjv_text: And John answered and said, Master, we saw one casting out devils in thy name; and we forbad him, because he followeth not with us.
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ Ἰωάννης εἶπεν, Ἐπιστάτα, εἴδομέν τινα ἐπὶ τῷ ὀνόματί σου ἐκβάλλοντα τὰ δαιμόνια, καὶ ἐκωλύσαμεν αὐτόν, ὅτι οὐκ ἀκολουθεῖ μεθ' ἡμῶν.
verses.transliteration: apokritheis de Iōannēs eipen, Epistata, eidomen tina epi tō onomati sou ekballonta ta daimonia, kai ekōlusamen auton, hoti ouk akolouthei meth' hēmōn.
verses.study_note: John's confession is connected to the previous teaching: we forbade someone who was doing kingdom work because he was not in our group. The disciples' sectarianism ("followeth not with us") reveals the same spirit of exclusivity just rebuked in the greatness dispute. They had just failed to cast out a demon (v.40), but they stopped someone who succeeded, because of group identity. The irony is acute.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said unto him, Forbid him not: for he that is not against us is for us.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς αὐτὸν ὁ Ἰησοῦς, Μὴ κωλύετε· ὃς γὰρ οὐκ ἔστιν καθ' ἡμῶν, ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: eipen de pros auton ho Iēsous, Mē kōluete; hos gar ouk estin kath' hēmōn, huper hēmōn estin.
verses.study_note: "He that is not against us is for us" — a maxim of kingdom generosity. The complement (12:30/Matt 12:30: "He that is not with me is against me") addresses those who are neutral or hostile; this verse addresses those who are working in the name of Jesus. Kingdom work done in Jesus's name belongs to the kingdom, regardless of institutional affiliation. The disciples had made group identity the criterion of authenticity; Jesus makes fruit (effective exorcism) the criterion.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when the time was come that he should be received up, he stedfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem,
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῷ συμπληροῦσθαι τὰς ἡμέρας τῆς ἀναλήψεως αὐτοῦ, καὶ αὐτὸς τὸ πρόσωπον ἐστήρισεν τοῦ πορεύεσθαι εἰς Ἱερουσαλήμ,
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tō sumplērousthai tas hēmeras tēs analēpseōs autou, kai autos to prosōpon estērisen tou poreuesthai eis Hierousalēm,
verses.study_note: The great Lukan hinge: v.51 initiates the "Travel Narrative" or "Journey to Jerusalem" (9:51-19:44) — a 10-chapter section unique to Luke. "Was come that he should be received up" (analēpseōs — ascension/taking up) — the entire journey is framed from the perspective of the Ascension, not merely the cross. "Stedfastly set his face" (to prosōpon estērisen — fixed/set his face, like flint — Isa 50:7) — absolute determination and resolve.

verses.kjv_text: And sent messengers before his face: and they went, and entered into a village of the Samaritans, to make ready for him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπέστειλεν ἀγγέλους πρὸ προσώπου αὐτοῦ· καὶ πορευθέντες εἰσῆλθον εἰς κώμην Σαμαρειτῶν, ὥστε ἑτοιμάσαι αὐτῷ·
verses.transliteration: kai apesteilen angelous pro prosōpou autou; kai poreuthentes eisēlthon eis kōmēn Samaritōn, hōste etoimasai autō;
verses.study_note: "Messengers before his face" (angelous pro prosōpou — literally: angels/messengers before his face). The language echoes the messenger saying (7:27: "I send my messenger before thy face"). Jesus's advance preparation team enters a Samaritan village — another ethnic boundary crossing. Samaritans were despised by Jews and vice versa (John 4:9). The rejection that follows (v.53) anticipates the larger rejection at Jerusalem.

verses.kjv_text: And they did not receive him, because his face was as though he would go to Jerusalem.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐκ ἐδέξαντο αὐτόν, ὅτι τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ ἦν πορευόμενον εἰς Ἱερουσαλήμ.
verses.transliteration: kai ouk edexanto auton, hoti to prosōpon autou ēn poreuomenon eis Hierousalēm.
verses.study_note: "Did not receive him" — the Samaritans refused hospitality because Jesus was headed for Jerusalem, the Jewish holy city they considered illegitimate (cf. John 4:20). Their rejection was based on theological rivalry, not personal animosity. Luke will later present a Samaritan as the exemplary neighbor (10:33) and ten lepers as a group where only the Samaritan returned to give thanks (17:16) — consistent reframing of Samaritan-Jewish boundaries.

verses.kjv_text: And when his disciples James and John saw this, they said, Lord, wilt thou that we command fire to come down from heaven, and consume them, even as Elias did?
verses.original_text: ἰδόντες δὲ οἱ μαθηταὶ Ἰάκωβος καὶ Ἰωάννης εἶπον, Κύριε, θέλεις εἴπωμεν πῦρ καταβῆναι ἀπὸ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ ἀναλῶσαι αὐτούς, ὡς καὶ Ἡλίας ἐποίησεν;
verses.transliteration: idontes de hoi mathētai Iakōbos kai Iōannēs eipon, Kurie, theleis eipōmen pur katabenai apo tou ouranou kai analōsai autous, hōs kai Ēlias epoiēsen?
verses.study_note: James and John — named "Boanerges" (Sons of Thunder) by Jesus (Mark 3:17) — demonstrate why. Their reference to Elijah's fire (2 Kgs 1:10-12) reveals the Old Covenant reflex: judgment on rejection. But Jesus has come not to destroy but to seek and save (19:10). Just five verses earlier, Elijah appeared at the Transfiguration discussing the Exodus — the liberation, not the incineration. The same disciples are now proposing exactly what Jesus's mission overturns.

verses.kjv_text: But he turned, and rebuked them, and said, Ye know not what manner of spirit ye are of.
verses.original_text: στραφεὶς δὲ ἐπετίμησεν αὐτοῖς.
verses.transliteration: strapheis de epetimēsen autois.
verses.study_note: "He turned and rebuked them" — the same verb epetimao used for rebuking demons (4:35,41; 8:24; 9:42) and the storm (8:24). The disciples' retaliatory spirit is treated as something requiring the same authoritative rebuke applied to unclean powers. The longer textual tradition adds "Ye know not what manner of spirit ye are of" — they were operating from a spirit of vengeance, not the Spirit of the Son of Man who came to save.

verses.kjv_text: For the Son of man is not come to destroy men's lives, but to save them. And they went to another village.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπορεύθησαν εἰς ἑτέραν κώμην.
verses.transliteration: kai eporeuethēsan eis heteran kōmēn.
verses.study_note: "The Son of man is not come to destroy men's lives, but to save them" (in the longer textual tradition). This mission statement — saving not destroying — is the interpretive key to the entire Travel Narrative. Rejection does not trigger retaliation; it redirects mission. "They went to another village" — Jesus moves on without vengeance. His face is still set toward Jerusalem. Neither rejection nor the disciples' failures alter his determination.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that, as they went in the way, a certain man said unto him, Lord, I will follow thee whithersoever thou goest.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ πορευομένων αὐτῶν ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ εἶπέν τις πρὸς αὐτόν, Ἀκολουθήσω σοι ὅπου ἐὰν ἀπέρχῃ, Κύριε.
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de poreuomenōn autōn en tē hodō eipen tis pros auton, Akolouthēsō soi hopou ean aperchē, Kurie.
verses.study_note: "Whithersoever thou goest" — an enthusiastic, unlimited offer. The would-be follower volunteers spontaneously. But his offer may be based on romanticized expectations. Jesus's response is not discouragement but realism: he offers a clear-eyed picture of what following him actually involves. The three sayings that follow (vv.57-62) are a trilogy of discipleship cost.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said unto him, Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests; but the Son of man hath not where to lay his head.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς, Αἱ ἀλώπεκες φωλεοὺς ἔχουσιν, καὶ τὰ πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ κατασκηνώσεις· ὁ δὲ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου οὐκ ἔχει ποῦ τὴν κεφαλὴν κλίνῃ.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autō ho Iēsous, Hai alōpekes phōleous echousin, kai ta peteina tou ouranou kataskēnōseis; ho de Huios tou anthrōpou ouk echei pou tēn kephalēn klinē.
verses.study_note: "No place to lay his head" — the Creator has no home; the Lord of the universe has no bed. This radical homelessness is not incidental but representative. Jesus has given up all earthly security to accomplish his mission. To follow him is to accept the same. The contrast between animal creatures (foxes, birds) that have homes and the Son of Man who does not is deliberately paradoxical and costly.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto another, Follow me. But he said, Lord, suffer me first to go and bury my father.
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς ἕτερον, Ἀκολούθει μοι. ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Κύριε, ἐπίτρεψόν μοι ἀπελθόντι πρῶτον θάψαι τὸν πατέρα μου.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de pros heteron, Akolouthei moi. ho de eipen, Kurie, epitrepson moi apelthonti prōton thapsai ton patera mou.
verses.study_note: "Suffer me first to bury my father" — the most sacred filial duty in Jewish culture. Burying one's father was a commandment that even overrode other religious obligations. However, the father may not yet have died; the man may be saying "let me stay until my father eventually dies, then I'll follow." Jesus's response is not callousness toward filial duty but a declaration that the kingdom call supersedes even the most binding human obligation.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus said unto him, Let the dead bury their dead: but go thou and preach the kingdom of God.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ, Ἄφες τοὺς νεκροὺς θάψαι τοὺς ἑαυτῶν νεκρούς· σὺ δὲ ἀπελθὼν διάγγελλε τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: eipen de autō, Aphes tous nekrous thapsai tous heautōn nekrous; su de apelthōn diangeile tēn basileian tou Theou.
verses.study_note: "Let the dead bury their dead" — a paradox: those who are spiritually dead can attend to the physical dead; this man who is alive to the kingdom call has a more urgent assignment. "Go thou and preach the kingdom" — the command replaces the burial with proclamation. The kingdom is so urgently needed that even the most legitimate human delay must yield to it. The kingdom's urgency defines the priorities of those who have been called.

verses.kjv_text: And another also said, Lord, I will follow thee; but let me first go bid them farewell, which are at home at my house.
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ καὶ ἕτερος, Ἀκολουθήσω σοί, Κύριε· πρῶτον δὲ ἐπίτρεψόν μοι ἀποτάξασθαι τοῖς εἰς τὸν οἶκόν μου.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de kai heteros, Akolouthēsō soi, Kurie; prōton de epitrepson moi apotaxasthai tois eis ton oikon mou.
verses.study_note: "Bid them farewell" — a reasonable-sounding request. Elisha made the same request of Elijah (1 Kgs 19:20) and was permitted. But Jesus's response is more demanding than Elijah's. The reference to Elisha's call is deliberate (cf. Jesus as greater than Elijah established in Luke 4:25-27). The problem is not the farewell itself but the "first" (prōton) — the prioritizing of family loyalty over the kingdom call.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said unto him, No man, having put his hand to the plough, and looking back, is fit for the kingdom of God.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς, Οὐδεὶς ἐπιβαλὼν τὴν χεῖρα ἐπ' ἄροτρον καὶ βλέπων εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω εὔθετός ἐστιν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: eipen de ho Iēsous, Oudeis epibalōn tēn cheira ep' arotron kai blepōn eis ta opisō euthetos estin tē basileia tou Theou.
verses.study_note: "Having put his hand to the plough, and looking back" — a ploughman who looks back produces a crooked furrow. Ploughing requires complete forward-facing attention. "Is fit" (euthetos — suitable, useful). Discipleship requires the same undivided forward orientation. The three sayings together describe the total claims of the kingdom: no homelessness exemption (v.58), no family obligation exemption (v.60), no farewell exemption (v.62). Jesus is setting his face to Jerusalem; his disciples must set theirs likewise.

meta.overview: Luke 10 is one of the richest chapters in the entire Gospel, containing three of the most theologically significant units in Luke's Travel Narrative. The chapter opens with the commissioning of seventy (or seventy-two) additional disciples — a doubling of the missionary force that signals the kingdom's expanding advance. Their mission brief echoes the Twelve's commission (9:1-6) but with sharper urgency: the harvest is plentiful, the labourers are few, the time is short. The woes on Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum (vv.13-16) reveal the gravity of rejecting the kingdom — greater culpability than Tyre, Sidon, or Sodom.
meta.theme: The return of the seventy (vv.17-24) is one of the most joyful passages in the Gospels. They report with amazement that "even the devils are subject unto us through thy name." Jesus responds with a vision of cosmic significance: he saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven. The successful mission anticipates the full defeat of the enemy at the cross. Yet Jesus immediately redirects their joy: "rejoice not that the spirits are subject unto you; but rather rejoice, because your names are written in heaven." The chapter's theology of joy is grounded not in successful ministry performance but in the security of belonging to God.
meta.keyWordOriginal: The parable of the Good Samaritan (vv.25-37) is Luke's most famous parable and one of the most influential stories in human history. It arises from a lawyer's question about eternal life and his follow-up question "Who is my neighbour?" Jesus inverts the question: instead of asking who qualifies as my neighbour (thereby defining the limits of obligation), the parable asks what it means to be a neighbour. The Samaritan — the despised outcast who had every cultural reason to pass by — embodies the love that crosses every boundary. "Go, and do thou likewise" is the shortest and most demanding sermon in the Gospel.
meta.keyWordMeaning: The chapter closes with the Mary and Martha episode (vv.38-42) — another unit unique to Luke. The contrast between the two sisters provides the final theological word of the chapter: the "good part" that will not be taken away is not service (however essential), but sitting at Jesus's feet and hearing his word. After the chapter's intense emphasis on active mission, the contemplative posture of Mary is presented as the necessary foundation of all service. Word and deed, hearing and doing — both belong together in Luke's portrait of the kingdom disciple.
outline: πλησίον (plēsion)

verses.kjv_text: After these things the Lord appointed other seventy also, and sent them two and two before his face into every city and place, whither he himself would come.
verses.original_text: Μετὰ δὲ ταῦτα ἀνέδειξεν ὁ Κύριος καὶ ἑτέρους ἑβδομήκοντα, καὶ ἀπέστειλεν αὐτοὺς ἀνὰ δύο πρὸ προσώπου αὐτοῦ εἰς πᾶσαν πόλιν καὶ τόπον οὗ ἤμελλεν αὐτὸς ἔρχεσθαι.
verses.transliteration: Meta de tauta anedeixe ho Kurios kai heterous hebdomēkonta, kai apesteilen autous ana duo pro prosōpou autou eis pasan polin kai topon hou ēmellen autos erchesthai.
verses.study_note: "Seventy" (or seventy-two in some manuscripts) — most likely echoing the seventy elders of Israel (Num 11:16-17,24-25) and/or the seventy nations of Genesis 10 (LXX has 72), symbolizing both the breadth of Israel's leadership and the universal reach of the mission. "Two and two" (ana duo) — pairs provide mutual support, accountability, and the two-witness authentication. "Before his face" — they are advance parties for Jesus's own coming, as the messenger before the Lord (7:27).

verses.kjv_text: Therefore said he unto them, The harvest truly is great, but the labourers are few: pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he would send forth labourers into his harvest.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγεν δὲ πρὸς αὐτούς, Ὁ μὲν θερισμὸς πολύς, οἱ δὲ ἐργάται ὀλίγοι· δεήθητε οὖν τοῦ κυρίου τοῦ θερισμοῦ ὅπως ἐκβάλῃ ἐργάτας εἰς τὸν θερισμὸν αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: elegen de pros autous, Ho men therismos polus, hoi de ergatai oligoi; deēthēte oun tou kuriou tou therismou hopōs ekbalē ergatas eis ton herismon autou.
verses.study_note: "The harvest truly is great" — the metaphor of harvest communicates both urgency and abundance. The fields are white with grain (cf. John 4:35); the grain will perish if not reaped at the right moment. "The Lord of the harvest" (ton kurion tou therismou) — God himself owns the harvest and authorizes the workers. The disciples are to pray for more workers — not to work harder themselves (though they must), but to pray for more to be sent. The harvest exceeds any one team's capacity.

verses.kjv_text: Go your ways: behold, I send you forth as lambs among wolves.
verses.original_text: ὑπάγετε· ἰδοὺ ἀποστέλλω ὑμᾶς ὡς ἄρνας ἐν μέσῳ λύκων.
verses.transliteration: hupagete; idou apostellō humas hōs arnas en mesō lukōn.
verses.study_note: "Lambs among wolves" — a stark image of vulnerability and danger. The disciples are not sent as lions but as lambs. The power of their mission is not self-generated strength but the authority of the one who sends them. The image also anticipates the cross: the Lamb of God himself was the ultimate lamb among wolves. Vulnerability is not a failure of mission design; it is part of its theology.

verses.kjv_text: Carry neither purse, nor scrip, nor shoes: and salute no man by the way.
verses.original_text: μὴ βαστάζετε βαλλάντιον, μὴ πήραν, μὴ ὑποδήματα· καὶ μηδένα κατὰ τὴν ὁδὸν ἀσπάσησθε.
verses.transliteration: mē bastazetē ballantion, mē pēran, mē hupodēmata; kai mēdena kata tēn hodon aspasēsthe.
verses.study_note: "Salute no man by the way" — Eastern greetings were elaborate and time-consuming affairs that could consume hours. The urgency of the harvest does not permit extended social ritual. This is not social rudeness but eschatological urgency: the kingdom's time-sensitive advance takes priority over customary social delay. The austerity of equipment (no purse, bag, sandals) again expresses dependence on God and the host community.

verses.kjv_text: And into whatsoever house ye enter, first say, Peace be to this house.
verses.original_text: εἰς ἣν δ' ἂν εἰσέλθητε οἰκίαν, πρῶτον λέγετε, Εἰρήνη τῷ οἴκῳ τούτῳ.
verses.transliteration: eis hēn d' an eiselthēte oikian, prōton legete, Eirēnē tō oikō toutō.
verses.study_note: "Peace be to this house" (Eirēnē tō oikō toutō) — the Hebrew shalom greeting is here elevated to a performative declaration. The missionaries' greeting is not merely a social formula but a pronouncement of divine peace that seeks a "son of peace" (v.6) to receive it. The peace of the gospel is offered first, before any teaching or miracle. Hospitality is the prerequisite for mission.

verses.kjv_text: And if the son of peace be there, your peace shall rest upon it: if not, it shall turn to you again.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐὰν ἐκεῖ ᾖ υἱὸς εἰρήνης, ἐπαναπαύσεται ἐπ' αὐτὸν ἡ εἰρήνη ὑμῶν· εἰ δὲ μή γε, ἐφ' ὑμᾶς ἀνακάμψει.
verses.transliteration: kai ean ekei ē huios eirēnēs, epanapausetai ep' auton hē eirēnē humōn; ei de mē ge, eph' humas anakampsei.
verses.study_note: "Son of peace" (huios eirēnēs) — a Hebraism: a person characterized by peace, ready to receive it. Peace does not simply fall on all indiscriminately; it finds those who are prepared to receive it. "Shall turn to you again" — the peace offered is not wasted; if rejected, it returns to the messenger. This means the missionaries' own peace is not depleted by rejection — they return with the same peace they brought, ready to offer it in the next place.

verses.kjv_text: And in the same house remain, eating and drinking such things as they give: for the labourer is worthy of his hire. Go not from house to house.
verses.original_text: ἐν αὐτῇ δὲ τῇ οἰκίᾳ μένετε, ἐσθίοντες καὶ πίνοντες τὰ παρ' αὐτῶν· ἄξιος γὰρ ὁ ἐργάτης τοῦ μισθοῦ αὐτοῦ ἐστίν· μὴ μεταβαίνετε ἐξ οἰκίας εἰς οἰκίαν.
verses.transliteration: en autē de tē oikia menete, esthiontes kai pinontes ta par' autōn; axios gar ho ergatēs tou misthou autou estin; mē metabainete ex oikias eis oikian.
verses.study_note: "The labourer is worthy of his hire" (axios ho ergatēs tou misthou) — cited by Paul in 1 Tim 5:18 as Scripture. The principle establishes that those who preach the gospel have a right to material support from those who benefit (1 Cor 9:14). "Go not from house to house" — stability prevents the appearance of seeking better conditions and avoids disruption of the host community. Content with initial provision, the missionary focuses on the mission.

verses.kjv_text: And into whatsoever city ye enter, and they receive you, eat such things as are set before you:
verses.original_text: καὶ εἰς ἣν ἂν πόλιν εἰσέρχησθε, καὶ δέχωνταί ὑμᾶς, ἐσθίετε τὰ παρατιθέμενα ὑμῖν,
verses.transliteration: kai eis hēn an polin eiserchēsthe, kai dechōntai humas, esthiete ta paratithemena humin,
verses.study_note: "Eat such things as are set before you" — a significant ruling for missionaries in Gentile contexts (anticipated by Paul in 1 Cor 10:27). The missionary does not impose food laws on hosts; they eat whatever is provided with gratitude. This posture of humble acceptance counters the arrogance that might otherwise accompany a sense of religious superiority, and makes the missionary accessible to those they are sent to reach.

verses.kjv_text: And heal the sick that are therein, and say unto them, The kingdom of God is come nigh unto you.
verses.original_text: καὶ θεραπεύετε τοὺς ἐν αὐτῇ ἀσθενεῖς, καὶ λέγετε αὐτοῖς, Ἤγγικεν ἐφ' ὑμᾶς ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai therapeuete tous en autē astheneis, kai legete autois, Ēngiken eph' humas hē basileia tou Theou.
verses.study_note: "The kingdom of God is come nigh unto you" (ēngiken — perfect tense: has come near and remains near). The healing demonstrates the arrival of the kingdom in power; the proclamation interprets its meaning. The kingdom is not merely announced — it is enacted. The proximity of the kingdom is the reason for both the urgency and the joy. This is the evangelistic core of the mission: the kingdom has drawn near in the person of Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: But into whatsoever city ye enter, and they receive you not, go your ways out into the streets of the same, and say,
verses.original_text: εἰς ἣν δ' ἂν πόλιν εἰσέλθητε καὶ μὴ δέχωνταί ὑμᾶς, ἐξελθόντες εἰς τὰς πλατείας αὐτῆς εἴπατε,
verses.transliteration: eis hēn d' an polin eiselthēte kai mē dechōntai humas, exelthontes eis tas plateias autēs eipate,
verses.study_note: The instruction for rejection is deliberate and public: go into the streets (the public square) and make a formal declaration. Rejection does not mean silent withdrawal; it means a public testimony that the offer was made and refused. This protects both the missionaries (from indefinite labor in unreceptive soil) and the city (from the false impression that no warning was given).

verses.kjv_text: Even the very dust of your city, which cleaveth on us, we do wipe off against you: notwithstanding be ye sure of this, that the kingdom of God is come nigh unto you.
verses.original_text: Καὶ τὸν κονιορτὸν τὸν κολληθέντα ἡμῖν ἐκ τῆς πόλεως ὑμῶν ἀπομασσόμεθα ὑμῖν· πλὴν τοῦτο γινώσκετε, ὅτι ἤγγικεν ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Kai ton koniorton ton kollēthenta hēmin ek tēs poleōs humōn apomassometha humin; plēn touto ginōskete, hoti ēngiken hē basileia tou Theou.
verses.study_note: Even in rejection, the kingdom's nearness is re-declared: "notwithstanding be ye sure of this" — the rejected city is left with no excuse. The dust-shaking (echoing 9:5) combined with the re-proclamation of kingdom nearness is a paradox: we leave, but we leave you with the truth we came to bring. The offer stands; the consequences of rejection also stand.

verses.kjv_text: But I say unto you, that it shall be more tolerable in that day for Sodom, than for that city.
verses.original_text: λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι Σοδόμοις ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ ἀνεκτότερον ἔσται ἢ τῇ πόλει ἐκείνῃ.
verses.transliteration: legō humin hoti Sodomois en tē hēmera ekeinē anektoteron estai ē tē polei ekeinē.
verses.study_note: "More tolerable for Sodom" — Sodom was the archetype of wickedness destroyed by divine judgment (Gen 19). Yet Sodom never had the gospel; cities that reject the seventy have had the kingdom brought to their door. Greater privilege creates greater accountability. "In that day" — the eschatological judgment day. The comparative "more tolerable" (anektoteron) implies degrees of punishment in final judgment, a principle consistently affirmed in Luke's eschatological teaching.

verses.kjv_text: Woe unto thee, Chorazin! woe unto thee, Bethsaida! for if the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon, which have been done in you, they had a great while ago repented, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.
verses.original_text: Οὐαί σοι, Χοραζίν· οὐαί σοι, Βηθσαϊδά· ὅτι εἰ ἐν Τύρῳ καὶ Σιδῶνι ἐγενήθησαν αἱ δυνάμεις αἱ γενόμεναι ἐν ὑμῖν, πάλαι ἂν ἐν σάκκῳ καὶ σποδῷ καθήμεναι μετενόησαν.
verses.transliteration: Ouai soi, Chorazin; ouai soi, Bēthsaida; hoti ei en Turō kai Sidōni egenēthēsan hai dunameis hai genomenai en humin, palai an en sakkō kai spodō kathēmenai metenoesan.
verses.study_note: Woes (ouai — laments/judgments) on Galilean cities that witnessed Jesus's miracles but did not repent. Chorazin and Bethsaida were near Capernaum on the northern shore of Galilee; the miracles performed there are not specifically recorded in the Gospels but were apparently extensive. Tyre and Sidon were ancient Phoenician cities condemned by the prophets (Isa 23; Ezek 26-28) — pagan cities of proverbial wickedness. Yet they would have repented in sackcloth. The failure of religious familiarity is more culpable than the failure of pagan ignorance.

verses.kjv_text: But it shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon at the judgment, than for you.
verses.original_text: πλὴν Τύρῳ καὶ Σιδῶνι ἀνεκτότερον ἔσται ἐν τῇ κρίσει ἢ ὑμῖν.
verses.transliteration: plēn Turō kai Sidōni anektoteron estai en tē krisei ē humin.
verses.study_note: "At the judgment" (en tē krisei) — the final eschatological reckoning. The comparative "more tolerable" again implies differential judgment based on available light and response. This principle has profound pastoral implications: those who sit under faithful gospel preaching but remain unmoved bear greater responsibility than those who have never heard. Chorazin and Bethsaida had "the mighty works" (dunameis — miracles) as evidence; Tyre and Sidon had neither.

verses.kjv_text: And thou, Capernaum, which art exalted to heaven, shalt be thrust down to hell.
verses.original_text: καὶ σύ, Καφαρναούμ, ἡ ἕως τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ὑψωθεῖσα, ἕως τοῦ ᾅδου καταβιβασθήσῃ.
verses.transliteration: kai su, Kapharnaum, hē heōs tou ouranou hupsōtheisa, heōs tou haidou katabibasthēsē.
verses.study_note: "Exalted to heaven" — Capernaum was the center of Jesus's Galilean ministry, the site of the most miracles recorded in the Gospels. Its elevation was spiritual privilege, not merely geographic. "Thrust down to hell" (hadou — Hades, the realm of the dead, the place of the departed). The language echoes the taunt against Babylon (Isa 14:13-15: "I will ascend to heaven... thou shalt be brought down to Sheol"). The greater the honour, the greater the fall of rejection.

verses.kjv_text: He that heareth you heareth me; and he that despiseth you despiseth me; and he that despiseth me despiseth him that sent me.
verses.original_text: Ὁ ἀκούων ὑμῶν ἐμοῦ ἀκούει· καὶ ὁ ἀθετῶν ὑμᾶς ἐμὲ ἀθετεῖ· ὁ δὲ ἐμὲ ἀθετῶν ἀθετεῖ τὸν ἀποστείλαντά με.
verses.transliteration: Ho akouōn humōn emou akouei; kai ho athetōn humas eme athetei; ho de eme athetōn athetei ton aposteilanta me.
verses.study_note: The apostolic chain of identification: the messenger's reception is the Lord's reception; the messenger's rejection is the Lord's rejection; the Lord's rejection is the Father's rejection. "Despiseth" (athetōn — sets aside, nullifies, treats as invalid). The word is used for setting aside a legal contract. To dismiss the apostles' message is to legally nullify the claims of both the Son and the Father. This gives apostolic preaching its ultimate weight and authority.

verses.kjv_text: And the seventy returned again with joy, saying, Lord, even the devils are subject unto us through thy name.
verses.original_text: Ὑπέστρεψαν δὲ οἱ ἑβδομήκοντα μετὰ χαρᾶς λέγοντες, Κύριε, καὶ τὰ δαιμόνια ὑποτάσσεται ἡμῖν ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί σου.
verses.transliteration: Hupestrepsan de hoi hebdomēkonta meta charas legontes, Kurie, kai ta daimonia hupotassetai hēmin en tō onomati sou.
verses.study_note: "With joy" (meta charas) — the return is celebratory. They had expected to preach; they discovered they had authority over demons. "Through thy name" (en tō onomati sou) — the authority is explicitly derived, not inherent. They do not say "we have power over demons"; they say "through your name they are subject to us." The name of Jesus is the operative authority, not the disciples' personal spiritual credentials.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, I beheld Satan as lightning fall from heaven.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτοῖς, Ἐθεώρουν τὸν Σατανᾶν ὡς ἀστραπὴν ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ πεσόντα.
verses.transliteration: eipen de autois, Etheorun ton Satanan hōs astrapen ek tou ouranou pesonta.
verses.study_note: "I beheld Satan as lightning fall from heaven" — a visionary declaration, not necessarily a reference to the primordial fall of Isaiah 14 (though that may inform the imagery), but to the ongoing defeat of Satan's kingdom through the disciples' mission. Each exorcism is a skirmish in the cosmic war; Jesus sees the aggregate effect: Satan's kingdom losing its heavenly footing. The lightning imagery suggests speed, brightness, and finality. The cross will complete this defeat (John 12:31; Col 2:15).

verses.kjv_text: Behold, I give unto you power to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy: and nothing shall by any means hurt you.
verses.original_text: ἰδοὺ δέδωκα ὑμῖν τὴν ἐξουσίαν τοῦ πατεῖν ἐπάνω ὄφεων καὶ σκορπίων, καὶ ἐπὶ πᾶσαν τὴν δύναμιν τοῦ ἐχθροῦ· καὶ οὐδὲν ὑμᾶς οὐ μὴ ἀδικήσῃ.
verses.transliteration: idou dedōka humin tēn exousian tou patein epanō opheōn kai skorpiōn, kai epi pasan tēn dunamin tou echthrou; kai ouden humas ou mē adikēsē.
verses.study_note: "Serpents and scorpions" — symbols of demonic danger and hostile powers (Ps 91:13; Isa 11:8). The authority to "tread upon" them echoes Genesis 3:15 (the heel that crushes the serpent's head) and points to the Messiah's ultimate victory. "Over all the power of the enemy" (pasan tēn dunamin tou echthrou — the entire power of the enemy). "Nothing shall hurt you" — not a promise of physical immunity but of spiritual invincibility in kingdom mission.

verses.kjv_text: Notwithstanding in this rejoice not, that the spirits are subject unto you; but rather rejoice, because your names are written in heaven.
verses.original_text: πλὴν ἐν τούτῳ μὴ χαίρετε, ὅτι τὰ πνεύματα ὑμῖν ὑποτάσσεται· χαίρετε δὲ ὅτι τὰ ὀνόματα ὑμῶν ἐγγέγραπται ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς.
verses.transliteration: plēn en toutō mē chairete, hoti ta pneumata humin hupotassetai; chairete de hoti ta onomata humōn engegraptai en tois ouranois.
verses.study_note: The decisive redirection of kingdom joy. "Rejoice not in this" — ministry success, however real and wonderful, is not the foundation of Christian joy. "Rejoice because your names are written in heaven" — the perfect tense (engegraptai — has been written and stands written) indicates a permanent, irrevocable registration. The Book of Life (Rev 3:5; 20:12; 21:27). Joy grounded in election and redemption is independent of ministry outcomes; it cannot be taken away by failure, opposition, or death.

verses.kjv_text: In that hour Jesus rejoiced in spirit, and said, I thank thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes: even so, Father; for so it seemed good in thy sight.
verses.original_text: Ἐν αὐτῇ τῇ ὥρᾳ ἠγαλλιάσατο ἐν τῷ Πνεύματι τῷ ἁγίῳ, καὶ εἶπεν, Ἐξομολογοῦμαί σοι, Πάτερ, Κύριε τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ τῆς γῆς, ὅτι ἀπέκρυψας ταῦτα ἀπὸ σοφῶν καὶ συνετῶν, καὶ ἀπεκάλυψας αὐτὰ νηπίοις· ναί, ὁ Πατήρ, ὅτι οὕτως εὐδοκία ἐγένετο ἔμπροσθέν σου.
verses.transliteration: En autē tē hōra ēgalliasato en tō Pneumati tō hagiō, kai eipen, Exomologoumai soi, Pater, Kurie tou ouranou kai tēs gēs, hoti apekrupsas tauta apo sophōn kai sunetōn, kai apekalupsas auta nēpiois; nai, ho Patēr, hoti houtōs eudokia egeneto emprosthen sou.
verses.study_note: "Jesus rejoiced in spirit" (ēgalliasato — exulted, leaped with joy) — Luke uniquely records Jesus's jubilation. It is the most explicit expression of Jesus's own emotional delight in the Gospels. "In the Holy Spirit" — the joy is Spirit-prompted. He thanks the Father for the sovereign mystery of revelation: hidden from the "wise and prudent" (those who trust in their own intellectual and religious resources), revealed to "babes" (nēpiois — infants, the humble, the unlearned). The sovereign pleasure of the Father (eudokia — good pleasure, gracious will) is the ultimate explanation.

verses.kjv_text: All things are delivered to me of my Father: and no man knoweth who the Son is, but the Father; and who the Father is, but the Son, and he to whom the Son will reveal him.
verses.original_text: Πάντα μοι παρεδόθη ὑπὸ τοῦ Πατρός μου· καὶ οὐδεὶς γινώσκει τίς ἐστιν ὁ Υἱὸς εἰ μὴ ὁ Πατήρ, καὶ τίς ἐστιν ὁ Πατὴρ εἰ μὴ ὁ Υἱὸς καὶ ᾧ ἐὰν βούλητai ὁ Υἱὸς ἀποκαλύψαι.
verses.transliteration: Panta moi paredothē hupo tou Patros mou; kai oudeis ginōskei tis estin ho Huios ei mē ho Patēr, kai tis estin ho Patēr ei mē ho Huios kai hō ean boulētai ho Huios apokalupsai.
verses.study_note: "All things are delivered to me of my Father" — a comprehensive claim to universal authority and inheritance. The mutual knowledge of Father and Son is exclusive and reciprocal: only the Father truly knows the Son's identity; only the Son truly knows the Father. This is sometimes called the "Johannine bolt" in the Synoptics — its content is characteristic of John's Gospel (1:18; 17:25). It establishes that knowledge of God is Christologically mediated: only through the Son's revelation is the Father known.

verses.kjv_text: And he turned him unto his disciples, and said privately, Blessed are the eyes which see the things that ye see:
verses.original_text: Καὶ στραφεὶς πρὸς τοὺς μαθητὰς κατ' ἰδίαν εἶπεν, Μακάριοι οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ οἱ βλέποντες ἃ βλέπετε·
verses.transliteration: Kai strapheis pros tous mathētas kat' idian eipen, Makarioi hoi ophthalmoi hoi blepontes ha blepete;
verses.study_note: "Privately" (kat' idian) — Jesus turns from the public praise to a private beatitude for the disciples. "Blessed are the eyes" — a beatitude of privilege. The disciples are witnesses to what countless faithful people longed to see but died without seeing. The passive recipients of revelation are declared blessed — not for their own merit but for their participation in the kairos moment of history.

verses.kjv_text: For I tell you, that many prophets and kings have desired to see those things which ye see, and have not seen them; and to hear those things which ye hear, and have not heard them.
verses.original_text: λέγω γὰρ ὑμῖν ὅτι πολλοὶ προφῆται καὶ βασιλεῖς ἠθέλησαν ἰδεῖν ἃ ὑμεῖς βλέπετε, καὶ οὐκ εἶδαν· καὶ ἀκοῦσαι ἃ ἀκούετε, καὶ οὐκ ἤκουσαν.
verses.transliteration: legō gar humin hoti polloi prophētai kai basileis ēthelēsan idein ha humeis blepete, kai ouk eidan; kai akousai ha akouete, kai ouk ēkousan.
verses.study_note: "Prophets and kings" — the most distinguished figures of the OT age. Abraham "saw" Christ's day from afar and rejoiced (John 8:56); David prophesied of his Lord's resurrection (Acts 2:25-31); Isaiah saw the glory (John 12:41). They saw by faith and prophecy; the disciples saw with physical eyes. The fulfilment of OT hope is the privilege of the disciples' generation. This is the theological climax of the mission section: what the seventy witnessed in their successful ministry was the fruit of millennia of prophetic anticipation.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, a certain lawyer stood up, and tempted him, saying, Master, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἰδοὺ νομικός τις ἀνέστη ἐκπειράζων αὐτὸν λέγων, Διδάσκαλε, τί ποιήσας ζωὴν αἰώνιον κληρονομήσω;
verses.transliteration: Kai idou nomikos tis anestē ekpeirazōn auton legōn, Didaskale, ti poiēsas zōēn aiōnion klēronomēsō?
verses.study_note: "A certain lawyer" (nomikos — an expert in Torah, a scribe). "Tempted him" (ekpeirazōn — testing, attempting to entrap). The question "what shall I do to inherit eternal life?" is sincere in form but adversarial in intent. Note the embedded theological assumption: he frames it as something to be "done" — earned. Jesus's response will use the lawyer's own Torah expertise to show him both the answer and his failure to live it.

verses.kjv_text: He said unto him, What is written in the law? how readest thou?
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτόν, Ἐν τῷ νόμῳ τί γέγραπται; πῶς ἀναγινώσκεις;
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen pros auton, En tō nomō ti gegraptai? pōs anaginōskeis?
verses.study_note: "How readest thou?" — Jesus uses the lawyer's own expertise as the instrument of instruction. Rather than providing an authoritative answer directly, he asks the expert to supply what he already knows. This Socratic approach forces the lawyer to own the answer before it convicts him. "What is written" (ti gegraptai — perfect: what stands written) — the permanent, authoritative Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: And he answering said, Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy strength, and with all thy mind; and thy neighbour as thyself.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν, Ἀγαπήσεις Κύριον τὸν Θεόν σου ἐξ ὅλης τῆς καρδίας σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς ψυχῆς σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς ἰσχύος σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς διανοίας σου, καὶ τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν.
verses.transliteration: ho de apokritheis eipen, Agapēseis Kurion ton Theon sou ex holēs tēs kardias sou kai ex holēs tēs psuchēs sou kai ex holēs tēs ischuos sou kai ex holēs tēs dianoias sou, kai ton plēsion sou hōs seauton.
verses.study_note: The lawyer correctly summarizes the whole Law: Deuteronomy 6:5 (the Shema — love of God) + Leviticus 19:18 (love of neighbour).
