The four-fold exhaustive love of God (heart, soul, strength, mind) describes love that leaves no compartment of the self uncommitted. The combination of the two commandments is confirmed by Jesus in 20:27 (Mark 12:29-31) as the summary of all Torah. The lawyer's answer is theologically impeccable — and spiritually impossible without grace.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, Thou hast answered right: this do, and thou shalt live.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ, Ὀρθῶς ἀπεκρίθης· τοῦτο ποίει καὶ ζήσῃ.
verses.transliteration: eipen de autō, Orthōs apekrithēs; touto poiei kai zēsē.
verses.study_note: "Thou hast answered right" (Orthōs apekrithēs) — Jesus commends the answer. "This do, and thou shalt live" — the conditional clause is the whole problem. The lawyer knows the law; the question is whether he lives it. Jesus is not offering a works-righteousness path to salvation but exposing the lawyer's actual situation: you know what is required; are you doing it? The follow-up question (v.29) reveals that the lawyer already knows the answer.

verses.kjv_text: But he, willing to justify himself, said unto Jesus, And who is my neighbour?
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ θέλων δικαιῶσαι ἑαυτόν, εἶπεν πρὸς τὸν Ἰησοῦν, Καὶ τίς ἐστίν μου πλησίον;
verses.transliteration: ho de thelōn dikaiōsai heauton, eipen pros ton Iēsoun, Kai tis estin mou plēsion?
verses.study_note: "Willing to justify himself" (thelōn dikaiōsai heauton) — a crucial psychological disclosure. He senses his failure at the love-of-God standard and seeks to limit the scope of "neighbour" to make his compliance plausible. "Who is my neighbour?" — asking for a definition that will permit exclusion: who qualifies? Who can I legitimately ignore? Jesus's parable will invert the question entirely: not "who must I love?" but "are you being the neighbour the situation requires?"

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answering said, A certain man went down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and fell among thieves, which stripped him of his raiment, and wounded him, and departed, leaving him half dead.
verses.original_text: ὑπολαβὼν δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν, Ἄνθρωπός τις κατέβαινεν ἀπὸ Ἱερουσαλὴμ εἰς Ἰεριχώ, καὶ λῃσταῖς περιέπεσεν, οἳ καὶ ἐκδύσαντες αὐτὸν καὶ πληγὰς ἐπιθέντες ἀπῆλθον, ἀφέντες ἡμιθανῆ τυγχάνοντα.
verses.transliteration: hupolabōn de ho Iēsous eipen, Anthrōpos tis katebaine apo Hierousalēm eis Ierichos, kai lēstais periesepsen, hoi kai ekdusantes auton kai plegas epithentes apēlthen, aphentes hēmithanē tunchanonta.
verses.study_note: The Jerusalem-Jericho road descended 3,300 feet in 17 miles through barren, rocky wilderness — notoriously dangerous (Jerome called it "the bloody way"). The traveler is unnamed: no ethnic or religious identity is assigned, making him representative of any human being in need. "Stripped him, wounded him, half dead" — three stages of complete vulnerability. Stripped: no identity markers left. Wounded: physically broken. Half dead: beyond self-rescue.

verses.kjv_text: And by chance there came down a certain priest that way: and when he saw him, he passed by on the other side.
verses.original_text: κατὰ συγκυρίαν δὲ ἱερεύς τις κατέβαινεν ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ ἐκείνῃ· καὶ ἰδὼν αὐτὸν ἀντιπαρῆλθεν.
verses.transliteration: kata sunkurian de hiereus tis katebaine en tē hodō ekeinē; kai idōn auton antiparēlthen.
verses.study_note: "By chance" (kata sunkurian — by coincidence). The priest "saw him" (idōn) — he looked, perceived, and still "passed by on the other side" (antiparēlthen — crossed to the opposite side of the road). The priest had a rationale: if the man was dead, contact would render him unclean for temple service (Lev 21:1-4); and priestly duties in Jerusalem had to be protected. The law-keeper's law became an excuse for lawlessness in the one commandment that superseded the ritual: love of neighbour.

verses.kjv_text: And likewise a Levite, when he was at the place, came and looked on him, and passed by on the other side.
verses.original_text: ὁμοίως δὲ καὶ Λευΐτης γενόμενος κατὰ τὸν τόπον, ἐλθὼν καὶ ἰδὼν ἀντιπαρῆλθεν.
verses.transliteration: homoiōs de kai Leuitēs genomenos kata ton topon, elthōn kai idōn antiparēlthen.
verses.study_note: The Levite "came and looked" — he went further than the priest (closer to the man) but still "passed by on the other side." He had even less ritual justification than the priest. Both religious professionals — the very people the law-expert expected to serve as positive examples — pass by. Their failure is not attributed to malice but to a failure of compassion in the face of obligation. The audience expected a third figure to be an Israelite layman; Jesus instead introduces a Samaritan.

verses.kjv_text: But a certain Samaritan, as he journeyed, came where he was: and when he saw him, he had compassion on him,
verses.original_text: Σαμαρείτης δέ τις ὁδεύων ἦλθεν κατ' αὐτὸν, καὶ ἰδὼν αὐτὸν ἐσπλαγχνίσθη,
verses.transliteration: Samareitēs de tis hodeuōn ēlthen kat' auton, kai idōn auton esplanchnisthē,
verses.study_note: The shocking reversal: the hero of the story is the despised Samaritan — the very people the disciples had wanted to incinerate (9:54), the people who had refused Jesus passage (9:53). "He had compassion" (esplanchnisthē — the visceral compassion of the bowels, the same word used of Jesus in 7:13). The Samaritan "also saw" the man (idōn — the same verb used of the priest and Levite), but where they saw and passed by, he saw and was moved. Compassion is the difference between seeing and acting.

verses.kjv_text: And went to him, and bound up his wounds, pouring in oil and wine, and set him on his own beast, and brought him to an inn, and took care of him.
verses.original_text: καὶ προσελθὼν κατέδησεν τὰ τραύματα αὐτοῦ, ἐπιχέων ἔλαιον καὶ οἶνον· ἐπιβιβάσας δὲ αὐτὸν ἐπὶ τὸ ἴδιον κτῆνος ἤγαγεν αὐτὸν εἰς πανδοχεῖον καὶ ἐπεμελήθη αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai proselthōn katedeesen ta traumata autou, epicheon elaion kai oinon; epibibasas de auton epi to idion ktēnos ēgagen auton eis pandocheion kai epemelēthē autou.
verses.study_note: Six acts of costly care: (1) went to him — crossed the social boundary; (2) bound his wounds; (3) poured oil and wine — standard ancient medicines, from his own supplies; (4) set him on his own beast — walked himself; (5) brought him to an inn; (6) took care of him through the night. Each action is concrete, specific, and costly. The Samaritan gave his time, his materials, his animal, and his money. Love is not sentiment; it is specific, costly action.

verses.kjv_text: And on the morrow when he departed, he took out two pence, and gave them to the host, and said unto him, Take care of him; and whatsoever thou spendest more, when I come again, I will repay thee.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπὶ τὴν αὔριον ἐκβαλὼν ἔδωκεν δύο δηνάρια τῷ πανδοχεῖ, καὶ εἶπεν, Ἐπιμελήθητι αὐτοῦ· καὶ ὅ τι ἂν προσδαπανήσῃς, ἐγὼ ἐν τῷ ἐπανέρχεσθαί με ἀποδώσω σοι.
verses.transliteration: kai epi tēn aurion ekbalōn edōken duo dēnaria tō pandochei, kai eipen, Epimelēthēti autou; kai hoti an prosdapanēsēs, egō en tō epanercheesthai me apodōsō soi.
verses.study_note: "Two pence" (duo dēnaria — two days' wages), enough for several weeks' lodging. "Whatsoever thou spendest more, I will repay" — an open-ended financial commitment to a stranger's welfare. The Samaritan's generosity has no upper limit: he trusts the innkeeper to spend what is needed and commits to cover whatever additional cost arises on his return. This is extravagant, overflowing generosity — the character of God himself toward sinners.

verses.kjv_text: Which now of these three, thinkest thou, was neighbour unto him that fell among the thieves?
verses.original_text: τίς τούτων τῶν τριῶν πλησίον δοκεῖ σοι γεγονέναι τοῦ ἐμπεσόντος εἰς τοὺς λῃστάς;
verses.transliteration: tis toutōn tōn triōn plēsion dokei soi gegonenai tou empesontos eis tous lēstas?
verses.study_note: The question inverts the lawyer's original question. He asked "who is my neighbour?" — seeking to define the limits of obligation. Jesus asks "which one became a neighbour?" — asking about action rather than status. The neighbour is not defined by proximity, ethnicity, or religion but by the act of compassionate service. The lawyer must answer out of his own mouth — and in doing so, must name the Samaritan (though note he cannot bring himself to say the name in v.37: "he that shewed mercy").

verses.kjv_text: And he said, He that shewed mercy on him. Then said Jesus unto him, Go, and do thou likewise.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Ὁ ποιήσας τὸ ἔλεος μετ' αὐτοῦ. εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς, Πορεύου καὶ σὺ ποίει ὁμοίως.
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen, Ho poiēsas to eleos met' autou. eipen de autō ho Iēsous, Poreuou kai su poiei homoiōs.
verses.study_note: "He that shewed mercy" (ho poiēsas to eleos — the one who did mercy). The lawyer cannot bring himself to say "the Samaritan." But he has answered correctly. "Go, and do thou likewise" (Poreuou kai su poiei homoiōs) — five words in Greek that constitute the most direct and demanding ethical command of the Sermon section. The parable was not merely illustrative but imperative: go and act as the Samaritan acted, regardless of the victim's identity. Mercy has no ethnic preconditions.

verses.kjv_text: Now it came to pass, as they went, that he entered into a certain village: and a certain woman named Martha received him into her house.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῷ πορεύεσθαι αὐτοὺς καὶ αὐτὸς εἰσῆλθεν εἰς κώμην τινά· γυνὴ δέ τις ὀνόματι Μάρθα ὑπεδέξατο αὐτὸν εἰς τὴν οἰκίαν αὐτῆς.
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tō poreuesthai autous kai autos eisēlthen eis kōmēn tina; gunē de tis onomati Martha hupedexato auton eis tēn oikian autēs.
verses.study_note: The village is not named here, but John 11:1 identifies Bethany as the home of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus. "Received him" (hupedexato — welcomed as a guest, the same verb used for Zacchaeus in 19:6). Martha takes initiative in hospitality — she is the householder, the one who "received" Jesus. Her service (diakonia, v.40) is honored in Luke as an essential form of discipleship. The problem is not the service but its proportion.

verses.kjv_text: And she had a sister called Mary, which also sat at Jesus' feet, and heard his word.
verses.original_text: καὶ τῇδε ἦν ἀδελφὴ καλουμένη Μαρία, ἣ καὶ παρακαθίσασα παρὰ τοὺς πόδας τοῦ Ἰησοῦ ἤκουεν τὸν λόγον αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai tēde ēn adelphē kaloumenē Maria, hē kai parakathisasa para tous podas tou Iēsou ēkouen ton logon autou.
verses.study_note: "Sat at Jesus' feet" (parakathisasa para tous podas) — the posture of a disciple receiving Torah from a rabbi (cf. Paul "at the feet of Gamaliel," Acts 22:3). In the first century, women did not typically sit at a rabbi's feet to be taught; Mary's posture is itself radical — she claims the right of discipleship. "Heard his word" (ēkouen ton logon — imperfect: was continuously hearing). She is the good-soil hearer of 8:15: one who hears and keeps the word.

verses.kjv_text: But Martha was cumbered about much serving, and came to him, and said, Lord, dost thou not care that my sister hath left me to serve alone? bid her therefore that she help me.
verses.original_text: ἡ δὲ Μάρθα περιεσπᾶτο περὶ πολλὴν διακονίαν· ἐπιστᾶσα δὲ εἶπεν, Κύριε, οὐ μέλει σοί ὅτι ἡ ἀδελφή μου μόνην με κατέλιπεν διακονεῖν; εἰπὲ οὖν αὐτῇ ἵνα μοι συναντιλάβηται.
verses.transliteration: hē de Martha periespato peri pollēn diakonian; epistasa de eipen, Kurie, ou melei soi hoti hē adelphē mou monēn me katelipen diakonein? eipe oun autē hina moi sunantilabētai.
verses.study_note: "Cumbered about" (periespato — distracted, pulled in all directions). The word is vivid: Martha was being yanked around by many tasks. "Much serving" (pollēn diakonian) — the word is noble (diakonia), but "much" indicates it had become excessive and anxiety-producing. Her appeal to Jesus — "dost thou not care?" — is a mild accusation. She wants Jesus to adjudicate the domestic dispute in her favor. But in coming to Jesus with her complaint, she has done what Mary had done — she has come to his feet.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answered and said unto her, Martha, Martha, thou art careful and troubled about many things:
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ εἶπεν αὐτῇ ὁ Κύριος, Μάρθα Μάρθα, μεριμνᾷς καὶ τυρβάζῃ περὶ πολλά,
verses.transliteration: apokritheis de eipen autē ho Kurios, Martha Martha, merimnas kai turbazetai peri polla,
verses.study_note: "Martha, Martha" — the doubled name is an address of tender rebuke (cf. "Simon, Simon" in 22:31; "Saul, Saul" in Acts 9:4). It is a gentle, loving correction. "Thou art careful and troubled" (merimnās — anxious; turbazetai — disturbed, unsettled). The two verbs describe both the internal state (anxiety) and the resulting disturbance. "About many things" (peri polla) — the multiplicity is the problem. Service has fragmented her attention into an anxious scatter.

verses.kjv_text: But one thing is needful: and Mary hath chosen that good part, which shall not be taken away from her.
verses.original_text: ἑνὸς δέ ἐστιν χρεία· Μαριὰμ γὰρ τὴν ἀγαθὴν μερίδα ἐξελέξατο, ἥτις οὐκ ἀφαιρεθήσεται αὐτῆς.
verses.transliteration: henos de estin chreia; Mariam gar tēn agathēn merida exelexato, hētis ouk aphairethēsetai autēs.
verses.study_note: "One thing is needful" (henos de estin chreia — but of one thing there is need). The "one thing" is not identified as Mary's behavior in isolation but as the principle it represents: hearing the word of Jesus has priority over all other activity. "Mary hath chosen that good part" (tēn agathēn merida — the good portion/share). "Which shall not be taken away from her" (ouk aphairethēsetai — future passive: will absolutely not be taken away). The word received and held is permanent; hospitality arrangements are temporary. This is the chapter's final word: the foundation of all mission and service is sitting at the feet of Jesus and hearing his word.

meta.overview: Luke 11 opens with Jesus praying — as he does at every great turning point in Luke's Gospel — and his disciples' earnest request: "Lord, teach us to pray" (v.1). The model prayer he gives (vv.2-4) is shorter than Matthew's version but equally comprehensive, addressing God's name, kingdom, provision, forgiveness, and deliverance. The parable of the friend at midnight (vv.5-8) introduces shamelessness (anaideia) as the posture of persistent prayer, followed by the triple promise of Ask-Seek-Knock (vv.9-13), which climaxes not in material provision but in the gift of the Holy Spirit — the supreme answer to prayer.
meta.theme: The Beelzebub controversy (vv.14-26) forms the chapter's theological battlefield. Jesus demonstrates by logic that his exorcisms cannot come from Satan — a divided kingdom falls — and by typology that his works fulfil the "finger of God" motif of the Exodus (v.20). The parable of the strong man overcome (vv.21-22) portrays the cross as the cosmic victory over the armed keeper of souls. The sobering warning about the returning unclean spirit (vv.24-26) applies to Israel's spiritual condition: swept clean by John's ministry but not filled with the Spirit.
meta.keyWordOriginal: The sign of Jonah (vv.29-32) is Jesus's only concession to sign-seekers, but given as a rebuke rather than a gift. The queen of Sheba and the Ninevites will condemn this generation because they responded to lesser messengers. The lamp and eye sayings (vv.33-36) press for undivided spiritual vision: only the "single eye" (haploūs — clear, generous, wholehearted) receives the full light of Christ. The chapter closes with a devastating series of seven woes against the Pharisees and lawyers (vv.37-54) — the most extensive public denunciation in Luke — for straining out external religion while ignoring justice, love, and the key of knowledge.
meta.keyWordMeaning: The setting of the woes in the Pharisee's dining room (v.37) replicates the dinner scene of chapter 7. The seven woes (three against Pharisees: vv.42-44; three against lawyers: vv.46-52; with the blood-guilt summary vv.49-51) build to the indictment that they have "taken away the key of knowledge" (v.52). The chapter ends with the scribes and Pharisees laying wait to catch something out of Jesus's mouth — the hostility that will eventually produce the crucifixion.
outline: προσεύχομαι (proseuchomai)

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that, as he was praying in a certain place, when he ceased, one of his disciples said unto him, Lord, teach us to pray, as John also taught his disciples.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ εἶναι αὐτὸν ἐν τόπῳ τινὶ προσευχόμενον, ὡς ἐπαύσατο, εἶπέν τις τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ πρὸς αὐτόν, Κύριε, δίδαξον ἡμᾶς προσεύχεσθαι, καθὼς καὶ Ἰωάννης ἐδίδαξεν τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en tō einai auton en topō tini proseuchomenon, hōs epausato, eipen tis tōn mathētōn autou pros auton, Kurie, didaxon hēmas proseuchesthai, kathōs kai Iōannēs edidaxen tous mathētas autou.
verses.study_note: The unspecified "certain place" draws attention not to geography but to the practice itself: Jesus is found praying, as at his baptism (3:21), before calling the twelve (6:12), at Caesarea Philippi (9:18), and the transfiguration (9:28). The disciples' request reveals that Jesus's prayer life was visibly distinctive — something that inspired imitation rather than instruction. John the Baptist also taught his disciples a form of prayer, indicating that distinctive prayer marked the school of each teacher.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, When ye pray, say, Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done, as in heaven, so in earth.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτοῖς, Ὅταν προσεύχησθε, λέγετε, Πάτερ, ἁγιασθήτω τὸ ὄνομά σου· ἐλθέτω ἡ βασιλεία σου·
verses.transliteration: eipen de autois, Hotan proseuchēsthe, legete, Pater, hagiasthētō to onoma sou; elthetō hē basileia sou;
verses.study_note: Luke's version is shorter than Matthew 6:9-13, omitting "which art in heaven," "thy will be done," "deliver us from evil," and the doxology. "Father" (Pater) alone — without qualification — reflects the Aramaic Abba, an intimacy unknown in pre-Christian Jewish public liturgy. "Hallowed be thy name" (hagiasthētō — aorist passive imperative) is not merely a wish but a petition: may you cause your name to be sanctified in the world. "Thy kingdom come" is the great eschatological longing driving all Christian prayer.

verses.kjv_text: Give us day by day our daily bread.
verses.original_text: τὸν ἄρτον ἡμῶν τὸν ἐπιούσιον δίδου ἡμῖν τὸ καθ' ἡμέραν·
verses.transliteration: ton arton hēmōn ton epiousion didou hēmin to kath' hēmeran;
verses.study_note: "Daily bread" translates epiousios — a rare Greek word found almost exclusively in the Lord's Prayer, possibly meaning "for the coming day," "sufficient for existence," or "supersubstantial." In Luke it is present tense (didou — keep giving, continuous action), emphasizing daily dependence in contrast to Matthew's aorist. The rhythm of daily petition forms disciples into a posture of continual trust rather than autonomous self-sufficiency.

verses.kjv_text: And forgive us our sins; for we also forgive every one that is indebted to us. And lead us not into temptation; but deliver us from evil.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἄφες ἡμῖν τὰς ἁμαρτίας ἡμῶν, καὶ γὰρ αὐτοὶ ἀφίομεν παντὶ ὀφείλοντι ἡμῖν· καὶ μὴ εἰσενέγκῃς ἡμᾶς εἰς πειρασμόν.
verses.transliteration: kai aphes hēmin tas hamartias hēmōn, kai gar autoi aphiomen panti opheilonti hēmin; kai mē eisenengkēs hēmas eis peirasmon.
verses.study_note: Luke uses "sins" (hamartias) where Matthew uses "debts" (opheilēmata). The linking of divine forgiveness with our forgiving others is conditional-explanatory, not transactional: our readiness to forgive demonstrates that we understand forgiveness and genuinely need it. "Lead us not into temptation" (peirasmon — trial, testing) petitions that God would not bring us into situations that would overwhelm us; it does not imply God tempts (Jas 1:13) but asks for merciful guidance around the severest tests.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Which of you shall have a friend, and shall go unto him at midnight, and say unto him, Friend, lend me three loaves;
verses.original_text: Καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς, Τίς ἐξ ὑμῶν ἕξει φίλον καὶ πορεύσεται πρὸς αὐτὸν μεσονυκτίου καὶ εἴπῃ αὐτῷ, Φίλε, χρῆσόν μοι τρεῖς ἄρτους,
verses.transliteration: Kai eipen pros autous, Tis ex humōn hexei philon kai poreusetai pros auton mesonuktiou kai eipē autō, Phile, chrēson moi treis artous,
verses.study_note: The parable begins with a rhetorical question expecting the answer "no one would refuse." "At midnight" (mesonuktiou) is the most inconvenient hour, heightening the test. Three loaves was a normal meal for one traveler. The social setting of ancient Palestinian hospitality meant that failure to provide for a guest was a community shame, compelling the host to seek help even at great personal cost.

verses.kjv_text: For a friend of mine in his journey is come to me, and I have nothing to set before him?
verses.original_text: ἐπειδὴ φίλος μου παρεγένετο ἐξ ὁδοῦ πρός με καὶ οὐκ ἔχω ὃ παραθήσω αὐτῷ·
verses.transliteration: epeidē philos mou paregeneto ex hodou pros me kai ouk echō ho parathēsō autō;
verses.study_note: The urgency is compounded: not a personal want but a sacred obligation of hospitality. A traveling friend has arrived unexpectedly — a common reality when reliable lodging was scarce. The host's "I have nothing" (ouk echō — absolute depletion) emphasizes that the request is made from genuine need, not convenience, establishing the right spirit for the petition.

verses.kjv_text: And he from within shall answer and say, Trouble me not: the door is now shut, and my children are with me in bed; I cannot rise and give thee.
verses.original_text: κἀκεῖνος ἔσωθεν ἀποκριθεὶς εἴπῃ, Μή μοι κόπους πάρεχε· ἤδη ἡ θύρα κέκλεισται, καὶ τὰ παιδία μου μετ' ἐμοῦ εἰς τὴν κοίτην εἰσίν· οὐ δύναμαι ἀναστὰς δοῦναί σοι.
verses.transliteration: kakEinos esōthen apokritheis eipē, Mē moi kopous pareche; ēdē hē thura kekleistai, kai ta paidia mou met' emou eis tēn koitēn eisin; ou dunamai anastas dounai soi.
verses.study_note: In a typical Palestinian peasant home — one room — the whole family slept together on a raised mat. Rising would disturb everyone. "The door is now shut" (kekleistai — perfect passive, firmly closed) suggests a heavy wooden bar. The refusal is practical, not malicious. The point of the parable is that even this reluctant neighbor will eventually help, making God's willingness to answer all the more certain.

verses.kjv_text: I say unto you, Though he will not rise and give him, because he is his friend, yet because of his importunity he will rise and give him as many as he needeth.
verses.original_text: λέγω ὑμῖν, εἰ καὶ οὐ δώσει αὐτῷ ἀναστὰς διὰ τὸ εἶναι φίλον αὐτοῦ, διά γε τὴν ἀναίδειαν αὐτοῦ ἐγερθεὶς δώσει αὐτῷ ὅσων χρῄζει.
verses.transliteration: legō humin, ei kai ou dōsei autō anastas dia to einai philon autou, dia ge tēn anaideian autou egertheis dōsei autō hosōn chrēzei.
verses.study_note: The key word is anaideia — translated "importunity" but literally "shamelessness" or "bold persistence that refuses to be shamed away." The argument a fortiori: if a reluctant human neighbor ultimately gives because of shameless persistence, how much more will the Father — who is neither reluctant nor needing to be woken — give to those who ask. Prayer is not overcoming God's reluctance but expressing our dependence.

verses.kjv_text: And I say unto you, Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you.
verses.original_text: κἀγὼ ὑμῖν λέγω, αἰτεῖτε, καὶ δοθήσεται ὑμῖν· ζητεῖτε, καὶ εὑρήσετε· κρούετε, καὶ ἀνοιγήσεται ὑμῖν.
verses.transliteration: kagō humin legō, aiteite, kai dothēsetai humin; zēteite, kai heurēsete; krouete, kai anoigēsetai humin.
verses.study_note: The three imperatives — ask (aiteite), seek (zēteite), knock (krouete) — are all present tense, indicating continuous action: keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking. This is not a one-time formula but a lifestyle of prayer. The verbs escalate in intensity: asking is verbal petition, seeking is active movement, knocking is deliberate assertive appeal at a closed door. All three carry the unconditional promise of divine response.

verses.kjv_text: For every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened.
verses.original_text: πᾶς γὰρ ὁ αἰτῶν λαμβάνει, καὶ ὁ ζητῶν εὑρίσκει, καὶ τῷ κρούοντι ἀνοιγήσεται.
verses.transliteration: pas gar ho aitōn lambanei, kai ho zētōn heuriskei, kai tō krouonti anoigēsetai.
verses.study_note: Verse 10 restates verse 9 as universal principle: "every one" (pas) who asks receives. The shift from command (v.9) to declaration (v.10) confirms the reliability of the promise. Present participles (the one asking, the one seeking, the one knocking) reinforce continuity of action. This is among the most absolute prayer promises in the NT; its qualification comes from context: asking the Father in alignment with his kingdom purposes.

verses.kjv_text: If a son shall ask bread of any of you that is a father, will he give him a stone? or if he ask a fish, will he for a fish give him a serpent?
verses.original_text: τίνα δὲ ἐξ ὑμῶν τὸν πατέρα αἰτήσει ὁ υἱὸς ἄρτον, μὴ λίθον ἐπιδώσει αὐτῷ; ἢ καὶ ἰχθύν, μὴ ἀντὶ ἰχθύος ὄφιν αὐτῷ ἐπιδώσει;
verses.transliteration: tina de ex humōn ton patera aitēsei ho huios arton, mē lithon epidōsei autō; ē kai ichthun, mē anti ichthyos ophin autō epidōsei;
verses.study_note: Jesus moves from human neighbor to human father — a closer, warmer relationship — to establish the certainty of God's giving. No father would give a stone when asked for bread (superficially similar shapes) or a serpent when asked for a fish. The rhetorical questions (mē — expecting "surely not!") argue that even fallen human parents instinctively give good gifts to their children.

verses.kjv_text: Or if he shall ask an egg, will he offer him a scorpion?
verses.original_text: ἢ καὶ αἰτήσει ᾠόν, μὴ ἐπιδώσει αὐτῷ σκορπίον;
verses.transliteration: ē kai aitēsei ōon, mē epidōsei autō skorpion;
verses.study_note: An egg and a curled-up scorpion have some visual resemblance. This third pair completes the trio of parental absurdities and is unique to Luke's version (Matthew 7:9-10 has only bread-stone and fish-serpent). Scorpions were a real danger in Palestine; the comparison was vivid and relatable. The escalating list of impossibles drives home that God cannot give harmful things to those who ask him.

verses.kjv_text: If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children: how much more shall your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to them that ask him?
verses.original_text: εἰ οὖν ὑμεῖς πονηροὶ ὑπάρχοντες οἴδατε δόματα ἀγαθὰ διδόναι τοῖς τέκνοις ὑμῶν, πόσῳ μᾶλλον ὁ πατὴρ ὁ ἐξ οὐρανοῦ δώσει πνεῦμα ἅγιον τοῖς αἰτοῦσιν αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: ei oun humeis ponēroi huparchontes oidate domata agatha didonai tois teknois humōn, posō mallon ho patēr ho ex ouranou dōsei pneuma hagion tois aitousin auton.
verses.study_note: The climax: if you — described honestly as "evil" (ponēroi — fallen, morally compromised) — know how to give good gifts, how much more will the Father give the Holy Spirit. Significantly, where Matthew 7:11 reads "good things," Luke reads "the Holy Spirit" — the supreme gift and the essence of all good gifts. This shifts the entire prayer promise from material to spiritual: the ultimate answer to all prayer is God himself given to us.

verses.kjv_text: And he was casting out a devil, and it was dumb. And it came to pass, when the devil was gone out, the dumb spake; and the people wondered.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἦν ἐκβάλλων δαιμόνιον, καὶ αὐτὸ ἦν κωφόν· ἐγένετο δὲ τοῦ δαιμονίου ἐξελθόντος ἐλάλησεν ὁ κωφός, καὶ ἐθαύμασαν οἱ ὄχλοι.
verses.transliteration: Kai ēn ekballōn daimonion, kai auto ēn kōphon; egeneto de tou daimoniou exelthontos elalēsen ho kōphos, kai ethaumasan hoi ochloi.
verses.study_note: The imperfect "was casting out" (ēn ekballōn) suggests an ongoing scene. "Dumb" (kōphon) means mute — the demonic affliction silenced the victim's voice. The instant restoration of speech upon exorcism visibly demonstrates Jesus's superior authority. The crowd's wonder (ethaumasan) is the expected response, but wonder alone does not equal faith, as the following controversy immediately reveals.

verses.kjv_text: But some of them said, He casteth out devils through Beelzebub the chief of the devils.
verses.original_text: τινὲς δὲ ἐξ αὐτῶν εἶπον, Ἐν Βεελζεβοὺλ τῷ ἄρχοντι τῶν δαιμονίων ἐκβάλλει τὰ δαιμόνια·
verses.transliteration: tines de ex autōn eipon, En Beelzeboul tō archonti tōn daimoniōn ekballei ta daimonia;
verses.study_note: Beelzebub (Beelzeboul in Greek) derives from Baal-zebul ("lord of the exalted house") — a Philistine deity mocked in the OT as Baal-zebub ("lord of flies," 2 Kgs 1:2). By the NT period it had become a name for Satan or a chief demon. The accusation is the most blasphemous possible — attributing the work of the Spirit to the devil — representing willful blindness to the most obvious truth.

verses.kjv_text: And others, tempting him, sought of him a sign from heaven.
verses.original_text: ἕτεροι δὲ πειράζοντες σημεῖον ἐξ οὐρανοῦ ἐζήτουν παρ' αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: heteroi de peirazOntes sēmeion ex ouranou ezētoun par' autou.
verses.study_note: A second group refuses to engage with the exorcism at all and demands a celestial sign — something astronomical and unmistakably divine. "Tempting" (peirazOntes — testing, putting to the proof) echoes the devil's temptation in chapter 4. To demand a sign after witnessing exorcisms is not evidence-seeking but a posture of pre-determined unbelief; no evidence will satisfy those committed to rejection.

verses.kjv_text: But he, knowing their thoughts, said unto them, Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation; and a house divided against a house falleth.
verses.original_text: αὐτὸς δὲ εἰδὼς αὐτῶν τὰ διανοήματα εἶπεν αὐτοῖς, Πᾶσα βασιλεία ἐφ' ἑαυτὴν διαμερισθεῖσα ἐρημοῦται, καὶ οἶκος ἐπὶ οἶκον πίπτει.
verses.transliteration: autos de eidōs autōn ta dianoēmata eipen autois, Pasa basileia eph' heautēn diameristheisa erēmoutai, kai oikos epi oikon piptei.
verses.study_note: "Knowing their thoughts" (eidōs ta dianoēmata) — supernatural perception (cf. 5:22; 6:8; 7:39-40), which is itself evidence of divine knowledge. The political analogy is immediately compelling: no kingdom destroys itself by fighting itself; division produces desolation (erēmoutai — made into a wasteland). The principle applies at every level from households to empires to the spiritual realm.

verses.kjv_text: If Satan also be divided against himself, how shall his kingdom stand? because ye say that I cast out devils through Beelzebub.
verses.original_text: εἰ δὲ καὶ ὁ Σατανᾶς ἐφ' ἑαυτὸν διεμερίσθη, πῶς σταθήσεται ἡ βασιλεία αὐτοῦ; ὅτι λέγετε ἐν Βεελζεβοὺλ ἐκβάλλειν με τὰ δαιμόνια.
verses.transliteration: ei de kai ho Satanas eph' heauton diemeristhē, pōs stathēsetai hē basileia autou; hoti legete en Beelzeboul ekballein me ta daimonia.
verses.study_note: The reductio ad absurdum: if I cast out Satan's demons by Satan's power, then Satan is fighting himself and his kingdom cannot stand. Yet the accusers observe that the kingdom of darkness remains active and coherent. Therefore the premise is false: the exorcisms cannot be Satanic. The argument is logically decisive, exposing the accusation as self-contradictory.

verses.kjv_text: And if I by Beelzebub cast out devils, by whom do your sons cast them out? therefore shall they be your judges.
verses.original_text: εἰ δὲ ἐγὼ ἐν Βεελζεβοὺλ ἐκβάλλω τὰ δαιμόνια, οἱ υἱοὶ ὑμῶν ἐν τίνι ἐκβάλλουσιν; διὰ τοῦτο αὐτοὶ ὑμῶν κριταὶ ἔσονται.
verses.transliteration: ei de egō en Beelzeboul ekballō ta daimonia, hoi huioi humōn en tini ekballousin; dia touto autoi humōn kritai esontai.
verses.study_note: Jewish exorcists existed (cf. Acts 19:13-14) and were regarded by the Pharisees as legitimate. If Jesus's exorcisms are demonic, by what principle do the Pharisees' own disciples operate? To accuse Jesus is to implicitly condemn their own students. "Your sons" — their disciples or fellow Jews who practiced exorcism — become unwitting judges against the Pharisees' double standard.

verses.kjv_text: But if I with the finger of God cast out devils, no doubt the kingdom of God is come upon you.
verses.original_text: εἰ δὲ ἐν δακτύλῳ Θεοῦ ἐκβάλλω τὰ δαιμόνια, ἄρα ἔφθασεν ἐφ' ὑμᾶς ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: ei de en daktulō Theou ekballō ta daimonia, ara ephthasen eph' humas hē basileia tou Theou.
verses.study_note: "The finger of God" (daktulō Theou) is the Exodus motif: the Egyptian magicians attributed the plague of gnats to "the finger of God" (Exod 8:19), recognizing power beyond human manipulation. Matthew 12:28 reads "Spirit of God" — "finger of God" is Luke's formulation, intensifying the OT typology. "Is come upon you" (ephthasen — has arrived, already come) declares the kingdom present, not merely near.

verses.kjv_text: When a strong man armed keepeth his palace, his goods are in peace:
verses.original_text: ὅταν ὁ ἰσχυρὸς καθωπλισμένος φυλάσσῃ τὴν ἑαυτοῦ αὐλήν, ἐν εἰρήνῃ ἐστὶν τὰ ὑπάρχοντα αὐτοῦ·
verses.transliteration: hotan ho ischuros kathōplismenos phulassē tēn heautou aulēn, en eirēnē estin ta huparchonta autou;
verses.study_note: The "strong man" (ischuros) represents Satan, who holds souls as his possessions. "Armed" (kathōplismenos — fully equipped, panoplied) indicates watchful, deliberate power. "His palace" (tēn aulēn — courtyard, homestead) is the world under his dominion. "In peace" ironically describes those enslaved to him — undisturbed, because no one stronger has yet appeared. The parable frames exorcism as a declaration of cosmic war.

verses.kjv_text: But when a stronger than he shall come upon him, and overcome him, he taketh from him all his armour wherein he trusted, and divideth his spoils.
verses.original_text: ἐπὰν δὲ ἰσχυρότερος αὐτοῦ ἐπελθὼν νικήσῃ αὐτόν, τὴν πανοπλίαν αὐτοῦ αἴρει ἐφ' ᾗ ἐπεποίθει, καὶ τὰ σκῦλα αὐτοῦ διαδίδωσιν.
verses.transliteration: epan de ischuroteros autou epelthōn nikēsē auton, tēn panoplian autou airei eph' hē epepoithei, kai ta skula autou diadidōsin.
verses.study_note: The "stronger one" (ischuroteros) is Jesus — announced by John (3:16: "one mightier than I"). The full armor (panoplian — the entire military equipment) is taken, stripping Satan of the authority he trusted. "Divideth his spoils" — the liberated captives are distributed as gifts: souls freed from bondage. The cross is the ultimate enactment of this parable (Col 2:15; John 12:31), of which every exorcism is an anticipation.

verses.kjv_text: He that is not with me is against me: and he that gathereth not with me scattereth.
verses.original_text: ὁ μὴ ὢν μετ' ἐμοῦ κατ' ἐμοῦ ἐστιν, καὶ ὁ μὴ συνάγων μετ' ἐμοῦ σκορπίζει.
verses.transliteration: ho mē ōn met' emou kat' emou estin, kai ho mē sunagōn met' emou skorpizei.
verses.study_note: In this eschatological moment — when the kingdom is arriving and the strong man is being bound — neutrality is impossible. "Gathereth" (sunagōn — assembling, harvesting) and "scattereth" (skorpizei — disperses the flock) are shepherd images. The statement directly addresses those who attempted to explain away Jesus's exorcisms without joining his mission. There is no middle ground: one either joins or opposes.

verses.kjv_text: When the unclean spirit is gone out of a man, he walketh through dry places, seeking rest; and finding none, he saith, I will return unto my house whence I came out.
verses.original_text: Ὅταν τὸ ἀκάθαρτον πνεῦμα ἐξέλθῃ ἀπὸ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου, διέρχεται δι' ἀνύδρων τόπων ζητοῦν ἀνάπαυσιν, καὶ μὴ εὑρίσκον λέγει, Ὑποστρέψω εἰς τὸν οἶκόν μου ὅθεν ἐξῆλθον.
verses.transliteration: Hotan to akatharton pneuma exelthē apo tou anthrōpou, dierchetai di' anudrōn topōn zētoun anapausin, kai mē heuriskon legei, Hupostrepsō eis ton oikon mou hothen exēlthon.
verses.study_note: "Dry places" (anhudrōn topōn) — waterless, desert regions associated with demonic habitation (Isa 13:21; 34:14). Demons are portrayed as seeking a resting place (anapausin — rest, cessation) within a human host. The spirit calls the former host "my house" — indicating the possessive claim demons make on those they inhabit. The primary application is to Israel after John's cleansing ministry: swept but not filled with the Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: And when he cometh, he findeth it swept and garnished.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐλθὸν εὑρίσκει σεσαρωμένον καὶ κεκοσμημένον.
verses.transliteration: kai elthon heuriskei sesarōmenon kai kekosmēmenon.
verses.study_note: "Swept" (sesarōmenon — brushed clean, having been swept) and "garnished" (kekosmēmenon — adorned, arranged, in order) describes a house cleaned but left empty. External reformation without spiritual infilling creates a dangerous vacuum. The house is presentable — the outward life may appear reformed — but it is unoccupied by the Holy Spirit, making it vulnerable to reoccupation by worse forces.

verses.kjv_text: Then goeth he, and taketh to him seven other spirits more wicked than himself; and they enter in, and dwell there: and the last state of that man is worse than the first.
verses.original_text: τότε πορεύεται καὶ παραλαμβάνει ἕτερα πνεύματα πονηρότερα ἑαυτοῦ ἑπτά, καὶ εἰσελθόντα κατοικεῖ ἐκεῖ· καὶ γίνεται τὰ ἔσχατα τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐκείνου χείρονα τῶν πρώτων.
verses.transliteration: tote poreuetai kai paralambanei hetera pneumata ponērotera heautou hepta, kai eiselthonta katoikei ekei; kai ginetai ta eschata tou anthrōpou ekeinou cheirona tōn prōtōn.
verses.study_note: "Seven" (hepta) is the number of completeness — a complete force of wickedness. "Dwell there" (katoikei — to settle permanently; stronger than paroikei, to sojourn) indicates settled occupation. "The last state worse than the first" (ta eschata cheirona tōn prōtōn) warns that incomplete deliverance not followed by true conversion and Spirit-filling results in worse bondage — applied directly to the generation hearing Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as he spake these things, a certain woman of the company lifted up her voice, and said unto him, Blessed is the womb that bare thee, and the paps which thou hast sucked.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῷ λέγειν αὐτὸν ταῦτα ἐπάρασά τις φωνὴν γυνὴ ἐκ τοῦ ὄχλου εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Μακαρία ἡ κοιλία ἡ βαστάσασά σε καὶ μαστοὶ οὓς ἐθήλασας.
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tō legein auton tauta eparasa tis phōnēn gunē ek tou ochlou eipen autō, Makaria hē koilia hē bastasasa se kai mastoi hous ethelasas.
verses.study_note: A woman spontaneously cries out a macarism (beatitude) — a natural Jewish expression of blessing for the mother of an honored son (cf. 1:42, Elizabeth's similar blessing). "Blessed is the womb" celebrates Mary's unique motherhood. Luke includes this precisely to lead into Jesus's corrective and deeper beatitude, redirecting blessing from biological kinship to spiritual receptivity.

verses.kjv_text: But he said, Yea rather, blessed are they that hear the word of God, and keep it.
verses.original_text: αὐτὸς δὲ εἶπεν, Μενοῦν μακάριοι οἱ ἀκούοντες τὸν λόγον τοῦ Θεοῦ καὶ φυλάσσοντες αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: autos de eipen, Menoun makarioi hoi akouontes ton logon tou Theou kai phulassontes auton.
verses.study_note: "Yea rather" (menoun — on the contrary, indeed) does not negate the blessing on Mary but redirects to a greater beatitude available to all. Physical kinship with Jesus, however privileged, is not the foundation of blessing. The blessing that truly matters is hearing (akouontes — continually hearing) and keeping (phulassontes — guarding, observing) the word of God. This echoes 8:21 and invites every hearer into the same privileged relationship Mary enjoyed.

verses.kjv_text: And when the people were gathered thick together, he began to say, This is an evil generation: they seek a sign; and there shall no sign be given it, but the sign of Jonas the prophet.
verses.original_text: Τῶν δὲ ὄχλων ἐπαθροιζομένων ἤρξατο λέγειν, Ἡ γενεὰ αὕτη γενεὰ πονηρά ἐστιν· σημεῖον ζητεῖ, καὶ σημεῖον οὐ δοθήσεται αὐτῇ εἰ μὴ τὸ σημεῖον Ἰωνᾶ.
verses.transliteration: Tōn de ochlōn epathroizomenōn ērxato legein, Hē genea hautē genea ponēra estin; sēmeion zētei, kai sēmeion ou dothēsetai autē ei mē to sēmeion Iōna.
verses.study_note: As the crowd swells, Jesus addresses the corporate spiritual condition of "this generation" — a term of moral evaluation. Demanding a sign after witnessing exorcisms reveals a heart that will not be satisfied by any evidence and uses sign-seeking as an escape from moral accountability. The only sign granted is "the sign of Jonas" — cryptic, requiring interpretation, pointing to resurrection and judgment.

verses.kjv_text: For as Jonas was a sign unto the Ninevites, so shall also the Son of man be to this generation.
verses.original_text: καθὼς γὰρ ἐγένετο Ἰωνᾶς τοῖς Νινευίταις σημεῖον, οὕτως ἔσται καὶ ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τῇ γενεᾷ ταύτῃ.
verses.transliteration: kathōs gar egeneto Iōnas tois Nineuitas sēmeion, houtōs estai kai ho Huios tou anthrōpou tē genea tautē.
verses.study_note: Jonah was himself the sign — his person and proclamation — not merely the miracle of the great fish. Matthew 12:40 identifies the sign as three days in the fish's belly (pointing to resurrection). Luke emphasizes the prophetic proclamation: as Jonah's preaching called Nineveh to repentance, so the Son of Man — his death and resurrection — will be the decisive sign of eschatological judgment and salvation.

verses.kjv_text: The queen of the south shall rise up in the judgment with the men of this generation, and condemn them: for she came from the utmost parts of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and, behold, a greater than Solomon is here.
verses.original_text: βασίλισσα νότου ἐγερθήσεται ἐν τῇ κρίσει μετὰ τῶν ἀνδρῶν τῆς γενεᾶς ταύτης καὶ κατακρινεῖ αὐτούς· ὅτι ἦλθεν ἐκ τῶν περάτων τῆς γῆς ἀκοῦσαι τὴν σοφίαν Σολομῶντος, καὶ ἰδοὺ πλεῖον Σολομῶντος ὧδε.
verses.transliteration: basilissa notou egerthēsetai en tē krisei meta tōn andrōn tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinei autous; hoti ēlthen ek tōn peratōn tēs gēs akousai tēn sophian Solomōntos, kai idou pleion Solomōntos hōde.
verses.study_note: The queen of Sheba (1 Kgs 10:1-13) traveled vast distances to hear wisdom. At the judgment she will stand as a witness against those who had Wisdom himself in their midst and refused to listen. "A greater than Solomon is here" (pleion Solomōntos — more than Solomon, a neuter comparative suggesting quality beyond category) — not merely a wiser teacher but the Wisdom of God incarnate.

verses.kjv_text: The men of Nineveh shall rise up in the judgment with this generation, and shall condemn it: for they repented at the preaching of Jonas; and, behold, a greater than Jonas is here.
verses.original_text: ἄνδρες Νινευῖται ἀναστήσονται ἐν τῇ κρίσει μετὰ τῆς γενεᾶς ταύτης καὶ κατακρινοῦσιν αὐτήν· ὅτι μετενόησαν εἰς τὸ κήρυγμα Ἰωνᾶ, καὶ ἰδοὺ πλεῖον Ἰωνᾶ ὧδε.
verses.transliteration: andres Nineuītai anastēsontai en tē krisei meta tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinousin autēn; hoti metenoēsan eis to kērugma Iōna, kai idou pleion Iōna hōde.
verses.study_note: The Ninevites repented (metenoēsan — changed mind and direction completely) at the preaching of a reluctant, ungracious prophet who gave them no miracles, only a brief word of judgment. Yet Israel, hearing the Son of God preach with signs and wonders, refuses to repent. The Ninevites' response condemns this generation because they did more with less. "Greater than Jonah" closes the double indictment.

verses.kjv_text: No man, when he hath lighted a candle, putteth it in a secret place, neither under a bushel, but on a candlestick, that they which come in may see the light.
verses.original_text: Οὐδεὶς λύχνον ἅψας εἰς κρύπτην τίθησιν οὐδὲ ὑπὸ τὸν μόδιον, ἀλλ' ἐπὶ τὴν λυχνίαν, ἵνα οἱ εἰσπορευόμενοι τὸ φέγγος βλέπωσιν.
verses.transliteration: Oudeis luchnon hapsas eis kruptēn tithēsin oude hupo ton modion, all' epi tēn luchnian, hina hoi eisporeuomenoi to phengos blepōsin.
verses.study_note: This lamp saying (cf. 8:16) applied in this context refers primarily to Jesus himself — he is the lamp placed on the stand for all who enter. His coming is not hidden but fully public. The "secret place" (kruptēn — crypt, hidden recess) and "bushel" (modion — a measuring basket) represent the obscuring of divine revelation, which Jesus refuses to do, placing his light where all may see.

verses.kjv_text: The light of the body is the eye: therefore when thine eye is single, thy whole body also is full of light; but when thine eye is evil, thy body also is full of darkness.
verses.original_text: ὁ λύχνος τοῦ σώματός ἐστιν ὁ ὀφθαλμός σου· ὅταν ὁ ὀφθαλμός σου ἁπλοῦς ᾖ, καὶ ὅλον τὸ σῶμά σου φωτεινόν ἐστιν· ἐπὰν δὲ πονηρὸς ᾖ, καὶ τὸ σῶμά σου σκοτεινόν ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: ho luchnos tou sōmatos estin ho ophthalmos sou; hotan ho ophthalmos sou haploūs ē, kai holon to sōma sou phōteinon estin; epan de ponēros ē, kai to sōma sou skoteinon estin.
verses.study_note: "Single" (haploūs — clear, sound, simple, generous) means an eye fully open to light, not squinting or clouded. In Jewish idiom an "evil eye" (ponēros) meant stinginess or envy (Prov 28:22), while a "single/good eye" meant generosity. Applied here: receptivity to Jesus's revelation — an open, willing, undivided heart — fills the whole person with divine light, while resistance to his word produces comprehensive moral darkness.

verses.kjv_text: Take heed therefore that the light which is in thee be not darkness.
verses.original_text: σκόπει οὖν μὴ τὸ φῶς τὸ ἐν σοὶ σκότος ἐστίν.
verses.transliteration: skopei oun mē to phōs to en soi skotos estin.
verses.study_note: A solemn warning: it is possible to believe one possesses light while actually being in darkness. The Pharisees were convinced they had the light of Torah and tradition while Jesus declares them darkened. "Take heed" (skopei — watch carefully, examine) calls for honest self-examination. The most dangerous form of darkness is the darkness that calls itself light — a condition Jesus will expose in the following woes.

verses.kjv_text: If thy whole body therefore be full of light, having no part dark, the whole shall be full of light, as when the bright shining of a candle doth give thee light.
verses.original_text: εἰ οὖν τὸ σῶμά σου ὅλον φωτεινόν, μὴ ἔχον τι μέρος σκοτεινόν, ἔσται φωτεινὸν ὅλον ὡς ὅταν ὁ λύχνος τῇ ἀστραπῇ φωτίζῃ σε.
verses.transliteration: ei oun to sōma sou holon phōteinon, mē echon ti meros skoteinon, estai phōteinon holon hōs hotan ho luchnos tē astrapē phōtizē se.
verses.study_note: The positive vision: a body entirely filled with light — no dark compartment, no hidden area of resistance — radiates like a lamp that illuminates with a brilliance (astrapē — lightning flash, radiance). The word astrapē (used for lightning in 17:24) suggests dazzling completeness. This is the condition of the fully receptive disciple — illumined entirely by Christ, with no sector of life held back from his lordship.

verses.kjv_text: And as he spake, a certain Pharisee besought him to dine with him: and he went in, and sat down to meat.
verses.original_text: Ἐν δὲ τῷ λαλῆσαι ἐρωτᾷ αὐτὸν Φαρισαῖός τις ὅπως ἀριστήσῃ παρ' αὐτῷ· εἰσελθὼν δὲ ἀνέπεσεν.
verses.transliteration: En de tō lalēsai erōtā auton Pharisaios tis hopōs aristēsē par' autō; eiselthōn de anepesen.
verses.study_note: For the second time in Luke (cf. 7:36), Jesus dines with a Pharisee. Jesus accepts hospitality across social boundaries — he is not sectarian — but every meal in Luke becomes a place of confrontation and teaching. "Sat down to meat" (anepesen — reclined) indicates the full formal dinner posture. What follows is not a personal attack but an exposure of systemic corruption in the religion of Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And when the Pharisee saw it, he marvelled that he had not first washed before dinner.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Φαρισαῖος ἰδὼν ἐθαύμασεν ὅτι οὐ πρῶτον ἐβαπτίσθη πρὸ τοῦ ἀρίστου.
verses.transliteration: ho de Pharisaios idōn ethaumasen hoti ou prōton ebaptisthē pro tou aristou.
verses.study_note: "Washed" (ebaptisthē — baptized, immersed) refers to the Pharisaic practice of ritual handwashing before meals (Lev 15; Mark 7:1-5). Jesus's omission was deliberate — a statement about the priority of inner purity over ritual washing. The Pharisee's shock (ethaumasen — marveled) reveals how central these external performances were to Pharisaic identity and how threatening their omission appeared.

verses.kjv_text: And the Lord said unto him, Now do ye Pharisees make clean the outside of the cup and the platter; but your inward part is full of ravening and wickedness.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ Κύριος πρὸς αὐτόν, Νῦν ὑμεῖς οἱ Φαρισαῖοι τὸ ἔξωθεν τοῦ ποτηρίου καὶ τοῦ πίνακος καθαρίζετε, τὸ δὲ ἔσωθεν ὑμῶν γέμει ἁρπαγῆς καὶ πονηρίας.
verses.transliteration: eipen de ho Kurios pros auton, Nun humeis hoi Pharisaioi to exōthen tou potēriou kai tou pinakos katharizete, to de esōthen humōn gemei harpagēs kai ponērias.
verses.study_note: Luke uses "the Lord" (Kurios) as a narrative title underscoring divine authority. The cup and plate imagery is vivid: they scrupulously clean the outer surface of vessels while the inside is full of robbery (harpagēs — extortion, violent seizure) and wickedness (ponērias). The metaphor indicts the entire Pharisaic religious system: externally meticulous, internally corrupt.

verses.kjv_text: Ye fools, did not he that made that which is without make that which is within also?
verses.original_text: ἄφρονες, οὐχ ὁ ποιήσας τὸ ἔξωθεν καὶ τὸ ἔσωθεν ἐποίησεν;
verses.transliteration: aphrones, ouch ho poiēsas to exōthen kai to esōthen epoiēsen?
verses.study_note: "Fools" (aphrones — mindless, without understanding) is a philosophical indictment: they lack the coherence to connect outward religion with inward reality. The rhetorical question is devastating: the God who created the outside of the cup also created the inside. He is concerned with both — indeed, with the inside first. To clean only the outside is to act as though God only sees surfaces, which is a profound theological error.

verses.kjv_text: But rather give alms of such things as ye have; and, behold, all things are clean unto you.
verses.original_text: πλὴν τὰ ἐνόντα δότε ἐλεημοσύνην, καὶ ἰδοὺ πάντα καθαρὰ ὑμῖν ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: plēn ta enonta dote eleēmosunēn, kai idou panta kathara humin estin.
verses.study_note: The solution to Pharisaic hypocrisy is not more ritual washing but charitable giving from the heart — almsgiving (eleēmosunēn) flowing from genuine compassion rather than legal obligation. "Give alms of such things as ye have" addresses the interior problem of avarice (v.39). When the inside is clean — covetousness replaced by generosity — the whole person and their acts are truly clean.

verses.kjv_text: But woe unto you, Pharisees! for ye tithe mint and rue and all manner of herbs, and pass over judgment and the love of God: these ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ οὐαὶ ὑμῖν τοῖς Φαρισαίοις, ὅτι ἀποδεκατοῦτε τὸ ἡδύοσμον καὶ τὸ πήγανον καὶ πᾶν λάχανον, καὶ παρέρχεσθε τὴν κρίσιν καὶ τὴν ἀγάπην τοῦ Θεοῦ· ταῦτα δὲ ἔδει ποιῆσαι κἀκεῖνα μὴ παρεῖναι.
verses.transliteration: alla ouai humin tois Pharisaiois, hoti apodekatousthe to hēduosmon kai to pēganon kai pan lachanon, kai parerchesthe tēn krisin kai tēn agapēn tou Theou; tauta de edei poiēsai kakeina mē pareinai.
verses.study_note: First woe against Pharisees. They tithed garden herbs — an extension of the Torah tithe (Deut 14:22-23) beyond grain, wine, and oil to the smallest plants — while "passing over" (parerchesthe — bypassing, neglecting) justice (krisin — righteous judgment, equity) and love of God. Jesus does not abolish tithing — "these ought ye to have done" — but declares that meticulous externals divorced from core moral obligations is perversion, not piety.

verses.kjv_text: Woe unto you, Pharisees! for ye love the uppermost seats in the synagogues, and greetings in the markets.
verses.original_text: οὐαὶ ὑμῖν τοῖς Φαρισαίοις, ὅτι ἀγαπᾶτε τὴν πρωτοκαθεδρίαν ἐν ταῖς συναγωγαῖς καὶ τοὺς ἀσπασμοὺς ἐν ταῖς ἀγοραῖς.
verses.transliteration: ouai humin tois Pharisaiois, hoti agapate tēn prōtokathedrian en tais sunagōgais kai tous aspasmous en tais agorais.
verses.study_note: Second woe against Pharisees. "The uppermost seats" (prōtokathedrian — the prominent seat facing the congregation in the synagogue) and public greetings (aspasmous — elaborate salutations acknowledging status) reveal a religion driven by the love of honor. "Ye love" (agapate) — the noblest word for love, here applied to vanity. True piety is content to be unknown; Pharisaic religion requires an audience.

verses.kjv_text: Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye are as graves which appear not, and the men that walk over them are not aware of them.
verses.original_text: οὐαὶ ὑμῖν, ὅτι ἐστὲ ὡς τὰ μνημεῖα τὰ ἄδηλα, καὶ οἱ ἄνθρωποι οἱ περιπατοῦντες ἐπάνω οὐκ οἴδασιν.
verses.transliteration: ouai humin, hoti este hōs ta mnēmeia ta adēla, kai hoi anthrōpoi hoi peripatountes epanō ouk oidasin.
verses.study_note: Third woe against Pharisees. In Matthew 23:27 the comparison is to whitewashed tombs — visible and beautiful outside. Here in Luke the emphasis is invisible unmarked graves (adēla — not apparent, obscure), which defile those who walk over them unknowingly (Num 19:16). The Pharisees contaminate those who associate with them without either party realizing the spiritual defilement occurring — corruption spreading invisibly.

verses.kjv_text: Then answered one of the lawyers, and said unto him, Master, thus saying thou reproachest us also.
verses.original_text: Ἀποκριθεὶς δέ τις τῶν νομικῶν λέγει αὐτῷ, Διδάσκαλε, ταῦτα λέγων καὶ ἡμᾶς ὑβρίζεις.
verses.transliteration: Apokritheis de tis tōn nomikōn legei autō, Didaskale, tauta legōn kai hēmas hubrizeis.
verses.study_note: A lawyer (nomikos — expert in Torah, distinct from but overlapping with Pharisees) protests that the woes apply to his group as well. His tone is that of a man offended (hubrizeis — you insult, dishonor) rather than convicted. He identifies with the Pharisees in their shame rather than separating himself in repentance — and thereby unwittingly invites Jesus to deliver three additional woes directly against lawyers.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Woe unto you also, ye lawyers! for ye lade men with burdens grievous to be borne, and ye yourselves touch not the burdens with one of your fingers.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Καὶ ὑμῖν τοῖς νομικοῖς οὐαί, ὅτι φορτίζετε τοὺς ἀνθρώπους φορτία δυσβάστακτα, καὶ αὐτοὶ ἑνὶ τῶν δακτύλων ὑμῶν οὐ προσψαύετε τοῖς φορτίοις.
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen, Kai humin tois nomikois ouai, hoti phortizete tous anthrōpous phortia dusbastakta, kai autoi heni tōn daktulōn humōn ou prospsauete tois phortiois.
verses.study_note: First woe against lawyers. They construct elaborate legal obligations — fence laws and interpretations — that crush ordinary people with impossible religious burdens (phortia dusbastakta — unbearable loads; cf. Matt 23:4). Yet they themselves do not touch these burdens with even one finger: they find exemptions and interpretive strategies that release the learned from what they impose on the ignorant — the ultimate betrayal of teaching authority.

verses.kjv_text: Woe unto you! for ye build the sepulchres of the prophets, and your fathers killed them.
verses.original_text: οὐαὶ ὑμῖν, ὅτι οἰκοδομεῖτε τὰ μνημεῖα τῶν προφητῶν, οἱ δὲ πατέρες ὑμῶν ἀπέκτειναν αὐτούς.
verses.transliteration: ouai humin, hoti oikodomEite ta mnēmeia tōn prophētōn, hoi de pateres humōn apekteinan autous.
verses.study_note: Second woe against lawyers. They build tombs for prophets whom their fathers murdered — an act of hypocritical commemoration. They honor the prophets in death while preparing to repeat their fathers' sin against the living Prophet in their midst. Tomb-building, far from demonstrating honor, becomes evidence that they know which side of history their family stands on and have done nothing to depart from it.

verses.kjv_text: Truly ye bear witness that ye allow the deeds of your fathers: for they indeed killed them, and ye build their sepulchres.
verses.original_text: ἄρα μάρτυρές ἐστε καὶ συνευδοκεῖτε τοῖς ἔργοις τῶν πατέρων ὑμῶν, ὅτι αὐτοὶ μὲν ἀπέκτειναν αὐτούς, ὑμεῖς δὲ οἰκοδομεῖτε.
verses.transliteration: ara martyres este kai sunEudokeite tois ergois tōn paterōn humōn, hoti autoi men apekteinan autous, humeis de oikodomEite.
verses.study_note: "Ye bear witness" (martyres este) — an ironic legal formulation: they are witnesses against themselves. "Allow" (sunEudokeite — you approve together with, consent to) is the same word Paul uses of those who applaud sin (Rom 1:32). By not condemning their fathers' actions but commemorating the prophets as martyred heroes while preparing the same treatment for Jesus, they complete the circuit of prophet-killing.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore also said the wisdom of God, I will send them prophets and apostles, and some of them they shall slay and persecute:
verses.original_text: διὰ τοῦτο καὶ ἡ σοφία τοῦ Θεοῦ εἶπεν, Ἀποστελῶ εἰς αὐτοὺς προφήτας καὶ ἀποστόλους, καὶ ἐξ αὐτῶν ἀποκτενοῦσιν καὶ ἐκδιώξουσιν,
verses.transliteration: dia touto kai hē sophia tou Theou eipen, Apostelō eis autous prophētas kai apostolous, kai ex autōn apoktenousin kai ekdiōxousin,
verses.study_note: "The wisdom of God" may be a citation formula for a lost wisdom tradition, or Jesus may be speaking as the personified Wisdom of God (Prov 8; cf. Matt 23:34 where Jesus says "I send"). Luke's formulation is more elevated. The sending of prophets and apostles — including NT missionaries — is the continued pattern of divine outreach that this generation would continue to resist, as Acts would demonstrate.

verses.kjv_text: That the blood of all the prophets, which was shed from the foundation of the world, may be required of this generation;
verses.original_text: ἵνα ἐκζητηθῇ τὸ αἷμα πάντων τῶν προφητῶν τὸ ἐκκεχυμένον ἀπὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου ἀπὸ τῆς γενεᾶς ταύτης,
verses.transliteration: hina ekzētēthē to haima pantōn tōn prophētōn to ekkechumEnon apo katabolēs kosmou apo tēs geneas tautēs,
verses.study_note: "Required" (ekzētēthē — sought out, called to account for). The generation that rejects Jesus — the culmination of the prophetic line — bears the accumulated guilt of all prophetic blood shed throughout redemptive history. This is not arbitrary collective punishment but the logic of corporate solidarity: they stand in the lineage of the murderers and are completing the work begun at the creation's dawn.

verses.kjv_text: From the blood of Abel unto the blood of Zacharias, which perished between the altar and the temple: verily I say unto you, It shall be required of this generation.
verses.original_text: ἀπὸ αἵματος Ἅβελ ἕως αἵματος Ζαχαρίου τοῦ ἀπολομένου μεταξὺ τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου καὶ τοῦ οἴκου· ναί, λέγω ὑμῖν, ἐκζητηθήσεται ἀπὸ τῆς γενεᾶς ταύτης.
verses.transliteration: apo haimatos Habel heōs haimatos Zachariou tou apolomenou metaxu tou thusiastēriou kai tou oikou; nai, legō humin, ekzētēthēsetai apo tēs geneas tautēs.
verses.study_note: Abel (Gen 4:8) is the first murder victim in the OT narrative; Zechariah son of Jehoiada (2 Chr 24:20-22) is the last in Hebrew canonical order, killed in the temple court "between the altar and the temple" — dying in the center of Israel's worship. This spans the entire OT martyrology. Jesus solemnly repeats the sentence, sealing the indictment with "Verily I say unto you."

verses.kjv_text: Woe unto you, lawyers! for ye have taken away the key of knowledge: ye entered not in yourselves, and them that were entering in ye hindered.
verses.original_text: οὐαὶ ὑμῖν τοῖς νομικοῖς, ὅτι ἤρατε τὴν κλεῖδα τῆς γνώσεως· αὐτοὶ οὐκ εἰσήλθατε, καὶ τοὺς εἰσερχομένους ἐκωλύσατε.
verses.transliteration: ouai humin tois nomikois, hoti ērate tēn kleida tēs gnōseōs; autoi ouk eisēlthate, kai tous eiserchomenous ekōlusate.
verses.study_note: Third and climactic woe against lawyers. "The key of knowledge" (tēn kleida tēs gnōseōs) — salvific understanding of the Scriptures that leads to the Messiah. By their interpretive traditions they have removed (ērate — taken away, confiscated) the very key that opens the door of salvation. They refused to enter and actively hindered those trying to enter — the ultimate professional betrayal of teachers who block the door of knowledge.

verses.kjv_text: And as he said these things unto them, the scribes and the Pharisees began to urge him vehemently, and to provoke him to speak of many things:
verses.original_text: Κἀκεῖθεν ἐξελθόντος αὐτοῦ ἤρξαντο οἱ γραμματεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι δεινῶς ἐνέχειν καὶ ἀποστοματίζειν αὐτὸν περὶ πλειόνων,
verses.transliteration: Kakeithen exelthontos autou ērxanto hoi grammateis kai hoi Pharisaioi deinōs enechein kai apostomatizein auton peri pleionōn,
verses.study_note: "Urge vehemently" (deinōs enechein — fiercely to hold against, to bear a bitter grudge) and "provoke to speak" (apostomatizein — to interrogate in order to trap, to elicit oral responses) reveal the transition from shocked silence to organized hostility. The seven woes have produced not repentance but rage, and a campaign of sharp questioning designed to extract something indictable.

verses.kjv_text: Laying wait for him, and seeking to catch something out of his mouth, that they might accuse him.
verses.original_text: ἐνεδρεύοντες αὐτόν, ζητοῦντες θηρεῦσαί τι ἐκ τοῦ στόματος αὐτοῦ, ἵνα κατηγορήσωσιν αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: enedreUontes auton, zētountes thēreusai ti ek tou stomatos autou, hina katēgorēsōsin autou.
verses.study_note: "Laying wait" (enedreUontes — setting an ambush, military terminology for a trap) and "to catch" (thēreusai — to hunt, to trap as a wild animal) portray the religious leaders as predators. They seek any quotable utterance that could be used as a legal accusation (katēgorēsōsin — to bring formal charges). This verse bridges to chapter 12, where Jesus warns his disciples of Pharisaic hypocrisy under the pressure of this mounting danger.

meta.overview: Luke 12 is one of the longest chapters in the Gospel and one of the most theologically rich in the entire NT. It opens with Jesus addressing his disciples in the presence of a surging crowd (vv.1-12): a warning against Pharisaic hypocrisy whose hidden corruption will inevitably be exposed, a call to fear God rather than men, and the assurance that the Holy Spirit will provide words when disciples face persecution. The chapter pivots on a request from the crowd about an inheritance dispute (vv.13-15), which Jesus refuses to adjudicate — not because justice is unimportant but because covetousness, not the legal question, is the real problem.
meta.theme: The parable of the rich fool (vv.16-21) is one of Luke's most arresting narratives: a prosperous farmer who, in a night of grand plans for security and self-indulgence, hears the word "fool" from God and loses everything. He was "rich toward self" but "not rich toward God." This leads directly into the extended teaching on anxiety and trust (vv.22-34), where Jesus draws on the ravens and lilies as living sermons of divine provision, climaxing in the command to "seek the kingdom" and the tender assurance: "Fear not, little flock; it is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom."
meta.keyWordOriginal: The second half of the chapter addresses watchfulness (vv.35-48): the disciples are to be dressed for service, lamps burning, like servants awaiting a master who could return at any hour. The parable of the faithful steward (vv.41-48) introduces the principle of proportional accountability: to whom much is given, much will be required. This applies with particular force to those who know the master's will — teachers, leaders, apostles — who face greater judgment for unfaithful stewardship.
meta.keyWordMeaning: The chapter closes with three sombre sayings: the fire Jesus came to cast on the earth (v.49), his baptism of suffering not yet accomplished (v.50), and the division that his coming will produce in the closest human relationships (vv.51-53). The final section (vv.54-59) turns to the crowd with a rebuke about spiritual discernment: they can read weather signs but cannot read the signs of the eschatological moment. The call to settle with one's adversary before reaching the judge applies both to interpersonal reconciliation and to the urgency of responding to Jesus before the day of judgment arrives.
outline: φυλάσσω (phulassō)

verses.kjv_text: In the mean time, when there were gathered together an innumerable multitude of people, insomuch that they trode one upon another, he began to say unto his disciples first of all, Beware ye of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.
verses.original_text: Ἐν οἷς ἐπισυναχθεισῶν τῶν μυριάδων τοῦ ὄχλου, ὥστε καταπατεῖν ἀλλήλους, ἤρξατο λέγειν πρὸς τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ πρῶτον, Προσέχετε ἑαυτοῖς ἀπὸ τῆς ζύμης τῶν Φαρισαίων, ἥτις ἐστὶν ὑπόκρισις.
verses.transliteration: En hois episunachtheisōn tōn muriadōn tou ochlou, hōste katapatein allēlous, ērxato legein pros tous mathētas autou prōton, Prosechete heautois apo tēs zumēs tōn Pharisaiōn, hētis estin hupokrisis.
verses.study_note: "Innumerable multitude" (muriadōn — ten-thousands, myriads) indicates enormous crowds pressing together. Significantly, Jesus turns first to his disciples — not the crowd — and warns them before all else of leaven (zumēs — a hidden, pervasive fermenting agent). Hypocrisy (hupokrisis — playing a role, acting a part from Greek theater) is the leaven of Pharisaism: a tiny amount invisibly permeates and corrupts the whole batch. The warning is preventive medicine for disciples under public pressure.

verses.kjv_text: For there is nothing covered, that shall not be revealed; neither hid, that shall not be known.
verses.original_text: οὐδὲν δὲ συγκεκαλυμμένον ἐστὶν ὃ οὐκ ἀποκαλυφθήσεται, καὶ κρυπτὸν ὃ οὐ γνωσθήσεται.
verses.transliteration: ouden de sungkekalummenon estin ho ouk apokaluphthēsetai, kai krupton ho ou gnōsthēsetai.
verses.study_note: The guarantee that makes hypocrisy ultimately futile: the divine light will penetrate every concealment. "Revealed" (apokaluphthēsetai — apocalypsed, unveiled) and "known" (gnōsthēsetai) are divine passives — God will be the one who discloses. The eschatological judgment is the ultimate unmasking. This verse applies to the Pharisees' hidden corruption (11:44) and simultaneously assures disciples that their persecutors' tactics will be exposed.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore whatsoever ye have spoken in darkness shall be heard in the light; and that which ye have spoken in the ear in closets shall be proclaimed upon the housetops.
verses.original_text: ἀνθ' ὧν ὅσα ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ εἴπατε ἐν τῷ φωτὶ ἀκουσθήσεται, καὶ ὃ πρὸς τὸ οὖς ἐλαλήσατε ἐν τοῖς ταμείοις κηρυχθήσεται ἐπὶ τῶν δωμάτων.
verses.transliteration: anth' hōn hosa en tē skotia eipate en tō phōti akousthēsetai, kai ho pros to ous elalēsate en tois tameiois kēruchthēsetai epi tōn dōmatōn.
verses.study_note: The antithesis is complete: darkness/light, whispered/proclaimed, inner room/housetop. The flat rooftops of Palestinian homes were used for public announcements. The saying may apply to both negative exposure (the Pharisees' hidden corruption will be exposed) and positive proclamation (what disciples receive privately from Jesus will be preached publicly). Secrecy is temporary; the word of God is ultimately public.

verses.kjv_text: And I say unto you my friends, Be not afraid of them that kill the body, and after that have no more that they can do.
verses.original_text: Λέγω δὲ ὑμῖν τοῖς φίλοις μου, μὴ φοβηθῆτε ἀπὸ τῶν ἀποκτεννόντων τὸ σῶμα, καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα μὴ ἐχόντων περισσότερόν τι ποιῆσαι.
verses.transliteration: Legō de humin tois philois mou, mē phobēthēte apo tōn apoktennontōn to sōma, kai meta tauta mē echontōn perissoteron ti poiēsai.
verses.study_note: "My friends" (tois philois mou) — a warm, intimate address that appears rarely in the Gospels. Jesus calls his disciples friends (John 15:14-15), not merely servants. The limit of human power over disciples is the body — after killing, persecutors "have no more that they can do" (perissoteron — nothing exceeding, no additional power). This is the foundation of Christian fearlessness: the worst humans can do is remove what God can restore at resurrection.

verses.kjv_text: But I will forewarn you whom ye shall fear: Fear him, which after he hath killed hath power to cast into hell; yea, I say unto you, Fear him.
verses.original_text: ὑποδείξω δὲ ὑμῖν τίνα φοβηθῆτε· φοβήθητε τὸν μετὰ τὸ ἀποκτεῖναι ἔχοντα ἐξουσίαν ἐμβαλεῖν εἰς τὴν γέενναν· ναί, λέγω ὑμῖν, τοῦτον φοβήθητε.
verses.transliteration: hupodeixō de humin tina phobēthēte; phobēthēte ton meta to apokteinai echonta exousian embalein eis tēn geennan; nai, legō humin, touton phobēthēte.
verses.study_note: The fear of God is presented not as the abolition of fear but as its right object. God's authority (exousian) extends beyond death — he has power to cast into Gehenna (geennan — the valley of Hinnom south of Jerusalem, used for refuse burning, a symbol of final judgment). The double "Fear him" gives the command rare rhetorical weight. Right fear of God displaces all lesser fears of human agents.

verses.kjv_text: Are not five sparrows sold for two farthings, and not one of them is forgotten before God?
verses.original_text: οὐχὶ πέντε στρουθία πωλοῦνται ἀσσαρίων δύο; καὶ ἓν ἐξ αὐτῶν οὐκ ἔστιν ἐπιλελησμένον ἐνώπιον τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: ouchi pente strouthia pōlountai assariōn duo; kai hen ex autōn ouk estin epilelēsmenon enōpion tou Theou.
verses.study_note: Five sparrows for two farthings (assariōn — the assarion was a small copper coin, worth 1/16 of a denarius). Luke's five sparrows for two farthings is better value than Matthew's two sparrows for one farthing (10:29) — the fifth comes free, yet not even the free sparrow is forgotten (epilelēsmenon — perfect passive: has been forgotten) by God. The argument is from lesser to greater: if God remembers every market sparrow, how much more will he remember his disciples?

verses.kjv_text: But even the very hairs of your head are all numbered. Fear not therefore: ye are of more value than many sparrows.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ καὶ αἱ τρίχες τῆς κεφαλῆς ὑμῶν πᾶσαι ἠρίθμηνται. μὴ φοβεῖσθε· πολλῶν στρουθίων διαφέρετε.
verses.transliteration: alla kai hai triches tēs kephalēs humōn pasai ērithmēntai. mē phobeisthe; pollōn strouthiōn diapherete.
verses.study_note: "Numbered" (ērithmēntai — perfect passive: have been counted and the count stands) — not a general approximation but a specific ongoing enumeration. The hairs of the head were proverbially uncountable (Ps 40:12); yet God's knowledge extends even to this detail. "Ye are of more value" (diapherete — you differ from, surpass) many sparrows. The comparison establishes not arrogance but assurance: disciples are infinitely more precious to God than the creatures he does not forget.

verses.kjv_text: Also I say unto you, Whosoever shall confess me before men, him shall the Son of man also confess before the angels of God:
verses.original_text: Λέγω δὲ ὑμῖν, πᾶς ὃς ἂν ὁμολογήσῃ ἐν ἐμοὶ ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀνθρώπων, καὶ ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ὁμολογήσει ἐν αὐτῷ ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀγγέλων τοῦ Θεοῦ·
verses.transliteration: Legō de humin, pas hos an homologēsē en emoi emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn, kai ho Huios tou anthrōpou homologēsei en autō emprosthen tōn angelōn tou Theou;
verses.study_note: "Confess me" (homologēsē en emoi — to speak the same word as, to agree with, to publicly acknowledge) — a courtroom term. The preposition en ("in me") indicates more than verbal affirmation: identification with Jesus. The cosmic reciprocity is exact: confess before men → confessed before angels; deny before men → denied before angels. The "angels of God" as witnesses at the eschatological tribunal (Dan 7:10; Rev 5:11) gives this solemn heavenly weight.

verses.kjv_text: But he that denieth me before men shall be denied before the angels of God.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἀρνησάμενός με ἐνώπιον τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἀπαρνηθήσεται ἐνώπιον τῶν ἀγγέλων τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: ho de arnēsamenos me enōpion tōn anthrōpōn aparnēthēsetai enōpion tōn angelōn tou Theou.
verses.study_note: "Denieth" (arnēsamenos — aorist: who has denied, made a definitive repudiation) stands against "confesseth" in v.8. The divine passive "shall be denied" (aparnēthēsetai) indicates that the Son of Man will be the agent of denial before the heavenly court. This is not a word about momentary weakness (Peter denied but was restored) but about a settled, unrepented posture of repudiation. The angels as witnesses emphasize the eschatological gravity.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever shall speak a word against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him: but unto him that blasphemeth against the Holy Ghost it shall not be forgiven.
verses.original_text: καὶ πᾶς ὃς ἐρεῖ λόγον εἰς τὸν Υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου, ἀφεθήσεται αὐτῷ· τῷ δὲ εἰς τὸ Ἅγιον Πνεῦμα βλασφημήσαντι οὐκ ἀφεθήσεται.
verses.transliteration: kai pas hos erei logon eis ton Huion tou anthrōpou, aphethēsetai autō; tō de eis to Hagion Pneuma blasphēmēsanti ouk aphethēsetai.
verses.study_note: This is among the most sobering verses in the Gospels. Speaking against the Son of Man — misunderstanding or even opposing Jesus in his earthly, veiled form — is forgivable because it may arise from ignorance (cf. Paul, 1 Tim 1:13). But blaspheming against the Holy Spirit — the deliberate, settled attribution of the Spirit's clear works to Satan (as the Pharisees did in 11:15) — is the sin that cannot be forgiven because it severs the very connection through which forgiveness comes.

verses.kjv_text: And when they bring you unto the synagogues, and unto magistrates, and powers, take ye no thought how or what thing ye shall answer, or what ye shall say:
verses.original_text: ὅταν δὲ εἰσφέρωσιν ὑμᾶς ἐπὶ τὰς συναγωγὰς καὶ τὰς ἀρχὰς καὶ τὰς ἐξουσίας, μὴ μεριμνήσητε πῶς ἢ τί ἀπολογήσησθε ἢ τί εἴπητε·
verses.transliteration: hotan de eispherōsin humas epi tas sunagōgas kai tas archas kai tas exousias, mē merimnēsēte pōs ē ti apologēsēsthe ē ti eipēte;
verses.study_note: "Synagogues" (religious court), "magistrates" (archas — rulers, officials), and "powers" (exousias — authorities, governmental forces) together cover all the arenas of persecution disciples would face. "Take ye no thought" (mē merimnēsēte — do not be anxious, do not worry beforehand) is not a prohibition of preparation but of anxious preoccupation. The prohibition specifically applies to the moment of crisis (v.12: "in the same hour"), not to careful long-term teaching.

verses.kjv_text: For the Holy Ghost shall teach you in the same hour what ye ought to say.
verses.original_text: τὸ γὰρ Ἅγιον Πνεῦμα διδάξει ὑμᾶς ἐν αὐτῇ τῇ ὥρᾳ ἃ δεῖ εἰπεῖν.
verses.transliteration: to gar Hagion Pneuma didaxei humas en autē tē hōra ha dei eipein.
verses.study_note: "In the same hour" (en autē tē hōra) — the moment of trial, not beforehand. The Holy Spirit as teacher-in-crisis (didaxei — future: will teach) provides the specific answer for the specific moment. This promise, fulfilled dramatically in Acts (4:8; 6:10; 7:1-53), assumes dependence on the Spirit rather than autonomous eloquence. "What ye ought to say" (ha dei eipein — what it is necessary to say) implies the Spirit's speech serves the divine redemptive agenda.

verses.kjv_text: And one of the company said unto him, Master, speak to my brother, that he divide the inheritance with me.
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δέ τις αὐτῷ ἐκ τοῦ ὄχλου, Διδάσκαλε, εἰπὲ τῷ ἀδελφῷ μου μερίσασθαι μετ' ἐμοῦ τὴν κληρονομίαν.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de tis autō ek tou ochlou, Didaskale, eipe tō adelphō mou merisasthai met' emou tēn klēronomian.
verses.study_note: The sudden interruption from the crowd is jarring — Jesus has been speaking of martyrdom and the Holy Spirit, and a man calls out about an inheritance dispute. This is a realistic portrait of human self-absorption. The request was culturally reasonable: rabbis frequently adjudicated family legal disputes. But the man's concern for his share while standing before the Lord of glory reveals misaligned priorities.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, Man, who made me a judge or a divider over you?
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Ἄνθρωπε, τίς με κατέστησεν κριτὴν ἢ μεριστὴν ἐφ' ὑμᾶς;
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen autō, Anthrōpe, tis me katestēsen kritēn ē meristēn eph' humas?
verses.study_note: "Man" (anthrōpe) — a gentle but distancing form of address, less warm than "friend" (4). Jesus refuses the judicial role not because inheritance justice is unimportant but because it is not his mission and because the questioner's real problem is not his brother's injustice but his own covetousness. The question echoes Moses in Exod 2:14; Jesus is a different kind of deliverer — one who addresses the heart, not the courtroom.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man's life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς αὐτούς, Ὁρᾶτε καὶ φυλάσσεσθε ἀπὸ πάσης πλεονεξίας· ὅτι οὐκ ἐν τῷ περισσεύειν τινὶ ἡ ζωὴ αὐτοῦ ἐστιν ἐκ τῶν ὑπαρχόντων αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: eipen de pros autous, Horate kai phulassesthe apo pasēs pleonexias; hoti ouk en tō perisseuein tini hē zōē autou estin ek tōn huparchontōn autou.
verses.study_note: Turning the private request into a public teaching moment, Jesus addresses "them" (the crowd). "Covetousness" (pleonexia — greed, the desire to have more, literally "the wanting of more"). "Life" (zōē — the true life, genuine human existence) "consisteth not" (ouk estin — is not) in the abundance of possessions. This is a direct contradiction of the prosperity assumption embedded in the man's request and in much ancient and modern thinking.

verses.kjv_text: And he spake a parable unto them, saying, The ground of a certain rich man brought forth plentifully:
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ παραβολὴν πρὸς αὐτούς, λέγων, Ἀνθρώπου τινὸς πλουσίου εὐφόρησεν ἡ χώρα·
verses.transliteration: Eipen de parabolēn pros autous, legōn, Anthrōpou tinos plousiou euphorēsen hē chōra;
verses.study_note: The parable of the rich fool follows immediately as the illustrative narrative for v.15. "The ground brought forth plentifully" (euphorēsen — bore abundantly) — the extraordinary harvest is a gift from God, not the achievement of the rich man. The man's response to divine abundance will reveal his character. Crucially, there is no villain in this story except covetousness itself: no one has wronged him.

verses.kjv_text: And he thought within himself, saying, What shall I do, because I have no room where to bestow my fruits?
verses.original_text: καὶ διελογίζετο ἐν ἑαυτῷ λέγων, Τί ποιήσω, ὅτι οὐκ ἔχω ποῦ συνάξω τοὺς καρπούς μου;
verses.transliteration: kai dielogizeto en heautō legōn, Ti poiēsō, hoti ouk echō pou sunaxō tous karpous mou?
verses.study_note: "Thought within himself" (dielogizeto en heautō) — internal deliberation, a monologue. The question "What shall I do?" is not asked of God, neighbors, or family — only of himself. The pronouns in the following verses are almost exclusively first person singular: my fruits, my barns, my goods, my soul.
