This relentless self-reference is the portrait of a soul sealed off from God and neighbor by material abundance.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, This will I do: I will pull down my barns, and build greater; and there will I bestow all my fruits and my goods.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν, Τοῦτο ποιήσω· καθελῶ μου τὰς ἀποθήκας καὶ μείζονας οἰκοδομήσω, καὶ συνάξω ἐκεῖ πάντα τὰ γενήματά μου καὶ τὰ ἀγαθά μου.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen, Touto poiēsō; kathelō mou tas apothēkas kai meizsonas oikodomēsō, kai sunaxō ekei panta ta genēmata mou kai ta agatha mou.
verses.study_note: The solution is purely architectural — build bigger barns — rather than redistributive (share with the poor) or devotional (return thanks to God). "Pull down" (kathelō — demolish) and "build greater" reveals that more is always the answer when material security is the goal. The phrase "all my fruits and my goods" — everything accumulated into self-storage — is the opposite of the kingdom generosity Jesus teaches throughout Luke.

verses.kjv_text: And I will say to my soul, Soul, thou hast much goods laid up for many years; take thine ease, eat, drink, and be merry.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐρῶ τῇ ψυχῇ μου, Ψυχή, ἔχεις πολλὰ ἀγαθὰ κείμενα εἰς ἔτη πολλά· ἀναπαύου, φάγε, πίε, εὐφραίνου.
verses.transliteration: kai erō tē psuchē mou, Psuchē, echeis polla agatha keimena eis etē polla; anapauou, phage, pie, euphrianou.
verses.study_note: The man addresses his soul — the real self, the person — and promises it material comfort. The tragic irony is that he confuses the material with the personal: "much goods" cannot satisfy a soul, which was made for God. "Many years" — but the night of this very speech, his soul will be required. "Eat, drink, and be merry" echoes Eccl 8:15; Isa 22:13 — the philosophy of those who have forgotten God and death.

verses.kjv_text: But God said unto him, Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee: then whose shall those things be, which thou hast provided?
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ Θεός, Ἄφρων, ταύτῃ τῇ νυκτὶ τὴν ψυχήν σου ἀπαιτοῦσιν ἀπὸ σοῦ· ἃ δὲ ἡτοίμασας, τίνι ἔσται;
verses.transliteration: eipen de autō ho Theos, Aphrōn, tautē tē nukti tēn psuchēn sou apaitousin apo sou; ha de hētoimasas, tini estai?
verses.study_note: "God said" — the divine voice interrupts the human monologue for the first and only time in the parable. "Thou fool" (Aphrōn — senseless, one who lacks understanding, the opposite of sophia) — not a casual insult but a moral-theological verdict (Ps 14:1). "This night" (tautē tē nukti) — the very night of grand planning is the night of death. "Thy soul shall be required of thee" (apaitousin — they will demand back, recall as a loan) suggests life is borrowed, not owned.

verses.kjv_text: So is he that layeth up treasure for himself, and is not rich toward God.
verses.original_text: οὕτως ὁ θησαυρίζων ἑαυτῷ καὶ μὴ εἰς Θεὸν πλουτῶν.
verses.transliteration: houtōs ho thēsaurizōn heautō kai mē eis Theon ploutōn.
verses.study_note: The parable's interpretation is given directly — rare in the Synoptics. "Layeth up treasure for himself" (thēsaurizōn heautō — hoarding toward oneself) is contrasted with "rich toward God" (eis Theon ploutōn — becoming rich in the direction of God). Richness toward God includes generosity, reliance on him, and investment in his kingdom. The contrast is not wealth versus poverty but self-directed versus God-directed stewardship.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto his disciples, Therefore I say unto you, Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat; neither for the body, what ye shall put on.
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ, Διὰ τοῦτο λέγω ὑμῖν, μὴ μεριμνᾶτε τῇ ψυχῇ τί φάγητε, μηδὲ τῷ σώματι τί ἐνδύσησθε.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de pros tous mathētas autou, Dia touto legō humin, mē merimnate tē psuchē ti phagēte, mēde tō sōmati ti endusēsthe.
verses.study_note: "Therefore" (dia touto) connects the warning against anxiety directly to the parable of the rich fool. "Take no thought" (mē merimnate — stop being anxious, do not be preoccupied with worry) is a present imperative prohibition: cease a current practice. The rich fool was anxious about abundance; disciples may be anxious about want. Both are the same root disease: failure to trust the Father's provision.

verses.kjv_text: The life is more than meat, and the body is more than raiment.
verses.original_text: ἡ γὰρ ψυχὴ πλεῖόν ἐστιν τῆς τροφῆς, καὶ τὸ σῶμα τοῦ ἐνδύματος.
verses.transliteration: hē gar psuchē pleion estin tēs trophēs, kai to sōma tou endumatos.
verses.study_note: A logical argument a maiori ad minus: if God has given the greater (life, body), will he not also give the lesser (food, clothing)? The soul/life (psuchē) is infinitely more valuable than the food that sustains it; the body more valuable than the clothing that covers it. To worry about food and clothing while possessing life and body is to anxiously guard the lesser while the greater is already secured by God.

verses.kjv_text: Consider the ravens: for they neither sow nor reap; which neither have storehouse nor barn; and God feedeth them: how much more are ye better than the fowls?
verses.original_text: κατανοήσατε τοὺς κόρακας ὅτι οὐ σπείρουσιν οὐδὲ θερίζουσιν, οἷς οὐκ ἔστιν ταμεῖον οὐδὲ ἀποθήκη, καὶ ὁ Θεὸς τρέφει αὐτούς· πόσῳ μᾶλλον ὑμεῖς διαφέρετε τῶν πετεινῶν.
verses.transliteration: katanoēsate tous korakas hoti ou speirousin oude therizousin, hois ouk estin tameion oude apothēkē, kai ho Theos trephei autous; posō mallon humeis diapherete tōn peteinōn.
verses.study_note: "Consider" (katanoēsate — contemplate carefully, study attentively) — not a casual glance but meditative observation. Luke uses ravens (korakas), which were unclean birds (Lev 11:15), heightening the point: God feeds even ceremonially unclean creatures. No storehouse, no barn — yet fed. The a fortiori question is decisive: "how much more are ye better" (posō mallon diapherete — how much more you differ from/surpass). If God feeds the ravens, his children are incomparably more precious.

verses.kjv_text: And which of you with taking thought can add to his stature one cubit?
verses.original_text: τίς δὲ ἐξ ὑμῶν μεριμνῶν δύναται ἐπὶ τὴν ἡλικίαν αὐτοῦ προσθεῖναι πῆχυν;
verses.transliteration: tis de ex humōn merimnōn dunatai epi tēn hēlikian autou prostheinai pēchun?
verses.study_note: "Stature" (hēlikian) may mean height or lifespan (both meanings are attested). Either way the argument holds: anxiety cannot add a single cubit to one's physical stature or a single moment to one's lifespan. The absurdity of worrying about what anxiety cannot change is exposed. Worry is not only spiritually wrong but practically useless — it neither produces the thing feared nor prevents it.

verses.kjv_text: If ye then be not able to do that thing which is least, why take ye thought for the rest?
verses.original_text: εἰ οὖν οὐδὲ ἐλάχιστον δύνασθε, τί περὶ τῶν λοιπῶν μεριμνᾶτε;
verses.transliteration: ei oun oude elachiston dunasthe, ti peri tōn loipōn merimnate?
verses.study_note: The rhetorical conclusion from v.25: if you cannot do the least thing (add a cubit by worrying), why be anxious about the greater things? The logic dismantles anxiety's pretension to usefulness. If worry is powerless over the smallest matter of existence, it is equally powerless over larger ones. The cure is not greater effort of mind but greater trust in God.

verses.kjv_text: Consider the lilies how they grow: they toil not, they spin not; and yet I say unto you, that Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.
verses.original_text: κατανοήσατε τὰ κρίνα πῶς αὐξάνει· οὐ κοπιᾷ οὐδὲ νήθει· λέγω δὲ ὑμῖν, οὐδὲ Σολομὼν ἐν πάσῃ τῇ δόξῃ αὐτοῦ περιεβάλετο ὡς ἓν τούτων.
verses.transliteration: katanoēsate ta krina pōs auxanei; ou kopiā oude nēthei; legō de humin, oude Solomōn en pasē tē doxē autou periebaleto hōs hen toutōn.
verses.study_note: The second creation-sermon: "Consider the lilies" — not just observe but study them attentively. The lilies "toil not" (ou kopiā — do not labor) and "spin not" (ou nēthei — do not weave), yet they are clothed in splendor surpassing Solomon. The royal courts of Solomon — Israel's wealthiest king — represent the pinnacle of human-made beauty. Yet a single field flower, dressed by God, exceeds it. The comparison is not merely aesthetic but theological: God's provision is incomparably superior to human achievement.

verses.kjv_text: If then God so clothe the grass, which is to day in the field, and to morrow is cast into the oven; how much more will he clothe you, O ye of little faith?
verses.original_text: εἰ δὲ τὸν χόρτον ἐν τῷ ἀγρῷ σήμερον ὄντα καὶ αὔριον εἰς κλίβανον βαλλόμενον ὁ Θεὸς οὕτως ἀμφιέζει, πόσῳ μᾶλλον ὑμᾶς, ὀλιγόπιστοι.
verses.transliteration: ei de ton chorton en tō agrō sēmeron onta kai aurion eis klibanon ballomenon ho Theos houtōs amphiezei, posō mallon humas, oligopistoi.
verses.study_note: The grass (chorton — the wild flowers, the whole category of field vegetation) is today in the field and tomorrow cast into the bread-oven as fuel — a brief, transient existence. Yet God clothes it magnificently. The a fortiori climax is personal and gentle: "O ye of little faith" (oligopistoi — a tender word, not a condemnation, acknowledging real but insufficient faith). The disciples have faith; they need more trust, not more activity.

verses.kjv_text: And seek not ye what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink, neither be ye of doubtful mind.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὑμεῖς μὴ ζητεῖτε τί φάγητε καὶ τί πίητε, καὶ μὴ μετεωρίζεσθε.
verses.transliteration: kai humeis mē zēteite ti phagēte kai ti piēte, kai mē meteōrizesthe.
verses.study_note: "Seek not" (mē zēteite — stop seeking) in contrast to "seek the kingdom" (zēteite tēn basileian) in v.31. The forbidden seeking is material preoccupation; the commanded seeking is kingdom orientation. "Be ye of doubtful mind" (meteōrizesthe — to be suspended in anxiety, to be tossed up like a ship on waves, literally to be "airborne with worry") is a vivid image of mental agitation that comes from misplaced priorities.

verses.kjv_text: For all these things do the nations of the world seek after: and your Father knoweth that ye have need of these things.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα γὰρ πάντα τὰ ἔθνη τοῦ κόσμου ἐπιζητοῦσιν· ὑμῶν δὲ ὁ πατὴρ οἶδεν ὅτι χρῄζετε τούτων.
verses.transliteration: tauta gar panta ta ethnē tou kosmou epizētousin; humōn de ho patēr oiden hoti chrēzete toutōn.
verses.study_note: "The nations of the world" (ta ethnē tou kosmou — Gentiles, pagans) pursue material security as the organizing principle of life. Disciples are not called to be post-material but to be differently motivated: "your Father knoweth" (oidEn — knows with complete, present knowledge) that you need these things. The Father's omniscience makes anxious seeking redundant — he already knows before you ask (Matt 6:8).

verses.kjv_text: But rather seek ye the kingdom of God; and all these things shall be added unto you.
verses.original_text: πλὴν ζητεῖτε τὴν βασιλείαν αὐτοῦ, καὶ ταῦτα πάντα προστεθήσεται ὑμῖν.
verses.transliteration: plēn zēteite tēn basileian autou, kai tauta panta prostethēsetai humin.
verses.study_note: "But rather" (plēn — however, on the contrary) — the great reorientation. The imperative "seek the kingdom" (zēteite tēn basileian) is the positive command replacing the forbidden seeking of v.29. "All these things shall be added" (prostethēsetai — future passive: will be added, granted additionally) — material provision is not pursued but received as the by-product of kingdom priority. The kingdom is sought; everything else is added.

verses.kjv_text: Fear not, little flock; for it is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom.
verses.original_text: μὴ φοβοῦ, τὸ μικρὸν ποίμνιον· ὅτι εὐδόκησεν ὁ πατὴρ ὑμῶν δοῦναι ὑμῖν τὴν βασιλείαν.
verses.transliteration: mē phobou, to mikron poimnion; hoti eudokēsen ho patēr humōn dounai humin tēn basileian.
verses.study_note: One of the tenderest verses in the Gospels. "Little flock" (mikron poimnion) — the smallness of the community is acknowledged without apology; what matters is whose flock it is. "Good pleasure" (eudokēsen — it pleased, he took delight in) — the kingdom is not given grudgingly or as a reward for seeking but from the Father's joyful, sovereign delight. The kingdom is the gift behind all other gifts; to give the kingdom is to give everything.

verses.kjv_text: Sell that ye have, and give alms; provide yourselves bags which wax not old, a treasure in the heavens that faileth not, where no thief approacheth, neither moth corrupteth.
verses.original_text: πωλήσατε τὰ ὑπάρχοντα ὑμῶν καὶ δότε ἐλεημοσύνην· ποιήσατε ἑαυτοῖς βαλλάντια μὴ παλαιούμενα, θησαυρὸν ἀνέκλειπτον ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς, ὅπου κλέπτης οὐκ ἐγγίζει οὐδὲ σὴς διαφθείρει·
verses.transliteration: pōlēsate ta huparchonta humōn kai dote eleēmosunēn; poiēsate heautois ballantia mē palaioumena, thēsauron anekleippton en tois ouranois, hopou kleptēs ouk engizei oude sēs diaphtheirei;
verses.study_note: The practical outworking of kingdom seeking: divestment from earthly security into heavenly investment through almsgiving. "Bags which wax not old" (ballantia mē palaioumena — purses that do not wear out) contrasts with the decaying barns of the rich fool. "Treasure in the heavens" (thēsauron anekleipston — inexhaustible, never-failing treasure) is protected from every earthly threat: theft (kleptēs) and decay (sēs — moth, corruption). Giving to the poor is depositing in the only truly secure account.

verses.kjv_text: For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.
verses.original_text: ὅπου γάρ ἐστιν ὁ θησαυρὸς ὑμῶν, ἐκεῖ καὶ ἡ καρδία ὑμῶν ἔσται.
verses.transliteration: hopou gar estin ho thēsauros humōn, ekei kai hē kardia humōn estai.
verses.study_note: "Heart" (kardia) in biblical thought is the center of will, intellect, and affection — the person's true self. The investment of treasure creates the orientation of the whole person. This is not merely an observation about human psychology but a practical guide: if you want your heart to be with God, invest your treasure in God's kingdom. The reverse is equally true: where treasure goes, the heart inevitably follows.

verses.kjv_text: Let your loins be girded about, and your lights burning;
verses.original_text: Ἔστωσαν ὑμῶν αἱ ὀσφύες περιεζωσμέναι καὶ οἱ λύχνοι καόμενοι·
verses.transliteration: Estōsan humōn hai osphues periezōsmenai kai hoi luchnoi kaomenoi;
verses.study_note: Two images of readiness: "loins girded" (osphues periezōsmenai — long robes tucked into the belt for unhindered movement, the Passover posture of Exod 12:11) and "lights burning" (luchnoi kaomenoi — lamps kept burning through the night). Both speak of sustained preparedness — not the sporadic excitement of occasional devotion but the continuous, costly alertness of servants who do not know the hour of the master's return.

verses.kjv_text: And ye yourselves like unto men that wait for their lord, when he will return from the wedding; that when he cometh and knocketh, they may open unto him immediately.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὑμεῖς ὅμοιοι ἀνθρώποις προσδεχομένοις τὸν κύριον ἑαυτῶν πότε ἀναλύσῃ ἐκ τῶν γάμων, ἵνα ἐλθόντος καὶ κρούσαντος εὐθέως ἀνοίξωσιν αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: kai humeis homoioi anthrōpois prosdechomenois ton kurion heautōn pote analusē ek tōn gamōn, hina elthontos kai krousantos eutheōs anoixōsin autō.
verses.study_note: "Wait for their lord" (prosdechomenois — expectantly receiving, actively welcoming) suggests not passive sitting but attentive orientation toward the expected arrival. A wedding feast could extend through the night — the master's return was uncertain in timing. "Immediately" (eutheōs — at once, without delay) characterizes the readiness: the door should open the moment he knocks, with no scrambling or delay.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are those servants, whom the lord when he cometh shall find watching: verily I say unto you, that he shall gird himself, and make them to sit down to meat, and will come forth and serve them.
verses.original_text: μακάριοι οἱ δοῦλοι ἐκεῖνοι, οὓς ἐλθὼν ὁ κύριος εὑρήσει γρηγοροῦντας· ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι περιζώσεται καὶ ἀνακλινεῖ αὐτοὺς καὶ παρελθὼν διακονήσει αὐτοῖς.
verses.transliteration: makarioi hoi douloi ekeinoi, hous elthōn ho kurios heurēsei grēgorountas; amēn legō humin hoti perizōsetai kai anaklInei autous kai parelthōn diakonēsei autois.
verses.study_note: A stunning reversal: the master girds himself and serves the servants who were watching. This is unique to Luke's version of the parable and anticipates the Last Supper (22:27) where Jesus, as the master, serves. The faithful servant finds not greater labor at the master's arrival but a reversal of roles: they are seated as guests (anaklInei — made to recline) while the Lord serves (diakonēsei — ministers, waiters upon). The eschatological banquet is the reward for faithful watchfulness.

verses.kjv_text: And if he shall come in the second watch, or come in the third watch, and find them so, blessed are those servants.
verses.original_text: κἂν ἐν τῇ δευτέρᾳ κἂν ἐν τῇ τρίτῃ φυλακῇ ἔλθῃ καὶ εὕρῃ οὕτως, μακάριοί εἰσιν οἱ δοῦλοι ἐκεῖνοι.
verses.transliteration: kan en tē deutera kan en tē tritē phulakē elthē kai heurē houtōs, makarioi eisin hoi douloi ekeinoi.
verses.study_note: Roman night watches divided the night into four watches (6pm-9pm, 9pm-12am, 12am-3am, 3am-6am). The second and third watches are the deepest, most difficult hours to remain alert. The beatitude is repeated for those who endure through the entire night of waiting — the perseverance of faith through long delay. The blessing is not contingent on the timing of the master's return but on the posture maintained throughout.

verses.kjv_text: And this know, that if the goodman of the house had known what hour the thief would come, he would have watched, and not have suffered his house to be broken through.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ γινώσκετε ὅτι εἰ ᾔδει ὁ οἰκοδεσπότης ποίᾳ ὥρᾳ ὁ κλέπτης ἔρχεται, ἐγρηγόρησεν ἂν καὶ οὐκ ἂν ἀφῆκεν διορυγῆναι τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: touto de ginōskete hoti ei ēdei ho oikodespotēs poia hōra ho kleptēs erchetai, egrēgorēsen an kai ouk an aphēken diorugēnai ton oikon autou.
verses.study_note: A second parable: the homeowner who does not know when the thief comes. The analogy is not to equate Jesus with a thief but to emphasize the unpredictability of the hour. "Broken through" (diorugēnai — dug through, tunneled through the mud-brick walls of a Palestinian home) is the common form of ancient burglary. The point: ignorance of the hour requires constant watchfulness, since you cannot prepare only in the final moment.

verses.kjv_text: Be ye therefore ready also: for the Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὑμεῖς γίνεσθε ἕτοιμοι, ὅτι ᾗ ὥρᾳ οὐ δοκεῖτε ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἔρχεται.
verses.transliteration: kai humeis ginesthe etoimoi, hoti hē hōra ou dokeite ho Huios tou anthrōpou erchetai.
verses.study_note: "Be ye ready" (ginesthe etoimoi — present imperative: keep becoming ready, maintain readiness) is the ongoing posture required. "The Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not" (hē hōra ou dokeite — the hour you do not suppose, when you least expect it). The unexpectedness is not a threat but a pastoral call to continuous rather than crisis-based readiness. Disciples cannot afford spiritual vacation.

verses.kjv_text: Then Peter said unto him, Lord, speakest thou this parable unto us, or even to all?
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ ὁ Πέτρος, Κύριε, πρὸς ἡμᾶς τὴν παραβολὴν ταύτην λέγεις ἢ καὶ πρὸς πάντας;
verses.transliteration: Eipen de ho Petros, Kurie, pros hēmas tēn parabolēn tautēn legeis ē kai pros pantas?
verses.study_note: Peter's question is pastoral and practical — he wants to know the scope of the parable's application. Is it for the apostolic circle alone or for the entire crowd? Jesus's answer does not directly answer but shifts to the faithful steward parable, implying that the parable applies especially to those in leadership who have specific responsibilities for others — Peter and the apostles most pointedly. The question implicitly separates leaders from the general crowd.

verses.kjv_text: And the Lord said, Who then is that faithful and wise steward, whom his lord shall make ruler over his household, to give them their portion of meat in due season?
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ Κύριος, Τίς ἄρα ἐστὶν ὁ πιστὸς οἰκονόμος ὁ φρόνιμος, ὃν καταστήσει ὁ κύριος ἐπὶ τῆς θεραπείας αὐτοῦ τοῦ διδόναι ἐν καιρῷ τὸ σιτομέτριον;
verses.transliteration: kai eipen ho Kurios, Tis ara estin ho pistos oikonomos ho phronimos, hon katastēsei ho kurios epi tēs therapeias autou tou didonai en kairō to sitometrion?
verses.study_note: The "faithful and wise steward" (pistos oikonomos ho phronimos — the trustworthy and prudent estate manager) combines two qualities: faithfulness (pistos — reliability, trustworthiness to the master) and wisdom (phronimos — practical wisdom, prudence in administration). His task is to give the household "their portion of meat in due season" (en kairō to sitometrion — the measured grain-ration at the right time). This describes the teaching ministry: distributing the word of God appropriately and faithfully to the household of faith.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed is that servant, whom his lord when he cometh shall find so doing.
verses.original_text: μακάριος ὁ δοῦλος ἐκεῖνος, ὃν ἐλθὼν ὁ κύριος αὐτοῦ εὑρήσει ποιοῦντα οὕτως.
verses.transliteration: makarios ho doulos ekeinos, hon elthōn ho kurios autou heurēsei poiounta houtōs.
verses.study_note: "Blessed is that servant" — the third beatitude in this chapter (vv.37,38,43). The blessing falls on the servant found actively doing his duty (poiōunta houtōs — doing thus, engaged in this task) at the moment of the master's return. Faithfulness is not a dramatic achievement at the end but the daily practice of ordinary responsibility. The master finds him doing — present tense, continuous action — not preparing at the last moment.

verses.kjv_text: Of a truth I say unto you, that he will make him ruler over all that he hath.
verses.original_text: ἀληθῶς λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐπὶ πᾶσιν τοῖς ὑπάρχουσιν αὐτοῦ καταστήσει αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: alēthōs legō humin hoti epi pasin tois huparchousin autou katastēsei auton.
verses.study_note: "Of a truth" (alēthōs — truly, certainly) introduces a solemn promise. The reward for faithful stewardship over the household's food is stewardship over "all that he hath" (pasin tois huparchousin autou — all his possessions). This echoes the parable of the minas (19:17: "have thou authority over ten cities") and establishes the pattern of kingdom reward: faithful small stewardship leads to greater responsibility, not retirement.

verses.kjv_text: But and if that servant say in his heart, My lord delayeth his coming; and shall begin to beat the menservants and maidens, and to eat and drink, and to be drunken;
verses.original_text: ἐὰν δὲ εἴπῃ ὁ δοῦλος ἐκεῖνος ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ αὐτοῦ, Χρονίζει ὁ κύριός μου ἔρχεσθαι, καὶ ἄρξηται τύπτειν τοὺς παῖδας καὶ τὰς παιδίσκας, ἐσθίειν τε καὶ πίνειν καὶ μεθύσκεσθαι,
verses.transliteration: ean de eipē ho doulos ekeinos en tē kardia autou, Chronizei ho kurios mou erchesthai, kai arxētai tuptein tous paidas kai tas paidiskas, esthiein te kai pinein kai methuskesthai,
verses.study_note: The unfaithful servant's sin begins not with violence but with a thought: "My lord delayeth" (Chronizei — is taking a long time, is delayed). Delayed return breeds entitlement, then abuse of authority. The servant begins to beat his fellow servants and indulge himself — the very opposite of the faithful steward's distribution of provision. The abuse of power over those in one's care (paidas kai paidiskas — men and women servants) is the specific manifestation of unfaithfulness.

verses.kjv_text: The lord of that servant will come in a day when he looketh not for him, and at an hour when he is not aware, and will cut him in sunder, and will appoint him his portion with the unbelievers.
verses.original_text: ἥξει ὁ κύριος τοῦ δούλου ἐκείνου ἐν ἡμέρᾳ ᾗ οὐ προσδοκᾷ καὶ ἐν ὥρᾳ ᾗ οὐ γινώσκει, καὶ διχοτομήσει αὐτὸν καὶ τὸ μέρος αὐτοῦ μετὰ τῶν ἀπίστων θήσει.
verses.transliteration: hēxei ho kurios tou doulou ekeinou en hēmera hē ou prosdoka kai en hōra hē ou ginōskei, kai dichotomēsei auton kai to meros autou meta tōn apistōn thēsei.
verses.study_note: "Will cut him in sunder" (dichotomēsei — to cut in two, bisect) is a severe image, possibly referring to the ancient punishment of bisection or metaphorically to severe judgment. "With the unbelievers" (meta tōn apistōn — with the faithless ones) — the unfaithful steward is assigned to the category of those who had no faith at all. This is the solemn judgment on those who occupy positions of spiritual leadership while secretly dismissing the Lord's return.

verses.kjv_text: And that servant, which knew his lord's will, and prepared not himself, neither did according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes.
verses.original_text: ἐκεῖνος δὲ ὁ δοῦλος ὁ γνοὺς τὸ θέλημα τοῦ κυρίου αὐτοῦ καὶ μὴ ἑτοιμάσας ἢ ποιήσας πρὸς τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ δαρήσεται πολλάς·
verses.transliteration: ekeinos de ho doulos ho gnous to thelēma tou kuriou autou kai mē hetoimasas ē poiēsas pros to thelēma autou darēsetai pollas;
verses.study_note: "Knew his lord's will" (gnous to thelēma — having known, with full knowledge) — knowledge increases accountability. The servant who knew and did not do will be beaten with many stripes. Knowledge of God's will without corresponding obedience is not spiritual advantage but increased culpability. This principle applies most severely to those who have received clear scriptural instruction: teachers, preachers, and those long trained in the faith.

verses.kjv_text: But he that knew not, and did commit things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few stripes. For unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required: and to whom men have committed much, of him they will ask the more.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ μὴ γνούς, ποιήσας δὲ ἄξια πληγῶν, δαρήσεται ὀλίγας. παντὶ δὲ ᾧ ἐδόθη πολύ, πολὺ ζητηθήσεται παρ' αὐτοῦ· καὶ ᾧ παρέθεντο πολύ, περισσότερον αἰτήσουσιν αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: ho de mē gnous, poiēsas de axia plēgōn, darēsetai oligas. panti de hō edothē polu, polu zētēthēsetai par' autou; kai hō parethento polu, perissoteron aitēsousin auton.
verses.study_note: "Unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required" — one of the great organizing principles of the NT. Privilege entails accountability. This maxim applies across all domains: spiritual knowledge, material wealth, relational trust, vocational gifts. The one who received less revelation and acted wrongly in ignorance is judged proportionally less. This does not mean the ignorant escape judgment but that judgment calibrates to light received.

verses.kjv_text: I am come to send fire on the earth; and what will I, if it already be kindled?
verses.original_text: Πῦρ ἦλθον βαλεῖν ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν, καὶ τί θέλω εἰ ἤδη ἀνήφθη.
verses.transliteration: Pur ēlthon balein epi tēn gēn, kai ti thelō ei ēdē anēphthē.
verses.study_note: "Fire" (pur) on the earth could refer to the fire of the Spirit (Acts 2:3: tongues of fire at Pentecost), the fire of eschatological judgment, or the fire of division and purification that Jesus's coming would ignite. "What will I, if it already be kindled?" (ti thelō ei ēdē anēphthē) — "How I wish it were already kindled!" — expressing Jesus's passionate longing for the completion of his mission, which the following verse (his baptism) will accomplish through suffering.

verses.kjv_text: But I have a baptism to be baptized with; and how am I straitened till it be accomplished!
verses.original_text: βάπτισμα δὲ ἔχω βαπτισθῆναι, καὶ πῶς συνέχομαι ἕως οὗ τελεσθῇ.
verses.transliteration: baptisma de echō baptisthēnai, kai pōs sunechomai heōs hou telesthē.
verses.study_note: "A baptism to be baptized with" — the cross, described in baptismal imagery (cf. Mark 10:38-39). Baptism signifies death and new life; Jesus's "baptism" is the overwhelming immersion in suffering, death, and the judgment of God (Ps 69:2,15; Isa 43:2). "How am I straitened" (sunechomai — I am constrained, pressed, held tight on all sides) reveals genuine internal anguish and longing for completion. This is one of the most revealing self-disclosures in Luke's Gospel.

verses.kjv_text: Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division:
verses.original_text: δοκεῖτε ὅτι εἰρήνην παρεγενόμην δοῦναι ἐν τῇ γῇ; οὐχί, λέγω ὑμῖν, ἀλλ' ἢ διαμερισμόν.
verses.transliteration: dokeite hoti eirēnēn paregenomēn dounai en tē gē; ouchi, legō humin, all' ē diamerismon.
verses.study_note: "Division" (diamerismon — division, splitting, distribution) — not Jesus's personal goal but the inevitable result of his coming. He came to give peace with God (2:14; John 14:27; Rom 5:1), but his coming forces a decision that divides those who accept from those who reject, even within families. The shalom of messianic peace cannot coexist with comfort in sin; its arrival disrupts false peacetimes.

verses.kjv_text: For from henceforth there shall be five in one house divided, three against two, and two against three.
verses.original_text: ἔσονται γὰρ ἀπὸ τοῦ νῦν πέντε ἐν ἑνὶ οἴκῳ διαμεμερισμένοι, τρεῖς ἐπὶ δυσὶν καὶ δύο ἐπὶ τρισίν.
verses.transliteration: esontai gar apo tou nun pente en heni oikō diamemerIsmenoi, treis epi dusin kai duo epi trisin.
verses.study_note: "From henceforth" (apo tou nun) — beginning with the ministry of Jesus, the eschatological crisis is underway and the division is already occurring. The household of five — a typical extended family — will split three against two. The mathematical precision (not even a simple majority) emphasizes that the division cuts through the most intimate structures of life, not merely the public or political sphere.

verses.kjv_text: The father shall be divided against the son, and the son against the father; the mother against the daughter, and the daughter against the mother; the mother in law against her daughter in law, and the daughter in law against her mother in law.
verses.original_text: διαμερισθήσεται πατὴρ ἐπὶ υἱῷ καὶ υἱὸς ἐπὶ πατρί, μήτηρ ἐπὶ θυγατρί καὶ θυγάτηρ ἐπὶ μητρί, πενθερὰ ἐπὶ τὴν νύμφην αὐτῆς καὶ νύμφη ἐπὶ τὴν πενθεράν.
verses.transliteration: diameristhēsetai patēr epi huiō kai huios epi patri, mētēr epi thugatri kai thugatēr epi mētri, penthera epi tēn numphēn autēs kai numphē epi tēn pentheran.
verses.study_note: This passage echoes Micah 7:6, applied now to the division caused by allegiance to Jesus. Every natural family bond — father/son, mother/daughter, mother-in-law/daughter-in-law — becomes a potential fault line. This is not a desirable outcome but an honest warning: loyalty to Jesus may cost the most precious earthly relationships. The disciple must count this cost (14:26) before committing.

verses.kjv_text: And he said also to the people, When ye see a cloud rise out of the west, straightway ye say, There cometh a shower; and so it is.
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν δὲ καὶ τοῖς ὄχλοις, Ὅταν ἴδητε νεφέλην ἀνατέλλουσαν ἀπὸ δυσμῶν, εὐθέως λέγετε ὅτι Ὄμβρος ἔρχεται, καὶ γίνεται οὕτως·
verses.transliteration: Elegen de kai tois ochlois, Hotan idēte nephelēn anatEllousan apo dusmōn, eutheōs legete hoti Ombros erchetai, kai ginetai houtōs;
verses.study_note: Jesus turns from the disciples to the crowd with a rebuke about discernment. In Palestine, a cloud rising from the west (the Mediterranean) reliably signals rain — the prevailing weather pattern. The crowd can read atmospheric signs with accuracy. The contrast with their inability to read spiritual signs (v.56) is the point of the rebuke. Natural discernment and spiritual discernment use the same faculty; the crowd applies it selectively.

verses.kjv_text: And when ye see the south wind blow, ye say, There will be heat; and it cometh to pass.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅταν νότον πνέοντα, λέγετε ὅτι Καύσων ἔσται, καὶ γίνεται.
verses.transliteration: kai hotan noton pneonta, legete hoti Kausōn estai, kai ginetai.
verses.study_note: A south wind in Palestine brings hot, dry air from the desert (the sirocco). "Heat" (kauson — scorching heat, as in Jas 1:11) was a predictable consequence. Two weather signs, two accurate predictions. The people are not fools in reading nature; they are willfully blind in reading the signs of the messianic age. This makes their spiritual blindness more culpable, not less, since it is selective rather than universal.

verses.kjv_text: Ye hypocrites, ye can discern the face of the sky and of the earth; but how is it that ye do not discern this time?
verses.original_text: ὑποκριταί, τὸ πρόσωπον τῆς γῆς καὶ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ οἴδατε δοκιμάζειν, τὸν δὲ καιρὸν τοῦτον πῶς οὐκ οἴδατε δοκιμάζειν;
verses.transliteration: hupokritai, to prosōpon tēs gēs kai tou ouranou oidate dokimazein, ton de kairon touton pōs ouk oidate dokimazein?
verses.study_note: "Hypocrites" (hupokritai) — applied here not to moral deceit but to selective use of perception. They have the faculty of discernment (dokimazein — to test, examine, evaluate, the word used for testing metals for genuineness) but apply it only to nature, not to "this time" (ton kairon touton — this decisive moment, the kairos of messianic fulfillment). The crisis of the present hour — the kingdom arriving in Jesus — demands the same careful reading they give to weather patterns.

verses.kjv_text: Yea, and why even of yourselves judge ye not what is right?
verses.original_text: τί δὲ καὶ ἀφ' ἑαυτῶν οὐ κρίνετε τὸ δίκαιον;
verses.transliteration: ti de kai aph' heautōn ou krinete to dikaion?
verses.study_note: An appeal to moral intuition: even apart from prophetic revelation or messianic signs, their own moral reasoning should tell them that repentance and reconciliation are right (to dikaion — the just thing, what is right). The question exposes not ignorance but suppression of conscience. Every person has sufficient moral light to recognize the need for righteousness; the failure to act on it is willful, not uninformed.

verses.kjv_text: When thou goest with thine adversary to the magistrate, as thou art in the way, give diligence that thou mayest be delivered from him; lest he hale thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and the officer cast thee into prison.
verses.original_text: ὡς γὰρ ὑπάγεις μετὰ τοῦ ἀντιδίκου σου ἐπ' ἄρχοντα, ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ δὸς ἐργασίαν ἀπηλλάχθαι ἀπ' αὐτοῦ, μήποτε κατασύρῃ σε πρὸς τὸν κριτήν, καὶ ὁ κριτής σε παραδώσῃ τῷ πράκτορι, καὶ ὁ πράκτωρ σε βαλεῖ εἰς φυλακήν.
verses.transliteration: hōs gar hupageis meta tou antidikou sou ep' archonta, en tē hodō dos ergasian apēllachthai ap' autou, mēpote katasurē se pros ton kritēn, kai ho kritēs se paradōsē tō praktori, kai ho praktōr se balei eis phulakēn.
verses.study_note: "Adversary" (antidikou — legal opponent) and the whole legal chain (magistrate → judge → officer → prison) mirrors the urgency of the spiritual moment. "Give diligence" (dos ergasian — make effort, work hard) to settle the case before judgment is rendered. Applied spiritually: the crowd is on the way to judgment; the time before the final verdict is now, and reconciliation with God is urgent while there is opportunity.

verses.kjv_text: I tell thee, thou shalt not depart thence, till thou hast paid the very last mite.
verses.original_text: λέγω σοι, οὐ μὴ ἐξέλθῃς ἐκεῖθεν ἕως καὶ τὸ ἔσχατον λεπτὸν ἀποδῷς.
verses.transliteration: legō soi, ou mē exelthēs ekeithen heōs kai to eschaton lepton apodōs.
verses.study_note: "The very last mite" (to eschaton lepton — the last lepton, the smallest coin in circulation, worth about 1/8 of a cent) — complete, exhaustive payment is required for release. In the judicial image, debts must be paid in full; in the spiritual application, the wages of sin (Rom 6:23) cannot be partially repaid. The urgency of the entire chapter converges here: reconcile now, respond to the kingdom now, before the judgment seat makes all reckoning irrevocable.

meta.overview: Luke 13 opens with two unsolicited news items about recent tragedies — Galileans killed by Pilate at the altar and eighteen people crushed by the tower of Siloam — that the crowd had apparently linked to divine judgment on the victims. Jesus refuses this theodicy twice with identical language: "Think ye that these were sinners above all?" (vv.2,4). The real implication of both disasters is not that those particular people deserved death, but that all are equally guilty and equally liable to sudden perishing unless they repent. The double demand — "Except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish" — establishes repentance as the chapter's organizing imperative.
meta.theme: The parable of the barren fig tree (vv.6-9) extends this call to repentance with a note of merciful delay: the owner wants to cut it down after three fruitless years, but the vinedresser intercedes for one more year of intensive cultivation. The fig tree is Israel; the three years are Jesus's ministry; the intercession is his grace before judgment. The healing of the bent woman on the Sabbath (vv.10-17) is Luke's last synagogue healing and the fifth Sabbath controversy in the Gospel. The woman's condition — eighteen years bent double — echoes the eighteen killed at Siloam, and her release on the Sabbath is declared the proper Sabbath activity: setting free what Satan has bound.
meta.keyWordOriginal: Two paired parables — the mustard seed and the leaven (vv.18-21) — address the apparent smallness of the kingdom movement: from an invisible seed comes a sheltering tree; from a pinch of leaven comes a leavened lump. Both parables address the disciples' discouragement at the kingdom's modest beginnings by promising organic, hidden growth toward ultimate manifestation. The question of the narrow door (vv.22-30) is the chapter's eschatological climax: Jesus is on his way to Jerusalem (v.22), and a questioner asks if only a few will be saved.
meta.keyWordMeaning: Jesus's answer does not satisfy the questioner's theoretical curiosity but issues a personal urgent call: "Strive to enter in at the strait gate." Many will seek to enter at the last moment and find the door shut. The Gentiles from east and west will recline at the patriarchal feast while those who assumed inclusion by ethnic right are excluded. The chapter closes with the Pharisees' warning about Herod's threat and Jesus's lament over Jerusalem (vv.31-35): the city that kills prophets will see her house left desolate, and Jesus will not be seen again until they say "Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord."
outline: μετανοέω (metanoeō)

verses.kjv_text: There were present at that season some that told him of the Galilaeans, whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.
verses.original_text: Παρῆσαν δέ τινες ἐν αὐτῷ τῷ καιρῷ ἀπαγγέλλοντες αὐτῷ περὶ τῶν Γαλιλαίων, ὧν τὸ αἷμα Πιλᾶτος ἔμιξεν μετὰ τῶν θυσιῶν αὐτῶν.
verses.transliteration: Parēsan de tines en autō tō kairō apangellontes autō peri tōn Galilaiōn, hōn to haima Pilatos emiXen meta tōn thusiōn autōn.
verses.study_note: This atrocity is not recorded elsewhere, but Pilate's brutal suppression of Galilean pilgrims at the temple is historically plausible given his well-documented cruelty (cf. Josephus). "Mingled with their sacrifices" (emiXen meta tōn thusiōn autōn) — killed while performing temple worship, so their blood mixed with the sacrificial blood. The report was clearly meant as a test: how would Jesus interpret this? Was it divine judgment on the victims?

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answering said unto them, Suppose ye that these Galilaeans were sinners above all the Galilaeans, because they suffered such things?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν αὐτοῖς, Δοκεῖτε ὅτι οἱ Γαλιλαῖοι οὗτοι ἁμαρτωλοὶ παρὰ πάντας τοὺς Γαλιλαίους ἐγένοντο, ὅτι τοιαῦτα πεπόνθασιν;
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis eipen autois, Dokeite hoti hoi Galilaioi houtoi hamartōloi para pantas tous Galilaiaus egenonto, hoti toiauta peponThasin?
verses.study_note: "Suppose ye" (Dokeite — do you think, is it your assumption?) directly challenges the folk theology that identifies unusual suffering with unusual sin. "Sinners above all" (hamartōloi para pantas — sinners in comparison with all) — the comparative reveals the questioner's implicit ranking system. Jesus refuses to grade sinners by their sufferings. The assumption that victims of tragedy deserve their fate more than survivors is both theologically false and morally dangerous.

verses.kjv_text: I tell you, Nay: but, except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish.
verses.original_text: οὐχί, λέγω ὑμῖν· ἀλλ' ἐὰν μὴ μετανοῆτε, πάντες ὁμοίως ἀπολεῖσθε.
verses.transliteration: ouchi, legō humin; all' ean mē metanoēte, pantes homoiōs apoleisthe.
verses.study_note: "Nay" (ouchi — emphatically no) — a direct negation of the theodicy. But the refutation is immediately turned into a warning: "except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish." The word "likewise" (homoiōs) is crucial — not by Pilate's sword or a falling tower necessarily, but perish you will, and just as suddenly and unexpectedly. The tragedies are mirrors showing every hearer their own mortality and need for repentance.

verses.kjv_text: Or those eighteen, upon whom the tower in Siloam fell, and slew them, think ye that they were sinners above all men that dwelt in Jerusalem?
verses.original_text: ἢ ἐκεῖνοι οἱ δεκαοκτώ, ἐφ' οὓς ἔπεσεν ὁ πύργος ἐν τῷ Σιλωάμ, καὶ ἀπέκτεινεν αὐτούς, δοκεῖτε ὅτι αὐτοὶ ὀφειλέται ἐγένοντο παρὰ πάντας ἀνθρώπους τοὺς κατοικοῦντας Ἰερουσαλήμ;
verses.transliteration: ē ekeinoi hoi dekaOktō, eph' hous epesen ho purgos en tō Silōam, kai apektEinen autous, dokeite hoti autoi opheiletai egenonto para pantas anthrōpous tous katoikountas Ierousalēm?
verses.study_note: Siloam was a pool near the south wall of Jerusalem (John 9:7). A tower collapse there — possibly during construction of Pilate's aqueduct — killed eighteen. "Debtors" (opheiletai — those who owe, those obligated, sinners in the sense of being in debt to divine justice). Jesus uses the same logical structure as v.2: do you think they owed more than everyone else in Jerusalem? The rhetorical question expects the same negative answer.

verses.kjv_text: I tell you, Nay: but, except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish.
verses.original_text: οὐχί, λέγω ὑμῖν· ἀλλ' ἐὰν μὴ μετανοῆτε, πάντες ὁμοίως ἀπολεῖσθε.
verses.transliteration: ouchi, legō humin; all' ean mē metanoēte, pantes homoiōs apoleisthe.
verses.study_note: The exact repetition of v.3 creates a refrain of urgency. Two different tragedies — one politically violent, one structurally catastrophic — both yield the identical lesson. The variety of the disasters (human cruelty, structural collapse) represents the range of ways death can come without warning. The twofold repetition of the summons to repentance functions liturgically, like a doubled "Selah" — pause, consider, this applies to you.

verses.kjv_text: He spake also this parable; A certain man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard; and he came and sought fruit thereon, and found none.
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν δὲ ταύτην τὴν παραβολήν· Συκῆν εἶχέν τις πεφυτευμένην ἐν τῷ ἀμπελῶνι αὐτοῦ, καὶ ἦλθεν ζητῶν καρπὸν ἐν αὐτῇ, καὶ οὐχ εὗρεν.
verses.transliteration: Elegen de tautēn tēn parabolēn; Sukēn eichen tis pephuteumenēn en tō ampelōni autou, kai ēlthen zētōn karpon en autē, kai ouch heuREn.
verses.study_note: The fig tree is a classic OT symbol for Israel (Hos 9:10; Mic 7:1; Jer 8:13). Planting a fig tree in a vineyard (ampelōni — associated with Israel in Isa 5:1-7) places both symbols together. The owner "came and sought fruit" (ēlthen zētōn karpon) — the Messiah's coming to Israel seeking the fruit of covenant faithfulness, and finding none (ouch heuREn — not finding it). The parable immediately follows the double call to repentance.

verses.kjv_text: Then said he unto the dresser of his vineyard, Behold, these three years I come seeking fruit on this fig tree, and find none: cut it down; why cumbereth it the ground?
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς τὸν ἀμπελουργόν, Ἰδοὺ τρία ἔτη ἀφ' οὗ ἔρχομαι ζητῶν καρπὸν ἐν τῇ συκῇ ταύτῃ, καὶ οὐχ εὑρίσκω· ἔκκοψον αὐτήν· ἵνα τί καὶ τὴν γῆν καταργεῖ;
verses.transliteration: eipen de pros ton ampElourGon, Idou tria etē aph' hou erchomai zētōn karpon en tē sukē tautē, kai ouch heuriskō; ekKopson autēn; hina ti kai tēn gēn katargei?
verses.study_note: "Three years" (tria etē) corresponds to Jesus's approximately three-year ministry before his crucifixion — the period during which Israel was visited by its Messiah with unprecedented signs and proclamation. "Cut it down" (ekKopson autēn — chop it out) — the owner's judgment is reasonable and just. "Why cumbereth it the ground?" (hina ti kai tēn gēn katargei — why does it also render the ground useless?) — a fruitless tree depletes the soil and prevents something productive from growing.

verses.kjv_text: And he answering said unto him, Lord, let it alone this year also, till I shall dig about it, and dung it:
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἀποκριθεὶς λέγει αὐτῷ, Κύριε, ἄφες αὐτὴν καὶ τοῦτο τὸ ἔτος, ἕως ὅτου σκάψω περὶ αὐτὴν καὶ βάλω κόπρια·
verses.transliteration: ho de apokritheis legei autō, Kurie, aphes autēn kai touto to etos, heōs hotou skapsō peri autēn kai balō kopria;
verses.study_note: The vinedresser (ampelourGos — identified with Jesus as the intercessor) pleads for one more year. "Let it alone" (aphes autēn — leave it, forgive/spare it; the word used for forgiveness). "Dig about it" (skapsō — to dig, loosen the soil) and "dung it" (balō kopria — apply manure) represent the intensive, costly ministry of Jesus: teaching, healing, calling to repentance — every possible means of producing fruit before judgment falls.

verses.kjv_text: And if it bear fruit, well: and if not, then after that thou shalt cut it down.
verses.original_text: κἂν μὲν ποιήσῃ καρπόν· εἰ δὲ μήγε, εἰς τὸ μέλλον ἐκκόψεις αὐτήν.
verses.transliteration: kan men poiēsē karpon; ei de mēge, eis to mellon ekkopseis autēn.
verses.study_note: The conditional structure preserves both hope and warning. "If it bear fruit" (poiēsē karpon — should it produce fruit) — the outcome is uncertain and depends on the response to the year of intensive grace. "Well" (implied — a suppressed apodosis, too obvious to state). "If not" (ei de mēge) — the judgment is deferred but not canceled; "thou shalt cut it down" (ekkopseis autēn) remains as the certain eventual outcome of persistent fruitlessness.

verses.kjv_text: And he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the sabbath.
verses.original_text: Ἦν δὲ διδάσκων ἐν μιᾷ τῶν συναγωγῶν ἐν τοῖς σάββασιν.
verses.transliteration: Ēn de didaskōn en mia tōn sunagōgōn en tois sabbasin.
verses.study_note: The setting shifts to a synagogue on the Sabbath — the fifth and final Sabbath controversy in Luke's Gospel (cf. 4:31; 6:1-5; 6:6-11; 14:1-6). The regular teaching role (ēn didaskōn — was teaching, imperfect: ongoing practice) establishes Jesus's authority in the synagogue setting, making the subsequent confrontation all the more pointed. The Sabbath day becomes the occasion for demonstrating what the Sabbath is truly for.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, there was a woman which had a spirit of infirmity eighteen years, and was bowed together, and could in no wise lift up herself.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ γυνὴ ἦν πνεῦμα ἔχουσα ἀσθενείας ἔτη δεκαοκτώ, καὶ ἦν συγκύπτουσα καὶ μὴ δυναμένη ἀνακύψαι εἰς τὸ παντελές.
verses.transliteration: kai idou gunē ēn pneuma echousa astheEnias etē dekaOktō, kai ēn sungkuptousa kai mē dunamenē anakupsai eis to panteles.
verses.study_note: "Behold" (idou — see! a narrative attention-marker) introduces a woman who has suffered eighteen years — the same number as those killed at Siloam (v.4), perhaps intentionally linking her suffering to the chapter's meditation on mortality and grace. "Bowed together" (sungkuptousa — bent completely forward, unable to stand erect) and "could in no wise lift up herself" (eis to panteles — to any extent whatsoever, in any degree) describes total, chronic incapacity. A spirit of infirmity (v.16 identifies it as satanic bondage) has imposed this condition.

verses.kjv_text: And when Jesus saw her, he called her to him, and said unto her, Woman, thou art loosed from thine infirmity.
verses.original_text: ἰδὼν δὲ αὐτὴν ὁ Ἰησοῦς προσεφώνησεν καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῇ, Γύναι, ἀπολέλυσαι τῆς ἀσθενείας σου·
verses.transliteration: idōn de autēn ho Iēsous prosephōnēsen kai eipen autē, Gunai, apolelUsai tēs astheEnias sou;
verses.study_note: "When Jesus saw her" — he took the initiative; no request was made. "Called her" (prosephōnēsen — called to her, summoned her forward) — a public, visible act in the synagogue setting. "Thou art loosed" (apolelUsai — perfect passive: you have been released, the release stands) — the divine pronouncement precedes the physical restoration. The word is the same used for releasing prisoners or debts (aphiēmi family); Jesus declares her release from an eighteen-year captivity.

verses.kjv_text: And he laid his hands on her: and immediately she was made straight, and glorified God.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπέθηκεν αὐτῇ τὰς χεῖρας· καὶ παραχρῆμα ἀνωρθώθη, καὶ ἐδόξαζεν τὸν Θεόν.
verses.transliteration: kai epethēken autē tas cheiras; kai parachrēma anōrthōthē, kai edoxazen ton Theon.
verses.study_note: "Laid his hands on her" — the physical touch of the healing. "Immediately" (parachrēma — instantly, at that very moment) — eighteen years undone in a moment. "Was made straight" (anōrthōthē — aorist passive: was straightened up, erected upright) — the passive indicates divine action. "Glorified God" (edoxazen ton Theon — imperfect: kept giving glory to God) — the immediate, spontaneous, and continuous response of the healed. The Sabbath healing produces praise, not controversy — from the woman's perspective.

verses.kjv_text: And the ruler of the synagogue answered with indignation, because that Jesus had healed on the sabbath day, and said unto the people, There are six days in which men ought to work: in them therefore come and be healed, and not on the sabbath day.
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ ὁ ἀρχισυνάγωγος, ἀγανακτῶν ὅτι τῷ σαββάτῳ ἐθεράπευσεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς, ἔλεγεν τῷ ὄχλῳ ὅτι Ἓξ ἡμέραι εἰσὶν ἐν αἷς δεῖ ἐργάζεσθαι· ἐν αὐταῖς οὖν ἐρχόμενοι θεραπεύεσθε, καὶ μὴ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τοῦ σαββάτου.
verses.transliteration: apokritheis de ho archisunagōgos, aganaktōn hoti tō sabbatō etherapeuse ho Iēsous, elegen tō ochlō hoti Hex hēmerai eisin en hais dei ergazesthai; en autais oun erchomenoi therapeuesthai, kai mē tē hēmera tou sabbatou.
verses.study_note: The ruler (archisunagōgos — the administrator of the synagogue, a lay leader) addresses the crowd rather than Jesus directly — a sign of intimidation. "With indignation" (aganaktōn — burning with anger, greatly displeased). His logic is technically correct by Shammaite interpretation: healing could wait. But he addresses the crowd as if the woman should have waited — not addressing the healing itself but managing its social implications. His religion values a rule above an eighteen-year-old suffering.

verses.kjv_text: The Lord then answered him, and said, Thou hypocrite, doth not each one of you on the sabbath loose his ox or his ass from the stall, and lead him away to watering?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ Κύριος καὶ εἶπεν, Ὑποκριτά, ἕκαστος ὑμῶν τῷ σαββάτῳ οὐ λύει τὸν βοῦν αὐτοῦ ἢ τὸν ὄνον ἀπὸ τῆς φάτνης καὶ ἀπαγαγὼν ποτίζει;
verses.transliteration: apekrithē de autō ho Kurios kai eipen, Hupokrita, hekastos humōn tō sabbatō ou luei ton boun autou ē ton onon apo tēs phatnēs kai apagagōn potizei?
verses.study_note: "Thou hypocrite" (hupokrita — singular, addressed directly to the ruler) — a personal indictment. Jesus exposes the inconsistency: every Jew untied (luei — looses, the same root as "thou art loosed" in v.12) his working animals on the Sabbath to water them. No one challenged this necessary act of animal care. The rhetorical question expects "yes, of course." The man values livestock watering more than a daughter of Abraham's healing.

verses.kjv_text: And ought not this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan hath bound, lo, these eighteen years, be loosed from this bond on the sabbath day?
verses.original_text: ταύτην δὲ θυγατέρα Ἀβραὰμ οὖσαν, ἣν ἔδησεν ὁ Σατανᾶς ἰδοὺ δεκαοκτὼ ἔτη, οὐκ ἔδει λυθῆναι ἀπὸ τοῦ δεσμοῦ τούτου τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τοῦ σαββάτου;
verses.transliteration: tautēn de thugatera Abraam ousan, hēn edēsen ho Satanas idou dekaOktō etē, ouk edei luthēnai apo tou desmou toutou tē hēmera tou sabbatou?
verses.study_note: "Daughter of Abraham" (thugatera Abraam) — she belongs to the covenant people; she has inheritance rights. "Whom Satan hath bound" (hēn edēsen ho Satanas — whom Satan has bound and the binding continues) — the perfect tense reveals the satanic character of her eighteen-year bondage. "Ought not" (ouk edei — was it not necessary, was it not fitting?) — the Sabbath, the day of liberation (Deut 15:15; Lev 25), is the most appropriate day to release a covenant daughter from satanic chains.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had said these things, all his adversaries were ashamed: and all the people rejoiced for all the glorious things that were done by him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ταῦτα λέγοντος αὐτοῦ κατῃσχύνοντο πάντες οἱ ἀντικείμενοι αὐτῷ, καὶ πᾶς ὁ ὄχλος ἔχαιρεν ἐπὶ πᾶσιν τοῖς ἐνδόξοις τοῖς γινομένοις ὑπ' αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai tauta legontos autou katēschuNonto pantes hoi antikeimenoi autō, kai pas ho ochlos echiairen epi pasin tois endoxois tois ginomenois hyp' autou.
verses.study_note: "Were ashamed" (katēschuNonto — put to shame, confounded) — the adversaries have no logical or theological response; they are reduced to silence and shame. The crowd's reaction is the opposite: "rejoiced" (echiairen — was joyful, kept rejoicing) over "all the glorious things" (epi pasin tois endoxois — upon all the glorious, splendid, honorable things). The same event produces shame in the religious establishment and joy in the people — Luke's characteristic contrast between leadership and populace.

verses.kjv_text: Then said he, Unto what is the kingdom of God like? and whereunto shall I resemble it?
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν οὖν, Τίνι ὁμοία ἐστὶν ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ, καὶ τίνι ὁμοιώσω αὐτήν;
verses.transliteration: Elegen oun, Tini homoia estin hē basileia tou Theou, kai tini homoiōsō autēn?
verses.study_note: The twin parables of mustard seed and leaven are connected by "then" (oun — therefore, consequently) to the Sabbath healing: the liberation of the bent woman is itself a manifestation of the kingdom. The rhetorical double question ("unto what is it like? whereunto shall I compare it?") signals a particularly important teaching moment, inviting the audience to join in the imaginative act of comparison.

verses.kjv_text: It is like a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and cast into his garden; and it grew, and waxed a great tree; and the fowls of the air lodged in the branches of it.
verses.original_text: ὁμοία ἐστὶν κόκκῳ σινάπεως, ὃν λαβὼν ἄνθρωπος ἔβαλεν εἰς κῆπον ἑαυτοῦ· καὶ ηὔξησεν καὶ ἐγένετο εἰς δένδρον μέγα, καὶ τὰ πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ κατεσκήνωσεν ἐν τοῖς κλάδοις αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: homoia estin kokkō sinapeōs, hon labōn anthrōpos ebalen eis kēpon heautou; kai ēuxēsen kai egeneto eis dendron mega, kai ta peteina tou ouranou kateskēnōsen en tois kladois autou.
verses.study_note: The mustard seed (kokkō sinapeōs) was proverbially the smallest seed in Jewish usage. Yet it grows into a "great tree" (dendron mega) — hyperbole for the mustard's actual shrub-like growth — in which birds nest (kateskēnōsen — roosted, made their nests), echoing Daniel 4:12 and Ezekiel 17:23 where the sheltering tree represents a great empire welcoming the nations. The birds represent Gentile nations finding shelter in the kingdom that began as a small Galilean movement.

verses.kjv_text: And again he said, Whereunto shall I liken the kingdom of God?
verses.original_text: Καὶ πάλιν εἶπεν, Τίνι ὁμοιώσω τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ Θεοῦ;
verses.transliteration: Kai palin eipen, Tini homoiōsō tēn basileian tou Theou?
verses.study_note: The repeated question introduces the second parable in the pair. The doubling of the comparison (mustard seed + leaven) reflects the Hebrew poetic pattern of saying the same important thing twice with different images. Both parables address the same theme from different angles: external visible growth (mustard seed) and internal invisible permeation (leaven), together describing the kingdom's comprehensive advance.

verses.kjv_text: It is like leaven, which a woman took and hid in three measures of meal, till the whole was leavened.
verses.original_text: ὁμοία ἐστὶν ζύμῃ, ἣν λαβοῦσα γυνὴ ἔκρυψεν εἰς ἀλεύρου σάτα τρία, ἕως οὗ ἐζυμώθη ὅλον.
verses.transliteration: homoia estin zumē, hēn labousa gunē ekrupsen eis aleurou sata tria, heōs hou ezumōthē holon.
verses.study_note: Leaven (zumē) was normally a symbol of corruption (12:1); here its pervasive, transformative power illustrates the kingdom's inward work. "Three measures" (sata tria — about 50 pounds of flour, enough for bread for 100+ people) is an enormous amount — a festive or communal baking. The leaven is "hidden" (ekrupsen — concealed within) and works invisibly until the whole is leavened. The kingdom transforms societies from within, not only by visible proclamation but by hidden, pervasive influence.

verses.kjv_text: And he went through the cities and villages, teaching, and journeying toward Jerusalem.
verses.original_text: Καὶ διεπορεύετο κατὰ πόλεις καὶ κώμας διδάσκων καὶ πορείαν ποιούμενος εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα.
verses.transliteration: Kai dieporeueto kata poleis kai kōmas didaskōn kai poreian poioumenos eis Hierosoluma.
verses.study_note: A travel notice that reminds the reader that the entire section from 9:51 takes place on the journey to Jerusalem. "Journeying toward Jerusalem" (poreian poioumenos eis Hierosoluma — making a journey toward Jerusalem) — the capital and the cross are never far from view. The cities and villages represent the comprehensive scope of Jesus's teaching mission even as he moves toward his appointed end. The question that follows arises from the atmosphere of this journey.

verses.kjv_text: Then said one unto him, Lord, are there few that be saved? And he said unto them,
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δέ τις αὐτῷ, Κύριε, εἰ ὀλίγοι οἱ σῳζόμενοι; ὁ δὲ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς,
verses.transliteration: eipen de tis autō, Kurie, ei oligoi hoi sōzomenoi; ho de eipen pros autous,
verses.study_note: The question "are there few that be saved?" (ei oligoi hoi sōzomenoi — whether few are those being saved?) is a theological discussion question common in rabbinic circles (b. Sanh. 97b-98a debated whether most or few would be saved). The person expects Jesus to arbitrate the debate. Jesus refuses to answer the speculative question and redirects to personal urgency — "strive to enter." He addresses the crowd ("unto them") not the individual, expanding the application.

verses.kjv_text: Strive to enter in at the strait gate: for many, I say unto you, will seek to enter in, and shall not be able.
verses.original_text: Ἀγωνίζεσθε εἰσελθεῖν διὰ τῆς στενῆς θύρας· ὅτι πολλοί, λέγω ὑμῖν, ζητήσουσιν εἰσελθεῖν καὶ οὐκ ἰσχύσουσιν.
verses.transliteration: Agōnizesthe eiselthein dia tēs stenēs thuras; hoti polloi, legō humin, zētēsousin eiselthein kai ouk ischuRousin.
verses.study_note: "Strive" (agōnizesthe — agonize, compete in an athletic contest, give maximum effort) — the entrance to salvation requires determined, active engagement, not casual assumption. "The strait gate" (tēs stenēs thuras — the narrow door) suggests that the entrance is limited in width: it accommodates one at a time, by personal decision. "Many will seek to enter and shall not be able" — not because the door is locked to them but because they seek at the wrong time or by the wrong means.

verses.kjv_text: When once the master of the house is risen up, and hath shut to the door, and ye begin to stand without, and to knock at the door, saying, Lord, Lord, open unto us; and he shall answer and say unto you, I know you not whence ye are:
verses.original_text: ἀφ' οὗ ἂν ἐγερθῇ ὁ οἰκοδεσπότης καὶ ἀποκλείσῃ τὴν θύρον, καὶ ἄρξησθε ἔξω ἑστάναι καὶ κρούειν τὴν θύρον λέγοντες, Κύριε, ἄνοιξον ἡμῖν· καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς ἐρεῖ ὑμῖν, Οὐκ οἶδα ὑμᾶς πόθεν ἐστέ.
verses.transliteration: aph' hou an egerthē ho oikodespotēs kai apokleisē tēn thuron, kai arxēsthe exō hestanai kai krouein tēn thuron legontes, Kurie, anoixon hēmin; kai apokritheis erei humin, Ouk oida humas pothen este.
verses.study_note: The imagery shifts to a closed door and a master who has risen and shut it (apokleisē — locked, shut tight). "I know you not whence ye are" (ouk oida humas pothen este — I do not know you or where you come from) — a formal statement of non-recognition. The door was open during the time of Jesus's ministry; his death and resurrection would close the era of grace. Those who intended to enter "later" discover that later has become too late.

verses.kjv_text: Then shall ye begin to say, We have eaten and drunk in thy presence, and thou hast taught in our streets.
verses.original_text: τότε ἄρξεσθε λέγειν, Ἐφάγομεν ἐνώπιόν σου καὶ ἐπίομεν, καὶ ἐν ταῖς πλατείαις ἡμῶν ἐδίδαξας·
verses.transliteration: tote arxesthe legein, Ephagomen enōpion sou kai epiomen, kai en tais plateiais hēmōn edidaxas;
verses.study_note: The excluded appeal to proximity — "we ate and drank in your presence, you taught in our streets." Physical proximity to Jesus, attending his meals, living in towns where he preached — none of these constitute the personal relationship that the narrow gate requires. The claim is geographic and cultural, not relational and transformative. This is the claim of nominal religion: being nearby without being within, hearing without obeying.

verses.kjv_text: But he shall say, I tell you, I know you not whence ye are; depart from me, all ye workers of iniquity.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐρεῖ λέγων ὑμῖν, Οὐκ οἶδα πόθεν ἐστέ· ἀπόστητε ἀπ' ἐμοῦ, πάντες ἐργάται ἀδικίας.
verses.transliteration: kai erei legōn humin, Ouk oida pothen este; apostēte ap' emou, pantes ergatai adikias.
verses.study_note: "Depart from me" (apostēte ap' emou — stand away from me, withdraw) echoes Psalm 6:8, which Jesus quotes (Matt 7:23 uses the same Psalm). "Workers of iniquity" (ergatai adikias — those who work unrighteousness, practitioners of injustice) — the excluded are not passive bystanders but active practitioners of wrong. Despite proximity to Jesus, their fundamental orientation was toward sin. The repetition of "I know you not whence ye are" is the double judgment, sealed with a command to depart.

verses.kjv_text: There shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth, when ye shall see Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, and all the prophets, in the kingdom of God, and you yourselves thrust out.
verses.original_text: ἐκεῖ ἔσται ὁ κλαυθμὸς καὶ ὁ βρυγμὸς τῶν ὀδόντων, ὅταν ὄψησθε Ἀβραὰμ καὶ Ἰσαὰκ καὶ Ἰακὼβ καὶ πάντας τοὺς προφήτας ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τοῦ Θεοῦ, ὑμᾶς δὲ ἐκβαλλομένους ἔξω.
verses.transliteration: ekei estai ho klauthmos kai ho brugmos tōn odontōn, hotan opsēsthe Abraam kai Isaak kai Iakōb kai pantas tous prophētas en tē basileia tou Theou, humas de ekballomenous exō.
verses.study_note: "Weeping and gnashing of teeth" (klauthmos kai brugmos tōn odontōn) — the combination of grief (weeping) and rage (gnashing) describes the specific torment of exclusion when one sees what might have been. "Ye shall see" (opsēsthe) — they will have clear vision of Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the prophets in the kingdom — their own patriarchal heritage — while being "thrust out" (ekballomenous exō — continuously being cast outside). The sight of what is lost intensifies the anguish.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall come from the east, and from the west, and from the north, and from the south, and shall sit down in the kingdom of God.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἥξουσιν ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν καὶ δυσμῶν καὶ ἀπὸ βορρᾶ καὶ νότου καὶ ἀνακλιθήσονται ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai hēxousin apo anatolōn kai dusmōn kai apo borra kai notou kai anakiithēsontai en tē basileia tou Theou.
verses.study_note: From all four compass points — a comprehensive universalism echoing Isaiah 43:5-6; 49:12; Psalm 107:3. "Shall sit down" (anakIithēsontai — will recline at table) — the great eschatological banquet (Isa 25:6). Gentiles from every direction will recline as honored guests at the messianic feast, while those who assumed ancestral right discover they are outside. The reversal is complete: the geographical outsiders become the insiders; the ethnic insiders become the outcasts.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, there are last which shall be first, and there are first which shall be last.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ εἰσὶν ἔσχατοι οἳ ἔσονται πρῶτοι, καὶ εἰσὶν πρῶτοι οἳ ἔσονται ἔσχατοι.
verses.transliteration: kai idou eisin eschatoi hoi esontai prōtoi, kai eisin prōtoi hoi esontai eschatoi.
verses.study_note: "Behold" (idou) signals a surprising, important statement. The reversal of first and last is one of Jesus's most repeated paradoxes (14:11; 18:14; Matt 19:30; 20:16; Mark 10:31), expressed here in its eschatological form. "Last" (eschatoi) refers to those currently marginalized — Gentiles, sinners — who will be first in the kingdom. "First" (prōtoi) refers to those who consider themselves at the front of the queue — religious insiders — who will be last. Eschatological priority is determined by response to grace, not ethnic or religious position.

verses.kjv_text: The same day there came certain of the Pharisees, saying unto him, Get thee out, and depart hence: for Herod will kill thee.
verses.original_text: Ἐν αὐτῇ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ προσῆλθάν τινες Φαρισαῖοι λέγοντες αὐτῷ, Ἔξελθε καὶ πορεύου ἐντεῦθεν, ὅτι Ἡρῴδης θέλει σε ἀποκτεῖναι.
verses.transliteration: En autē tē hēmera prosēlthon tines Pharisaioi legontes autō, Exelthe kai poreuou enteuthen, hoti Hērōdēs thelei se apokteinai.
verses.study_note: Uniquely, Pharisees warn Jesus about Herod's threat — an unusual scene suggesting either genuine concern or a political maneuver to frighten Jesus away from his mission. Herod Antipas (who killed John the Baptist, 3:19-20) was tetrarch of Galilee and Perea. "Get thee out" (Exelthe kai poreuou — go out and depart from here) — whether friendly warning or veiled threat, the message is the same: leave. Jesus's response refuses to be hurried.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Go ye, and tell that fox, Behold, I cast out devils, and I do cures to day and to morrow, and the third day I shall be perfected.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς, Πορευθέντες εἴπατε τῇ ἀλώπεκι ταύτῃ, Ἰδοὺ ἐκβάλλω δαιμόνια καὶ ἰάσεις ἀποτελῶ σήμερον καὶ αὔριον, καὶ τῇ τρίτῃ τελειοῦμαι.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autois, Poreuthentes eipate tē alōpeki tautē, Idou ekballō daimonia kai iaseis apotelō sēmeron kai aurion, kai tē tritē telEioumai.
verses.study_note: "That fox" (tē alōpeki tautē — that fox) — in Jewish tradition, a fox was a cunning but weak animal compared to a lion. Jesus dismisses Herod as a petty schemer rather than a genuine threat to his mission. "Today and tomorrow" symbolize the continuing ministry; "the third day I shall be perfected" (telEioumai — completed, accomplished, brought to full completion) points to the cross and resurrection as the fulfillment of his redemptive purpose, not a death imposed by Herod.

verses.kjv_text: Nevertheless I must walk to day, and to morrow, and the day following: for it cannot be that a prophet perish out of Jerusalem.
verses.original_text: πλὴν δεῖ με σήμερον καὶ αὔριον καὶ τῇ ἐχομένῃ πορεύεσθαι· ὅτι οὐκ ἐνδέχεται προφήτην ἀπολέσθαι ἔξω Ἱερουσαλήμ.
verses.transliteration: plēn dei me sēmeron kai aurion kai tē echomenē poreuesthai; hoti ouk endechetai prophētēn apolEsthai exō Hierousalēm.
verses.study_note: "I must" (dei me — it is necessary for me) — the divine necessity that governs Jesus's entire journey. Herod cannot terminate his mission because the appointed place of a prophet's death is Jerusalem (see v.34). The bitter irony is sharp: the holy city, the city of God's chosen dwelling, is the city that kills prophets. Jerusalem does not protect its prophets — it destroys them. Jesus will not be deflected from his destiny by a Galilean tetrarch.

verses.kjv_text: O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, which killest the prophets, and stonest them that are sent unto thee; how often would I have gathered thy children together, as a hen doth gather her brood under her wings, and ye would not!
verses.original_text: Ἰερουσαλήμ, Ἰερουσαλήμ, ἡ ἀποκτείνουσα τοὺς προφήτας καὶ λιθοβολοῦσα τοὺς ἀπεσταλμένους πρὸς αὐτήν· ποσάκις ἠθέλησα ἐπισυνάξαι τὰ τέκνα σου ὃν τρόπον ὄρνις τὴν ἑαυτῆς νοσσιὰν ὑπὸ τὰς πτέρυγας, καὶ οὐκ ἠθελήσατε.
verses.transliteration: Hierousalēm, Hierousalēm, hē apokteInousa tous prophētas kai lithobolousa tous apestalmenous pros autēn; posakis ēthelēsa episunaxai ta tekna sou hon tropon ornis tēn heautēs nossian hupo tas pterugas, kai ouk ēthelēsate.
verses.study_note: The double address "Jerusalem, Jerusalem" is a classic lament form (cf. "Absalom, Absalom!" 2 Sam 19:4; "Martha, Martha" 10:41). The hen-and-chicks image (ornis — a female bird, perhaps a hen) is one of the most tender Christological images in the Gospels: the divine longing to shelter, protect, and gather. "How often I would" (posakis ēthelēsa — how many times I desired, with personal longing) — the repeated divine initiative met by "ye would not" (ouk ēthelēsate — you did not will it, you refused). The tragedy of rejected grace.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, your house is left unto you desolate: and verily I say unto you, Ye shall not see me, until the time come when ye shall say, Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord.
verses.original_text: ἰδοὺ ἀφίεται ὑμῖν ὁ οἶκος ὑμῶν. λέγω δὲ ὑμῖν, οὐ μὴ ἴδητέ με ἕως ἥξει ὅτε εἴπητε, Εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι Κυρίου.
verses.transliteration: idou aphietai humin ho oikos humōn. legō de humin, ou mē idēte me heōs hēxei hote eipēte, Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati Kuriou.
verses.study_note: "Your house is left unto you desolate" (aphietai — is abandoned, given up, forsaken) — "your house" not "my Father's house." The divine presence is withdrawing from the temple and the city that has rejected its prophets. This prophecy anticipates the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70 (21:5-24). "Ye shall not see me until" — not permanent absence but conditional future restoration: when they cry "Blessed is he that cometh" (Ps 118:26), quoting the Passover hallel, the reunion will occur — at the triumphal entry (19:38) and ultimately at the parousia.

meta.overview: Luke 14 is set almost entirely at a Sabbath dinner in the house of a leading Pharisee (v.1). This banquet setting is not incidental — it becomes the vehicle for three interlocking teachings: a healing that provokes controversy (vv.1-6), wisdom about seating at feasts (vv.7-11), instructions for guest lists (vv.12-14), and the parable of the great supper (vv.15-24). The entire chapter wrestles with the social, economic, and spiritual dynamics of who is included and excluded, who chooses humility and who pursues honor, and what it truly means to respond to God's invitation.
meta.theme: The healing of the man with dropsy (vv.1-6) is the sixth and final Sabbath healing in Luke and the only instance of this particular condition. The Pharisees' watchful silence (v.1) reveals a trap being set. Jesus heals without asking permission, argues from lesser to greater (rescuing an ox from a pit on the Sabbath), and leaves his opponents speechless. The parable about banquet seats (vv.7-11) observes the scramble for honor at the table and recommends the lowest seat — not as social strategy but as spiritual posture: "For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted."
meta.keyWordOriginal: The instruction about guest lists (vv.12-14) is radical: do not invite those who can repay you but the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind — those who cannot. This is kingdom economy: "thou shalt be recompensed at the resurrection of the just." The parable of the great supper (vv.15-24) — triggered by a dinner guest's pious exclamation about eating bread in the kingdom — portrays three waves of invited guests who all make excuses (land, oxen, marriage) and are replaced by street-gathered outcasts (poor, maimed, halt, blind), and then by those from the highways and hedges, filling the house for the final Gentile mission.
meta.keyWordMeaning: The chapter's second half (vv.25-35) steps outside the dinner and addresses the great crowd following Jesus with blunt discipleship demands: whoever does not hate family, life, and possessions cannot be his disciple; whoever does not bear his cross cannot follow. Two parables — the tower-builder and the warring king — press the necessity of counting the cost before committing. The chapter concludes with the salt sayings (vv.34-35): salt that has lost its savor is fit for nothing. Discipleship without genuine cost-counting and commitment is like tasteless salt — it has lost its essential quality.
outline: ταπεινόω (tapeinōō)

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as he went into the house of one of the chief Pharisees to eat bread on the sabbath day, that they watched him.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ ἐλθεῖν αὐτὸν εἰς οἶκόν τινος τῶν ἀρχόντων τῶν Φαρισαίων σαββάτῳ φαγεῖν ἄρτον, καὶ αὐτοὶ ἦσαν παρατηρούμενοι αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en tō elthein auton eis oikon tinos tōn archontōn tōn Pharisaiōn sabbatō phagein arton, kai autoi ēsan paratēroumenoi auton.
verses.study_note: "Chief Pharisees" (archontōn tōn Pharisaiōn — rulers, leading members of the Pharisaic party) — the host is among the most prominent religious leaders. "They watched him" (paratēroumenoi — closely observing, scrutinizing, the word used for spying on criminals or watching for dangerous symptoms) — the dinner is a surveillance operation. Jesus enters knowing he is being watched, and he acts anyway. His healings are never a reaction to surveillance but a response to human need.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, there was a certain man before him which had the dropsy.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἄνθρωπός τις ἦν ὑδρωπικὸς ἔμπροσθεν αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai idou anthrōpos tis ēn hudrōpikos emprosthen autou.
verses.study_note: "Behold" (idou) signals the narrative surprise: a sick man is present at an elite Pharisaic dinner. "Dropsy" (hudrōpikos — hydropsy, from hudōr, water) is a condition of fluid retention causing severe swelling, often a symptom of underlying organ failure (kidney or liver disease). The man's presence may have been arranged as a test, or he may have gained access as an uninvited guest (as the sinful woman of 7:37). Either way, his need and Jesus's response are the focus.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answering spake unto the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν πρὸς τοὺς νομικοὺς καὶ Φαρισαίους λέγων, Εἰ ἔξεστιν τῷ σαββάτῳ θεραπεῦσαι ἢ οὔ;
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis ho Iēsous eipen pros tous nomikous kai Pharisaious legōn, Ei exestin tō sabbatō therapeusai ē ou?
verses.study_note: "Answering" (apokritheis) — Jesus responds to their watching with a direct question before they can formulate their trap. He places the question openly before them: "Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day?" (Ei exestin — whether it is permissible, legally allowed). This forces them to choose between two bad options: say yes (undermining their critique), or say no (appearing merciless). Their silence (v.4) is the only way to avoid the dilemma.

verses.kjv_text: And they held their peace. And he took him, and healed him, and let him go;
verses.original_text: καὶ οἱ ἡσύχασαν. καὶ ἐπιλαβόμενος ἰάσατο αὐτὸν καὶ ἀπέλυσεν.
verses.transliteration: kai hoi hēsuchazan. kai epilabomenos iasato auton kai apelusen.
verses.study_note: "They held their peace" (hēsuchazan — were silent, kept quiet) — strategic silence rather than principled answer. Jesus does not wait for permission or consensus. He "took him" (epilabomenos — taking hold of him, grasping him) — the physical touch of deliberate contact — "healed him" (iasato — cured him completely, aorist) — instantly — "and let him go" (apelusen — released him, dismissed him) — the word of freedom and departure. The healing is done with three rapid verbs: touch, heal, release.

verses.kjv_text: And answered them, saying, Which of you shall have an ass or an ox fallen into a pit, and will not straightway pull him out on the sabbath day?
verses.original_text: καὶ πρὸς αὐτοὺς εἶπεν, Τίνος ὑμῶν υἱὸς ἢ βοῦς εἰς φρέαρ πεσεῖται, καὶ οὐκ εὐθέως ἀνασπάσει αὐτὸν ἐν ἡμέρᾳ τοῦ σαββάτου;
verses.transliteration: kai pros autous eipen, Tinos humōn huios ē bous eis phrear peseitai, kai ouk eutheōs anaspasei auton en hēmera tou sabbatou?
verses.study_note: The argument a fortiori intensifies from Luke 13:15 (ox or ass loosed for watering) to a well or pit rescue. "Son or ox" — some manuscripts read "son," others "ass"; either reading makes the point stronger (a child fallen in a pit would certainly be rescued immediately). "Straightway" (eutheōs — immediately, without hesitation) — no one would wait to rescue a child or animal from a pit until after the Sabbath. The logic is irresistible: if rescue urgency overrides Sabbath for animals, how much more for a human being?

verses.kjv_text: And they could not answer him again to these things.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐκ ἴσχυσαν ἀνταποκριθῆναι αὐτῷ πρὸς ταῦτα.
verses.transliteration: kai ouk ischusan antapokrithenai autō pros tauta.
verses.study_note: "Could not answer" (ouk ischusan — had no power, lacked the strength/ability) "again" (antapokrithenai — to answer back, to respond in counter-argument) — a legal term for not being able to reply in a judicial dispute. Their silence is complete capitulation on the theological level. They remain personally opposed but cannot offer a rebuttal. The healing stands, the argument stands, and the Pharisees are left in wordless resentment. The pattern will repeat until the cross provides their ultimate "answer."

verses.kjv_text: And he put forth a parable to those which were bidden, when he marked how they chose out the chief rooms; saying unto them,
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν δὲ πρὸς τοὺς κεκλημένους παραβολήν, ἐπέχων πῶς τὰς πρωτοκλισίας ἐξελέγοντο, λέγων πρὸς αὐτούς,
verses.transliteration: Elegen de pros tous keklēmenous parabolēn, epechōn pōs tas prōtoklisias exelegonto, legōn pros autous,
verses.study_note: "Marked" (epechōn — taking notice, observing carefully, fixing attention on) — Jesus is watching the social drama of the dinner as a teacher reads a text. "Chose out the chief rooms" (prōtoklisias exelegonto — were selecting for themselves the places of honor, the first reclining places). The reclining order at a Greco-Roman banquet directly indicated social rank. The guests' scramble for the best positions becomes the text of the parable.

verses.kjv_text: When thou art bidden of any man to a wedding, sit not down in the highest room; lest a more honourable man than thou be bidden of him;
verses.original_text: Ὅταν κληθῇς ὑπό τινος εἰς γάμους, μὴ κατακλιθῇς εἰς τὴν πρωτοκλισίαν, μήποτε ἐντιμότερός σου ᾖ κεκλημένος ὑπ' αὐτοῦ,
