verses.transliteration: Hotan klēthēs hupo tinos eis gamous, mē katakliTHēs eis tēn prōtoklisian, mēpote entimoteros sou ē keklēmenos hyp' autou,
verses.study_note: The surface level of the parable is practical wisdom: do not take the highest seat at a wedding (gamous — a wedding banquet, the most formal social occasion) lest a more honored guest arrives (entimoteros — more esteemed, more honored). This echoes Proverbs 25:6-7, which warns against presuming a place of honor before the king. But the parable is not ultimately about social strategy — it becomes a theological statement about kingdom values.

verses.kjv_text: And he that bade thee and him come and say to thee, Give this man place; and thou begin with shame to take the lowest room.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐλθὼν ὁ σε καὶ αὐτὸν καλέσας ἐρεῖ σοι, Δὸς τούτῳ τόπον· καὶ τότε ἄρξῃ μετὰ αἰσχύνης τὸν ἔσχατον τόπον κατέχειν.
verses.transliteration: kai elthōn ho se kai auton kalesas erei soi, Dos toutō topon; kai tote arxē meta aischunēs ton eschaton topon katechein.
verses.study_note: "With shame" (meta aischunēs — with disgrace, embarrassment, the burning feeling of public humiliation) — the person must surrender the high seat in full public view and process to the last available place. The very honor they seized becomes the occasion of maximum shame. Social aggression has produced its opposite. The parable uses the mechanics of ancient banquet culture to illustrate the spiritual dynamic of those who grasp for kingdom position.

verses.kjv_text: But when thou art bidden, go and sit down in the lowest room; that when he that bade thee cometh, he may say unto thee, Friend, go up higher: then shalt thou have worship in the presence of them that sit at meat with thee.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ' ὅταν κληθῇς, πορευθεὶς ἀνάπεσε εἰς τὸν ἔσχατον τόπον, ἵνα ὅταν ἔλθῃ ὁ κεκληκώς σε ἐρεῖ σοι, Φίλε, προσανάβηθι ἀνώτερον· τότε ἔσται σοι δόξα ἐνώπιον πάντων τῶν συνανακειμένων σοι.
verses.transliteration: all' hotan klēthēs, poreutheis anapese eis ton eschaton topon, hina hotan elthē ho keklēkōs se erei soi, Phile, prosanabēthi anōteron; tote estai soi doxa enōpion pantōn tōn sunanakeimenōn soi.
verses.study_note: "Friend" (Phile — a warm address from the host) — the one who took the lowest seat is called "friend" and invited to move up. "Glory" (doxa — honor, reputation) before all the dining companions is the result of self-humbling. The reversal: the humble person receives public honor without having grasped for it; the honor comes from the host's initiative. Theologically, this is God's pattern of exaltation: not rewarding self-promotion but honoring genuine humility.

verses.kjv_text: For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted.
verses.original_text: ὅτι πᾶς ὁ ὑψῶν ἑαυτὸν ταπεινωθήσεται, καὶ ὁ ταπεινῶν ἑαυτὸν ὑψωθήσεται.
verses.transliteration: hoti pas ho hupsōn heauton tapeinōthēsetai, kai ho tapeinōn heauton hupsōthēsetai.
verses.study_note: The parable's theological key, repeated also in 18:14 and elsewhere (Matt 23:12; Jas 4:10; 1 Pet 5:6). "Shall be abased" (tapeinōthēsetai — divine passive: will be brought low by God) and "shall be exalted" (hupsōthēsetai — divine passive: will be lifted up by God) — both verbs are passives, indicating that God is the agent of both reversals. Human self-elevation triggers divine lowering; human self-lowering triggers divine elevation. The principle governs all kingdom relationships.

verses.kjv_text: Then said he also to him that bade him, When thou makest a dinner or a supper, call not thy friends, nor thy brethren, neither thy kinsmen, nor thy rich neighbours; lest they also bid thee again, and a recompence be made thee.
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν δὲ καὶ τῷ κεκληκότι αὐτόν, Ὅταν ποιῇς ἄριστον ἢ δεῖπνον, μὴ φώνει τοὺς φίλους σου μηδὲ τοὺς ἀδελφούς σου μηδὲ τοὺς συγγενεῖς σου μηδὲ γείτονας πλουσίους, μήποτε καὶ αὐτοὶ ἀντικαλέσωσίν σε καὶ γένηται ἀνταπόδομά σοι.
verses.transliteration: Elegen de kai tō keklēkoti auton, Hotan poiēs ariston ē deipnon, mē phōnei tous philous sou mēde tous adelphous sou mēde tous suggeneis sou mēde geitonas plousious, mēpote kai autoi antikalesōsin se kai genētai antapodoma soi.
verses.study_note: Jesus now addresses the host himself — a daring move since the host is a leading Pharisee. The prohibition is not absolute (he does not ban inviting friends forever) but challenges the social economy of reciprocal hospitality. "Lest they also bid thee again" (antikalesōsin — invite back in return) and "a recompence be made thee" (antapodoma — a return-gift, repayment). Inviting those who can repay transforms hospitality into a social investment that yields no kingdom return.

verses.kjv_text: But when thou makest a feast, call the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind:
verses.original_text: ἀλλ' ὅταν δοχὴν ποιῇς, κάλει πτωχούς, ἀναπείρους, χωλούς, τυφλούς·
verses.transliteration: all' hotan dochēn poiēs, kalei ptōchous, anapeirous, chōlous, tuphlous;
verses.study_note: The positive instruction replaces the elite guest list with those Jesus calls "the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind" (ptōchous, anapeirous, chōlous, tuphlous) — precisely the categories of people the great supper host gathers from the streets (v.21) and the list that defines messianic ministry (7:22; Isa 35:5-6; 61:1). Inviting these people is kingdom investment: it cannot be repaid in the present age and must be rewarded by God himself at the resurrection.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt be blessed; for they cannot recompense thee: for thou shalt be recompensed at the resurrection of the just.
verses.original_text: καὶ μακάριος ἔσῃ, ὅτι οὐκ ἔχουσιν ἀνταποδοῦναί σοι· ἀνταποδοθήσεται γάρ σοι ἐν τῇ ἀναστάσει τῶν δικαίων.
verses.transliteration: kai makarios esē, hoti ouk echousin antapodounai soi; antapodothēsetai gar soi en tē anastasei tōn dikaiōn.
verses.study_note: "Thou shalt be blessed" (makarios esē — you will be blessed, in a state of divine favor) — a beatitude addressed to the host. "They cannot recompense thee" (ouk echousin antapodounai — they do not have the means to repay) — the inability of the guests to repay is the very basis of the blessing. "The resurrection of the just" (anastasei tōn dikaiōn) — a specifically Jewish phrase (Dan 12:2) for the resurrection of the righteous, which is the only occasion when divine repayment for genuine kingdom hospitality will occur.

verses.kjv_text: And when one of them that sat at meat with him heard these things, he said unto him, Blessed is he that shall eat bread in the kingdom of God.
verses.original_text: Ἀκούσας δέ τις τῶν συνανακειμένων ταῦτα εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Μακάριος ὅστις φάγεται ἄρτον ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Akousas de tis tōn sunanakeimenōn tauta eipen autō, Makarios hostis phAgetai arton en tē basileia tou Theou.
verses.study_note: A dinner guest responds to the mention of the resurrection of the just with a pious exclamation: "Blessed is he that shall eat bread in the kingdom of God." This is a comfortable theological statement — a platitude about the future that deflects from the present demand. The man assumed he would be among the blessed diners in the kingdom; Jesus's parable will gently but devastatingly question that assumption.

verses.kjv_text: Then said he unto him, A certain man made a great supper, and bade many:
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Ἄνθρωπός τις ἐποίει δεῖπνον μέγα, καὶ ἐκάλεσεν πολλούς·
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen autō, Anthrōpos tis epoiei deipnon mega, kai ekalesen pollous;
verses.study_note: "A great supper" (deipnon mega — a great dinner, the main meal of the day) with "many" (pollous) invited — suggesting an important social event requiring advance preparation and formal invitation. In ancient Mediterranean culture, a two-stage invitation was customary: a preliminary invitation well in advance, then a same-day summons when the feast was ready. The "many" are a large group of those who had already accepted the first invitation.

verses.kjv_text: And sent his servant at supper time to say to them that were bidden, Come; for all things are now ready.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπέστειλεν τὸν δοῦλον αὐτοῦ τῇ ὥρᾳ τοῦ δείπνου εἰπεῖν τοῖς κεκλημένοις, Ἔρχεσθε, ὅτι ἤδη ἕτοιμά ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: kai apesteilen ton doulon autou tē hōra tou deipnou eipein tois keklēmenois, Erchesthe, hoti ēdē hetoima estin.
verses.study_note: "His servant" (ton doulon autou — the slave, singular) represents the messengers of God's invitation — prophets, John, Jesus, and the apostles. "Come; for all things are now ready" (Erchesthe, hoti ēdē hetoima estin — Come! because already they are prepared) — the meal is ready; the moment of response has arrived. This second-stage summons corresponds to the present proclamation of the gospel: the kingdom is prepared; the invitation is now urgent.

verses.kjv_text: And they all with one consent began to make excuse. The first said unto him, I have bought a piece of ground, and I must needs go and see it: I pray thee have me excused.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἤρξαντο ἀπὸ μιᾶς πάντες παραιτεῖσθαι. ὁ πρῶτος εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Ἀγρὸν ἠγόρασα καὶ ἔχω ἀνάγκην ἐξελθεῖν καὶ ἰδεῖν αὐτόν· ἐρωτῶ σε, ἔχε με παρῃτημένον.
verses.transliteration: kai ērxanto apo mias pantes paraitEisthai. ho prōtos eipen autō, Agron ēgorasa kai echō anankēn exelthein kai idein auton; erōtō se, eche me parētēmenon.
verses.study_note: "With one consent" (apo mias — from one, unanimously) — despite three different reasons, the refusal is unanimous. The first excuse: "I have bought a piece of ground" — a new land purchase that needs inspection. But buying a field sight-unseen and then inspecting after purchase is commercially senseless. The excuse is a polite lie. "I pray thee have me excused" (parētēmenon — literally: hold me as having asked to be released) — formal, legalistic language for declining an obligation.

verses.kjv_text: And another said, I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to prove them: I pray thee have me excused.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἕτερος εἶπεν, Ζεύγη βοῶν ἠγόρασα πέντε καὶ πορεύομαι δοκιμάσαι αὐτά· ἐρωτῶ σε, ἔχε με παρῃτημένον.
verses.transliteration: kai heteros eipen, Zeugē boōn ēgorasa pente kai poreuomai dokimasai auta; erōtō se, eche me parētēmenon.
verses.study_note: The second excuse: "five yoke of oxen" (zeugē boōn — pairs of oxen, ten oxen total) bought and needing testing (dokimasai — proving their pulling quality). Again the sequence is backwards: oxen are tested before purchase, not after. Five yoke suggests a large farm operation; this is a wealthy man. As with the first excuse, the economic activity is the priority over the banquet. Property and business replace relationship with the host.

verses.kjv_text: And another said, I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἕτερος εἶπεν, Γυναῖκα ἔγημα καὶ διὰ τοῦτο οὐ δύναμαι ἐλθεῖν.
verses.transliteration: kai heteros eipen, Gunaika egēma kai dia touto ou dunamai elthein.
verses.study_note: The third excuse is the most abrupt — no apology, no formula of regret ("I pray thee have me excused"), no pretense of politeness: "I have married a wife and therefore I cannot come." Marriage exempted a man from certain obligations (Deut 20:7; 24:5) but not from social commitments already accepted. The bluntness reveals that this man does not even feel the need to pretend; the refusal is complete and unapologetic. Luke uses this as the sharpest of the three: family attachment overrides the kingdom invitation.

verses.kjv_text: So that servant came, and shewed his lord these things. Then the master of the house being angry said to his servant, Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in hither the poor, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blind.
verses.original_text: καὶ παραγενόμενος ὁ δοῦλος ἐκεῖνος ἀπήγγειλεν τῷ κυρίῳ αὐτοῦ ταῦτα. τότε ὀργισθεὶς ὁ οἰκοδεσπότης εἶπεν τῷ δούλῳ αὐτοῦ, Ἔξελθε ταχέως εἰς τὰς πλατείας καὶ ῥύμας τῆς πόλεως, καὶ τοὺς πτωχοὺς καὶ ἀναπείρους καὶ τυφλοὺς καὶ χωλοὺς εἰσάγαγε ὧδε.
verses.transliteration: kai paragenomenos ho doulos ekeinos apēngeilen tō kuriō autou tauta. tote orgistheis ho oikodespotēs eipen tō doulō autou, Exelthe tacheōs eis tas plateias kai rhumas tēs poleōs, kai tous ptōchous kai anapeirous kai tuphlous kai chōlous eisagage hōde.
verses.study_note: "Being angry" (orgistheis — enraged, moved to indignation) — the host's anger is righteous: the guests have broken a solemn social obligation and dishonored him publicly. His immediate response is not to close the feast but to redirect it. "Quickly" (tacheōs — at once, without delay) — the urgency of the mission to the urban poor matches the urgency of apostolic proclamation. The four categories (poor, maimed, halt, blind) are precisely those Jesus listed for the host to invite (v.13) — now the host himself does what he was commanded.

verses.kjv_text: And the servant said, Lord, it is done as thou hast commanded, and yet there is room.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ δοῦλος, Κύριε, γέγονεν ὡς ἐπέταξας, καὶ ἔτι τόπος ἐστίν.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen ho doulos, Kurie, gegonen hōs epetaxas, kai eti topos estin.
verses.study_note: "It is done as thou hast commanded" (gegonen — it has been done, completed, the perfect tense shows a completed action with present results) — the servant has carried out the urgent city mission and the hall is filling. "Yet there is room" (eti topos estin — still place remains, the house is not yet full) — the grace of God is not exhausted by the first wave of urban outreach; the feast is large enough for more. The second mission follows.

verses.kjv_text: And the lord said unto the servant, Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ κύριος πρὸς τὸν δοῦλον, Ἔξελθε εἰς τὰς ὁδοὺς καὶ φραγμούς, καὶ ἀνάγκασον εἰσελθεῖν, ἵνα γεμισθῇ μου ὁ οἶκος.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen ho kurios pros ton doulon, Exelthe eis tas hodous kai phragmous, kai anankason eiselthein, hina gemisthē mou ho oikos.
verses.study_note: "Highways and hedges" (hodous kai phragmous — roads and fence lines, the countryside outside the city) — the Gentile world beyond the city of Israel. "Compel them to come in" (anankason eiselthein — constrain, press urgently) — not violent coercion but the urgency of pressing invitation to reluctant people who do not feel worthy to enter (cf. 24:29 where Jesus is "constrained" to stay with Emmaus disciples). "My house may be filled" — the fullness of the Gentile mission fills the house; God's purpose cannot be frustrated by the original guests' refusal.

verses.kjv_text: For I say unto you, That none of those men which were bidden shall taste of my supper.
verses.original_text: λέγω γὰρ ὑμῖν ὅτι οὐδεὶς τῶν ἀνδρῶν ἐκείνων τῶν κεκλημένων γεύσεταί μου τοῦ δείπνου.
verses.transliteration: legō gar humin hoti oudeis tōn andrōn ekeinōn tōn keklēmenōn geusetai mou tou deipnou.
verses.study_note: "None of those men" (oudeis tōn andrōn ekeinōn — not one of those men) — absolute and total exclusion of the original invited guests. "Shall taste" (geusetai — will taste, experience) — the denial is complete. The shift to "I say unto you" (legō gar humin) suggests the host is now speaking as the divine voice addressing the Pharisees who are present. Those who have the first invitation — Israel's religious establishment — will not partake in the messianic feast they declined.

verses.kjv_text: And there went great multitudes with him: and he turned, and said unto them,
verses.original_text: Συνεπορεύοντο δὲ αὐτῷ ὄχλοι πολλοί, καὶ στραφεὶς εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς,
verses.transliteration: Suneporeunto de autō ochloi polloi, kai strapheis eipen pros autous,
verses.study_note: The scene shifts from the dinner to the road. "Great multitudes" (ochloi polloi — many crowds, plural) accompany Jesus on his journey to Jerusalem. "Turned" (strapheis — turning around to face them) — a deliberate, physical redirection of attention. The crowds who follow Jesus enthusiastically need to hear what genuine discipleship costs. What follows is not encouragement but challenge: Jesus is about to test whether the large crowd is following for the right reasons.

verses.kjv_text: If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple.
verses.original_text: Εἴ τις ἔρχεται πρός με καὶ οὐ μισεῖ τὸν πατέρα ἑαυτοῦ καὶ τὴν μητέρα καὶ τὴν γυναῖκα καὶ τὰ τέκνα καὶ τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς καὶ τὰς ἀδελφάς, ἔτι τε καὶ τὴν ψυχὴν ἑαυτοῦ, οὐ δύναταί μου εἶναι μαθητής.
verses.transliteration: Ei tis erchetai pros me kai ou misei ton patera heautou kai tēn mētera kai tēn gunaika kai ta tekna kai tous adelphous kai tas adelphas, eti te kai tēn psuchēn heautou, ou dunatai mou einai mathētēs.
verses.study_note: "Hate" (misei) is a Semitic comparative idiom meaning "love less by comparison" (cf. Gen 29:30-31 where Jacob "loves" Rachel and "hates" Leah, translated in meaning as "loved more" and "loved less"). Matthew 10:37 reads "love more than" — the parallel clarifies the meaning. This is not permission for literal hatred but a demand that allegiance to Jesus surpasses every other loyalty, including self (tēn psuchēn heautou — his own soul/life). Every other relationship must be secondary to discipleship.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever doth not bear his cross, and come after me, cannot be my disciple.
verses.original_text: ὅστις οὐ βαστάζει τὸν σταυρὸν ἑαυτοῦ καὶ ἔρχεται ὀπίσω μου, οὐ δύναταί μου εἶναι μαθητής.
verses.transliteration: hostis ou bastazei ton stauron heautou kai erchetai opisō mou, ou dunatai mou einai mathētēs.
verses.study_note: "Bear his cross" (bastazei ton stauron heautou — carries his own cross) — said to people who had seen criminals carrying their execution instruments through the streets to the place of death. The cross was a public symbol of condemned, shameful death. Jesus requires his disciples to adopt this posture voluntarily: public association with him that includes shame, rejection, and potentially death. "And come after me" (erchetai opisō mou — continues coming behind me) — the following is continuous, not a one-time act.

verses.kjv_text: For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it?
verses.original_text: τίς γὰρ ἐξ ὑμῶν θέλων πύργον οἰκοδομῆσαι οὐχὶ πρῶτον καθίσας ψηφίζει τὴν δαπάνην, εἰ ἔχει εἰς ἀπαρτισμόν;
verses.transliteration: tis gar ex humōn thelōn purgon oikodomēsai ouchi prōton kathisas psēphizei tēn dapanēn, ei echei eis apartismon?
verses.study_note: The first cost-counting parable: a tower builder. "Sitteth not down first" (prōton kathisas — first sitting down, a deliberate posture of calculation) and "counteth the cost" (psēphizei tēn dapanēn — calculates the expense, psēphizō from psēphos a voting stone, hence counting) — whether sufficient (eis apartismon — for completion). The prudent builder does not begin construction until he knows he can finish. The point: discipleship is a long-term, costly commitment; count what it will require before you begin.

verses.kjv_text: Lest haply, after he hath laid the foundation, and is not able to finish it, all that behold it begin to mock him,
verses.original_text: ἵνα μήποτε θέντος αὐτοῦ θεμέλιον καὶ μὴ ἰσχύοντος ἐκτελέσαι, πάντες οἱ θεωροῦντες ἄρξωνται ἐμπαίζειν αὐτῷ,
verses.transliteration: hina mēpote thentos autou themelion kai mē ischuontos ektelesai, pantes hoi theōrountes arxōntai empaizein autō,
verses.study_note: "Begin to mock him" (arxōntai empaizein — begin to ridicule, jeer at) — public mockery is the consequence of abandoned construction. An unfinished tower stands as a monument to the builder's failure of calculation. Applied to discipleship: the disciple who begins enthusiastically without counting the cost, then abandons the way under pressure, becomes an object of ridicule and brings dishonor to the name of Christ. The damage is both personal and evangelistic.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, This man began to build, and was not able to finish.
verses.original_text: λέγοντες ὅτι Οὗτος ὁ ἄνθρωπος ἤρξατο οἰκοδομεῖν καὶ οὐκ ἴσχυσεν ἐκτελέσαι.
verses.transliteration: legontes hoti Houtos ho anthrōpos ērxato oikodomein kai ouk ischusen ektelesai.
verses.study_note: "This man began and could not finish" — the epitaph of a half-built tower and a half-formed disciple. The saying "he began but could not finish" (ēRxato...ouk ischusen ektelesai) is a pair of past tenses (aorists) describing a completed failure: the beginning happened; the finishing did not. The brevity of the comment captures the contempt of onlookers. Jesus uses this vivid social disgrace as a warning against superficial, uncalculated commitment to the kingdom.

verses.kjv_text: Or what king, going to make war against another king, sitteth not down first, and consulteth whether he be able with ten thousand to meet him that cometh against him with twenty thousand?
verses.original_text: ἢ τίς βασιλεὺς πορευόμενος ἑτέρῳ βασιλεῖ συμβαλεῖν εἰς πόλεμον οὐχὶ καθίσας πρῶτον βουλεύσεται εἰ δυνατός ἐστιν ἐν δέκα χιλιάσιν ὑπαντῆσαι τῷ μετὰ εἴκοσι χιλιάδων ἐρχομένῳ ἐπ' αὐτόν;
verses.transliteration: ē tis basileus poreuomenos heterō basilei sumbalein eis polemon ouchi kathisas prōton bouleUsetai ei dunatos estin en deka chiliasin hupantēsai tō meta eikosi chiliAdōn erchomenō ep' auton?
verses.study_note: The second parable: a king with 10,000 facing a king with 20,000. "Sitteth not down first" (prōton kathisas — the same deliberate sitting of calculation) and "consulteth" (bouleuSetai — deliberates, plans strategically, takes counsel). The odds are against him — two to one — and he must honestly assess whether his 10,000 can defeat the enemy's 20,000. The military context raises the stakes: in war, poor calculation costs not just money but lives. Discipleship has similarly irreversible consequences.

verses.kjv_text: Or else, while the other is yet a great way off, he sendeth an ambassage, and desireth conditions of peace.
verses.original_text: εἰ δὲ μήγε, ἔτι αὐτοῦ πόρρω ὄντος, πρεσβείαν ἀποστείλας ἐρωτᾷ τὰ πρὸς εἰρήνην.
verses.transliteration: ei de mēge, eti autou porrō ontos, presbeian aposteilas erōtā ta pros eirēnēn.
verses.study_note: "If not" — if he cannot win — the wise king sends an embassy (presbeian — ambassadors, a peace delegation) while the enemy is still far off (porrō ontos — at a distance, while there is still time). He negotiates for peace rather than inviting certain defeat. The application is striking: if you are not willing and able to pay the full cost of discipleship, it is better to honestly assess this before committing than to pursue it partway and then be overwhelmed.

verses.kjv_text: So likewise, whosoever he be of you that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple.
verses.original_text: οὕτως οὖν πᾶς ἐξ ὑμῶν ὃς οὐκ ἀποτάσσεται πᾶσιν τοῖς ἑαυτοῦ ὑπάρχουσιν, οὐ δύναταί μου εἶναι μαθητής.
verses.transliteration: houtōs oun pas ex humōn hos ouk apotassetai pasin tois heautou huparchousin, ou dunatai mou einai mathētēs.
verses.study_note: "Forsaketh not all that he hath" (ouk apotassetai pasin tois heautou huparchousin — does not renounce/say farewell to all his own possessions) — apotassetai is the word for saying a formal farewell, taking leave of, renouncing. The renunciation of "all" is the cost: not a percentage, not what is convenient, but all. This does not necessarily mean literal destitution (the disciples retained homes and boats) but means holding all things as available for the kingdom's call. No possession can be off-limits to the lordship of Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be seasoned?
verses.original_text: Καλὸν οὖν τὸ ἅλας· ἐὰν δὲ καὶ τὸ ἅλας μωρανθῇ, ἐν τίνι ἀρτυθήσεται;
verses.transliteration: Kalon oun to halas; ean de kai to halas mōranthē, en tini artuthēsetai?
verses.study_note: The salt saying (cf. Matt 5:13; Mark 9:50) closes the discipleship discourse. "Salt is good" (Kalon to halas) — salt's functions are indispensable: preserving, flavoring, purifying. "If the salt have lost his savour" (mōranthē — become foolish, insipid, tasteless; the word is from mōros — fool — linked to "insipid" through loss of sharp quality). How do you re-salt salt? It is an impossibility: the very substance that does the seasoning cannot itself be seasoned. Discipleship without genuine commitment is like this — it has lost its essential quality.

verses.kjv_text: It is neither fit for the land, nor yet for the dunghill; but men cast it out. He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.
verses.original_text: οὔτε εἰς γῆν οὔτε εἰς κοπρίαν εὔθετόν ἐστιν, ἔξω βάλλουσιν αὐτό. ὁ ἔχων ὦτα ἀκούειν ἀκουέτω.
verses.transliteration: oute eis gēn oute eis koprian euthetOn estin, exō ballousin auto. ho echōn ōta akouein akouetō.
verses.study_note: Tasteless salt is useless — not even fit as fertilizer (eis gēn — for the soil) or as a compost activator (eis koprian — for the manure heap). "Men cast it out" (exō ballousin auto — they throw it away, outside) — the final fate of the tasteless disciple. "He that hath ears to hear, let him hear" (akouetō — present imperative: keep hearing, keep listening) — the solemn formula that marks a saying of supreme importance, calling for the kind of attentive, obedient hearing that the entire chapter demanded.

meta.overview: Luke 15 is justly regarded as the crown of Luke's Gospel and one of the most theologically profound chapters in the entire NT. The setting is a crisis: tax collectors and sinners are drawing near to Jesus (v.1), and the Pharisees and scribes are murmuring — "This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them" (v.2). Jesus's response is three parables, each escalating in emotional intensity and theological depth, each making exactly the same point from a different angle: God actively seeks what is lost, and heaven explodes with joy when the lost is found.
meta.theme: The parable of the lost sheep (vv.3-7) features a shepherd who leaves ninety-nine sheep to search for one. The parable of the lost coin (vv.8-10) features a woman who lights a lamp and sweeps her entire house to find one silver coin. Both parables end with the same refrain: rejoicing with friends and neighbours, and "I say unto you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner that repenteth." Both are deliberately short; both are designed to shame the Pharisees who murmur rather than rejoice at sinners' recovery.
meta.keyWordOriginal: The parable of the prodigal son (vv.11-32) is the longest and most complex of the three. It is properly the parable of two sons and a father. The younger son demands his inheritance, squanders it in a far country, "comes to himself," returns, and is met by a running father whose embrace and celebration are extravagant beyond all social expectation. The language of the father's welcome (robe, ring, shoes, fatted calf) is the language of restoration to full sonship. At the center of the parable (v.24) stands the great resurrection formula: "this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found."
meta.keyWordMeaning: The parable's second half (vv.25-32) introduces the elder son — a portrait of the murmuring Pharisee — who refuses to enter the celebration. His complaint reveals a spirit of legal merit, resentment at grace, and failure to understand the father's heart. The father goes out to him as well, pleading gently. The parable ends without resolution — the elder son's response is left open — because Luke's reader (the Pharisee) must decide. All three parables answer the Pharisees' murmur not with argument but with a portrayal of God's own joy over the found.
outline: εὑρίσκω (heuriskō)

verses.kjv_text: Then drew near unto him all the publicans and sinners for to hear him.
verses.original_text: Ἦσαν δὲ αὐτῷ ἐγγίζοντες πάντες οἱ τελῶναι καὶ οἱ ἁμαρτωλοὶ ἀκούειν αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Ēsan de autō engizOntes pantes hoi telōnai kai hoi hamartōloi akouein autou.
verses.study_note: "Drew near" (engizOntes — were drawing near, approaching, an imperfect indicating continuous movement) — the tax collectors and sinners are not merely present but actively pressing toward Jesus to hear. Their hunger for his words is contrasted with the Pharisees' hostility. "All" (pantes) — the comprehensiveness of the attraction: people regarded as the most disqualified found Jesus most magnetic. This social reality becomes the occasion for all three parables.

verses.kjv_text: And the Pharisees and scribes murmured, saying, This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them.
verses.original_text: καὶ διεγόγγυζον οἵ τε Φαρισαῖοι καὶ οἱ γραμματεῖς λέγοντες ὅτι Οὗτος ἁμαρτωλοὺς προσδέχεται καὶ συνεσθίει αὐτοῖς.
verses.transliteration: kai diegogguzon hoi te Pharisaioi kai hoi grammateis legontes hoti Houtos hamartōlous prosdechetai kai sunesthiei autois.
verses.study_note: "Murmured" (diegogguzon — kept grumbling, the imperfect describing ongoing complaint; a word that echoes Israel's wilderness murmuring against God, Num 11:1). "Receiveth" (prosdechetai — welcomes, accepts, receives as a guest) and "eateth with them" (sunesthiei — eats together with) — table fellowship was an intimate social act signaling acceptance and equality. The Pharisees' complaint is that Jesus not only tolerates sinners but actively welcomes them and eats with them. This is the theological crisis that the three parables address.

verses.kjv_text: And he spake this parable unto them, saying,
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς αὐτοὺς τὴν παραβολὴν ταύτην λέγων,
verses.transliteration: eipen de pros autous tēn parabolēn tautēn legōn,
verses.study_note: The singular "parable" (parabolēn — singular, yet what follows are three parables) suggests that all three stories are one unified response to the same murmur. Luke frames them as a single extended reply. Jesus addresses his answer not defensively ("I have the right to eat with sinners") but offensively ("let me show you the heart of God"). The parables do not justify Jesus's behavior; they portray God's joy, which the Pharisees do not share.

verses.kjv_text: What man of you, having an hundred sheep, if he lose one of them, doth not leave the ninety and nine in the wilderness, and go after that which is lost, until he find it?
verses.original_text: Τίς ἄνθρωπος ἐξ ὑμῶν ἔχων ἑκατὸν πρόβατα καὶ ἀπολέσας ἐξ αὐτῶν ἓν οὐ καταλείπει τὰ ἐνενήκοντα ἐννέα ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ καὶ πορεύεται ἐπὶ τὸ ἀπολωλὸς ἕως εὕρῃ αὐτό;
verses.transliteration: Tis anthrōpos ex humōn echōn hekaton probata kai apolEsas ex autōn hen ou kataleipei ta enenēkonta ennea en tē erēmō kai poreuetai epi to apolōlos heōs heurē auto?
verses.study_note: The rhetorical question (Tis anthrōpos ex humōn — which man among you) invites the Pharisees into the shepherd's perspective — they are expected to agree that a shepherd would leave the ninety-nine to search for the one. "Until he find it" (heōs heurē auto — until the moment he finds it) — the search is persistent and uninterrupted. The shepherd does not give up, adjust the odds, or settle for 99%. The one lost sheep has the full, undivided attention of the shepherd until found.

verses.kjv_text: And when he hath found it, he layeth it on his shoulders, rejoicing.
verses.original_text: καὶ εὑρὼν ἐπιτίθησιν ἐπὶ τοὺς ὤμους αὐτοῦ χαίρων·
verses.transliteration: kai heurōn epitithēsin epi tous ōmous autou chairōn;
verses.study_note: "Layeth it on his shoulders" (epitithēsin epi tous ōmous autou — places it upon his own shoulders) — not driving it back but carrying it. A lost, exhausted sheep cannot walk home; the shepherd bears it. This is a physical image of redemption: the shepherd does not merely find and point the way but lifts the straying creature onto himself for the journey home. "Rejoicing" (chairōn — present participle: actively rejoicing, while carrying) — the joy begins not at arrival but at the moment of finding.

verses.kjv_text: And when he cometh home, he calleth together his friends and neighbours, saying unto them, Rejoice with me; for I have found my sheep which was lost.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐλθὼν εἰς τὸν οἶκον συγκαλεῖ τοὺς φίλους καὶ τοὺς γείτονας, λέγων αὐτοῖς, Συγχάρητέ μοι, ὅτι εὗρον τὸ πρόβατόν μου τὸ ἀπολωλός.
verses.transliteration: kai elthōn eis ton oikon sungkalei tous philous kai tous geitonas, legōn autois, Sungcharēte moi, hoti heuron to probaton mou to apolōlos.
verses.study_note: "Calleth together his friends and neighbours" (sungkalei — summons, calls together) — the joy is too large to contain alone; it demands community. "Rejoice with me" (sungcharēte moi — rejoice together with me) — share in my joy. "My sheep which was lost" (to probaton mou to apolōlos — my sheep, the one that was lost, the perfect participle indicating the state of lostness that has now been reversed) — the possessive "my" reveals the shepherd's personal attachment. It was not just a sheep but his sheep.

verses.kjv_text: I say unto you, that likewise joy shall be in heaven over one sinner that repenteth, more than over ninety and nine just persons, which need no repentance.
verses.original_text: λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι οὕτως χαρὰ ἐν τῷ οὐρανῷ ἔσται ἐπὶ ἑνὶ ἁμαρτωλῷ μετανοοῦντι ἢ ἐπὶ ἐνενήκοντα ἐννέα δικαίοις οἵτινες οὐ χρείαν ἔχουσιν μετανοίας.
verses.transliteration: legō humin hoti houtōs chara en tō ouranō estai epi heni hamartōlō metanounti ē epi enenēkonta ennea dikaiois hoitines ou chreian echousin metanoias.
verses.study_note: "Joy in heaven" (chara en tō ouranō) — heaven's joy over one repenting sinner exceeds its joy over ninety-nine just persons who need no repentance. The "ninety-nine just persons who need no repentance" is ironic: the Pharisees considered themselves in this category, but in fact no human being qualifies. The statement both honors genuine righteousness and exposes the comparative indifference that fails to rejoice at a sinner's return — the very emotion the Pharisees displayed in v.2.

verses.kjv_text: Either what woman having ten pieces of silver, if she lose one piece, doth not light a candle, and sweep the house, and seek diligently till she find it?
verses.original_text: Ἢ τίς γυνὴ δραχμὰς ἔχουσα δέκα, ἐὰν ἀπολέσῃ δραχμὴν μίαν, οὐχὶ ἅπτει λύχνον καὶ σαροῖ τὴν οἰκίαν καὶ ζητεῖ ἐπιμελῶς ἕως οὗ εὕρῃ;
verses.transliteration: Ē tis gunē drachmas echousa deka, ean apolEsē drachmēn mian, ouchi haptei luchnon kai saroi tēn oikian kai zētei epimelōs heōs hou heurē?
verses.study_note: The second parable shifts to a woman — Luke's characteristic pairing of male (shepherd) and female (woman) examples (cf. 13:18-21). A drachma was roughly equivalent to a denarius — a day's wage. Ten drachmas may have been her entire savings or possibly her dowry coins worn as a headdress. "Light a candle" (haptei luchnon — lights a lamp), "sweep the house" (saroi — sweeps carefully), "seek diligently" (zētei epimelōs — searches with care and attention) — three actions escalating in effort. The enclosed Palestinian house required a lamp to find a small coin.

verses.kjv_text: And when she hath found it, she calleth her friends and her neighbours together, saying, Rejoice with me; for I have found the piece which I had lost.
verses.original_text: καὶ εὑροῦσα συγκαλεῖ τὰς φίλας καὶ γείτονας λέγουσα, Συγχάρητέ μοι, ὅτι εὗρον τὴν δραχμὴν ἣν ἀπώλεσα.
verses.transliteration: kai heurousa sungkalei tas philas kai geitonas legousa, Sungcharēte moi, hoti heuron tēn drachmēn hēn apōlesa.
verses.study_note: The woman's response mirrors the shepherd's exactly: she summons her female friends and neighbors (philas — feminine form) to share her joy. "Rejoice with me" (sungcharēte moi) — the same call. "Which I had lost" (hēn apōlesa — which I lost, aorist: a specific past event) — the personal past loss and the present finding together constitute the reason for communal joy. The community's shared joy reflects heaven's; those who refuse to rejoice with the woman are refusing to share in God's own emotion.

verses.kjv_text: Likewise, I say unto you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner that repenteth.
verses.original_text: οὕτως λέγω ὑμῖν, χαρὰ γίνεται ἐνώπιον τῶν ἀγγέλων τοῦ Θεοῦ ἐπὶ ἑνὶ ἁμαρτωλῷ μετανοοῦντι.
verses.transliteration: houtōs legō humin, chara ginetai enōpion tōn angelōn tou Theou epi heni hamartōlō metanounti.
verses.study_note: "In the presence of the angels" (enōpion tōn angelōn tou Theou) — the joy is before, in the presence of, the angelic court. Some commentators suggest the joy belongs to God himself ("before the angels" meaning in God's own throne room); others that the angels themselves rejoice. Either way, a single sinner's repentance reverberates through the entire heavenly realm. The Pharisees who murmur at sinners' return are not in sympathy with heaven's own emotion — they stand against the heart of God.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, A certain man had two sons:
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δέ, Ἄνθρωπός τις εἶχεν δύο υἱούς.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de, Anthrōpos tis eichen duo huious.
verses.study_note: The third and longest parable begins with stark economy: "a certain man had two sons" — eight words in Greek, three in English. No names, no location, no backstory. The focus is entirely on relationships and responses. The "two sons" structure is crucial: the parable is not only about the younger son's redemption but about the elder son's resentment. Both sons have a broken relationship with the father; one is restored, the other's restoration remains open. The father is the center of both stories.

verses.kjv_text: And the younger of them said to his father, Father, give me the portion of goods that falleth to me. And he divided unto them his living.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ νεώτερος αὐτῶν τῷ πατρί, Πάτερ, δός μοι τὸ ἐπιβάλλον μέρος τῆς οὐσίας. ὁ δὲ διεῖλεν αὐτοῖς τὸν βίον.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen ho neōteros autōn tō patri, Pater, dos moi to epiballon meros tēs ousias. ho de dieilen autois ton bion.
verses.study_note: In Jewish law, the younger son could receive his share (one third) only at the father's death or by special request; to demand it while the father lives was equivalent to wishing him dead — a profound social dishonor. "Give me" (dos moi — hand over to me, imperative with no courtesy) reveals the son's posture. The father's response — "divided unto them his living" (dieilen autois ton bion — divided between them his means of life) — is neither vindictive nor weak; it is the extraordinary love that allows freedom even at great cost.

verses.kjv_text: And not many days after the younger son gathered all together, and took his journey into a far country, and there wasted his substance with riotous living.
verses.original_text: καὶ μετ' οὐ πολλὰς ἡμέρας συναγαγὼν ἅπαντα ὁ νεώτερος υἱὸς ἀπεδήμησεν εἰς χώραν μακράν, καὶ ἐκεῖ διεσκόρπισεν τὴν οὐσίαν αὐτοῦ ζῶν ἀσώτως.
verses.transliteration: kai met' ou pollas hēmeras sunagagōn hapanta ho neōteros huios apedēmesen eis chōran makran, kai ekei dieskorpisen tēn ousian autou zōn asōtōs.
verses.study_note: "Not many days after" — no gradual drift but an immediate departure. "Gathered all together" (sunagagōn hapanta — collecting everything, liquidating all assets) — he converts the estate portion to portable wealth. "Far country" (chōran makran — a distant land) — maximum distance from the father. "Wasted his substance" (dieskorpisen tēn ousian autou — scattered, squandered his property, the same word used for scattering seed randomly) "with riotous living" (zōn asōtōs — living without saving, dissolute, profligate).

verses.kjv_text: And when he had spent all, there arose a mighty famine in that land; and he began to be in want.
verses.original_text: δαπανήσαντος δὲ αὐτοῦ πάντα ἐγένετο λιμὸς ἰσχυρὸς κατὰ τὴν χώραν ἐκείνην, καὶ αὐτὸς ἤρξατο ὑστερεῖσθαι.
verses.transliteration: dapanēsantos de autou panta egeneto limos ischuros kata tēn chōran ekeinēn, kai autos ērxato hustereisthai.
verses.study_note: "When he had spent all" (dapanēsantos de autou panta — when he had used up everything, aorist genitive absolute) — complete depletion and the timing of the famine are not coincidental in the narrative; they are compounded disciplines. "Mighty famine" (limos ischuros — a severe, powerful famine) — he has no resources to weather the crisis. "Began to be in want" (ērxato hustereisthai — started to lack, to fall behind, to be in deficit) — the movement from profligacy to poverty is rapid and complete.

verses.kjv_text: And he went and joined himself to a citizen of that country; and he sent him into his fields to feed swine.
verses.original_text: καὶ πορευθεὶς ἐκολλήθη ἑνὶ τῶν πολιτῶν τῆς χώρας ἐκείνης, καὶ ἔπεμψεν αὐτὸν εἰς τοὺς ἀγροὺς αὐτοῦ βόσκειν χοίρους.
verses.transliteration: kai poreutheis ekollēthē heni tōn politōn tēs chōras ekeinēs, kai epempsen auton eis tous agrous autou boskein choirous.
verses.study_note: "Joined himself" (ekollēthē — cleaved to, attached himself, the word for a close bond, as of marriage) — the desperation of attachment to any available help. "Feed swine" (boskein choirous) — for a Jewish young man, the lowest imaginable employment: tending ceremonially unclean animals (Lev 11:7). He is not only in a far country but at the furthest possible distance from Jewish identity, Torah, and home. The employment is a measure of his complete degradation.

verses.kjv_text: And he would fain have filled his belly with the husks that the swine did eat: and no man gave unto him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπεθύμει γεμίσαι τὴν κοιλίαν αὐτοῦ ἀπὸ τῶν κερατίων ὧν ἤσθιον οἱ χοῖροι, καὶ οὐδεὶς ἐδίδου αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: kai epethumei gemisai tēn koilian autou apo tōn keratōn hōn ēsthion hoi choiroi, kai oudeis edidou autō.
verses.study_note: "Would fain have filled his belly" (epethumei gemisai — was longing, craving to fill his stomach, the imperfect of intense unfulfilled desire) "with the husks" (keratiōn — carob pods, the food of pigs and the poorest people). "No man gave unto him" (oudeis edidou autō — no one was giving to him) — complete abandonment by every human source. The far country offers nothing. He who left his father's house with wealth is now beneath the pigs he tends. The bottom has been reached.

verses.kjv_text: And when he came to himself, he said, How many hired servants of my father's have bread enough and to spare, and I perish with hunger!
verses.original_text: εἰς ἑαυτὸν δὲ ἐλθὼν εἶπεν, Πόσοι μίσθιοι τοῦ πατρός μου περισσεύουσιν ἄρτων, ἐγὼ δὲ λιμῷ ὧδε ἀπόλλυμαι.
verses.transliteration: eis heauton de elthōn eipen, Posoi misthioi tou patros mou perisseuousin artōn, egō de limō hōde apollumai.
verses.study_note: "Came to himself" (eis heauton de elthōn — having come to himself, returned to his senses, a Greek idiom for recovery of reason after delusion or stupor) — one of the most psychologically precise phrases in the NT. Sin is a form of self-alienation; repentance is the recovery of one's true self. "How many hired servants of my father's" — even the lowest-paid workers in his father's household have more than enough (perisseuousin — have abundance). The comparison strikes his conscience: he has exchanged a servant's abundance for a pig-feeder's starvation.

verses.kjv_text: I will arise and go to my father, and will say unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and before thee,
verses.original_text: ἀναστὰς πορεύσομαι πρὸς τὸν πατέρα μου καὶ ἐρῶ αὐτῷ, Πάτερ, ἥμαρτον εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ ἐνώπιόν σου,
verses.transliteration: anastas poreusomai pros ton patera mou kai erō autō, Pater, hēmarton eis ton ouranon kai enōpion sou,
verses.study_note: "I will arise" (anastas — rising up, a word also used for resurrection) and "go to my father" — the decision to return. "Father, I have sinned" (Pater, hēmarton — I sinned, aorist: a completed act with ongoing consequences) "against heaven" (eis ton ouranon — toward heaven, against God) "and before thee" (enōpion sou — in your sight). The confession has two dimensions: vertical (against God) and horizontal (against the father). True repentance acknowledges both, and the son rehearses his speech — a sign of genuine forethought, not mere impulse.

verses.kjv_text: And am no more worthy to be called thy son: make me as one of thy hired servants.
verses.original_text: οὐκέτι εἰμὶ ἄξιος κληθῆναι υἱός σου· ποίησόν με ὡς ἕνα τῶν μισθίων σου.
verses.transliteration: oukEti eimi axios klēthēnai huios sou; poiEson me hōs hena tōn misthiōn sou.
verses.study_note: "No more worthy to be called thy son" (oukEti eimi axios klēthēnai huios sou — I am no longer worthy to be named your son) — a realistic self-assessment without false modesty. Sonship has been forfeited by his actions. "Make me as one of thy hired servants" (misthiōn — day laborers, who were employed temporarily and had no household status) — he will not ask to be reinstated as a son but only for servant employment. This is the planned content of his confession; what the father actually does will far exceed his request.

verses.kjv_text: And he arose, and came to his father. But when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀναστὰς ἦλθεν πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ἑαυτοῦ. ἔτι δὲ αὐτοῦ μακρὰν ἀπέχοντος εἶδεν αὐτὸν ὁ πατὴρ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐσπλαγχνίσθη, καὶ δραμὼν ἐπέπεσεν ἐπὶ τὸν τράχηλον αὐτοῦ καὶ κατεφίλησεν αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: kai anastas ēlthen pros ton patera heautou. eti de autou makran apechontos eiden auton ho patēr autou kai esplanchnisthē, kai dramōn epepesen epi ton trachēlon autou kai katephilēsen auton.
verses.study_note: "Yet a great way off" (makran apechontos — while still far distant) — the father was watching. "Saw him" — the father recognizes him at a distance, suggesting he had been watching the road. "Had compassion" (esplanchnisthē — was moved in the bowels, deep visceral compassion). "Ran" (dramōn — running) — an undignified act for a Middle Eastern patriarch, scandalous in that culture. He runs to intercept the shame before it fully arrives. "Fell on his neck" and "kissed him" (katephilēsen — kissed tenderly, repeatedly) — the embrace prevents the son from completing his prostration.

verses.kjv_text: And the son said unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in thy sight, and am no more worthy to be called thy son.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ υἱός, Πάτερ, ἥμαρτον εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ ἐνώπιόν σου, οὐκέτι εἰμὶ ἄξιος κληθῆναι υἱός σου.
verses.transliteration: eipen de ho huios, Pater, hēmarton eis ton ouranon kai enōpion sou, oukEti eimi axios klēthēnai huios sou.
verses.study_note: The son delivers his rehearsed confession — but only the first part. He confesses sin against heaven and the father, and declares himself unworthy of sonship. He never gets to say "make me as one of thy hired servants" (v.19) because the father interrupts with commands of restoration. The confession is genuine and complete; the father does not need to hear the servant-request because he has already decided to restore sonship. The father's response exceeds everything the son planned to ask.

verses.kjv_text: But the father said to his servants, Bring forth the best robe, and put it on him; and put a ring on his hand, and shoes on his feet:
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ πατὴρ πρὸς τοὺς δούλους αὐτοῦ, Ταχὺ ἐξενέγκατε στολὴν τὴν πρώτην καὶ ἐνδύσατε αὐτόν, καὶ δότε δακτύλιον εἰς τὴν χεῖρα αὐτοῦ καὶ ὑποδήματα εἰς τοὺς πόδας,
verses.transliteration: eipen de ho patēr pros tous doulous autou, Tachu exenegkate stolēn tēn prōtēn kai endusate auton, kai dote daktulion eis tēn cheira autou kai hupodēmata eis tous podas,
verses.study_note: "The best robe" (stolēn tēn prōtēn — the first robe, the finest robe, place of honor) — placed on the ragged wanderer immediately. "A ring" (daktulion — a signet ring, symbol of authority and family identity, perhaps the family seal). "Shoes on his feet" — slaves went barefoot; free men wore sandals. The three gifts together restore full sonship: dignity (robe), authority (ring), freedom (shoes). There is no probationary period, no testing of sincerity — the restoration is immediate, complete, and public.

verses.kjv_text: And bring hither the fatted calf, and kill it; and let us eat, and be merry:
verses.original_text: καὶ φέρετε τὸν μόσχον τὸν σιτευτόν, θύσατε, καὶ φαγόντες εὐφρανθῶμεν,
verses.transliteration: kai pherete ton moschon ton siteuton, thusate, kai phagontes euphrantōmen,
verses.study_note: "The fatted calf" (ton moschon ton siteuton — the fattened young bull, grain-fed and reserved for a special occasion) — the most expensive possible celebration, enough meat for the entire village. A fatted calf is not slaughtered for a small family dinner but for a communal feast. The father throws the village a party. "Let us eat, and be merry" (phagontes euphrantōmen — having eaten let us rejoice) — the language of genuine, exuberant celebration, not solemn ceremony.

verses.kjv_text: For this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found. And they began to be merry.
verses.original_text: ὅτι οὗτος ὁ υἱός μου νεκρὸς ἦν καὶ ἀνέζησεν, καὶ ἀπολωλὼς ἦν καὶ εὑρέθη. καὶ ἤρξαντο εὐφραίνεσθαι.
verses.transliteration: hoti houtos ho huios mou nekros ēn kai anEzēsen, kai apolōlos ēn kai heurethē. kai ērxanto euphrainesth​ai.
verses.study_note: "This my son" (houtos ho huios mou) — "my son" — the father never stopped calling him son even when he was in the far country. "Was dead, and is alive again" (nekros ēn kai anEzEsen — dead and came to life again) — the resurrection language is explicit: the prodigal's return is a kind of resurrection, a coming back from the dead. "Was lost, and is found" (apolōlos ēn kai heurethē — the echo of the lost sheep and coin). The two pairs of contrasts — dead/alive, lost/found — are the theological key of the entire chapter and of the gospel of grace.

verses.kjv_text: Now his elder son was in the field: and as he came and drew nigh to the house, he heard musick and dancing.
verses.original_text: Ἦν δὲ ὁ υἱὸς αὐτοῦ ὁ πρεσβύτερος ἐν ἀγρῷ· καὶ ὡς ἐρχόμενος ἤγγισεν τῇ οἰκίᾳ, ἤκουσεν συμφωνίας καὶ χορῶν,
verses.transliteration: Ēn de ho huios autou ho presbUteros en agrō; kai hōs erchomenos ēngisen tē oikia, ēkousen sumphōnias kai chorōn,
verses.study_note: The narrative's second half pivots to the elder son — in the field, working. He is the model of outward faithfulness: in the field, not the far country. As he approaches home (ēngisen — drew near, the same verb as the sinners in v.1 drawing near to Jesus), he hears music and dancing (sumphōnias kai chorōn — symphony and circle dances). He does not know the reason; he must ask. His distance from his father's joy, despite physical proximity, is the parable's second tragedy.

verses.kjv_text: And he called one of the servants, and asked what these things meant.
verses.original_text: καὶ προσκαλεσάμενος ἕνα τῶν παίδων ἐπυνθάνετο τί ἂν εἴη ταῦτα.
verses.transliteration: kai proskalesamenos hena tōn paidōn epunthaneto ti an eiē tauta.
verses.study_note: "Called one of the servants" (proskalesamenos hena tōn paidōn) — he does not go in and find out himself but sends for information. He maintains distance even before he knows the reason. "Asked what these things meant" (epunthaneto ti an eiē tauta — was inquiring what these things might be) — the use of the optative mood (eiē — might be) in the indirect question suggests polite formality; he is controlling his approach. His subsequent refusal to enter (v.28) shows that the distance was emotional before it was physical.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, Thy brother is come; and thy father hath killed the fatted calf, because he hath received him safe and sound.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτῷ ὅτι Ὁ ἀδελφός σου ἥκει, καὶ ἔθυσεν ὁ πατήρ σου τὸν μόσχον τὸν σιτευτόν, ὅτι ὑγιαίνοντα αὐτὸν ἀπέλαβεν.
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen autō hoti Ho adelphos sou hēkei, kai ethusen ho patēr sou ton moschon ton siteuton, hoti hugiainonta auton apElaben.
verses.study_note: "Thy brother is come" (Ho adelphos sou hēkei — your brother has arrived) — the servant identifies the returned man as "thy brother," emphasizing the family relationship. "Safe and sound" (hugiainonta — in good health, whole, the same word family as "save"). The elder son's reaction will show that he does not currently regard the younger as a brother — he will refer to him as "this thy son" (v.30), refusing the fraternal relationship. The servant's simple report contains the seed of the elder son's crisis.

verses.kjv_text: And he was angry, and would not go in: therefore came his father out, and intreated him.
verses.original_text: ὠργίσθη δὲ καὶ οὐκ ἤθελεν εἰσελθεῖν. ὁ δὲ πατὴρ αὐτοῦ ἐξελθὼν παρεκάλει αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: ōrgisthē de kai ouk ēthelen eiselthein. ho de patēr autou exelthōn parekalei auton.
verses.study_note: "He was angry" (ōrgisthē — he became enraged, aorist) "and would not go in" (ouk ēthelen eiselthein — was not willing to enter) — a deliberate, willful refusal. The elder son's anger is the Pharisees' murmur translated into the parable. As the Pharisees refused to rejoice at sinners drawing near (v.2), the elder son refuses to enter the celebration of his brother's return. "His father came out" (exelthōn) — again the father takes the initiative; he went out to the prodigal; he goes out to the elder son. Grace pursues both.

verses.kjv_text: And he answering said to his father, Lo, these many years do I serve thee, neither transgressed I at any time thy commandment: and yet thou never gavest me a kid, that I might make merry with my friends:
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν τῷ πατρὶ αὐτοῦ, Ἰδοὺ τοσαῦτα ἔτη δουλεύω σοι καὶ οὐδέποτε ἐντολήν σου παρῆλθον, καὶ ἐμοὶ οὐδέποτε ἔδωκας ἔριφον ἵνα μετὰ τῶν φίλων μου εὐφρανθῶ·
verses.transliteration: ho de apokritheis eipen tō patri autou, Idou tosanta etē douleuō soi kai oudepote entolēn sou parēlthon, kai emoi oudepote edōkas eRiphon hina meta tōn philōn mou euphrantōmen;
verses.study_note: "Lo, these many years do I serve thee" (douleuō soi — I serve/slave for you; the slave-word, not the son-word!) — a devastating self-revelation: the elder son understands his relationship to the father as servitude, not sonship. He has been in the house all along but as a servant earning wages, not as a son living in love. "Never gavest me a kid" — even a small animal for his friends, not the best calf. The resentment of legal religion: meticulous obedience but no experience of the father's joy.

verses.kjv_text: But as soon as this thy son was come, which hath devoured thy living with harlots, thou hast killed for him the fatted calf.
verses.original_text: ὅτε δὲ ὁ υἱός σου οὗτος ὁ καταφαγὼν σου τὸν βίον μετὰ πορνῶν ἦλθεν, ἔθυσας αὐτῷ τὸν μόσχον τὸν σιτευτόν.
verses.transliteration: hote de ho huios sou houtos ho kataphagōn sou ton bion meta pornōn ēlthen, ethuSas autō ton moschon ton siteuton.
verses.study_note: "This thy son" (ho huios sou houtos — this son of yours) — the elder son cannot say "my brother"; he distances himself from both the prodigal and the relationship. "Which hath devoured thy living with harlots" (meta pornōn — with prostitutes) — information the father has not mentioned; the elder son assumes the worst. The word pornōn will resonate with the Pharisees who knew Luke's readers were aware of the prodigal's "riotous living" (v.13). The elder son's contempt for grace reveals his fundamental misunderstanding of the father's heart.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, Son, thou art ever with me, and all that I have is thine.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Τέκνον, σὺ πάντοτε μετ' ἐμοῦ εἶ, καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐμὰ σά ἐστιν·
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen autō, Teknon, su pantote met' emou ei, kai panta ta ema sa estin;
verses.study_note: "Son" (Teknon — child, a tender, intimate form of address) — the father does not rebuke but reassures. "Thou art ever with me" (pantote met' emou ei — you are always with me) — this is precisely the blessing the elder son has not recognized: constant access to the father's presence. "All that I have is thine" (panta ta ema sa estin — all my things are yours) — the elder son already possesses everything; he has not needed to ask because it was always his. His poverty was not material but experiential — he did not know what he had.

verses.kjv_text: It was meet that we should make merry, and be glad: for this thy brother was dead, and is alive again; and was lost, and is found.
verses.original_text: εὐφρανθῆναι δὲ καὶ χαρῆναι ἔδει, ὅτι ὁ ἀδελφός σου οὗτος νεκρὸς ἦν καὶ ἀνέζησεν, καὶ ἀπολωλὼς καὶ εὑρέθη.
verses.transliteration: euphranthenai de kai charēnai edei, hoti ho adelphos sou houtos nekros ēn kai anEzēsen, kai apolōlos kai heurethē.
verses.study_note: "It was meet" (edei — it was necessary, it was fitting, it had to be) — the celebration is not arbitrary or excessive; it is morally and theologically necessary given the facts. The father now says "this thy brother" (ho adelphos sou houtos) — deliberately using the fraternal language the elder son refused. The parable ends with the same resurrection formula as v.24: "was dead, and is alive again; was lost, and is found." The open ending — we never learn if the elder son enters — is the literary genius of the parable: the Pharisee must decide how the story ends for himself.

meta.overview: Luke 16 contains two of the most difficult and most discussed parables in the Gospels: the unjust steward (vv.1-13) and the rich man and Lazarus (vv.19-31), with a section of standalone sayings about money, law, and divorce in between (vv.14-18). The chapter is unified by its persistent concern with the relationship between wealth and eternity. The Pharisees, who "were covetous" (v.14), serve as the audience for both major units, and their sneering response to Jesus's teaching on money becomes the occasion for some of the most penetrating moral and eschatological instruction in the Gospel.
meta.theme: The parable of the unjust steward (vv.1-9) is widely acknowledged as the most difficult parable in Luke. A dishonest manager, about to be dismissed, reduces his master's debtors' bills to secure future goodwill. Remarkably, his master commends him — not for the dishonesty but for the shrewdness. Jesus draws the lesson: the "children of this world" are more shrewd in worldly matters than the "children of light" are in using wealth for kingdom purposes. The point is not to imitate the steward's dishonesty but his urgency and shrewdness in using present resources to secure a future.
meta.keyWordOriginal: The four standalone sayings (vv.10-13) apply the parable's lesson: faithfulness in small things predicts faithfulness in large; one cannot serve both God and mammon. These sayings are interrupted by the Pharisees' derision (v.14), which provokes Jesus's address about the law and the kingdom (vv.15-18): God knows the heart (v.15); the law and prophets were until John — now the kingdom is preached (v.16); the law is easier to abandon than heaven and earth (v.17); divorce is a matter of the law's integrity (v.18). These verses are contextual bridges, not isolated fragments.
meta.keyWordMeaning: The parable of the rich man and Lazarus (vv.19-31) is Luke's most extended narrative about the afterlife. A nameless rich man feasts lavishly while Lazarus — the only named character in any of Jesus's parables — begs at his gate. Both die; their positions are reversed. Lazarus rests in Abraham's bosom; the rich man is tormented in Hades. A fixed gulf prevents movement between the realms. The rich man's requests (water, then a resurrection mission to warn his brothers) are both denied. The final word is devastating: "If they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead" — pointing both to Israel's coming rejection of the risen Jesus and to the sufficiency of Scripture.
outline: οἰκονόμος (oikonomos)

verses.kjv_text: And he said also unto his disciples, There was a certain rich man, which had a steward; and the same was accused unto him that he had wasted his goods.
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν δὲ καὶ πρὸς τοὺς μαθητάς, Ἄνθρωπός τις ἦν πλούσιος ὃς εἶχεν οἰκονόμον, καὶ οὗτος διεβλήθη αὐτῷ ὡς διασκορπίζων τὰ ὑπάρχοντα αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Elegen de kai pros tous mathētas, Anthrōpos tis ēn plousios hos eichen oikonomōn, kai houtos diEblēthē autō hōs diaskorpizōn ta huparchonta autou.
verses.study_note: "Also unto his disciples" — the audience shifts from the Pharisees (chapter 15) to the disciples, though the Pharisees are still present (v.14). "A steward" (oikonomōn — estate manager, the one who manages the master's household). "Was accused" (diEblēthē — was brought as an accusation against, slandered; from diaballō, to throw across, slander) — the source of the report is unclear; the accusation may be malicious or accurate. "Wasted his goods" (diaskorpizōn — scattering, squandering; the same word used of the prodigal in 15:13) — connecting the two chapters.

verses.kjv_text: And he called him, and said unto him, How is it that I hear this of thee? give an account of thy stewardship; for thou mayest be no longer steward.
verses.original_text: καὶ φωνήσας αὐτὸν εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Τί τοῦτο ἀκούω περὶ σοῦ; ἀπόδος τὸν λόγον τῆς οἰκονομίας σου· οὐ γὰρ δύνῃ ἔτι οἰκονομεῖν.
verses.transliteration: kai phōnēsas auton eipen autō, Ti touto akouō peri sou; apodos ton logon tēs oikonomias sou; ou gar dunē eti oikonomEin.
verses.study_note: "Give an account" (apodos ton logon tēs oikonomias — render the reckoning of your stewardship) — a formal audit is demanded. "For thou mayest be no longer steward" (ou gar dunē eti oikonomEin — you can no longer manage the estate) — the dismissal is imminent but not yet final. A window of time exists, and the steward will use it with remarkable shrewdness. The parable depicts the crisis of accountability — a moment every human being will ultimately face before God.

verses.kjv_text: Then the steward said within himself, What shall I do? for my lord taketh away from me the stewardship: I cannot dig; to beg I am ashamed.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ἐν ἑαυτῷ ὁ οἰκονόμος, Τί ποιήσω, ὅτι ὁ κύριός μου ἀφαιρεῖται τὴν οἰκονομίαν ἀπ' ἐμοῦ; σκάπτειν οὐκ ἰσχύω, ἐπαιτεῖν αἰσχύνομαι.
verses.transliteration: eipen de en heautō ho oikonomos, Ti poiēsō, hoti ho kurios mou aphaireitai tēn oikonomian ap' emou; skaptein ouk ischuō, epaitein aischUnomai.
verses.study_note: "Said within himself" (en heautō — thinking within, the internal monologue cf. the prodigal in 15:17) — a moment of crisis calculation. "I cannot dig" (skaptein ouk ischuō — I lack the strength for manual labor) and "to beg I am ashamed" (epaitein aischunomai — begging would humiliate me) — two options excluded. The steward is neither physically able to labor nor willing to beg. He must find a third way. His analysis is clear-eyed and his solution will be bold.

verses.kjv_text: I am resolved what to do, that, when I am put out of the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses.
verses.original_text: ἔγνων τί ποιήσω, ἵνα, ὅταν μετασταθῶ τῆς οἰκονομίας, δέξωνταί με εἰς τοὺς οἴκους αὐτῶν.
verses.transliteration: egnōn ti poiēsō, hina, hotan metastathō tēs oikonomias, dexōntai me eis tous oikous autōn.
verses.study_note: "I am resolved" (egnōn — I know, I have decided, aorist of decisive knowledge) — the solution crystallizes in a moment. "When I am put out" (metastathō — when I am transferred, removed, dismissed) — his dismissal is certain; the question is what happens after. "They may receive me into their houses" (dexōntai me — they will welcome me) — securing a social network before the axe falls. The shrewdness is in creating future obligations from present authority before that authority expires.

verses.kjv_text: So he called every one of his lord's debtors unto him, and said unto the first, How much owest thou unto my lord?
verses.original_text: καὶ προσκαλεσάμενος ἕνα ἕκαστον τῶν χρεοφειλετῶν τοῦ κυρίου ἑαυτοῦ ἔλεγεν τῷ πρώτῳ, Πόσον ὀφείλεις τῷ κυρίῳ μου;
verses.transliteration: kai proskalesamenos hena hekaston tōn chreOpheiletōn tou kuriou heautou elegen tō prōtō, Poson opheileis tō kuriō mou?
verses.study_note: "Every one of his lord's debtors" (hena hekaston tōn chreophEiletōn — each one of the debt-holders) — a systematic operation, not a random act. These were likely tenant farmers or merchants who owed rent or lease payments to the master, perhaps denominated in produce rather than money. The steward still has authority over the estate's business records; he acts within his (expiring) legal authority to restructure debts — possibly reducing the portion that represented his own commission.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, An hundred measures of oil. And he said unto him, Take thy bill, and sit down quickly, and write fifty.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Ἑκατὸν βάτους ἐλαίου. ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Δέξαι σου τὸ γράμμα καὶ καθίσας ταχέως γράψον πεντήκοντα.
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen, Hekaton batous elaiou. ho de eipen autō, Dexai sou to gramma kai kathisas tacheōs grapsOn pentēkonta.
verses.study_note: A hundred baths (batous) of oil equaled about 875 gallons — a large quantity representing perhaps a year's production from a significant olive grove. Reducing it to fifty was a 50% reduction, worth about 500 denarii (18 months' wages). "Quickly" (tacheōs — at once, without hesitation) — the steward is acting under time pressure before his dismissal is formalized. The debtors' enthusiasm to rewrite the bills (they received a windfall) secures their future goodwill toward him.

verses.kjv_text: Then said he to another, And how much owest thou? And he said, An hundred measures of wheat. And he said unto him, Take thy bill, and write fourscore.
verses.original_text: ἔπειτα ἑτέρῳ εἶπεν, Σὺ δὲ πόσον ὀφείλεις; ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Ἑκατὸν κόρους σίτου. λέγει αὐτῷ, Δέξαι σου τὸ γράμμα καὶ γράψον ὀγδοήκοντα.
verses.transliteration: epeita heterō eipen, Su de poson opheileis; ho de eipen, Hekaton korous sitou. legei autō, Dexai sou to gramma kai grapsOn ogdoēkonta.
verses.study_note: A hundred cors (korous) of wheat equaled about 1,100 bushels — a large harvest debt. Reducing it to eighty (a 20% reduction) was still significant. The different percentage reductions (50% oil, 20% wheat) may reflect different interest rates or simply the steward's calculation of what was needed to secure each debtor's friendship. The systematic reduction of debts creates an entire social network of people indebted to the steward's generosity before his dismissal takes effect.

verses.kjv_text: And the lord commended the unjust steward, because he had done wisely: for the children of this world are in their generation wiser than the children of light.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπῄνεσεν ὁ κύριος τὸν οἰκονόμον τῆς ἀδικίας ὅτι φρονίμως ἐποίησεν· ὅτι οἱ υἱοὶ τοῦ αἰῶνος τούτου φρονιμώτεροι ὑπὲρ τοὺς υἱοὺς τοῦ φωτὸς εἰς τὴν γενεὰν τὴν ἑαυτῶν εἰσιν.
verses.transliteration: kai epēnesen ho kurios ton oikonomOn tēs adikias hoti phronimōs epoiēsen; hoti hoi huioi tou aiōnos toutou phronimōteroi huper tous huious tou phōtos eis tēn genean tēn heautōn eisin.
verses.study_note: "Commended" (epēnesen — praised, commended the wisdom, not the dishonesty) "the unjust steward" (ton oikonomon tēs adikias — the steward of unrighteousness) — Luke clarifies the steward's moral character while reporting the master's specific admiration for his shrewdness (phronimōs — prudently, wisely). "Children of this world" (huioi tou aiōnos toutou) versus "children of light" (huious tou phōtos) — the children of this age apply more practical wisdom to their earthly concerns than believers apply to kingdom concerns.

verses.kjv_text: And I say unto you, Make to yourselves friends of the mammon of unrighteousness; that, when ye fail, they may receive you into everlasting habitations.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐγὼ ὑμῖν λέγω, ποιήσατε ἑαυτοῖς φίλους ἐκ τοῦ μαμωνᾶ τῆς ἀδικίας, ἵνα ὅταν ἐκλίπῃ δέξωνται ὑμᾶς εἰς τὰς αἰωνίους σκηνάς.
verses.transliteration: kai egō humin legō, poiēsate heautois philous ek tou mamōna tēs adikias, hina hotan eklipē dexōntai humas eis tas aiōnious skēnas.
verses.study_note: "Mammon of unrighteousness" (mamōna tēs adikias — wealth of this age, tainted worldly money; Aramaic māmōnāʾ) — not money per se but the money of this fallen world system. "Make friends" — use wealth generously so that when it fails (eklipē — runs out, gives out) those you helped "may receive you into everlasting habitations" (tas aiōnious skēnas — the eternal dwellings, the tents/tabernacles of the kingdom). The point: use transient earthly wealth to invest in eternal relationships and kingdom causes.

verses.kjv_text: He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much: and he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much.
verses.original_text: ὁ πιστὸς ἐν ἐλαχίστῳ καὶ ἐν πολλῷ πιστός ἐστιν, καὶ ὁ ἐν ἐλαχίστῳ ἄδικος καὶ ἐν πολλῷ ἄδικός ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: ho pistos en elachistō kai en pollō pistos estin, kai ho en elachistō adikos kai en pollō adikos estin.
verses.study_note: A standalone proverb that draws the lesson from the parable. "Faithful in that which is least" (pistos en elachistō — trustworthy in the smallest thing) — character is consistent: the person who is faithful with small matters will be faithful with large ones; the person who cheats in small things will cheat in large ones. Money is one of the smallest tests of character (v.11: "unrighteous mammon"); how you handle it reveals what you would do with true, eternal riches.

verses.kjv_text: If therefore ye have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will commit to your trust the true riches?
verses.original_text: εἰ οὖν ἐν τῷ ἀδίκῳ μαμωνᾷ πιστοὶ οὐκ ἐγένεσθε, τὸ ἀληθινὸν τίς ὑμῖν πιστεύσει;
verses.transliteration: ei oun en tō adikō mamōna pistoi ouk egenesthe, to alēthinon tis humin pisteusei?
verses.study_note: "Unrighteous mammon" (tō adikō mamōna — the dishonest wealth, worldly money) as a test of "true riches" (to alēthinon — the genuine, real thing, a Johannine word here in Luke). If the test of earthly money is failed — through greed, hoarding, or misuse — who will entrust to that person the real, eternal resources of the kingdom? Material wealth is the proving ground for spiritual faithfulness. Generosity with money demonstrates readiness for kingdom responsibility.

verses.kjv_text: And if ye have not been faithful in that which is another man's, who shall give you that which is your own?
verses.original_text: καὶ εἰ ἐν τῷ ἀλλοτρίῳ πιστοὶ οὐκ ἐγένεσθε, τὸ ὑμέτερον τίς δώσει ὑμῖν;
verses.transliteration: kai ei en tō allotriō pistoi ouk egenesthe, to humeteron tis dōsei humin?
verses.study_note: "Another man's" (tō allotriō — belonging to another, foreign to oneself) — all earthly wealth belongs to God; we are stewards, not owners. "That which is your own" (to humeteron — what is genuinely yours) — the kingdom inheritance that believers will receive as their own (Eph 1:14; Rom 8:17). The contrast is striking: present wealth is borrowed; eternal inheritance is truly personal. Faithfulness with borrowed earthly goods qualifies the steward for ownership of eternal ones.

verses.kjv_text: No servant can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon.
verses.original_text: Οὐδεὶς οἰκέτης δύναται δυσὶ κυρίοις δουλεύειν· ἢ γὰρ τὸν ἕνα μισήσει καὶ τὸν ἕτερον ἀγαπήσει, ἢ ἑνὸς ἀνθέξεται καὶ τοῦ ἑτέρου καταφρονήσει. οὐ δύνασθε Θεῷ δουλεύειν καὶ μαμωνᾷ.
verses.transliteration: Oudeis oiketēs dunatai dusin kuriois douleuein; ē gar ton hena misēsei kai ton heteron agapēsei, ē henos anthexetai kai tou heterou kataphronēsei. ou dunasthe Theō douleuein kai mamōna.
verses.study_note: "No servant" (Oudeis oiketēs — not one household slave) can serve two masters who have competing demands. The verb "serve" (douleuein — slave-service, total loyalty) requires undivided allegiance. The two possible responses — love/hate, hold to/despise — describe not occasional preference but fundamental orientation of the will. "Ye cannot" (ou dunasthe — you are not able, it is an impossibility) to give ultimate loyalty to both God and money. This is the chapter's theological center and the definition of the Pharisees' failure (v.14).

verses.kjv_text: And the Pharisees also, who were covetous, heard all these things: and they derided him.
verses.original_text: Ἤκουον δὲ ταῦτα πάντα καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι φιλάργυροι ὑπάρχοντες, καὶ ἐξεμυκτήριζον αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: Ēkouon de tauta panta kai hoi Pharisaioi philarguroi huparchontes, kai exemuktērizon auton.
verses.study_note: "Covetous" (philarguroi — money-lovers, literally: loving silver; philos + arguros) — Luke identifies the Pharisees' characteristic sin in relation to this discourse. They "heard all these things" but their response was derision, not conviction. "Derided him" (exemuktērizon — were sneering at him, literally: turning up the nose at, contemptuous mockery) — the imperfect suggests continuous, repeated sneering. Their response to teaching about money reveals their enslavement to it; the mockery is the proof of the indictment.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Ye are they which justify yourselves before men; but God knoweth your hearts: for that which is highly esteemed among men is abomination in the sight of God.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς, Ὑμεῖς ἐστε οἱ δικαιοῦντες ἑαυτοὺς ἐνώπιον τῶν ἀνθρώπων, ὁ δὲ Θεὸς γινώσκει τὰς καρδίας ὑμῶν· ὅτι τὸ ἐν ἀνθρώποις ὑψηλὸν βδέλυγμα ἐνώπιον τοῦ Θεοῦ ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autois, Humeis este hoi dikaioUntes heautous enōpion tōn anthrōpōn, ho de Theos ginōskei tas kardias humōn; hoti to en anthrōpois hupsēlon bdelugma enōpion tou Theou estin.
verses.study_note: "Justify yourselves before men" (dikaioUntes heautous enōpion tōn anthrōpōn — declaring yourselves righteous in men's eyes, performing righteousness for human approval) — the Pharisaic system of public reputation management. "God knoweth your hearts" (Theos ginōskei tas kardias — God knows, with complete present knowledge, the hearts) — the divine counter-evaluation. "That which is highly esteemed among men is abomination" (bdelugma — an abomination, the strongest OT term for ritual defilement) — what receives the highest human honor may be the most repugnant to God. Their money and reputation are their idols.

verses.kjv_text: The law and the prophets were until John: since that time the kingdom of God is preached, and every man presseth into it.
verses.original_text: Ὁ νόμος καὶ οἱ προφῆται μέχρι Ἰωάννου· ἀπὸ τότε ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ εὐαγγελίζεται καὶ πᾶς εἰς αὐτὴν βιάζεται.
verses.transliteration: Ho nomos kai hoi prophētai mechri Iōannou; apo tote hē basileia tou Theou euangelizEtai kai pas eis autēn biAzetai.
verses.study_note: "Until John" (mechri Iōannou — up to John, as far as John) — John is the hinge between the age of the Law and Prophets and the age of the kingdom's proclamation. "Is preached" (euangelizEtai — is being gospelized, proclaimed as good news; the present tense indicates ongoing proclamation). "Every man presseth into it" (pas eis autēn biAzetai — everyone forces his way into it; biAzetai from bia, force or violence) — the kingdom's arrival is creating urgent, even violent pressing from those desperate to enter, while the Pharisees sneer from outside.

verses.kjv_text: And it is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one tittle of the law to fail.
verses.original_text: εὐκοπώτερον δέ ἐστιν τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν παρελθεῖν ἢ τοῦ νόμου μίαν κεραίαν πεσεῖν.
verses.transliteration: eukopōteron de estin ton ouranon kai tēn gēn parelthein ē tou nomou mian keraian pesein.
verses.study_note: "One tittle" (mian keraian — one keraia, the smallest stroke of a letter, the ornamental serif) — the most minimal, insignificant mark in the written Law. "Heaven and earth to pass" — the dissolution of the cosmos — is described as easier than the failure of a single tittle of the Law. This is a hyperbolic affirmation of the Law's eternal validity. In context, the Pharisees' money-love violates the Law they claim to uphold; Jesus is more faithful to the Law's demands than they are.

verses.kjv_text: Whosoever putteth away his wife, and marrieth another, committeth adultery: and whosoever marrieth her that is put away from her husband committeth adultery.
verses.original_text: Πᾶς ὁ ἀπολύων τὴν γυναῖκα αὐτοῦ καὶ γαμῶν ἑτέραν μοιχεύει, καὶ πᾶς ὁ ἀπολελυμένην ἀπὸ ἀνδρὸς γαμῶν μοιχεύει.
verses.transliteration: Pas ho apoluōn tēn gunaika autou kai gamōn heteran moicheuei, kai pas ho apolelumenēn apo andros gamōn moicheuei.
verses.study_note: A standalone saying on divorce, contextually linked to the Law's permanence (v.17). The Pharisees may have used Hillel's broad interpretation of Deut 24:1 ("any cause") as a loophole — and the Pharisees' covetousness (v.14) may have included divorcing wives to pursue wealthier matches. Jesus applies the Law's integrity to marriage: divorce and remarriage constitutes adultery (moicheuei) against the original covenant. The saying defends both the woman's dignity and the Law's intention regarding covenant faithfulness.

verses.kjv_text: There was a certain rich man, which was clothed in purple and fine linen, and fared sumptuously every day:
verses.original_text: Ἄνθρωπος δέ τις ἦν πλούσιος, καὶ ἐνεδιδύσκετο πορφύραν καὶ βύσσον εὐφραινόμενος καθ' ἡμέραν λαμπρῶς.
verses.transliteration: Anthrōpos de tis ēn plousios, kai enedidusketo porphuran kai busson euphraInomenos kath' hēmeran lamprōs.
verses.study_note: "Purple and fine linen" (porphuran kai busson) — purple (porphura) was the color of royalty, made from Murex shellfish and extraordinarily expensive; fine linen (bussos — byssus, Egyptian linen of the highest quality) was used for royal and priestly garments. He wore these daily (enedidusketo — was continuously dressed in them, imperfect). "Fared sumptuously" (euphrainomenos lamprōs — feasting brilliantly, in splendid celebration) every day — not occasional luxury but sustained, daily extravagance. His wealth is emphasized to sharpen the contrast with Lazarus.

verses.kjv_text: And there was a certain beggar named Lazarus, which was laid at his gate, full of sores,
verses.original_text: πτωχὸς δέ τις ἦν ὀνόματι Λάζαρος, ἐβέβλητο πρὸς τὸν πυλῶνα αὐτοῦ εἱλκωμένος,
verses.transliteration: ptōchos de tis ēn onomati Lazaros, ebeblēto pros ton pulōna autou heilkōmenos,
verses.study_note: "A certain beggar named Lazarus" — the only named character in any of Jesus's parables. "Lazarus" (Lazaros — a Greek form of the Hebrew Eleazar, meaning "God helps" or "God is my help") — the name is theologically significant; God is the only help for the helpless poor. "Laid" (ebeblēto — had been thrown down, placed, a past completed action; he was deposited there by others) "at his gate" — the threshold between the rich man's world and the street. "Full of sores" (heilkōmenos — covered with ulcers, festering wounds).

verses.kjv_text: And desiring to be fed with the crumbs which fell from the rich man's table: moreover the dogs came and licked his sores.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπιθυμῶν χορτασθῆναι ἀπὸ τῶν πιπτόντων ἀπὸ τῆς τραπέζης τοῦ πλουσίου· ἀλλὰ καὶ οἱ κύνες ἐρχόμενοι ἀπέλειχον τὰ ἕλκη αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai epithumōn chortasthēnai apo tōn piptontōn apo tēs trapezēs tou plousiou; alla kai hoi kunes erchomenoi apeleichon ta helkē autou.
