verses.study_note: "Desiring to be fed" (epithumōn chortasthēnai — longing intensely to be satisfied, the same longing-word used of the prodigal for pig food in 15:16) — with the crumbs (piptontōn — falling things, scraps) from the table. "The dogs came and licked his sores" (hoi kunes...apelichon ta helkē autou) — unclean animals (Gentile dogs, free-roaming scavengers) showed more attention to Lazarus than the rich man did. The dogs' licking may have been painful; there is no comfort for Lazarus in the present age.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that the beggar died, and was carried by the angels into Abraham's bosom: the rich man also died, and was buried;
verses.original_text: ἐγένετο δὲ ἀποθανεῖν τὸν πτωχὸν καὶ ἀπενεχθῆναι αὐτὸν ὑπὸ τῶν ἀγγέλων εἰς τὸν κόλπον Ἀβραάμ· ἀπέθανεν δὲ καὶ ὁ πλούσιος καὶ ἐτάφη.
verses.transliteration: egeneto de apothanein ton ptōchon kai apenechthenai auton hypo tōn angelōn eis ton kolpon Abraam; apethanen de kai ho plousios kai etaphē.
verses.study_note: Both die — death is the great equalizer. But the rich man "was buried" (etaphē) — a noted social honor; Lazarus's burial, presumably in a pauper's grave, is not mentioned. Lazarus "was carried by the angels" (apenechthenai hypo tōn angelōn — was carried away by angels) to "Abraham's bosom" (ton kolpon Abraam — the place of intimate fellowship with the patriarch, the place of honor at the heavenly banquet). The invisible has become visible; the hidden spiritual reality is now narrated openly.

verses.kjv_text: And in hell he lift up his eyes, being in torments, and seeth Abraham afar off, and Lazarus in his bosom.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐν τῷ ᾅδῃ ἐπάρας τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ, ὑπάρχων ἐν βασάνοις, ὁρᾷ Ἀβραὰμ ἀπὸ μακρόθεν καὶ Λάζαρον ἐν τοῖς κόλποις αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai en tō hadē eparas tous ophthalmous autou, huparchōn en basanois, hora Abraam apo makrothen kai Lazaron en tois kolpois autou.
verses.study_note: "Hades" (hadē — the realm of the dead, the underworld, not specifically eternal punishment but the intermediate state of the unrighteous). "Being in torments" (huparchōn en basanois — in a state of anguish, torment, the word used for testing the genuineness of metals). "Seeth Abraham afar off" (makrothen — at a great distance) — vision is preserved in the intermediate state; he recognizes Abraham and Lazarus. The one who ignored Lazarus at his gate now sees him from an infinite distance in blessing.

verses.kjv_text: And he cried and said, Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame.
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτὸς φωνήσας εἶπεν, Πάτερ Ἀβραάμ, ἐλέησόν με καὶ πέμψον Λάζαρον ἵνα βάψῃ τὸ ἄκρον τοῦ δακτύλου αὐτοῦ ὕδατος καὶ καταψύξῃ τὴν γλῶσσάν μου, ὅτι ὀδυνῶμαι ἐν τῇ φλογὶ ταύτῃ.
verses.transliteration: kai autos phōnēsas eipen, Pater Abraam, eleēson me kai pempson Lazaron hina bapsē to akron tou daktulou autou hudatos kai katahupsē tēn glōssan mou, hoti odunōmai en tē phlogI tautē.
verses.study_note: "Father Abraham" — the rich man appeals to ethnic lineage, the last refuge of covenant presumption. "Have mercy" (eleēson — the mercy-cry, the same cry of the blind man in 18:38) — but mercy extended across the fixed gulf is impossible. "The tip of his finger" — not a cup of water but the slightest possible relief. "I am tormented in this flame" (odunōmai en tē phlogI tautē — I am being continuously tortured in this flame) — the request reveals both the reality of conscious suffering and the complete reversal of earthly conditions.

verses.kjv_text: But Abraham said, Son, remember that thou in thy lifetime receivedst thy good things, and likewise Lazarus evil things: but now he is comforted, and thou art tormented.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ Ἀβραάμ, Τέκνον, μνήσθητι ὅτι ἀπέλαβες τὰ ἀγαθά σου ἐν τῇ ζωῇ σου, καὶ Λάζαρος ὁμοίως τὰ κακά· νῦν δὲ ὧδε παρακαλεῖται, σὺ δὲ ὀδυνᾶσαι.
verses.transliteration: eipen de Abraam, Teknon, mnēsthēti hoti apElabes ta agatha sou en tē zōē sou, kai Lazaros homoiōs ta kaka; nun de hōde parakaleitai, su de odunasai.
verses.study_note: "Son" (Teknon — child, a word of genuine relationship) — Abraham acknowledges the rich man as a descendant. "Remember" (mnēsthēti — an aorist imperative: fix your memory on this). "Thou receivedst thy good things" (apElabes ta agatha sou — you received back your good things, as if claiming a pre-paid account) — in life, he had his comfort; Lazarus had none. "Now" (nun) — the great reversal is in effect. The word parakEitai (is comforted) for Lazarus is the same root as Paraclete, the divine Comforter.

verses.kjv_text: And beside all this, between us and you there is a great gulf fixed: so that they which would pass from hence to you cannot; neither can they pass to us, that would come from thence.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπὶ πᾶσι τούτοις μεταξὺ ἡμῶν καὶ ὑμῶν χάσμα μέγα ἐστήρικται, ὅπως οἱ θέλοντες διαβῆναι ἔνθεν πρὸς ὑμᾶς μὴ δύνωνται, μηδὲ οἱ ἐκεῖθεν πρὸς ἡμᾶς διαπερῶσιν.
verses.transliteration: kai epi pasi toutois metaxu hēmōn kai humōn chasma mega estēriktai, hopōs hoi thelontes diabēnai enthen pros humas mē dūnōntai, mēde hoi ekeithen pros hēmas diaperasin.
verses.study_note: "A great gulf fixed" (chasma mega estēRiktai — a vast chasm has been established, set firmly; a perfect passive indicating permanent, settled reality). The gulf prevents movement in both directions: no relief can come from blessing to torment; no saint can descend to comfort the suffering. The irrevocability of post-mortem conditions is the parable's most sobering feature: every choice made in this life fixes the eternal destination. There is no purgatory, no second chance, no crossing this gulf.

verses.kjv_text: Then he said, I pray thee therefore, father, that thou wouldest send him to my father's house:
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δέ, Ἐρωτῶ σε οὖν, πάτερ, ἵνα πέμψῃς αὐτὸν εἰς τὸν οἶκον τοῦ πατρός μου,
verses.transliteration: eipen de, Erōtō se oun, pater, hina pEMpsēs auton eis ton oikon tou patros mou,
verses.study_note: The first request denied, the rich man makes a second: send Lazarus to his family as a missionary to warn them. He has five brothers still living in the same luxury and indifference. The concern for his family reveals that he is not completely hardened — love for his brothers persists. But the request is still framed as using Lazarus for his own purposes (send him — the same language he had used to treat Lazarus as a servant); even in torment, his social condescension persists.

verses.kjv_text: For I have five brethren; that he may testify unto them, lest they also come into this place of torment.
verses.original_text: ἔχω γὰρ πέντε ἀδελφούς, ὅπως διαμαρτύρηται αὐτοῖς, ἵνα μὴ καὶ αὐτοὶ ἔλθωσιν εἰς τὸν τόπον τοῦτον τῆς βασάνου.
verses.transliteration: echō gar pente adelphous, hopōs diamartUrētai autois, hina mē kai autoi elthōsin eis ton topon touton tēs basanou.
verses.study_note: "Testify unto them" (diamartUrētai — to solemnly testify, to warn with authority) — he wants a resurrection appearance as an undeniable evidence. "This place of torment" (ton topon touton tēs basanou) — he is fully aware of the nature of his condition. The request is theologically significant: he implicitly claims that he and his brothers did not have sufficient evidence to believe. Abraham's response will directly refute this claim — they have Moses and the prophets, and that is sufficient.

verses.kjv_text: Abraham saith unto him, They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them.
verses.original_text: λέγει δὲ Ἀβραάμ, Ἔχουσι Μωϋσέα καὶ τοὺς προφήτας· ἀκουσάτωσαν αὐτῶν.
verses.transliteration: legei de Abraam, Echousi Mōūsea kai tous prophētas; akousatōsan autōn.
verses.study_note: "Moses and the prophets" (Mōūsea kai tous prophētas) — the entire OT Scripture. "Let them hear them" (akousatōsan autōn — let them listen to, obey, heed them). The Scripture is declared sufficient for the knowledge of God, his moral demands, and the way of repentance. The implication is that the rich man and his brothers had this sufficient revelation and chose to ignore it. A resurrection appearance would not change hearts that have already rejected the Scripture — it would be another miracle to explain away.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Nay, father Abraham: but if one went unto them from the dead, they will repent.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Οὐχί, πάτερ Ἀβραάμ· ἀλλ' ἐάν τις ἀπὸ νεκρῶν πορευθῇ πρὸς αὐτούς, μετανοήσουσιν.
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen, Ouchi, pater Abraam; all' ean tis apo nekrōn poreuthē pros autous, metanoēsousin.
verses.study_note: "Nay, father Abraham" — the rich man disagrees with Abraham's assessment of the Scripture's sufficiency. He remains convinced that a resurrection appearance would be more effective than the written Word. "If one went unto them from the dead, they will repent" (metanoēsousin — they will change their minds completely). This is the voice of natural theology: extraordinary miracles should produce repentance. But Jesus's entire ministry of miracles has already disproved this — miracles were witnessed and rejected (see 11:29-32; John 12:37).

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, If they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ, Εἰ Μωϋσέως καὶ τῶν προφητῶν οὐκ ἀκούουσιν, οὐδὲ ἐάν τις ἐκ νεκρῶν ἀναστῇ πεισθήσονται.
verses.transliteration: eipen de autō, Ei Mōūseōs kai tōn prophētōn ouk akouousin, oude ean tis ek nekrōn anastē peisthēsontai.
verses.study_note: "If they hear not Moses and the prophets" (ouk akouousin — present tense: they are not hearing, they are currently not listening/obeying) — the issue is not lack of information but willful refusal of the revelation already given. "Neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead" (oude...anastē peisthēsontai — not even if someone rises will they be persuaded). This verse prophesies the response to Jesus's own resurrection: the Sanhedrin would bribe soldiers rather than believe (Matt 28:12-13). The heart that resists Scripture will rationalize away even resurrection.

meta.overview: Luke 17 falls into two distinct halves: a series of discipleship sayings (vv.1-19) and an extended eschatological discourse (vv.20-37). The discipleship sayings address four interconnected themes: the gravity of causing another to stumble (vv.1-2), the obligation of repeated forgiveness (vv.3-4), the nature of faith (vv.5-6), and the proper posture of service (vv.7-10). Together they form a catechesis for community life in the kingdom, focusing on the disciple's responsibilities toward fellow believers and toward God.
meta.theme: The healing of the ten lepers (vv.11-19) is one of Luke's most distinctive narratives. Ten men cry from a distance for mercy; Jesus sends them to the priests while they are still unclean — testing their faith. All ten are cleansed on the way, but only one returns to give thanks, and he is a Samaritan. Jesus's question — "Were there not ten cleansed? but where are the nine?" — is one of the most poignant in the Gospels. The Samaritan's faith and gratitude are linked to salvation (v.19: "thy faith hath made thee whole") in a way the other nine's physical healing was not.
meta.keyWordOriginal: The eschatological discourse (vv.20-37) begins with a Pharisaic question about when the kingdom comes. Jesus corrects the premise: the kingdom does not come "with observation" (meta paratērēseōs — with signs to be watched for); it is already "within you" or "among you" (entos humōn — in your midst). He then shifts to address the disciples about the final coming of the Son of Man: first a period of suffering and apparent defeat (v.25), then a sudden, unmistakable appearing like lightning (v.24).
meta.keyWordMeaning: The days of Noah and Lot provide the typological framework: ordinary life continues until sudden catastrophic judgment falls, with no warning for those not watching. "Remember Lot's wife" (v.32) — the backward glance that cost her life — is the warning against clinging to the present age when the Son of Man comes. The paradoxes of saving and losing life (v.33) and the selective taking at night (vv.34-35) emphasize that the coming will make sudden, irreversible separations. The eagles gathered around the carcass (v.37) is a proverbial saying about the location of judgment — where the dead body is, there the vultures gather.
outline: πίστις (pistis)

verses.kjv_text: Then said he unto the disciples, It is impossible but that offences will come: but woe unto him, through whom they come!
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς τοὺς μαθητάς, Ἀνένδεκτόν ἐστιν τοῦ τὰ σκάνδαλα μὴ ἐλθεῖν· πλὴν οὐαὶ δι' οὗ ἔρχεται·
verses.transliteration: Eipen de pros tous mathētas, AnEndekton estin tou ta skandala mē elthein; plēn ouai di' hou erchetai;
verses.study_note: "It is impossible" (AnEndekton — inadmissible, unthinkable, not allowable, a rare word used only here in the NT) that offences (skandala — stumbling blocks, traps, snares) will not come — fallen humanity guarantees their arrival. "But woe" (plēn ouai — nevertheless, woe) to the individual through whom they come. The inevitability of temptation does not diminish individual culpability. The universal reality of sin's presence does not excuse the personal responsibility of those who introduce it.

verses.kjv_text: It were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and he cast into the sea, than that he should offend one of these little ones.
verses.original_text: λυσιτελεῖ αὐτῷ εἰ λίθος μυλικὸς περίκειται περὶ τὸν τράχηλον αὐτοῦ καὶ ἔρριπται εἰς τὴν θάλασσαν, ἢ ἵνα σκανδαλίσῃ τῶν μικρῶν τούτων ἕνα.
verses.transliteration: lusitelei autō ei lithos mulikos perikeitai peri ton trachēlon autou kai erriptai eis tēn thalassan, ē hina skandalisē tōn mikrōn toutōn hena.
verses.study_note: "It were better" (lusitelei — it profits more, it is more advantageous) — a striking commercial metaphor: being drowned in the sea with a millstone is a better deal than the eschatological cost of causing a little one to stumble. "A millstone" (lithos mulikos — a large grinding stone, turned by a donkey) was massive — certainly lethal if used as a necklace in water. "Little ones" (mikrōn) — probably new or weak believers, the spiritually vulnerable within the community. Their protection is a primary community obligation.

verses.kjv_text: Take heed to yourselves: If thy brother trespass against thee, rebuke him; and if he repent, forgive him.
verses.original_text: προσέχετε ἑαυτοῖς. ἐὰν ἁμάρτῃ ὁ ἀδελφός σου, ἐπιτίμησον αὐτῷ, καὶ ἐὰν μετανοήσῃ, ἄφες αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: prosechete heautois. ean hamartē ho adelphos sou, epitimēson autō, kai ean metanoēsē, aphes autō.
verses.study_note: "Take heed to yourselves" (prosechete heautois — watch out for yourselves, pay attention to your own souls) before attending to the brother. "Rebuke him" (epitimēson — rebuke, admonish, correct; the word used for rebuking demons and storms) — the rebuke is an act of love, not condemnation. "If he repent" (ean metanoEsē — if he changes his mind and direction) "forgive him" (aphes autō — release him, grant forgiveness). The conditional "if he repent" qualifies the forgiveness — forgiveness requires repentance, unlike God's prevenient grace.

verses.kjv_text: And if he trespass against thee seven times in a day, and seven times in a day turn again to thee, saying, I repent; thou shalt forgive him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐὰν ἑπτάκις τῆς ἡμέρας ἁμαρτήσῃ εἰς σέ, καὶ ἑπτάκις ἐπιστρέψῃ πρὸς σέ, λέγων, Μετανοῶ, ἀφήσεις αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: kai ean heptakis tēs hēmeras hamartēsē eis se, kai heptakis epistrepsē pros se, legōn, Metanoō, aphēseis autō.
verses.study_note: "Seven times in a day" — a number of completeness emphasizing an extreme case. Each time the brother turns (epistrepsē — returns, reverses direction) and says "I repent" (Metanoō — I am repenting, present tense), "thou shalt forgive him" (aphēseis — you will release, future tense as a command). The forgiveness is not for the unrepentant but matches each act of repentance. The demand is severe: seven offenses, seven returns, seven forgivenesses — all in one day. This is the economy of the kingdom, not natural human patience.

verses.kjv_text: And the apostles said unto the Lord, Lord, increase our faith.
verses.original_text: Καὶ εἶπαν οἱ ἀπόστολοι τῷ Κυρίῳ, Πρόσθες ἡμῖν πίστιν.
verses.transliteration: Kai eipan hoi apostoloi tō Kuriō, Prosthes hēmin pistin.
verses.study_note: The apostles' response to the demands of vv.1-4 is a prayer for more faith — they feel inadequate for what has just been required. "Increase our faith" (Prosthes hēmin pistin — add to us faith, give us more faith, prosthes is the aorist imperative of prostithēmi — to add to). The request implies they have some faith but feel it insufficient for the demands of community forgiveness and the avoidance of causing stumbling. Jesus's answer will reframe the question: it is not about the quantity of faith but about the object.

verses.kjv_text: And the Lord said, If ye had faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye might say unto this sycamine tree, Be thou plucked up by the root, and be thou planted in the sea; and it should obey you.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ Κύριος, Εἰ ἔχετε πίστιν ὡς κόκκον σινάπεως, ἐλέγετε ἂν τῇ συκαμίνῳ ταύτῃ, Ἐκριζώθητι καὶ φυτεύθητι ἐν τῇ θαλάσσῃ· καὶ ὑπήκουσεν ἂν ὑμῖν.
verses.transliteration: eipen de ho Kurios, Ei echete pistin hōs kokkon sinapeōs, elegete an tē sukaminō tautē, Ekrizōthēti kai phutEuthēti en tē thalassē; kai hupēkousen an humin.
verses.study_note: "A grain of mustard seed" — the proverbially smallest seed represents genuine (even tiny) faith. The "sycamine tree" (sukaminō — the black mulberry tree, noted for its extremely deep, extensive root system, the most difficult tree to uproot) would obey even the smallest real faith. The point is not arithmetic — more faith moves bigger trees — but that the disciples already have sufficient faith. The problem is not the quantity of their faith but their failure to exercise what they have. True faith, however small, accomplishes what God has willed.

verses.kjv_text: But which of you, having a servant plowing or feeding cattle, will say unto him by and by, when he is come from the field, Go and sit down to meat?
verses.original_text: Τίς δὲ ἐξ ὑμῶν δοῦλον ἔχων ἀροτριῶντα ἢ ποιμαίνοντα, ὃς εἰσελθόντι ἐκ τοῦ ἀγροῦ ἐρεῖ αὐτῷ, Εὐθέως παρελθὼν ἀνάπεσε;
verses.transliteration: Tis de ex humōn doulon echōn arotriōnta ē poimainonta, hos eiselthonti ek tou agrou erei autō, Eutheōs parelthōn anapese?
verses.study_note: A new parable addressing the disciples' self-congratulatory attitudes after obedient service. The rhetorical question (Tis ex humōn — which of you) expects the answer "no one" — no master immediately rewards a returning field slave with dinner. "Go and sit down to meat" (parelthōn anapese — come in and recline, take your place as a guest) would be absurd before the master's own needs are met. The parable establishes that discipleship obligations are not fulfilled by one's own satisfaction but by the master's.

verses.kjv_text: And will not rather say unto him, Make ready wherewith I may sup, and gird thyself, and serve me, till I have eaten and drunken; and afterward thou shalt eat and drink?
verses.original_text: ἀλλ' οὐχὶ ἐρεῖ αὐτῷ, Ἑτοίμασον τί δειπνήσω, καὶ περιζωσάμενος διακόνει μοι ἕως φάγω καὶ πίω, καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα φάγεσαι καὶ πίεσαι σύ;
verses.transliteration: all' ouchi erei autō, Hetoimason ti deipnēsō, kai perizōsamenos diakonei moi heōs phagō kai piō, kai meta tauta phagesai kai piesai su?
verses.study_note: "Gird thyself, and serve me" (perizōsamenos diakonei moi — put on your apron and wait on me, serve as a table servant) — the servant's work does not end when the field work ends; it transitions from one form of service to another. "Afterward thou shalt eat and drink" — the servant's own satisfaction comes after the master's. Jesus himself modeled this at the Last Supper (22:27: "I am among you as he that serveth"), but the pattern is established here as the norm for kingdom service.

verses.kjv_text: Doth he thank that servant because he did the things that were commanded him? I trow not.
verses.original_text: μὴ ἔχει χάριν τῷ δούλῳ ἐκείνῳ ὅτι ἐποίησεν τὰ διαταχθέντα; οὐ δοκῶ.
verses.transliteration: mē echei charin tō doulō ekeinō hoti epoiEsen ta diatachthenta; ou dokō.
verses.study_note: "Doth he thank that servant" (mē echei charin — does he have grace/gratitude for that slave, does he owe him thanks?) — the expected answer is "no." A slave who does what is commanded earns no special gratitude; he has merely fulfilled his obligation. "I trow not" (ou dokō — I think not) — a colloquial Lucan aside. The point: obedient disciples should not expect special commendation for fulfilling basic duties. The mentality of merit and reward is inappropriate for those who understand their servant status.

verses.kjv_text: So likewise ye, when ye shall have done all those things which are commanded you, say, We are unprofitable servants: we have done that which was our duty to do.
verses.original_text: οὕτως καὶ ὑμεῖς, ὅταν ποιήσητε πάντα τὰ διαταχθέντα ὑμῖν, λέγετε ὅτι Δοῦλοι ἀχρεῖοί ἐσμεν, ὅτι ὃ ὠφείλομεν ποιῆσαι πεποιήκαμεν.
verses.transliteration: houtōs kai humeis, hotan poiēsēte panta ta diatachthenta humin, legete hoti Douloi achreIoi esmen, hoti ho ōpheilomen poiēsai pepoiēkamen.
verses.study_note: "Unprofitable servants" (douloi achreIoi — useless slaves, literally: without benefit or advantage) — not a word of self-condemnation but of realistic assessment: we have not put God in our debt. "We have done that which was our duty to do" (ho ōpheilomen poiēsai pepoiēkamen — what we were obligated to do we have done). All obedience is the fulfillment of pre-existing obligation; it creates no surplus credit account with God. This statement guards against the Pharisaic assumption that accumulated good deeds merit divine reward.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as he went to Jerusalem, that he passed through the midst of Samaria and Galilee.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ πορεύεσθαι εἰς Ἰερουσαλήμ, καὶ αὐτὸς διήρχετο διὰ μέσον Σαμαρείας καὶ Γαλιλαίας.
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en tō poreuesthai eis Ierousalēm, kai autos diērcheto dia meson Samareias kai Galilaias.
verses.study_note: A geographical transition reminder (cf. 9:51; 13:22): the journey to Jerusalem continues. "Through the midst of Samaria and Galilee" — the border region between the two territories, a liminal zone where social categories blurred. The setting is precisely where Jews and Samaritans lived in closest proximity and deepest tension — the ideal setting for a story about a Samaritan's exemplary response that would challenge Jewish assumptions about insiders and outsiders.

verses.kjv_text: And as he entered into a certain village, there met him ten men that were lepers, which stood afar off:
verses.original_text: καὶ εἰσερχομένου αὐτοῦ εἴς τινα κώμην, ἀπήντησαν αὐτῷ δέκα λεπροὶ ἄνδρες, οἳ ἔστησαν πόρρωθεν,
verses.transliteration: kai eiserchomEnou autou eis tina kōmēn, apēntēsan autō deka leproi andres, hoi estēsan porrōthen,
verses.study_note: "Ten lepers" (deka leproi) — a group, mixed in ethnicity (one Samaritan, presumably nine Jews), united by their common outcaste status. Leprosy (a general term for various skin diseases) required social isolation under the Law (Lev 13:45-46). "Stood afar off" (estēsan porrōthen — stood at a distance, as the Law required) — maintaining the prescribed separation even in their desperate appeal. The group's shared suffering created a community across ethnic lines that would not have existed under normal social conditions.

verses.kjv_text: And they lifted up their voices, and said, Jesus, Master, have mercy on us.
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτοὶ ἦραν φωνὴν λέγοντες, Ἰησοῦ ἐπιστάτα, ἐλέησον ἡμᾶς.
verses.transliteration: kai autoi ēran phōnēn legontes, Iēsou epistata, eleēson hēmas.
verses.study_note: "Master" (epistata — superintendent, overseer; Luke's equivalent of Mark's Rabbi, used by the disciples) — a title of authority and respect. "Have mercy on us" (eleēson hēmas — the mercy-cry, used also by the blind beggar in 18:38-39) — they appeal not to merit but to mercy. The plural "us" — the group appeals together, perhaps the only time a group of people so diverse in ethnicity prayed together. Their shared helplessness produces common prayer, a pattern that transcends social divisions.

verses.kjv_text: And when he saw them, he said unto them, Go shew yourselves unto the priests. And it came to pass, that, as they went, they were cleansed.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδὼν εἶπεν αὐτοῖς, Πορευθέντες ἐπιδείξατε ἑαυτοὺς τοῖς ἱερεῦσιν. καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ ὑπάγειν αὐτοὺς ἐκαθαρίσθησαν.
verses.transliteration: kai idōn eipen autois, Poreuthentes epideixate heautous tois hiereusin. kai egeneto en tō hupagein autous ekatharisthēsan.
verses.study_note: "Go shew yourselves unto the priests" (epideixate heautous tois hiereusin) — the priestly examination was the required certification of cleansing (Lev 14:1-32). They are sent while still unclean — an act of faith required before the healing is visible. "As they went, they were cleansed" (en tō hupagein autous ekatharisthēsan — while they were going, in the process of obeying, they were cleansed) — obedient movement in faith preceded visible cleansing. This is a pattern of Lucan faith: response to the word before the evidence arrives.

verses.kjv_text: And one of them, when he saw that he was healed, turned back, and with a loud voice glorified God,
verses.original_text: εἷς δὲ ἐξ αὐτῶν, ἰδὼν ὅτι ἰάθη, ὑπέστρεψεν μετὰ φωνῆς μεγάλης δοξάζων τὸν Θεόν,
verses.transliteration: heis de ex autōn, idōn hoti iathē, hupestrepsen meta phōnēs megalēs doxazōn ton Theon,
verses.study_note: "One of them" (heis de ex autōn) — the singular standing out from the nine. "When he saw that he was healed" (idōn hoti iathē) — the recognition of healing precedes the return. "Turned back" (hupestrepsen — returned, reversed course) — against the direction of the priest-journey, choosing relationship over regulation, gratitude over procedure. "With a loud voice glorified God" (meta phōnēs megalēs doxazōn ton Theon) — the praise is public, vocal, unreserved. He does not go quietly back; he shouts his thanksgiving.

verses.kjv_text: And fell down on his face at his feet, giving him thanks: and he was a Samaritan.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔπεσεν ἐπὶ πρόσωπον παρὰ τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ εὐχαριστῶν αὐτῷ· καὶ αὐτὸς ἦν Σαμαρίτης.
verses.transliteration: kai epesen epi prosōpon para tous podas autou eucharistōn autō; kai autos ēn Samaritēs.
verses.study_note: "Fell down on his face" (epesen epi prosōpon — prostrated himself) — the posture of worship. "At his feet" (para tous podas autou) — the posture of ultimate reverence (cf. 7:38; 8:41; John 11:32). "Giving thanks" (eucharistōn — offering eucharist, thanksgiving, the root of the Eucharist). The identity is revealed last, for maximum narrative impact: "he was a Samaritan" (Samaritēs) — the theological outsider, the half-breed, the one who had the least reason (by Jewish reckoning) to come to Jesus's feet.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answering said, Were there not ten cleansed? but where are the nine?
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν, Οὐχὶ οἱ δέκα ἐκαθαρίσθησαν; οἱ δὲ ἐννέα ποῦ;
verses.transliteration: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen, Ouchi hoi deka ekatharisthēsan; hoi de ennea pou?
verses.study_note: "Were there not ten cleansed?" (Ouchi hoi deka ekatharisthēsan — were not the ten cleansed?) — the passive divine healing is affirmed. "But where are the nine?" (hoi de ennea pou) — one of the most poignant questions in the Gospels, expressing not merely the surprise at ingratitude but genuine grief over missed encounter. All ten were healed; only one returned to give thanks and receive the fullness of what healing could mean. The nine went on to their appointment with the priests, their religious duty, their restored social status — but not to Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: There are not found that returned to give glory to God, save this stranger.
verses.original_text: οὐχ εὑρέθησαν ὑποστρέψαντες δοῦναι δόξαν τῷ Θεῷ εἰ μὴ ὁ ἀλλογενὴς οὗτος;
verses.transliteration: ouch heurethēsan hupostrepsantes dounai doxan tō Theō ei mē ho allogenēs houtos?
verses.study_note: "Not found" (ouch heurethēsan — they were not found, not discovered) — a passive suggesting Jesus was looking, expecting. "To give glory to God" (dounai doxan tō Theō) — the OT phrase for acknowledging God's righteous action (Josh 7:19; John 9:24). "Save this stranger" (ei mē ho allogEnēs houtos — except this foreigner, this one of another race) — allogEnēs (from allos, other + genos, race/kind) is a stronger word than Samaritēs. The foreigner of another race gives glory to God while the covenant insiders proceed with their religious appointments.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, Arise, go thy way: thy faith hath made thee whole.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Ἀναστὰς πορεύου· ἡ πίστις σου σέσωκέν σε.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autō, Anastas poreuou; hē pistis sou sesōken se.
verses.study_note: "Thy faith hath made thee whole" (hē pistis sou sesōken se — your faith has saved you, the perfect tense: has saved and the salvation stands). The same formula as 7:50 (the sinful woman) and 8:48 (the hemorrhaging woman). The nine received physical healing; the Samaritan receives something more — sōtēria, salvation, wholeness of the entire person. The word sōzō encompasses both healing and salvation. His return, prostration, and thanksgiving were not the means of salvation but the evidence that saving faith was present in him, unlike in the nine who were healed but not transformed.

verses.kjv_text: And when he was demanded of the Pharisees, when the kingdom of God should come, he answered them and said, The kingdom of God cometh not with observation:
verses.original_text: Ἐπερωτηθεὶς δὲ ὑπὸ τῶν Φαρισαίων πότε ἔρχεται ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ, ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς καὶ εἶπεν, Οὐκ ἔρχεται ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ μετὰ παρατηρήσεως,
verses.transliteration: Eperōtētheis de hupo tōn Pharisaiōn pote erchetai hē basileia tou Theou, apekrithē autois kai eipen, Ouk erchetai hē basileia tou Theou meta paratērēseōs,
verses.study_note: "Demanded" (eperōtētheis — was questioned, the passive suggests the Pharisees' initiative) — a technical theological question about eschatological timing, perhaps designed to discredit Jesus if his answer conflicted with popular expectation. "Not with observation" (meta paratērēseōs — with careful watching for signs, with astronomical observation, with the scrutiny of those searching for visible portents) — the kingdom does not arrive as a visible, predictable, observable phenomenon that can be tracked.

verses.kjv_text: Neither shall they say, Lo here! or, lo there! for, behold, the kingdom of God is within you.
verses.original_text: οὐδὲ ἐροῦσιν, Ἰδοὺ ὧδε ἤ, Ἐκεῖ ἰδού· ἰδοὺ γὰρ ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ ἐντὸς ὑμῶν ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: oude erousin, Idou hōde ē, Ekei idou; idou gar hē basileia tou Theou entos humōn estin.
verses.study_note: "Within you" or "in your midst" (entos humōn — entos can mean either "inside" as an interior location or "in your midst" as a present, tangible reality among people). Given the Pharisaic audience, "in your midst" (among you) is more likely — Jesus himself is the kingdom's embodiment standing in their presence. The kingdom is not coming at some distant future point to be tracked on a prophetic chart; it has already arrived in the person and ministry of Jesus, and they are failing to recognize it.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto the disciples, The days will come, when ye shall desire to see one of the days of the Son of man, and ye shall not see it.
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς τοὺς μαθητάς, Ἐλεύσονται ἡμέραι ὅτε ἐπιθυμήσετε μίαν τῶν ἡμερῶν τοῦ Υἱοῦ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἰδεῖν, καὶ οὐκ ὄψεσθε.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de pros tous mathētas, Eleusontai hēmerai hote epithumēsete mian tōn hēmerōn tou Huiou tou anthrōpou idein, kai ouk opsesthe.
verses.study_note: Jesus shifts from the Pharisees to the disciples — the eschatological discourse proper. "The days will come" — a transition to prophetic future. "Desire to see one of the days of the Son of man" — during the period of persecution and suffering that will follow Jesus's departure, disciples will long for even one day of the kingdom's visible manifestation (as during the Galilean ministry). "And ye shall not see it" — the era between Jesus's ascension and return will be characterized by longing without fulfillment.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall say to you, See here; or, see there: go not after them, nor follow them.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐροῦσιν ὑμῖν, Ἰδοὺ ἐκεῖ, ἤ, Ἰδοὺ ὧδε· μὴ ἀπέλθητε μηδὲ διώξητε.
verses.transliteration: kai erousin humin, Idou ekei, ē, Idou hōde; mē apelthēte mēde diōxēte.
verses.study_note: "See here; or, see there" — false messiahs and apocalyptic enthusiasts who claim to know the location of the kingdom's next manifestation. "Go not after them, nor follow them" (mē apelthēte mēde diōxēte — do not go away and do not pursue) — a double prohibition against following eschatological fads. The warning is prophylactic: the disciples will encounter many false "here is the kingdom" announcements and must resist the natural desire to follow any claim of special revelation about the time and place of the parousia.

verses.kjv_text: For as the lightning, that lighteneth out of the one part under heaven, shineth unto the other part under heaven; so shall also the Son of man be in his day.
verses.original_text: ὥσπερ γὰρ ἡ ἀστραπὴ ἀστράπτουσα ἐκ τῆς ὑπ' οὐρανὸν εἰς τὴν ὑπ' οὐρανὸν λάμπει, οὕτως ἔσται ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: hōsper gar hē astrapē astraptOusa ek tēs hyp' ouranon eis tēn hyp' ouranon lampei, houtōs estai ho Huios tou anthrōpou en tē hēmera autou.
verses.study_note: "As the lightning" (hōsper gar hē astrapē — just as the lightning flash) — lightning cannot be missed, predicted, or located in advance; it simply blazes across the entire sky instantaneously. "From one part under heaven...unto the other" — hemisphere to hemisphere visibility, universal and unmistakable. The return of the Son of Man will be equally obvious: no one will need to be told "look here" or "look there" (v.23). Anyone who claims the kingdom has arrived secretly, in a hidden location, has already falsified it by that very claim.

verses.kjv_text: But first must he suffer many things, and be rejected of this generation.
verses.original_text: πρῶτον δὲ δεῖ αὐτὸν πολλὰ παθεῖν καὶ ἀποδοκιμασθῆναι ἀπὸ τῆς γενεᾶς ταύτης.
verses.transliteration: prōton de dei auton polla pathein kai apodokimasthēnai apo tēs geneas tautēs.
verses.study_note: "First must he suffer" (prōton de dei auton polla pathein — first it is necessary for him to suffer many things) — the cross precedes the parousia. "Must" (dei — divine necessity, the same word used throughout Luke for Jesus's redemptive destiny) — the suffering is not accidental but essential. "Rejected of this generation" (apodokimasthēnai — declared unfit after testing, as a stone rejected by builders, Ps 118:22) — the cross is not a deviation from the kingdom plan but the foundation of it.

verses.kjv_text: And as it was in the days of Noe, so shall it be also in the days of the Son of man.
verses.original_text: καὶ καθὼς ἐγένετο ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις Νῶε, οὕτως ἔσται καὶ ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις τοῦ Υἱοῦ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου·
verses.transliteration: kai kathōs egeneto en tais hēmerais Nōe, houtōs estai kai en tais hēmerais tou Huiou tou anthrōpou;
verses.study_note: "As it was in the days of Noah" — the Flood typology for the parousia is drawn from Jewish apocalyptic tradition (1 Enoch 106-107) and Genesis 6-8. The analogy is not the wickedness of Noah's day (though that existed) but the ordinariness of life up to the moment of judgment. People were eating, drinking, marrying — not necessarily sinful activities — but utterly unaware of the approaching catastrophe. The parousia will similarly interrupt normal life without gradual warning.

verses.kjv_text: They did eat, they drank, they married wives, they were given in marriage, until the day that Noe entered into the ark, and the flood came, and destroyed them all.
verses.original_text: ἤσθιον, ἔπινον, ἐγάμουν, ἐγαμίζοντο, ἄχρι ἧς ἡμέρας εἰσῆλθεν Νῶε εἰς τὴν κιβωτόν, καὶ ἦλθεν ὁ κατακλυσμὸς καὶ ἀπώλεσεν ἅπαντας.
verses.transliteration: ēsthion, epinon, egamoun, egamizonto, achri hēs hēmeras eisēlthen Nōe eis tēn kibōton, kai ēlthen ho kataklUsmos kai apōlesen hapantas.
verses.study_note: The four imperfect verbs — were eating, were drinking, were marrying, were being given in marriage — describe habitual, continuous, ordinary activity. The aorist "the flood came" (ēlthen ho kataklUsmos) marks the sudden rupture of the ordinary. "And destroyed them all" (apōlesen hapantas — utterly destroyed all) — the comprehensive judgment. The key point: there was no progressive series of warnings that would have allowed those not in the ark to prepare at the last moment. Noah entered the ark; the door closed; the flood came.

verses.kjv_text: Likewise also as it was in the days of Lot; they did eat, they drank, they bought, they sold, they planted, they builded;
verses.original_text: ὁμοίως καθὼς ἐγένετο ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις Λώτ· ἤσθιον, ἔπινον, ἠγόραζον, ἐπώλουν, ἐφύτευον, ᾠκοδόμουν·
verses.transliteration: homoiōs kathōs egeneto en tais hēmerais Lōt; ēsthion, epinon, ēgorazon, epōloun, ephuteuon, ōkodOmoun;
verses.study_note: The Lot typology (Gen 18-19) expands the picture of normal life: six activities — eating, drinking, buying, selling, planting, building — a comprehensive economic picture of a thriving city. Sodom was prosperous and commercially active right up to the moment of its destruction. The list of activities is longer than Noah's list, emphasizing the greater apparent normalcy and stability of Sodom on the eve of its judgment. Prosperity can be a lull that blinds to approaching catastrophe.

verses.kjv_text: But the same day that Lot went out of Sodom it rained fire and brimstone from heaven, and destroyed them all.
verses.original_text: ᾗ δὲ ἡμέρᾳ ἐξῆλθεν Λὼτ ἀπὸ Σοδόμων, ἔβρεξεν πῦρ καὶ θεῖον ἀπ' οὐρανοῦ, καὶ ἀπώλεσεν ἅπαντας.
verses.transliteration: hē de hēmera exēlthen Lōt apo Sodomōn, ebrexen pur kai theion ap' ouranou, kai apōlesen hapantas.
verses.study_note: "The same day" (hē de hēmera) — the day of Lot's departure is the day of Sodom's destruction; the two events are simultaneous. "Fire and brimstone from heaven" (pur kai theion ap' ouranou — the divine judgment of Genesis 19:24) — a supernatural catastrophe with no natural analog. "Destroyed them all" (apōlesen hapantas) — again complete destruction. The pattern repeats: removal of the righteous remnant coincides precisely with the onset of judgment on those who remain.

verses.kjv_text: Even thus shall it be in the day when the Son of man is revealed.
verses.original_text: κατὰ τὰ αὐτὰ ἔσται ᾗ ἡμέρᾳ ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἀποκαλύπτεται.
verses.transliteration: kata ta auta estai hē hēmera ho Huios tou anthrōpou apokaluptetai.
verses.study_note: "Even thus shall it be" (kata ta auta estai — it will be exactly like this) — the Noah and Lot typologies are not mere illustrations but structural templates for the parousia. "When the Son of man is revealed" (apokaluptetai — is unveiled, apocalypsed; the theological word for the eschatological disclosure) — the revelation of the Son of Man will share the suddenness, the ordinariness of surrounding life, and the comprehensive judgment of both Flood and Sodom. No gradual warning; no transition period; no last-minute escape for the unprepared.

verses.kjv_text: In that day, he which shall be upon the housetop, and his stuff in the house, let him not come down to take it away: and he that is in the field, let him likewise not return back.
verses.original_text: ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ὃς ἔσται ἐπὶ τοῦ δώματος καὶ τὰ σκεύη αὐτοῦ ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ, μὴ καταβάτω ἆραι αὐτά, καὶ ὁ ἐν ἀγρῷ ὁμοίως μὴ ἐπιστρεψάτω εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω.
verses.transliteration: en ekeinē tē hēmera hos estai epi tou dōmatos kai ta skeuē autou en tē oikia, mē katabatō arai auta, kai ho en agrō homoiōs mē epistrepsatō eis ta opisō.
verses.study_note: "In that day" — referring primarily to AD 70's fall of Jerusalem but also typologically to the final parousia. "Upon the housetop" — the flat Palestinian roof used for storage and leisure. "Let him not come down to take it away" — no time for the collection of possessions. The flight must be immediate. The principle: at the moment of crisis, no material possession is worth the time required to retrieve it. Attachment to things in the moment of eschatological urgency is spiritually fatal.

verses.kjv_text: Remember Lot's wife.
verses.original_text: μνημονεύετε τῆς γυναικὸς Λώτ.
verses.transliteration: mnēmoneuete tēs gunaikos Lōt.
verses.study_note: The shortest command in the Gospels — three words in Greek — yet among the most charged. Lot's wife "looked back" (Gen 19:26) when fleeing Sodom and became a pillar of salt. The backward glance cost her life. The warning is not about nostalgia but about divided allegiance at the moment of decisive departure: turning back toward what is being left behind (the old life, earthly attachments) when the moment demands complete forward movement. "Remember" (mnēmoneuete — keep remembering, present imperative) — this is a standing order for disciples throughout the age.

verses.kjv_text: Whosoever shall seek to save his life shall lose it; and whosoever shall lose his life shall preserve it.
verses.original_text: ὃς ἐὰν ζητήσῃ τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ σῶσαι, ἀπολέσει αὐτήν, καὶ ὃς ἂν ἀπολέσει, ζῳογονήσει αὐτήν.
verses.transliteration: hos ean zētēsē tēn psuchēn autou sōsai, apOlEsei autēn, kai hos an apolEsei, zōogonēsei autēn.
verses.study_note: "Seek to save his life" (zētēsē tēn psuchēn autou sōsai — who tries to preserve his own soul/life) "shall lose it" (apOlEsei — will destroy it). The paradox is repeated elsewhere (9:24; Matt 10:39; 16:25; Mark 8:35; John 12:25). In the context of the parousia: clinging to present life at the cost of kingdom allegiance results in ultimate loss. "Preserve it" (zōogonēsei — will give it life, will preserve it alive; the same word used of preserving animals alive, Gen 6:19 LXX) — the life lost for Jesus is the life truly found.

verses.kjv_text: I tell you, in that night there shall be two men in one bed; the one shall be taken, and the other shall be left.
verses.original_text: λέγω ὑμῖν, ταύτῃ τῇ νυκτὶ ἔσονται δύο ἐπὶ κλίνης μιᾶς, ὁ εἷς παραλημφθήσεται καὶ ὁ ἕτερος ἀφεθήσεται.
verses.transliteration: legō humin, tautē tē nukti esontai duo epi klinēs mias, ho heis paralēmphthēsetai kai ho heteros aphethēsetai.
verses.study_note: "In that night" — the parousia will occur across different time zones simultaneously (some in bed at night, v.34; some grinding at daytime, v.35). "Shall be taken" (paralēmphthēsetai — will be taken alongside, received) — the taken one is received into blessing (cf. 9:28 where Jesus "takes" the three to the transfiguration); "left" (aphEthēsetai — will be left behind, released) — left to judgment. The intimacy of the pairs (same bed, same millstone) emphasizes that the division is not social or geographic but spiritual.

verses.kjv_text: Two women shall be grinding together; the one shall be taken, and the other left.
verses.original_text: ἔσονται δύο ἀλήθουσαι ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτό, ἡ μία παραλημφθήσεται καὶ ἡ ἑτέρα ἀφεθήσεται.
verses.transliteration: esontai duo alēthousai epi to auto, hē mia paralēmphthēsetai kai hē hetera aphEthēsetai.
verses.study_note: Women grinding grain together (alēthousai — present participle: grinding, the standard morning domestic activity) in the same physical location doing the same work will be divided. The selection is not by skill, activity, or social proximity but by the invisible reality of faith and allegiance. The pairing of bed (night) and grinding mill (morning activity) covers both the nocturnal and diurnal hours, indicating simultaneity across the globe. The same principle applies across every common context of shared human activity.

verses.kjv_text: Two men shall be in the field; the one shall be taken, and the other left.
verses.original_text: δύο ἔσονται ἐν τῷ ἀγρῷ, ὁ εἷς παραλημφθήσεται καὶ ὁ ἕτερος ἀφεθήσεται.
verses.transliteration: duo esontai en tō agrō, ho heis paralēmphthēsetai kai ho heteros aphEthēsetai.
verses.study_note: Present in the Textus Receptus and KJV, this verse extends the series of paired separations from the domestic (bed, grinding mill) to the agricultural field. Two men engaged in identical outdoor labour will be sundered at the parousia by the invisible criterion of allegiance to the Son of Man. "Shall be taken" (paralēmphthēsetai — received, taken alongside) and "left" (aphEthēsetai — released, left behind) — the same verbs as vv.34-35, reinforcing the irrevocable and instantaneous nature of the separation. No common occupation, location, or shared experience shields against the coming division.

verses.kjv_text: And they answered and said unto him, Where, Lord? And he said unto them, Wheresoever the body is, thither will the eagles be gathered together.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθέντες λέγουσιν αὐτῷ, Ποῦ, Κύριε; ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς, Ὅπου τὸ σῶμα, ἐκεῖ καὶ οἱ ἀετοὶ ἐπισυναχθήσονται.
verses.transliteration: kai apokrithentes legousin autō, Pou, Kurie; ho de eipen autois, Hopou to sōma, ekei kai hoi aetoi episunachthēsontai.
verses.study_note: "Where, Lord?" (Pou, Kurie) — the disciples ask about the location of the taking. Jesus refuses to give a map and instead gives a proverb: "where the body is, there the eagles will be gathered." Eagles (or vultures — aetoi can mean either) gather instinctively where the carcass is. Applied: when the Son of Man appears, his people will be drawn to him as naturally and inevitably as carrion birds to a carcass. The "where" of the parousia cannot be predetermined; it will be as self-evident as vultures over a carcass — unmistakable to all.

meta.overview: Luke 18 opens with two parables on prayer that bracket a sharp contrast between presumptuous and humble approaches to God (vv.1-14). The parable of the Persistent Widow (vv.1-8) teaches that God's elect must pray persistently and not lose heart, with the implied argument from lesser to greater: if an unjust judge yields to persistent request, how much more will the just God answer his own? The parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector (vv.9-14) then exposes the theological catastrophe of self-righteous prayer versus the saving power of humble confession.
meta.theme: The central section (vv.15-30) brings together three interconnected episodes on the conditions of kingdom entry: children as the paradigm of receptive faith (vv.15-17), the rich young ruler whose attachment to wealth reveals the idol beneath his religious compliance (vv.18-25), and the disciples' bewilderment — answered by Jesus's declaration that what is impossible for humans is possible for God and rewarded by his promise of hundredfold return to those who have genuinely left all (vv.26-30). These three episodes form a progressive exploration of what it means to "receive the kingdom of God as a little child" (v.17).
meta.keyWordOriginal: The third passion prediction (vv.31-34) is the most detailed in Luke's Gospel, explicitly naming the Gentiles, the mocking, the spitting, the scourging, and the resurrection on the third day. Yet Luke adds a remarkable editorial note: "they understood none of these things: and this saying was hid from them, neither knew they the things which were spoken" (v.34). The disciples' incomprehension is not intellectual failure but divine veiling — God purposefully withholds understanding until resurrection light makes the cross interpretable.
meta.keyWordMeaning: The chapter concludes with the healing of Blind Bartimaeus (vv.35-43; cf. Mark 10:46-52), who is identified only as "a certain blind man" in Luke's account. His persistent cry — "Jesus, thou Son of David, have mercy on me" (v.38) — echoes the widow's persistence, the tax collector's humble plea, and the lepers' mercy-cry of chapter 17. The crowd's attempt to silence him mirrors the disciples' attempt to exclude the children (v.15). Jesus stops for him, restores his sight, and the man follows him "glorifying God" (v.43) — becoming a model of the response to salvation that the rich ruler failed to make.
outline: ἐκλεκτός (eklektos)

verses.kjv_text: And he spake a parable unto them to this end, that men ought always to pray, and not to faint;
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν δὲ παραβολὴν αὐτοῖς πρὸς τὸ δεῖν πάντοτε προσεύχεσθαι αὐτοὺς καὶ μὴ ἐγκακεῖν,
verses.transliteration: Elegen de parabolēn autois pros to dein pantote proseuchesthai autous kai mē enkakein,
verses.study_note: Luke provides an unusually explicit statement of a parable's purpose before telling it — rare in the Synoptics. "To this end" (pros to — introducing the purpose clause). "Ought always to pray" (dein pantote proseuchesthai — it is necessary, continually, to pray; dein is the same divine necessity word used for the cross in 9:22) — persistent prayer is not optional but structurally essential to kingdom life. "Not to faint" (mē enkakein — not to grow weary, not to give up, not to become cowardly; the word has a military connotation of deserting one's post). The parable addresses the danger of prayerlessness born of apparent divine silence.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, There was in a city a judge, which feared not God, neither regarded man:
verses.original_text: λέγων, Κριτής τις ἦν ἔν τινι πόλει τὸν Θεὸν μὴ φοβούμενος καὶ ἄνθρωπον μὴ ἐντρεπόμενος.
verses.transliteration: legōn, Kritēs tis ēn en tini polei ton Theon mē phoboumenos kai anthrōpon mē entrepomenos.
verses.study_note: "Feared not God, neither regarded man" (ton Theon mē phoboumenos kai anthrōpon mē entrepomenos) — a double negative portrait of a judge with no vertical or horizontal accountability. "Feared not God" removes divine accountability; "neither regarded man" (mē entrepomenos — showed no shame before, was not put to shame by) removes social accountability. Such a judge is the worst possible arbiter of justice — his decisions rest entirely on personal interest. The parable's power comes from this judge being the lowest possible baseline: if even he yields, how much more God?

verses.kjv_text: And there was a widow in that city; and she came unto him, saying, Avenge me of mine adversary.
verses.original_text: χήρα δὲ ἦν ἐν τῇ πόλει ἐκείνῃ, καὶ ἤρχετο πρὸς αὐτὸν λέγουσα, Ἐκδίκησόν με ἀπὸ τοῦ ἀντιδίκου μου.
verses.transliteration: chēra de ēn en tē polei ekeinē, kai ērcheto pros auton legousa, Ekdikēson me apo tou antidikou mou.
verses.study_note: "A widow" (chēra) — in the ancient world, widows had no independent legal standing and no male advocate; they were the most legally vulnerable members of society (cf. Isa 1:17; Jas 1:27). "She came unto him" (ērcheto — imperfect tense: she kept coming, repeatedly coming) — her persistence is embedded in the verb tense before the parable even states it. "Avenge me" (Ekdikēson me — give me justice, vindicate me, execute the right on my behalf; ek + dikē, justice out from the situation) — a legal demand for justice from the only authority available to her.

verses.kjv_text: And he would not for a while: but afterward he said within himself, Though I fear not God, nor regard man;
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐκ ἤθελεν ἐπὶ χρόνον· μετὰ δὲ ταῦτα εἶπεν ἐν ἑαυτῷ, Εἰ καὶ τὸν Θεὸν οὐ φοβοῦμαι καὶ ἄνθρωπον οὐκ ἐντρέπομαι,
verses.transliteration: kai ouk ēthelen epi chronon; meta de tauta eipen en heautō, Ei kai ton Theon ou phoboumai kai anthrōpon ouk entrepomai,
verses.study_note: "He would not for a while" (ouk ēthelen epi chronon — he was not willing for a time, he kept refusing) — the delay mirrors the apparent silence of God in the face of his people's prayers. "Said within himself" (eipen en heautō — a Lucan soliloquy device, also in 12:17; 15:17; 16:3) — the reader is given privileged access to the judge's interior reasoning. He openly acknowledges his own character: "Though I fear not God, nor regard man" — the self-description is repeated verbatim from v.2, making the judge himself agree with the narrator's assessment.

verses.kjv_text: Yet because this widow troubleth me, I will avenge her, lest by her continual coming she weary me.
verses.original_text: διά γε τὸ παρέχειν μοι κόπον τὴν χήραν ταύτην, ἐκδικήσω αὐτήν, ἵνα μὴ εἰς τέλος ἐρχομένη ὑπωπιάζῃ με.
verses.transliteration: dia ge to parechein moi kopon tēn chēran tautēn, ekdikēsō autēn, hina mē eis telos erchomenē hupōpiazē me.
verses.study_note: "Troubleth me" (parechein moi kopon — causing me trouble, wearing me out, giving me weariness) — the widow has become more inconvenient than ruling against her. "Lest by her continual coming she weary me" (hupōpiazē — literally: to strike under the eye, to give a black eye; figuratively: to wear out, exhaust, beat down). This vivid word is used in 1 Cor 9:27 for Paul's bodily discipline. The judge fears being metaphorically "blackened" by the widow's relentless petitioning — a hyperbolic image of how persistence overcomes even the most obdurate resistance.

verses.kjv_text: And the Lord said, Hear what the unjust judge saith.
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ ὁ Κύριος, Ἀκούσατε τί ὁ κριτὴς τῆς ἀδικίας λέγει·
verses.transliteration: Eipen de ho Kurios, Akousate ti ho kritēs tēs adikias legei;
verses.study_note: "The unjust judge" (ho kritēs tēs adikias — the judge of unrighteousness, the unrighteous judge) — the genitive construction (judge of unrighteousness) is a Semitic idiom for "the thoroughly unrighteous judge," echoing the similar construction in 16:8 ("the steward of unrighteousness"). Jesus calls attention to the punch of the parable: the argument from the worst case to the best case. If the most corrupt possible judge yields to persistence, the conclusion about God follows with logical force.

verses.kjv_text: And shall not God avenge his own elect, which cry day and night unto him, though he bear long with them?
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Θεὸς οὐ μὴ ποιήσῃ τὴν ἐκδίκησιν τῶν ἐκλεκτῶν αὐτοῦ τῶν βοώντων αὐτῷ ἡμέρας καὶ νυκτός, καὶ μακροθυμεῖ ἐπ' αὐτοῖς;
verses.transliteration: ho de Theos ou mē poiēsē tēn ekdikēsin tōn eklektōn autou tōn boōntōn autō hēmeras kai nuktos, kai makrothumei ep' autois?
verses.study_note: "His own elect" (tōn eklektōn autou — of his chosen ones) — God's relationship to his people is not the indifferent aloofness of the unjust judge but the covenant bond of election. "Cry day and night" (boōntōn — crying out, shouting, the word used for loud distress cries; hēmeras kai nuktos — by day and by night, a merism for continuous prayer). "Though he bear long with them" (makrothumei — is long-suffering, patient; the question may mean either "though he seems slow to act" or "while he exercises patience with the enemies of the elect"). The patience of God is not indifference but purposeful restraint.

verses.kjv_text: I tell you that he will avenge them speedily. Nevertheless when the Son of man cometh, shall he find faith on the earth?
verses.original_text: λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι ποιήσει τὴν ἐκδίκησιν αὐτῶν ἐν τάχει. πλὴν ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐλθὼν ἄρα εὑρήσει τὴν πίστιν ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς;
verses.transliteration: legō humin hoti poiēsei tēn ekdikēsin autōn en tachei. plēn ho Huios tou anthrōpou elthōn ara heurēsei tēn pistin epi tēs gēs?
verses.study_note: "Speedily" (en tachei — with swiftness, quickly; not meaning immediately but with the swiftness characteristic of God's action once he moves). "Nevertheless" (plēn — but, however, the strong adversative) — a surprising pivot. The question "shall he find faith on the earth?" (heurēsei tēn pistin — will he find the faith, with definite article: that specific faith of persistent, humble crying out) introduces a note of eschatological uncertainty. The parable's concern is not whether God will act but whether the elect will maintain the persistent, humble, dependent prayer that characterizes true faith until he does.

verses.kjv_text: And he spake this parable unto certain which trusted in themselves that they were righteous, and despised others:
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ καὶ πρός τινας τοὺς πεποιθότας ἐφ' ἑαυτοῖς ὅτι εἰσὶν δίκαιοι καὶ ἐξουθενοῦντας τοὺς λοιπούς, τὴν παραβολὴν ταύτην·
verses.transliteration: Eipen de kai pros tinas tous pepoithotas eph' heautois hoti eisin dikaioi kai exouthenountas tous loipous, tēn parabolēn tautēn;
verses.study_note: Luke again provides explicit audience identification before the parable. "Which trusted in themselves" (tous pepoithotas eph' heautois — those who had put their confidence in themselves, perfect participle: a settled state of self-trust) "that they were righteous" (hoti eisin dikaioi — that they were righteous, dikaioi in the judicial sense of being in right standing). "Despised others" (exouthenountas — holding as nothing, treating with contempt; ex + outhen, from nothing — to count as nothing). Self-righteousness and contempt are inseparable: one's elevation requires others' diminishment.

verses.kjv_text: Two men went up into the temple to pray; the one a Pharisee, and the other a publican.
verses.original_text: Ἄνθρωποι δύο ἀνέβησαν εἰς τὸ ἱερὸν προσεύξασθαι, ὁ εἷς Φαρισαῖος καὶ ὁ ἕτερος τελώνης.
verses.transliteration: Anthrōpoi duo anebēsan eis to hieron proseuxasthai, ho heis Pharisaios kai ho heteros telōnēs.
verses.study_note: "Went up" (anebēsan — ascended, as the Temple Mount required physical ascent) "into the temple to pray" — both men come for the same stated purpose. The contrast is between a Pharisee (the most religiously admired class) and a tax collector (telōnēs — the most socially despised, associated with Rome and financial corruption). The listener's initial sympathy is entirely with the Pharisee; the parable will invert those sympathies. The parable works by refusing to confirm the audience's prior religious categories.

verses.kjv_text: The Pharisee stood and prayed thus with himself, God, I thank thee, that I am not as other men are, extortioners, unjust, adulterers, or even as this publican.
verses.original_text: ὁ Φαρισαῖος σταθεὶς πρὸς ἑαυτὸν ταῦτα προσηύχετο, Ὁ Θεός, εὐχαριστῶ σοί, ὅτι οὐκ εἰμὶ ὥσπερ οἱ λοιποὶ τῶν ἀνθρώπων, ἅρπαγες, ἄδικοι, μοιχοί, ἢ καὶ ὡς οὗτος ὁ τελώνης·
verses.transliteration: ho Pharisaios statheis pros heauton tauta prosēucheto, Ho Theos, eucharistō soi, hoti ouk eimi hōsper hoi loipoi tōn anthrōpōn, harpages, adikoi, moichoi, ē kai hōs houtos ho telōnēs;
verses.study_note: "Stood and prayed thus with himself" (statheis pros heauton — having stood, toward himself) — the telling phrase: the Pharisee is praying toward himself rather than toward God, despite the address. "I thank thee" (eucharistō soi) — the prayer begins with thanksgiving, a proper form, but the content reveals the problem: the thanks is for personal superiority. "Not as other men" — the prayer defines righteousness negatively through contrast. Three categories (extortioners, unjust, adulterers) and one specific comparison (this tax collector) make up the entire theological content of his prayer; God is addressed but not genuinely approached.

verses.kjv_text: I fast twice in the week, I give tithes of all that I possess.
verses.original_text: νηστεύω δὶς τοῦ σαββάτου, ἀποδεκατῶ πάντα ὅσα κτῶμαι.
verses.transliteration: nēsteuō dis tou sabbatou, apodekatō panta hosa ktōmai.
verses.study_note: "Twice in the week" (dis tou sabbatou — twice of the Sabbath week, Mondays and Thursdays according to Jewish custom) — the Law required fasting only once a year on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:29-31); twice-weekly fasting was a supererogatory piety. "Tithes of all that I possess" (apodekatō panta — I tithe everything I acquire; the Law required tithing of agricultural produce, not every acquisition) — again, exceeding legal requirements. The Pharisee's prayer rehearses genuine religious achievements, which makes the parable's verdict more striking: these achievements do not produce justification.

verses.kjv_text: And the publican, standing afar off, would not lift up so much as his eyes unto heaven, but smote upon his breast, saying, God be merciful to me a sinner.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ τελώνης μακρόθεν ἑστὼς οὐκ ἤθελεν οὐδὲ τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς ἐπᾶραι εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν, ἀλλ' ἔτυπτεν τὸ στῆθος αὐτοῦ λέγων, Ὁ Θεός, ἱλάσθητί μοι τῷ ἁμαρτωλῷ.
verses.transliteration: ho de telōnēs makrothen hestōs ouk ēthelen oude tous ophthalmous eparai eis ton ouranon, all' etupen to stēthos autou legōn, Ho Theos, hilasthēti moi tō hamartōlō.
verses.study_note: "Standing afar off" (makrothen hestōs — having stood at a distance) — the posture of unworthiness, echoing the lepers of 17:12. "Would not lift up so much as his eyes unto heaven" — the refusal to assume the customary prayer posture of raised eyes (cf. Ps 123:1) expresses shame. "Smote upon his breast" (etupen to stēthos — beat his chest, the gesture of grief and self-reproach, used at the crucifixion in 23:48). "God be merciful to me a sinner" (hilasthēti moi tō hamartōlō — be propitiated to me, be graciously appeased toward me; the sinner, with the definite article, identifying himself as the sinner) — hilaskomai is the word for atonement, used in Heb 2:17. His prayer is a cry for propitiation, not performance.

verses.kjv_text: I tell you, this man went down to his house justified rather than the other: for every one that exalteth himself shall be humbled; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted.
verses.original_text: λέγω ὑμῖν, κατέβη οὗτος δεδικαιωμένος εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ παρ' ἐκεῖνον· ὅτι πᾶς ὁ ὑψῶν ἑαυτὸν ταπεινωθήσεται, ὁ δὲ ταπεινῶν ἑαυτὸν ὑψωθήσεται.
verses.transliteration: legō humin, katebē houtos dedikaiōmenos eis ton oikon autou par' ekeinon; hoti pas ho hupsōn heauton tapeinōthēsetai, ho de tapeinōn heauton hupsōthēsetai.
verses.study_note: "This man went down...justified" (katebē houtos dedikaiōmenos — this one descended having been declared righteous, a perfect passive participle indicating a completed forensic act). "Rather than the other" (par' ekeinon — alongside that one, in contrast to that one) — the Pharisee descends unjustified despite his genuine religious achievements. "Justified" (dedikaiōmenos) is a forensic term from the law court: declared righteous in God's sight, acquitted. The tax collector's prayer was directed not at his own record but at God's mercy; that orientation is what the doctrine of justification requires. The concluding aphorism (also in 14:11) is the interpretive key.

verses.kjv_text: And they brought unto him also infants, that he would touch them: but when his disciples saw it, they rebuked them.
verses.original_text: Προσέφερον δὲ αὐτῷ καὶ τὰ βρέφη ἵνα αὐτῶν ἅπτηται· ἰδόντες δὲ οἱ μαθηταὶ ἐπετίμων αὐτοῖς.
verses.transliteration: Prosepheron de autō kai ta brephē hina autōn haptētai; idontes de hoi mathētai epetimōn autois.
verses.study_note: "Infants" (brephē — newborns, infants, a more specific word than paidia/children used in v.16-17) — Luke's term emphasizes the absolute helplessness of those brought. "That he would touch them" (hina autōn haptētai — that he would touch them, lay hands on them, a gesture of blessing) — the parents desire blessing for the most vulnerable. "His disciples saw it, they rebuked them" (epetimōn — they were rebuking, imperfect: repeatedly rebuking, the same word used for rebuking storms and demons) — the disciples' rebuke reveals their misunderstanding of kingdom priorities: the kingdom belongs precisely to those who are dependent and seemingly unproductive.

verses.kjv_text: But Jesus called them unto him, and said, Suffer little children to come unto me, and forbid them not: for of such is the kingdom of God.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς προσεκαλέσατο αὐτά, λέγων, Ἄφετε τὰ παιδία ἔρχεσθαι πρός με καὶ μὴ κωλύετε αὐτά· τῶν γὰρ τοιούτων ἐστὶν ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: ho de Iēsous prosekalEsato auta, legōn, Aphete ta paidia erchesthai pros me kai mē kōluete auta; tōn gar toioutōn estin hē basileia tou Theou.
verses.study_note: "Called them unto him" (prosekalEsato — summoned them to himself, a deliberate counteraction to the disciples' exclusion). "Suffer...come unto me, and forbid them not" (Aphete...kai mē kōluete — allow...and stop forbidding; two imperatives, the second being a present imperative of prohibition indicating the disciples must stop an ongoing action). "Of such is the kingdom of God" (tōn gar toioutōn estin — of such ones, of those like this, the kingdom belongs) — not because of childhood innocence (a romantic notion Luke does not teach) but because of the child's characteristic dependence, receptivity, and inability to earn anything.

verses.kjv_text: Verily I say unto you, Whosoever shall not receive the kingdom of God as a little child shall in no wise enter therein.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν, ὃς ἐὰν μὴ δέξηται τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ Θεοῦ ὡς παιδίον, οὐ μὴ εἰσέλθῃ εἰς αὐτήν.
verses.transliteration: amēn legō humin, hos ean mē dexētai tēn basileian tou Theou hōs paidion, ou mē eiselthē eis autēn.
verses.study_note: "Shall in no wise enter therein" (ou mē eiselthē — the double negative, strongest possible negation in Greek) — the absolute exclusion of those who do not receive the kingdom as a child. "As a little child" (hōs paidion) — the manner of reception, not the recipient's age. The child's manner of receiving is the pattern: empty-handed, dependent, without negotiation or merit. The immediately following episode of the rich young ruler (vv.18-25) illustrates what the opposite of receiving as a child looks like — coming with achievements, assets, and conditions.

verses.kjv_text: And a certain ruler asked him, saying, Good Master, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐπηρώτησέν τις αὐτὸν ἄρχων λέγων, Διδάσκαλε ἀγαθέ, τί ποιήσας ζωὴν αἰώνιον κληρονομήσω;
verses.transliteration: Kai epērōtēsen tis auton archōn legōn, Didaskale agathe, ti poiēsas zōēn aiōnion klēronomēsō?
verses.study_note: "A certain ruler" (archōn — a ruler, a person of authority; perhaps a synagogue ruler or a member of the Sanhedrin; Mark identifies him as young, Matthew identifies him as young and wealthy) — a contrast to the infants of v.15, this man comes with status and religious credentials. "Good Master" (Didaskale agathe — Good Teacher; the address is unusual, as teachers were not typically called "good" as an honorific) — the flattery is perhaps genuine. "What shall I do to inherit eternal life?" (ti poiēsas — what having done; the question frames eternal life as an achievement, the opposite of receiving as a child).

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said unto him, Why callest thou me good? none is good, save one, that is, God.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς, Τί με λέγεις ἀγαθόν; οὐδεὶς ἀγαθὸς εἰ μὴ εἷς ὁ Θεός.
verses.transliteration: eipen de autō ho Iēsous, Ti me legeis agathon? oudeis agathos ei mē heis ho Theos.
verses.study_note: "Why callest thou me good?" (Ti me legeis agathon — why do you call me good?) — Jesus does not reject the address but interrogates it, forcing the ruler to consider its implications. "None is good, save one, that is, God" (oudeis agathos ei mē heis ho Theos) — if "good" is a divine attribute, then calling Jesus good requires either recognizing Jesus as God or recalibrating the address. Jesus is not denying his own goodness but pressing the ruler: do you know who you are speaking to? The question is an indirect Christological claim — if goodness belongs to God alone, and you call me good, what does that make me?

verses.kjv_text: Thou knowest the commandments, Do not commit adultery, Do not kill, Do not steal, Do not bear false witness, Honour thy father and thy mother.
verses.original_text: τὰς ἐντολὰς οἶδας, Μὴ μοιχεύσῃς, Μὴ φονεύσῃς, Μὴ κλέψῃς, Μὴ ψευδομαρτυρήσῃς, Τίμα τὸν πατέρα σου καὶ τὴν μητέρα σου.
verses.transliteration: tas entolas oidas, Mē moicheusēs, Mē phoneusēs, Mē klepsēs, Mē pseudomarturēsēs, Tima ton patera sou kai tēn mētera sou.
verses.study_note: Jesus directs the ruler to the commandments — specifically the second table (Decalogue vv.6-10), the social commandments governing relationships with other humans. Notably, the first table (commandments concerning God) is not listed — perhaps pointing toward what will be revealed as the ruler's actual deficiency: the idolatry of wealth (v.22). "Honour thy father and thy mother" is placed last rather than first (as in the Decalogue), perhaps because it represents the principle of giving to others what is due to them — the same principle at issue with the ruler's possessions.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, All these have I kept from my youth up.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Ταῦτα πάντα ἐφύλαξα ἐκ νεότητός μου.
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen, Tauta panta ephulaxa ek neotētos mou.
verses.study_note: "All these have I kept" (panta ephulaxa — all I have guarded, the aorist suggesting a lifetime of observance) "from my youth up" (ek neotētos — from childhood) — a remarkable claim, but not dismissible as obviously false. The man is sincere and his claim may be largely accurate at the level of external compliance. His very sincerity makes the coming diagnosis more devastating: external legal compliance, even genuine, does not touch the interior disorder of a soul whose ultimate allegiance is to its own wealth. Jesus does not contest his self-assessment; instead he reveals what the commandments have not reached.

verses.kjv_text: Now when Jesus heard these things, he said unto him, Yet lackest thou one thing: sell all that thou hast, and distribute unto the poor, and thou shalt have treasure in heaven: and come, follow me.
verses.original_text: ἀκούσας δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Ἔτι ἕν σοι λείπει· πάντα ὅσα ἔχεις πώλησον καὶ διάδος πτωχοῖς, καὶ ἕξεις θησαυρὸν ἐν οὐρανῷ, καὶ δεῦρο ἀκολούθει μοι.
verses.transliteration: akousas de ho Iēsous eipen autō, Eti hen soi leipei; panta hosa echeis pōlēson kai diadōs ptōchois, kai hexeis thēsauron en ouranō, kai deuro akolouthei moi.
verses.study_note: "Yet lackest thou one thing" (Eti hen soi leipei — still one thing is lacking for you) — one deficiency beneath all the compliance. "Sell all" (panta...pōlēson — sell everything; the universal scope is deliberate) — Jesus is not issuing a general command for all disciples (Zacchaeus was not required to sell all in 19:8) but a personal diagnostic: this man's specific idol is his wealth. "Distribute unto the poor" (diadōs ptōchois — give it through [distribute] to the poor) — not one gift but a distribution. "Treasure in heaven" offers the exchange: earthly treasure for heavenly. "Come, follow me" — the invitation is to personal discipleship, the same given to the Twelve.

verses.kjv_text: And when he heard this, he was very sorrowful: for he was very rich.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἀκούσας ταῦτα περίλυπος ἐγενήθη· ἦν γὰρ πλούσιος σφόδρα.
verses.transliteration: ho de akousas tauta perilupos egenēthē; ēn gar plousios sphodra.
verses.study_note: "Very sorrowful" (perilupos — surrounded by grief, deeply grieved; peri = around/thoroughly + lupē = grief; the same word used of Jesus in Gethsemane, Mark 14:34) — genuine emotional distress, not contempt for Jesus. "For he was very rich" (ēn gar plousios sphodra — for he was extremely wealthy) — the explanation is placed at the end for maximum emphasis. His sorrow is real; his attachment to wealth is also real. He cannot have both. The tragedy is that his grief is genuine yet insufficient to produce action; he leaves (v.23) without the invitation to follow being accepted.

verses.kjv_text: And when Jesus saw that he was very sorrowful, he said, How hardly shall they that have riches enter into the kingdom of God!
verses.original_text: Ἰδὼν δὲ αὐτὸν ὁ Ἰησοῦς περίλυπον γενόμενον εἶπεν, Πῶς δυσκόλως οἱ τὰ χρήματα ἔχοντες εἰς τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ Θεοῦ εἰσπορεύονται·
verses.transliteration: Idōn de auton ho Iēsous perilupon genomenon eipen, Pōs duskolōs hoi ta chrēmata echontes eis tēn basileian tou Theou eisporeuontai;
verses.study_note: "How hardly" (Pōs duskolōs — how with difficulty; duskolos means hard to please, fastidious, then difficult; a rare adverb) — the adverb is emphatic. "They that have riches" (hoi ta chrēmata echontes — those having goods, the wealthy) — Jesus makes a general statement from the specific case. The wealth itself is not the problem (Abraham was wealthy) but the idolatrous attachment to it that displaces God. When wealth becomes one's functional security and identity, it becomes a god, and no person can serve two masters (16:13).

verses.kjv_text: For it is easier for a camel to go through a needle's eye, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God.
verses.original_text: εὐκοπώτερον γάρ ἐστιν κάμηλον διὰ τρήματος βελόνης εἰσελθεῖν ἢ πλούσιον εἰς τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ Θεοῦ εἰσελθεῖν.
verses.transliteration: eukopōteron gar estin kamēlon dia trēmatos belonēs eiselthein ē plousión eis tēn basileian tou Theou eiselthein.
verses.study_note: "A camel through a needle's eye" (kamēlon dia trēmatos belonēs — camel through the hole of a needle; belonē is a sewing needle, trēma is its eye) — a humorous hyperbole, the largest common animal through the smallest common hole. The interpretive tradition that this refers to a small gate called "the Needle's Eye" is medieval and unattested in ancient sources; the saying is a deliberate impossibility. The point is that wealth-attachment creates an impossibility for kingdom entry. The disciples' shocked response in v.26 confirms they understood the saying literally.

verses.kjv_text: And they that heard it said, Who then can be saved?
verses.original_text: Εἶπαν δὲ οἱ ἀκούσαντες, Καὶ τίς δύναται σωθῆναι;
verses.transliteration: Eipan de hoi akousantes, Kai tis dunatai sōthēnai?
verses.study_note: "Who then can be saved?" (Kai tis dunatai sōthēnai — and who is able to be saved?) — the disciples' question betrays their assumption that wealth was a sign of divine favor (a common Jewish belief rooted in Deut 28 blessings). If even the wealthy cannot be saved, the disciples reason, the less wealthy have even less hope. The question is despairing: the disciples have heard a statement about rich men and extrapolated it into a statement about everyone. Jesus's answer will not narrowly contradict them but will reframe the question entirely.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, The things which are impossible with men are possible with God.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Τὰ ἀδύνατα παρὰ ἀνθρώποις δυνατὰ παρὰ τῷ Θεῷ ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen, Ta adunata para anthrōpois dunata para tō Theō estin.
verses.study_note: "The things which are impossible with men" (ta adunata para anthrōpois — the impossible things beside/from the side of men) "are possible with God" (dunata para tō Theō — possible beside/from the side of God). The impossibility of the rich man's entry is not a final verdict but a statement about human impossibility met by divine possibility. The salvation of any person — rich or poor — is impossible as a human achievement and possible only as a divine act. This is not merely comfort but a theological statement: salvation is entirely a work of God. The parable of the Pharisee and tax collector (vv.9-14) reaches the same conclusion: justification is God's declarative act, not human accumulation.

verses.kjv_text: Then Peter said, Lo, we have left all, and followed thee.
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ ὁ Πέτρος, Ἰδοὺ ἡμεῖς ἀφήκαμεν τὰ ἴδια καὶ ἠκολουθήσαμέν σοι.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de ho Petros, Idou hēmeis aphēkamen ta idia kai ēkolouthēsamen soi.
verses.study_note: "Lo, we have left all" (Idou hēmeis aphēkamen ta idia — Look, we have left our own things) — Peter's statement is implicitly a question: what about us? We did what the ruler refused to do. "Ta idia" (our own things, our personal possessions) — not as comprehensive as the rich man's wealth, but genuinely sacrificial: fishing boats, family income, homes. Peter is not boasting but asking: does our sacrifice qualify us for what the ruler forfeited? The disciples have met the external condition; Jesus will describe the internal reward.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Verily I say unto you, There is no man that hath left house, or parents, or brethren, or wife, or children, for the kingdom of God's sake,
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς, Ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι οὐδείς ἐστιν ὃς ἀφῆκεν οἰκίαν ἢ γονεῖς ἢ ἀδελφοὺς ἢ γυναῖκα ἢ τέκνα ἕνεκεν τῆς βασιλείας τοῦ Θεοῦ,
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen autois, Amēn legō humin hoti oudeis estin hos aphēken oikian ē goneis ē adelphous ē gunaika ē tekna heneken tēs basileias tou Theou,
verses.study_note: "Amēn" — the solemn affirmation introducing a promise. The list comprehensively covers the primary attachments of life: home, parents, siblings, wife, children — all the relationships and securities that constitute a person's world. "For the kingdom of God's sake" (heneken tēs basileias tou Theou) — the motivation determines whether the leaving is genuine sacrifice or mere social rupture. The kingdom itself, and loyalty to Jesus its king, must be the reason for the leaving.

verses.kjv_text: Who shall not receive manifold more in this present time, and in the world to come life everlasting.
verses.original_text: ὃς οὐχὶ μὴ ἀπολάβῃ πολλαπλασίονα ἐν τῷ καιρῷ τούτῳ, καὶ ἐν τῷ αἰῶνι τῷ ἐρχομένῳ ζωὴν αἰώνιον.
verses.transliteration: hos ouchi mē apolabē pollaplasiona en tō kairō toutō, kai en tō aiōni tō erchomenō zōēn aiōnion.
verses.study_note: "Manifold more in this present time" (pollaplasiona — many times more, manifold; en tō kairō toutō — in this season, this present age) — the reward is not deferred entirely to eternity but begins in the community of the church: new family (brothers, sisters, mothers) in the kingdom community. "In the world to come life everlasting" (en tō aiōni tō erchomenō zōēn aiōnion — in the coming age, eternal life) — the ultimate eschatological reward. The juxtaposition of present and future reward addresses the ruler's attachment: what is left behind is not lost but multiplied in a different mode.

verses.kjv_text: Then he took unto him the twelve, and said unto them, Behold, we go up to Jerusalem, and all things that are written by the prophets concerning the Son of man shall be accomplished.
verses.original_text: Παραλαβὼν δὲ τοὺς δώδεκα εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς, Ἰδοὺ ἀναβαίνομεν εἰς Ἰερουσαλήμ, καὶ τελεσθήσεται πάντα τὰ γεγραμμένα διὰ τῶν προφητῶν τῷ Υἱῷ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου·
verses.transliteration: Paralabōn de tous dōdeka eipen pros autous, Idou anabainomen eis Ierousalēm, kai telesthēsetai panta ta gegrammena dia tōn prophētōn tō Huiō tou anthrōpou;
verses.study_note: "Took unto him the twelve" (Paralabōn tous dōdeka — having taken the Twelve alongside, a private disclosure) — the passion prediction is given to the inner circle only, not to the crowds. "All things that are written by the prophets" (panta ta gegrammena dia tōn prophētōn — all the things having been written through the prophets) — the coming events are not surprises but the fulfillment of a written script. "Shall be accomplished" (telesthēsetai — will be completed, fulfilled, brought to their telos) — the divine necessity of the cross is anchored in prophetic Scripture.
