verses.kjv_text: For he shall be delivered unto the Gentiles, and shall be mocked, and spitefully entreated, and spitted on:
verses.original_text: παραδοθήσεται γὰρ τοῖς ἔθνεσιν καὶ ἐμπαιχθήσεται καὶ ὑβρισθήσεται καὶ ἐμπτυσθήσεται,
verses.transliteration: paradothēsetai gar tois ethnesin kai empaichthēsetai kai hubristhēsetai kai emptusthēsetai,
verses.study_note: "Delivered unto the Gentiles" (paradothēsetai tois ethnesin — handed over to the nations, the Gentiles) — this detail is unique to Luke in this passion prediction (cf. Isa 53:12: "poured out his soul unto death...and he was numbered with the transgressors"). "Mocked" (empaichthēsetai), "spitefully entreated" (hubristhēsetai — treated with insolent contempt, violated), "spitted on" (emptusthēsetai — spat upon) — three passive verbs of humiliation, echoing the Servant Songs. The specificity of the prediction (Gentiles, mocking, spitting, scourging, v.33) is extraordinary.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall scourge him, and put him to death: and the third day he shall rise again.
verses.original_text: καὶ μαστιγώσαντες ἀποκτενοῦσιν αὐτόν, καὶ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ ἀναστήσεται.
verses.transliteration: kai mastigōsantes apoktenousin auton, kai tē hēmera tē tritē anastēsetai.
verses.study_note: "Scourge him" (mastigōsantes — having flogged, the Roman flagellum, a brutal pre-crucifixion punishment) "put him to death" (apoktenousin — will kill him) — the passion is predicted explicitly. "The third day he shall rise again" (tē hēmera tē tritē anastēsetai — on the third day he will rise up) — the resurrection is integral to the prediction, not added as an afterthought. The disciples' inability to understand (v.34) means the resurrection promise offers them no comfort at this point; it will only become comprehensible in resurrection light.

verses.kjv_text: And they understood none of these things: and this saying was hid from them, neither knew they the things which were spoken.
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτοὶ οὐδὲν τούτων συνῆκαν, καὶ ἦν τὸ ῥῆμα τοῦτο κεκρυμμένον ἀπ' αὐτῶν, καὶ οὐκ ἐγίνωσκον τὰ λεγόμενα.
verses.transliteration: kai autoi ouden toutōn sunēkan, kai ēn to rhēma touto kekrummenon ap' autōn, kai ouk eginōskon ta legomena.
verses.study_note: Three increasingly emphatic statements of incomprehension: (1) "understood none of these things" (ouden toutōn sunēkan — they grasped none of it); (2) "this saying was hid from them" (kekrummenon — perfect passive participle: had been concealed, the hiding was a prior divine act, not momentary failure); (3) "neither knew they the things which were spoken" (ouk eginōskon — they kept not knowing, imperfect: continuous non-understanding). Luke's emphasis on divine concealment (here and 9:45) prepares the resurrection narrative where the hiding is reversed: "Then opened he their understanding, that they might understand the scriptures" (24:45).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that as he was come nigh unto Jericho, a certain blind man sat by the way side begging:
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῷ ἐγγίζειν αὐτὸν εἰς Ἰεριχώ, τυφλός τις ἐκάθητο παρὰ τὴν ὁδὸν προσαιτῶν.
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tō engizein auton eis Ierichō, tuphlos tis ekathēto para tēn hodon prosaitōn.
verses.study_note: "As he was come nigh unto Jericho" — the journey to Jerusalem (begun in 9:51) is nearing its destination; Jericho is approximately 15 miles northeast of Jerusalem. "A certain blind man sat by the way side begging" (tuphlos tis ekathēto para tēn hodon prosaitōn — a blind certain-man was sitting beside the road begging; the participle prosaitōn — persistently begging) — like the widow and the tax collector before him, he is at the margins of society, dependent, without resources. Unlike the rich ruler who came with a question, he will come with a cry.

verses.kjv_text: And hearing the multitude pass by, he asked what it meant.
verses.original_text: ἀκούσας δὲ ὄχλου διαπορευομένου ἐπυνθάνετο τί εἴη τοῦτο.
verses.transliteration: akousas de ochlou diaporeuomenou epunthaneto ti eiē touto.
verses.study_note: "Hearing the multitude pass by" (akousas ochlou diaporeuomenou — having heard a crowd going through) — his blindness means he must rely on his ears. "He asked what it meant" (epunthaneto ti eiē touto — he kept inquiring what this might be; the optative eiē expressing uncertainty; the imperfect epunthaneto indicating repeated asking). He cannot see what is happening; he can only ask. His inquiry will lead to the information that gives him the opportunity to cry out. The narrative pace slows to include this detail, emphasizing his active response to what he cannot see.

verses.kjv_text: And they told him, that Jesus of Nazareth passeth by.
verses.original_text: ἀπήγγειλαν δὲ αὐτῷ ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ὁ Ναζωραῖος παρέρχεται.
verses.transliteration: apēngeilan de autō hoti Iēsous ho Nazōraios parerchetai.
verses.study_note: "Jesus of Nazareth passeth by" (Iēsous ho Nazōraios parerchetai — Jesus the Nazarene is passing by; the present tense indicating immediacy) — the information is simple, factual, and momentous. The identification "of Nazareth" is how Jesus was commonly known in the popular mind. Armed with this identification, the blind man will use a different, theologically loaded title: "Son of David" (v.38) — a messianic title that goes beyond local geographic identity to royal genealogical claim. The contrast between the crowd's title and his own is deliberate.

verses.kjv_text: And he cried, saying, Jesus, thou Son of David, have mercy on me.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐβόησεν λέγων, Ἰησοῦ, υἱὲ Δαυίδ, ἐλέησόν με.
verses.transliteration: kai eboēsen legōn, Iēsou, huie Dauid, eleēson me.
verses.study_note: "Cried" (eboēsen — he shouted, bellowed; the word for a loud, urgent cry; used of the wilderness voice in 3:4) — public, unrestrained calling. "Son of David" (huie Dauid — Davidic son, the messianic title drawing on 2 Sam 7:12-16; Isa 11:1; Ps 110) — a recognition of messianic identity that exceeds the disciples' ordinary understanding at this point. "Have mercy on me" (eleēson me — the same mercy-cry as the lepers in 17:13 and the tax collector in v.13) — not a request for information or a theological discussion but a direct, urgent plea for grace. He knows what he needs and who can provide it.

verses.kjv_text: And they which went before rebuked him, that he should hold his peace: but he cried so much the more, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me.
verses.original_text: καὶ οἱ προάγοντες ἐπετίμων αὐτῷ ἵνα σιγήσῃ· αὐτὸς δὲ πολλῷ μᾶλλον ἔκραζεν, Υἱὲ Δαυίδ, ἐλέησόν με.
verses.transliteration: kai hoi proagontes epetimōn autō hina sigēsē; autos de pollō mallon ekrazen, Huie Dauid, eleēson me.
verses.study_note: "They which went before rebuked him" (hoi proagontes epetimōn — those going ahead were rebuking him; the imperfect: repeatedly rebuking) — the crowd's rebuke mirrors the disciples' rebuke of the children in v.15. In both cases, Jesus overrides the crowd's exclusion and calls the rejected to himself. "That he should hold his peace" (hina sigēsē — that he would be silent, an aorist of finality). "But he cried so much the more" (autos de pollō mallon ekrazen — but he himself cried out all the more; pollō mallon = much more, by a great deal) — opposition intensified his persistence rather than silencing it.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus stood, and commanded him to be brought unto him: and when he was come near, he asked him,
verses.original_text: σταθεὶς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐκέλευσεν αὐτὸν ἀχθῆναι πρὸς αὐτόν· ἐγγίσαντος δὲ αὐτοῦ ἐπηρώτησεν αὐτόν,
verses.transliteration: statheis de ho Iēsous ekeleusen auton achthēnai pros auton; engisantos de autou epērōtēsen auton,
verses.study_note: "Jesus stood" (statheis — having stopped, coming to a halt) — the journey to Jerusalem pauses for one blind beggar. The entire entourage stops. "Commanded him to be brought unto him" (ekeleusen auton achthēnai pros auton — commanded him to be led to him) — the command overrides the crowd's rejection with an authoritative summons. The same crowd that rebuked him now leads him forward. "When he was come near, he asked him" — the personal, face-to-face encounter: Jesus does not heal from a distance but draws the man close and then asks him a question.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, What wilt thou that I shall do unto thee? And he said, Lord, that I may receive my sight.
verses.original_text: λέγων, Τί σοι θέλεις ποιήσω; ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Κύριε, ἵνα ἀναβλέψω.
verses.transliteration: legōn, Ti soi theleis poiēsō? ho de eipen, Kurie, hina anablepsō.
verses.study_note: "What wilt thou that I shall do unto thee?" (Ti soi theleis poiēsō — what do you want me to do for you?) — the same question Jesus will ask the sons of Zebedee in Matthew (20:21), revealing his habit of drawing out an explicit articulation of need. The question dignifies the blind man: he is not a passive recipient of charity but an active participant in a personal exchange with Jesus. "That I may receive my sight" (hina anablepsō — that I may see again, the prefix ana = again suggesting he was not born blind but had lost his sight) — a simple, direct, specific request.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said unto him, Receive thy sight: thy faith hath saved thee.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Ἀνάβλεψον· ἡ πίστις σου σέσωκέν σε.
verses.transliteration: kai ho Iēsous eipen autō, Anablepson; hē pistis sou sesōken se.
verses.study_note: "Receive thy sight" (Anablepson — see again, an aorist imperative: an immediate command) — there is no prayer, no ceremony, no spittle or touch (contrast 7:14; Mark 8:23): only the direct word of command. "Thy faith hath saved thee" (hē pistis sou sesōken se — your faith has saved you, the same formula as 7:50 and 8:48 and 17:19) — the formula that joins physical restoration to whole-person salvation. His persistent cry, his refusal to be silenced, his use of the messianic title, his specific request — all express the faith that sōzō encompasses. He receives both sight and salvation.

verses.kjv_text: And immediately he received his sight, and followed him, glorifying God: and all the people, when they saw it, gave praise unto God.
verses.original_text: καὶ παραχρῆμα ἀνέβλεψεν, καὶ ἠκολούθει αὐτῷ δοξάζων τὸν Θεόν· καὶ πᾶς ὁ λαὸς ἰδὼν ἔδωκεν αἶνον τῷ Θεῷ.
verses.transliteration: kai parachrēma aneblepsen, kai ēkolouthei autō doxazōn ton Theon; kai pas ho laos idōn edōken ainon tō Theō.
verses.study_note: "Immediately" (parachrēma — on the spot, instantly) — the healing is instantaneous and complete. "Followed him, glorifying God" (ēkolouthei autō doxazōn ton Theon — he was following him, glorifying God; the imperfect tense suggests continuous action) — he becomes a disciple on the road to Jerusalem. His response is the opposite of the rich ruler who "went away very sorrowful" (v.23): this man, who had nothing but his cry, leaves the roadside behind and follows. "All the people...gave praise unto God" (edōken ainon — gave praise, offered a hymn of praise) — the crowd that tried to silence the cry now joins the praise.

meta.overview: Luke 19 is a chapter of arrivals — the arrival of salvation to Zacchaeus (vv.1-10), the arrival of accountability in the Parable of the Minas (vv.11-27), the arrival of the King in Jerusalem (vv.28-44), and the arrival of judgment in the Temple (vv.45-48). The chapter opens with the most personal of salvation encounters: Jesus stops in Jericho, looks up at a wealthy, despised tax collector in a tree, and announces that he must stay at his house today. Zacchaeus's response — standing and declaring immediate, generous restitution — demonstrates the fruit of genuine conversion. Jesus's declaration, "This day is salvation come to this house" (v.9), encapsulates Luke's central soteriology.
meta.theme: The Parable of the Minas (vv.11-27) is given specifically to correct the disciples' expectation that the kingdom of God would immediately appear (v.11). It distinguishes between three categories: faithful servants who multiply the entrusted resources (rewarded with governance authority), unfaithful servants who preserve but do not invest (rebuked and stripped), and hostile citizens who reject the master's kingship (executed). The parable's political framework (a nobleman going to receive a kingdom while his citizens send an embassy of rejection) may reflect Archelaus's trip to Rome in 4 BC; it serves to reframe kingdom expectations: the king departs, entrusts, then returns for accounting.
meta.keyWordOriginal: The Triumphal Entry (vv.28-44) is Luke's most distinctive version of the event, shaped by his theological concerns. The disciples' praise — "Blessed be the King that cometh in the name of the Lord" (v.38) — is explicitly royal, citing Psalm 118:26. The Pharisees' request to silence the disciples is met with Jesus's declaration that if they were silent, the stones would cry out (v.40). But the entry reaches its emotional climax in the lament over Jerusalem (vv.41-44) — unique to Luke — where Jesus weeps (eklaien — imperfect: he kept weeping) over the city's imminent destruction, which he attributes to its failure to recognize "the time of thy visitation" (v.44).
meta.keyWordMeaning: The cleansing of the Temple (vv.45-48) is remarkably compressed in Luke compared to Mark and John. Jesus drives out the sellers and establishes a daily teaching ministry in the Temple — which will occupy chapters 20-21. The conflict over his authority (20:1-8) and the conspiracy to kill him (19:47; 20:19) are both introduced here, framing the Temple teaching as the penultimate act before the passion. The people's hanging on his words (v.48: exekremato — hung suspended from his teaching, a vivid verb) and the chief priests' inability to find a way to destroy him creates a temporary standoff that drives the passion narrative forward.
outline: σωτηρία (sōtēria)

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus entered and passed through Jericho.
verses.original_text: Καὶ εἰσελθὼν διήρχετο τὴν Ἰεριχώ.
verses.transliteration: Kai eiselthōn diērcheto tēn Ierichō.
verses.study_note: "Entered and passed through Jericho" (eiselthōn diērcheto — having entered he was passing through) — the imperfect suggests a journey in progress, not a halt. Jericho was a prosperous city, the last major town before the ascent to Jerusalem. Verse 1 establishes the setting without fanfare; the significance of the stop will be revealed through what Jesus does rather than where he is. The brevity of the geographical note signals that what matters is not the city but the encounter about to occur.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, there was a man named Zacchaeus, which was the chief among the publicans, and he was rich.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἰδοὺ ἀνὴρ ὀνόματι καλούμενος Ζακχαῖος, καὶ αὐτὸς ἦν ἀρχιτελώνης, καὶ αὐτὸς πλούσιος.
verses.transliteration: Kai idou anēr onomati kaloumenos Zakchaios, kai autos ēn architelōnēs, kai autos plousios.
verses.study_note: "Behold" (idou — see! a narrative attention signal) introduces the main character. "Zacchaeus" (Zakchaios — from the Hebrew Zaccai, meaning pure or innocent, a name ironically at odds with his profession). "Chief among the publicans" (architelōnēs — chief tax collector, a word found only here in all of Greek literature; arch = chief + telōnēs = tax collector) — a superintendent of tax collection, overseeing multiple collectors, hence doubly despised. "He was rich" — the identical characterization of the rich ruler (18:23: plousios sphodra); but Zacchaeus's story will end entirely differently.

verses.kjv_text: And he sought to see Jesus who he was; and could not for the press, because he was little of stature.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐζήτει ἰδεῖν τὸν Ἰησοῦν τίς ἐστιν, καὶ οὐκ ἠδύνατο ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄχλου, ὅτι τῇ ἡλικίᾳ μικρὸς ἦν.
verses.transliteration: kai ezētei idein ton Iēsoun tis estin, kai ouk ēdunato apo tou ochlou, hoti tē hēlikia mikros ēn.
verses.study_note: "He sought to see Jesus who he was" (ezētei idein ton Iēsoun tis estin — he was seeking to see who Jesus is; imperfect: persistent seeking) — the curiosity is genuine and active. "Could not for the press" (ouk ēdunato apo tou ochlou — was not able because of the crowd) — his social marginalization (crowds would not give way for a hated tax collector) and his physical limitation compound. "Little of stature" (tē hēlikia mikros — small in bodily stature) — a charming physical detail unique to Luke, which sets up the sycamore scene. His small stature may also be a narrative metaphor for his social diminishment.

verses.kjv_text: And he ran before, and climbed up into a sycomore tree to see him: for he was to pass that way.
verses.original_text: καὶ προδραμὼν εἰς τὸ ἔμπροσθεν ἀνέβη ἐπὶ συκομορέαν ἵνα ἴδῃ αὐτόν, ὅτι ἐκείνης ἤμελλεν διέρχεσθαι.
verses.transliteration: kai prodramōn eis to emprosthen anebē epi sukomorean hina idē auton, hoti ekeinēs ēmellen dierchesthai.
verses.study_note: "He ran before" (prodramōn — having run ahead, an aorist of quick decisive action) — a wealthy, important official running and climbing a tree is deliberately undignified, echoing the father running to the prodigal son (15:20). "Climbed up into a sycomore tree" (anebē epi sukomorean — went up onto a fig-mulberry tree; the sycamore-fig, common in Jericho, is easy to climb with its low horizontal branches) — the posture is ridiculous by the standards of his social status, which makes the scene both comic and touching. His desperate desire to see Jesus overcomes his dignity.

verses.kjv_text: And when Jesus came to the place, he looked up, and saw him, and said unto him, Zacchaeus, make haste, and come down; for to day I must abide at thy house.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὡς ἦλθεν ἐπὶ τὸν τόπον, ἀναβλέψας ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶδεν αὐτόν, καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτόν, Ζακχαῖε, σπεύσας κατάβηθι· σήμερον γὰρ ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ σου δεῖ με μεῖναι.
verses.transliteration: kai hōs ēlthen epi ton topon, anablepsas ho Iēsous eiden auton, kai eipen pros auton, Zakchaie, speusas katabēthi; sēmeron gar en tō oikō sou dei me meinai.
verses.study_note: "Looked up, and saw him" (anablepsas eiden auton) — the same word anablepō used for the blind man receiving sight in 18:42; here Jesus looks up to see the man seeking sight of him. "Zacchaeus" — Jesus addresses him by name without introduction; the divine foreknowledge of names (cf. Simon, 5:8; Saul, Acts 9:4) signals that this encounter was not accidental from Jesus's side. "I must abide at thy house" (dei me meinai — it is necessary for me to stay; dei = divine necessity) — the same divine compulsion that drove Jesus toward the cross drives him to Zacchaeus's house. Salvation comes not because Zacchaeus invited Jesus but because Jesus invited himself.

verses.kjv_text: And he made haste, and came down, and received him joyfully.
verses.original_text: καὶ σπεύσας κατέβη, καὶ ὑπεδέξατο αὐτὸν χαίρων.
verses.transliteration: kai speusas katebē, kai hypedexato auton chairōn.
verses.study_note: "Made haste, and came down" (speusas katebē — having hurried he came down) — immediate, unhesitating obedience to Jesus's command. "Received him joyfully" (hypedexato auton chairōn — welcomed him rejoicing) — the same verb hypodechomai used of Martha in 10:38, of hospitable receiving; here it is full of joy (chairōn — the present participle expresses continuous state of joy). The joy is not manufactured but spontaneous, the natural response to the unexpected grace of being sought and named by Jesus. His response stands in sharp contrast to the rich ruler's sorrow (18:23).

verses.kjv_text: And when they saw it, they all murmured, saying, That he was gone to be guest with a man that is a sinner.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδόντες πάντες διεγόγγυζον λέγοντες ὅτι Παρὰ ἁμαρτωλῷ ἀνδρὶ εἰσῆλθεν καταλῦσαι.
verses.transliteration: kai idontes pantes dieggongyzon legontes hoti Para hamartōlō andri eisēlthen katalusai.
verses.study_note: "They all murmured" (pantes diegonguzon — all were grumbling, the intensified compound verb; the same root as Israel's wilderness murmuring, Exod 16:7 LXX; found in Luke at 5:30; 15:2) — the crowd's reaction mirrors the Pharisees' complaint about Jesus eating with sinners throughout Luke. "A man that is a sinner" (hamartōlō andri — a sinful man) — the word sinner in Luke's social setting designates not merely a moral failing but a categorical outsider. The murmuring crowd does not understand that Jesus's presence with sinners is not contamination but invasion — the kingdom entering enemy territory.

verses.kjv_text: And Zacchaeus stood, and said unto the Lord; Behold, Lord, the half of my goods I give to the poor; and if I have taken any thing from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold.
verses.original_text: σταθεὶς δὲ Ζακχαῖος εἶπεν πρὸς τὸν Κύριον, Ἰδοὺ τὰ ἡμίσιά μου τῶν ὑπαρχόντων, Κύριε, τοῖς πτωχοῖς δίδωμι, καὶ εἴ τινός τι ἐσυκοφάντησα, ἀποδίδωμι τετραπλοῦν.
verses.transliteration: statheis de Zakchaios eipen pros ton Kurion, Idou ta hēmisia mou tōn huparchontōn, Kurie, tois ptōchois didōmi, kai ei tinos ti esukophantēsa, apodidōmi tetraploun.
verses.study_note: "Stood" (statheis — having stood, taking a public formal position) — the act of standing to speak indicates a solemn public declaration. "The half of my goods I give to the poor" (ta hēmisia mou tōn huparchontōn...didōmi — the half of my possessions I give; present tense: I am now giving, an immediate commitment) — voluntarily giving half, without being asked. "If I have taken any thing from any man by false accusation" (ei tinos ti esukophantēsa — if I have extorted anything from anyone; sukophantēō is the word for extortion, blackmail, false accusation) "I restore him fourfold" (apodidōmi tetraploun) — exceeding Mosaic law's requirement (Exod 22:1 required fourfold restitution only for stolen sheep). Salvation has immediately restructured his economics.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said unto him, This day is salvation come to this house, forsomuch as he also is a son of Abraham.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς αὐτὸν ὁ Ἰησοῦς, Σήμερον σωτηρία τῷ οἴκῳ τούτῳ ἐγένετο, καθότι καὶ αὐτὸς υἱὸς Ἀβραάμ ἐστιν·
verses.transliteration: eipen de pros auton ho Iēsous, Sēmeron sōtēria tō oikō toutō egeneto, kathoti kai autos huios Abraam estin;
verses.study_note: "This day is salvation come to this house" (Sēmeron sōtēria tō oikō toutō egeneto — today salvation to this house has happened) — salvation is dated ("today," sēmeron, a key word in Luke: 2:11; 4:21; 23:43), located ("to this house"), and declared (egeneto — aorist: it happened, it has arrived). "He also is a son of Abraham" (autos huios Abraam estin — he himself is a son of Abraham) — not merely by ethnic lineage but by faith like Abraham's (Gal 3:7; Rom 4:11-12). The crowd had categorized him as a sinner; Jesus recategorizes him as a son of Abraham. His restitution is not the cause of salvation but its fruit and evidence.

verses.kjv_text: For the Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost.
verses.original_text: ἦλθεν γὰρ ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ζητῆσαι καὶ σῶσαι τὸ ἀπολωλός.
verses.transliteration: ēlthen gar ho Huios tou anthrōpou zētēsai kai sōsai to apolōlos.
verses.study_note: "The Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost" (ēlthen...zētēsai kai sōsai to apolōlos — came...to seek and to save the one having been lost) — a programmatic mission statement, echoing 15:3-7 (the lost sheep). "To seek" (zētēsai) and "to save" (sōsai) are infinitives of purpose: this is why he came. "That which was lost" (to apolōlos — the perfect passive participle: the one in a state of lostness, the permanently lost one) — the same verb used throughout chapter 15. The Zacchaeus episode is the final concrete illustration of the lost-and-found theology of chapters 15-19 before the passion narrative begins.

verses.kjv_text: And as they heard these things, he added and spake a parable, because he was nigh to Jerusalem, and because they thought that the kingdom of God should immediately appear.
verses.original_text: Ἀκουόντων δὲ αὐτῶν ταῦτα, προσθεὶς εἶπεν παραβολήν, διὰ τὸ ἐγγὺς αὐτὸν εἶναι Ἰερουσαλὴμ καὶ δοκεῖν αὐτοὺς ὅτι παραχρῆμα μέλλει ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ ἀναφαίνεσθαι.
verses.transliteration: Akouontōn de autōn tauta, prostheis eipen parabolēn, dia to engus auton einai Ierousalēm kai dokein autous hoti parachrēma mellei hē basileia tou Theou anaphainesthai.
verses.study_note: Luke explicitly states the occasion and purpose of the parable: proximity to Jerusalem has inflamed the disciples' expectation that the kingdom of God would "immediately appear" (parachrēma — instantly, at once; anaphainesthai — be manifested, become visible). The misconception is that the Messiah's arrival in Jerusalem will trigger the kingdom's immediate visible establishment, overthrowing Rome and restoring Israel's national sovereignty. The parable will correct this timeline: the king departs, entrusts resources, and returns later for accountability. The interval between departure and return is the present age.

verses.kjv_text: He said therefore, A certain nobleman went into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom, and to return.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν, Ἄνθρωπός τις εὐγενὴς ἐπορεύθη εἰς χώραν μακράν, λαβεῖν ἑαυτῷ βασιλείαν καὶ ὑποστρέψαι.
verses.transliteration: eipen oun, Anthrōpos tis eugenēs eporeuthē eis chōran makran, labein heautō basileian kai hupostrepsai.
verses.study_note: "A certain nobleman" (Anthrōpos tis eugenēs — a certain noble-born man, a man of high birth) "went into a far country" (eporeuthē eis chōran makran — traveled to a distant land) "to receive for himself a kingdom" (labein heautō basileian — to receive for himself a kingdom, to obtain royal authority). The political framework almost certainly alludes to Archelaus's journey to Rome in 4 BC to have his rule confirmed — an event well-known to Jesus's Jewish listeners. The "far country" thus represents the heavenly realm to which Christ ascends; the interval represents the present age; the return is the parousia.

verses.kjv_text: And he called his ten servants, and delivered them ten pounds, and said unto them, Occupy till I come.
verses.original_text: καλέσας δὲ δέκα δούλους ἑαυτοῦ ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς δέκα μνᾶς καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς, Πραγματεύσασθε ἐν ᾧ ἔρχομαι.
verses.transliteration: kalEsas de deka doulous heautou edōken autois deka mnas kai eipen pros autous, Pragmateusasthe en hō erchomai.
verses.study_note: "Ten servants" (deka doulous) — one mina each; contrast Matthew's parable of the talents where each servant receives different amounts. "Ten pounds" (deka mnas — ten minas; one mina = approximately 100 denarii, about three months' wages for a laborer) — a modest sum, unlike Matthew's talents (one talent = 6,000 denarii). "Occupy till I come" (Pragmateusasthe en hō erchomai — do business, trade actively while I am coming) — the present tense erchomai (I am coming) emphasizes the ongoing nature of the master's approach; the servant's trading activity is the appropriate response to the master's continuing claim on them.

verses.kjv_text: But his citizens hated him, and sent a message after him, saying, We will not have this man to reign over us.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ πολῖται αὐτοῦ ἐμίσουν αὐτόν, καὶ ἀπέστειλαν πρεσβείαν ὀπίσω αὐτοῦ λέγοντες, Οὐ θέλομεν τοῦτον βασιλεῦσαι ἐφ' ἡμᾶς.
verses.transliteration: hoi de politai autou emisoun auton, kai apesteilan presbeian opisō autou legontes, Ou thelomen touton basilEusai eph' hēmas.
verses.study_note: "His citizens hated him" (hoi politai autou emisoun auton — the citizens of his city hated him, imperfect: a settled state of hatred) — the political framework introduces a third category beyond the servants: citizens who reject the nobleman's right to rule. "Sent a message after him" (apesteilan presbeian — dispatched an embassy, a formal diplomatic delegation) — a coordinated act of political rejection. "We will not have this man to reign over us" (Ou thelomen touton basileusai eph' hēmas — we do not wish this one to reign over us) — the refusal of Christ's kingship by those who heard and rejected his message.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that when he was returned, having received the kingdom, then he commanded these servants to be called unto him, to whom he had given the money, that he might know how much every man had gained by trading.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ ἐπανελθεῖν αὐτὸν λαβόντα τὴν βασιλείαν, καὶ εἶπεν φωνηθῆναι αὐτῷ τοὺς δούλους τούτους οἷς ἔδωκεν τὸ ἀργύριον, ἵνα γνῷ τί διεπραγματεύσαντο.
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en tō epanelthein auton labonta tēn basileian, kai eipen phōnēthēnai autō tous doulous toutous hois edōken to argurion, hina gnō ti diepragmateusanto.
verses.study_note: "When he was returned, having received the kingdom" (epanelthein auton labonta tēn basileian — when he came back having received the kingdom) — the return is certain and the kingdom is already obtained when he returns. "That he might know how much every man had gained by trading" (hina gnō ti diepragmateusanto — in order that he might know what they had transacted, what they had earned through business) — the master conducts an accounting review, not a punishment session; the review is diagnostic, revealing the character of each servant's stewardship.

verses.kjv_text: Then came the first, saying, Lord, thy pound hath gained ten pounds.
verses.original_text: παρεγένετο δὲ ὁ πρῶτος λέγων, Κύριε, ἡ μνᾶ σου δέκα μνᾶς προσηργάσατο.
verses.transliteration: paregeneto de ho prōtos legōn, Kurie, hē mna sou deka mnas prosērgasato.
verses.study_note: "Thy pound hath gained ten pounds" (hē mna sou deka mnas prosērgasato — your mina has earned in addition ten minas; prosērgasato — earned additionally, a profit verb) — a 1,000% return. The servant attributes the gain to the master's mina ("thy pound") rather than to his own skill — an important theological signal: the servant is a steward, not an owner. The ten minas are not the servant's to boast about but the master's to receive. This posture of stewardship (not ownership) characterizes the faithful servant.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, Well, thou good servant: because thou hast been faithful in a very little, have thou authority over ten cities.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Εὖγε, ἀγαθὲ δοῦλε· ὅτι ἐν ἐλαχίστῳ πιστὸς ἐγένου, ἴσθι ἐξουσίαν ἔχων ἐπάνω δέκα πόλεων.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autō, Euge, agathe doule; hoti en elachistō pistos egenou, isthi exousian echōn epanō deka poleōn.
verses.study_note: "Well" (Euge — Bravo! Well done! an exclamation of enthusiastic approval, used only here in the NT) — the master's delight is genuine and enthusiastic. "Faithful in a very little" (en elachistō pistos — faithful in the smallest thing; elachiston = superlative of small) — the mina is characterized as very small in comparison to the reward, establishing the principle of 16:10: "he that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much." "Authority over ten cities" — the reward for faithfulness is expanded governance, not retirement. Kingdom faithfulness leads to kingdom service at greater scale.

verses.kjv_text: And the second came, saying, Lord, thy pound hath gained five pounds.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦλθεν ὁ δεύτερος λέγων, Κύριε, ἡ μνᾶ σου ἐποίησεν πέντε μνᾶς.
verses.transliteration: kai ēlthen ho deuteros legōn, Kurie, hē mna sou epoiēsen pente mnas.
verses.study_note: "Thy pound hath gained five pounds" (hē mna sou epoiEsen pente mnas — your mina produced five minas) — a 500% return, less than the first servant but still significant. Notably the master's commendation (v.19) mirrors v.17 except for the number of cities — "five cities" rather than "ten." The principle is consistent: faithful stewardship, proportionate reward. The parable does not grade the servants against each other but evaluates each in relation to what was entrusted and what was done with it.

verses.kjv_text: And he said likewise to him, Be thou also over five cities.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ καὶ τούτῳ, Καὶ σὺ ἐπάνω γίνου πέντε πόλεων.
verses.transliteration: eipen de kai toutō, Kai su epanō ginou pente poleōn.
verses.study_note: "Be thou also over five cities" (Kai su epanō ginou pente poleōn — you also become over five cities; the imperative ginou — become, as a standing command) — the reward structure is symmetrical with the service: five minas earned, five cities governed. The "also" (kai su — you also) places this servant alongside the first in the category of the faithful; there is no rebuke, no graduated evaluation suggesting he is less valued than the first servant. Both faithful servants receive the kingdom's governance in proportion to their stewardship.

verses.kjv_text: And another came, saying, Lord, behold, here is thy pound, which I have kept laid up in a napkin:
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ ἕτερος ἦλθεν λέγων, Κύριε, ἰδοὺ ἡ μνᾶ σου, ἣν εἶχον ἀποκειμένην ἐν σουδαρίῳ·
verses.transliteration: kai ho heteros ēlthen legōn, Kurie, idou hē mna sou, hēn eichon apokeimEnēn en soudariō;
verses.study_note: "Here is thy pound, which I have kept laid up in a napkin" (ēn eichon apokeimEnēn en soudariō — which I had stored away in a cloth; soudariō — the same word for the cloth over Lazarus's face in John 11:44) — not buried (as in Matthew's parable) but wrapped and stored. The napkin suggests careful preservation rather than negligent disregard, but the result is the same: no investment, no growth, no risk. The servant will claim his motive was fear (v.21). Preservation of the entrusted gift without deployment is unfaithfulness regardless of the motive.

verses.kjv_text: For I feared thee, because thou art an austere man: thou takest up that thou layedst not down, and reapest that thou didst not sow.
verses.original_text: ἐφοβούμην γάρ σε, ὅτι ἄνθρωπος αὐστηρὸς εἶ, αἴρεις ὃ οὐκ ἔθηκας, καὶ θερίζεις ὃ οὐκ ἔσπειρας.
verses.transliteration: ephoboumēn gar se, hoti anthrōpos austēros ei, aireis ho ouk ethēkas, kai therizeis ho ouk espeiras.
verses.study_note: "I feared thee" (ephoboumEn — I was fearing you, an imperfect of continuous fear) "because thou art an austere man" (austēros — harsh, strict, exacting; from austēros, related to the word for dried-out, astringent, harsh to the taste) — the characterization of the master as harsh is the servant's self-justification, not an accurate description of the master who celebrated the faithful servants with delight (Euge, v.17). The servant projects severity onto the master as a rationalization for his own paralysis. "Thou takest up that thou layedst not down" — the servant claims the master's demands are unreasonable, but this description is precisely what the first servant demonstrated could be met.

verses.kjv_text: And he saith unto him, Out of thine own mouth will I judge thee, thou wicked servant. Thou knewest that I was an austere man, taking up that I laid not down, and reaping that I did not sow:
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ, Ἐκ τοῦ στόματός σου κρινῶ σε, πονηρὲ δοῦλε. ᾔδεις ὅτι ἐγὼ ἄνθρωπος αὐστηρός εἰμι, αἴρων ὃ οὐκ ἔθηκα καὶ θερίζων ὃ οὐκ ἔσπειρα·
verses.transliteration: legei autō, Ek tou stomatos sou krinō se, ponēre doule. ēdeis hoti egō anthrōpos austēros eimi, airōn ho ouk ethēka kai therizōn ho ouk espeira;
verses.study_note: "Out of thine own mouth will I judge thee" (Ek tou stomatos sou krinō se — from your own mouth I will judge you) — the servant's self-incrimination: if he genuinely believed the master was demanding, the logical response would have been maximum effort, not minimum effort. "Thou wicked servant" (ponēre doule — evil servant, the opposite of the "good servant" in v.17) — the judgment is not because he failed to achieve but because his inaction revealed his character. "Thou knewest" (ēdeis — you knew, you had knowledge) — the judgment rests on the servant's own stated awareness of the master's expectations.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore then gavest not thou my money into the bank, that at my coming I might have required mine own with usury?
verses.original_text: καὶ διὰ τί οὐκ ἔδωκάς μου τὸ ἀργύριον ἐπὶ τράπεζαν, κἀγὼ ἐλθὼν σὺν τόκῳ ἂν αὐτὸ ἔπραξα;
verses.transliteration: kai dia ti ouk edōkas mou to argurion epi trapezan, kagō elthōn sun tokō an auto epraza?
verses.study_note: "Gavest not thou my money into the bank" (epi trapezan — upon the table, the money-changers' table; trapeza = table, then bank) "that at my coming I might have required mine own with usury?" (sun tokō — with interest, with offspring; tokos = birth, interest as the offspring of capital) — the absolute minimum expectation was banking the mina for interest, which required no personal risk. The servant chose instead the option of zero risk, zero gain, and zero service. The master's question exposes that even by the servant's own stated logic, passive banking would have been better than wrapping in a napkin.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them that stood by, Take from him the pound, and give it to him that hath ten pounds.
verses.original_text: καὶ τοῖς παρεστῶσιν εἶπεν, Ἄρατε ἀπ' αὐτοῦ τὴν μνᾶν καὶ δότε τῷ τὰς δέκα μνᾶς ἔχοντι.
verses.transliteration: kai tois parestōsin eipen, Arate ap' autou tēn mnan kai dote tō tas deka mnas echonti.
verses.study_note: "Take from him the pound" (Arate ap' autou tēn mnan — take away from him the mina) — the unfaithful servant loses not only his future opportunity but his present entrustment. "Give it to him that hath ten pounds" — the gift is given to the most productive servant, not distributed equally. The economy of the kingdom is not redistributive equality but reward proportional to faithfulness. This principle will be stated explicitly in v.26.

verses.kjv_text: (And they said unto him, Lord, he hath ten pounds.)
verses.original_text: (καὶ εἶπαν αὐτῷ, Κύριε, ἔχει δέκα μνᾶς.)
verses.transliteration: (kai eipan autō, Kurie, echei deka mnas.)
verses.study_note: A brief interjection from bystanders, perhaps expressing surprise at the distribution: why give more to the one who already has the most? The parenthetical objection (unique to Luke's version) serves to set up the master's clarifying principle in v.26. The bystanders' logic is egalitarian: it seems unfair to give the already-enriched servant even more. Jesus's response will challenge this instinct with a different economy.

verses.kjv_text: For I say unto you, That unto every one which hath shall be given; and from him that hath not, even that he hath shall be taken away from him.
verses.original_text: λέγω γὰρ ὑμῖν ὅτι παντὶ τῷ ἔχοντι δοθήσεται, ἀπὸ δὲ τοῦ μὴ ἔχοντος καὶ ὃ ἔχει ἀρθήσεται.
verses.transliteration: legō gar humin hoti panti tō echonti dothēsetai, apo de tou mē echontos kai ho echei arthēsetai.
verses.study_note: "Unto every one which hath shall be given" (panti tō echonti dothēsetai — to everyone who has it will be given) — the kingdom's economy: faithful use of what is entrusted creates capacity for more; the active steward's resources multiply. "From him that hath not, even that he hath shall be taken away" (apo de tou mē echontos kai ho echei arthēsetai — from the one not having, even what he has will be taken) — the inactive steward, by refusing to deploy his resources, has already forfeited his stewardship in principle; the formal stripping merely makes visible what was already spiritually true. This principle appears also in 8:18 and Matthew 25:29.

verses.kjv_text: But those mine enemies, which would not that I should reign over them, bring hither, and slay them before me.
verses.original_text: πλὴν τοὺς ἐχθρούς μου τούτους τοὺς μὴ θελήσαντάς με βασιλεῦσαι ἐπ' αὐτούς, ἀγάγετε ὧδε καὶ κατασφάξατε αὐτοὺς ἔμπροσθέν μου.
verses.transliteration: plēn tous echthrous mou toutous tous mē thelEsantas me basilEusai ep' autous, agagete hōde kai katasphaxate autous emprosthen mou.
verses.study_note: "Those mine enemies, which would not that I should reign over them" (tous mē thelēsantas me basileusai ep' autous — those not having wished me to reign over them) — the third category: not negligent servants but active rejecters of the king's sovereignty, the embassy-senders of v.14. "Bring hither, and slay them before me" (agagete hōde kai katasphaxate — lead here and slaughter; katasphazō is a strong word for killing in battle) — the harsh conclusion of the political allegory. In historical terms, this was literally fulfilled in the Roman destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had thus spoken, he went before, ascending up to Jerusalem.
verses.original_text: Καὶ εἰπὼν ταῦτα ἐπορεύετο ἔμπροσθεν, ἀναβαίνων εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα.
verses.transliteration: Kai eipōn tauta eporeueto emprosthen, anabainōn eis Hierosoluma.
verses.study_note: "He went before, ascending up to Jerusalem" (eporeueto emprosthen, anabainōn eis Hierosoluma — he was going ahead, going up to Jerusalem) — the journey to Jerusalem, announced in 9:51, is now reaching its climax. "Went before" (emprosthen — in front, leading the way) — Jesus leads the way into what he has just described in prophetic parable. The ascent to Jerusalem (anabainōn — going up, as the city was on a hill, always "going up" in Jewish usage) is both physical and typological: the king approaches his capital and his cross.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when he was come nigh to Bethphage and Bethany, at the mount called the mount of Olives, he sent two of his disciples,
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ὡς ἤγγισεν εἰς Βηθφαγὴ καὶ Βηθανίαν πρὸς τὸ ὄρος τὸ καλούμενον Ἐλαιών, ἀπέστειλεν δύο τῶν μαθητῶν
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto hōs ēngisen eis Bēthphagē kai Bēthanian pros to oros to kaloumenon Elaiōn, apesteilen duo tōn mathētōn
verses.study_note: "Bethphage and Bethany" — two villages on the southeastern slope of the Mount of Olives, approximately two miles from Jerusalem. "The mount called the mount of Olives" (Elaiōn — the Olive Garden/Mount; from elaia, olive) — the eastern approach to Jerusalem, associated with Zechariah's eschatological prophecy (Zech 14:4: "his feet shall stand in that day upon the mount of Olives") and with David's flight from Absalom (2 Sam 15:30). The geography is deliberately Davidic and eschatological.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, Go ye into the village over against you; in the which at your entering ye shall find a colt tied, whereon yet never man sat: loose him, and bring him hither.
verses.original_text: λέγων, Ὑπάγετε εἰς τὴν κατέναντι κώμην, ἐν ᾗ εἰσπορευόμενοι εὑρήσετε πῶλον δεδεμένον, ἐφ' ὃν οὐδεὶς πώποτε ἀνθρώπων ἐκάθισεν, καὶ λύσαντες αὐτὸν ἀγάγετε.
verses.transliteration: legōn, Hupagete eis tēn katenanti kōmēn, en hē eisporeuomenoi heurēsete pōlon dedemenon, eph' hon oudeis pōpote anthrōpōn ekathisen, kai lusantes auton agagete.
verses.study_note: "A colt tied, whereon yet never man sat" (pōlon dedemenon, eph' hon oudeis pōpote anthrōpōn ekathisen — a colt having been tied, on which no one ever of men sat) — the unridden colt is ritually significant: an animal not yet used for work was considered appropriate for sacred purposes (Num 19:2; Deut 21:3; 1 Sam 6:7). The detail fulfills Zechariah 9:9: "thy King cometh unto thee...riding upon a colt the foal of an ass." The prearranged plan (Jesus knew the colt would be there) suggests prior arrangements, or alternatively demonstrates prophetic foreknowledge.

verses.kjv_text: And if any man ask you, Why do ye loose him? thus shall ye say unto him, Because the Lord hath need of him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐάν τις ὑμᾶς ἐρωτᾷ, Διατί λύετε; οὕτως ἐρεῖτε αὐτῷ ὅτι Ὁ Κύριος αὐτοῦ χρείαν ἔχει.
verses.transliteration: kai ean tis humas erōtā, Diatī luete? houtōs ereite autō hoti Ho Kurios autou chreian echei.
verses.study_note: "The Lord hath need of him" (Ho Kurios autou chreian echei — the Lord of it has need) — the title "Lord" (Kurios) applied to Jesus here carries its full weight: the Lord of all things claims what is his own. "Hath need of" (chreian echei — has a need of, has a use for) — the King commandeers what he needs. The combination of sovereignty and need is characteristic of the Incarnation: the one who owns all things nonetheless operates within the conditions of genuine human need and historical contingency.

verses.kjv_text: And they that were sent went their way, and found even as he had said unto them.
verses.original_text: ἀπελθόντες δὲ οἱ ἀπεσταλμένοι εὗρον καθὼς εἶπεν αὐτοῖς.
verses.transliteration: apelthontes de hoi apestalmenoi heuron kathōs eipen autois.
verses.study_note: "Found even as he had said unto them" (heuron kathōs eipen autois — they found exactly as he had said) — the precise fulfillment of Jesus's word. The disciples find exactly what they were told they would find, exactly where they were told to look. The detail is a quiet confirmation of Jesus's prophetic foreknowledge and demonstrates that the entry into Jerusalem is a deliberate, orchestrated act, not an improvised popular reaction.

verses.kjv_text: And as they were loosing the colt, the owners thereof said unto them, Why loose ye the colt?
verses.original_text: λυόντων δὲ αὐτῶν τὸν πῶλον, εἶπαν οἱ κύριοι αὐτοῦ πρὸς αὐτούς, Τί λύετε τὸν πῶλον;
verses.transliteration: luontōn de autōn ton pōlon, eipan hoi kurioi autou pros autous, Ti luete ton pōlon?
verses.study_note: "The owners thereof" (hoi kurioi autou — the lords/owners of it; kurios can mean both Lord and owner) — a wordplay may be present: the owners (kurioi) of the colt ask why it is being taken; the answer will be "the Lord" (ho Kurios, v.34) needs it. The human owners do not initially recognize that the Lord of the colt transcends their ownership of it. The owners' question is natural and legitimate; the disciples have the ready answer prepared.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, The Lord hath need of him.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ εἶπαν ὅτι Ὁ Κύριος αὐτοῦ χρείαν ἔχει.
verses.transliteration: hoi de eipan hoti Ho Kurios autou chreian echei.
verses.study_note: The disciples repeat Jesus's exact words verbatim: "The Lord hath need of him" — the phrase functions as both an explanation and a claim of sovereignty. In the wider Lucan context, the "Lord" is always Jesus (cf. 1:43, 45; 2:11); the claim that "the Lord needs it" is a statement about who Jesus is, not merely an explanation of borrowing. The owners' apparent willingness to release the colt on this basis suggests either prior arrangement or a divinely ordered willingness — both are consistent with Luke's theology.

verses.kjv_text: And they brought him to Jesus: and they cast their garments upon the colt, and they set Jesus thereon.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἤγαγον αὐτὸν πρὸς τὸν Ἰησοῦν· καὶ ἐπιρίψαντες αὐτῶν τὰ ἱμάτια ἐπὶ τὸν πῶλον ἐπεβίβασαν τὸν Ἰησοῦν.
verses.transliteration: kai ēgagon auton pros ton Iēsoun; kai epiripsantes autōn ta himatia epi ton pōlon epebibasan ton Iēsoun.
verses.study_note: "Cast their garments upon the colt" (epiripsantes ta himatia epi ton pōlon — having thrown their garments onto the colt) — the placing of garments on the riding animal is an honor gesture, reflecting the royal inauguration of Jehu in 2 Kings 9:13 where the people spread their garments beneath him on the steps. The disciples' action thus carries royal inauguration overtones. "They set Jesus thereon" (epebibasan ton Iēsoun — they mounted Jesus onto it) — a note of the disciples' active participation in the royal proclamation.

verses.kjv_text: And as he went, they spread their clothes in the way.
verses.original_text: πορευομένου δὲ αὐτοῦ, ὑπεστρώννυον τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτῶν ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ.
verses.transliteration: poreuomenou de autou, hupestōnnuon ta himatia autōn en tē hodō.
verses.study_note: "They spread their clothes in the way" (hupestōnnuon ta himatia autōn en tē hodō — they were spreading their garments in the road; the imperfect continuous: a continuing action as the procession moved forward) — the carpet of garments is a royal honor gesture, again echoing 2 Kings 9:13 and the entry of a king. The road from the Mount of Olives to Jerusalem descends into the Kidron Valley and then ascends sharply to the city gates — a dramatic visual spectacle, visible to the whole city.

verses.kjv_text: And when he was come nigh, even now at the descent of the mount of Olives, the whole multitude of the disciples began to rejoice and praise God with a loud voice for all the mighty works that they had seen;
verses.original_text: Ἐγγίζοντος δὲ αὐτοῦ ἤδη πρὸς τῇ καταβάσει τοῦ ὄρους τῶν Ἐλαιῶν, ἤρξαντο ἅπαν τὸ πλῆθος τῶν μαθητῶν χαίροντες αἰνεῖν τὸν Θεὸν φωνῇ μεγάλῃ περὶ πασῶν ὧν εἶδον δυνάμεων,
verses.transliteration: Engizōn de autou ēdē pros tē katabAsei tou orous tōn Elaiōn, ērxanto hapan to plēthos tōn mathētōn chairontes ainein ton Theon phōnē megalē peri pasōn hōn eidon dunameōn,
verses.study_note: "At the descent of the mount of Olives" — the moment the city comes into view from the downward slope is the moment the disciples' praise erupts. "The whole multitude of the disciples" (hapan to plēthos tōn mathētōn — all the mass of the disciples) — not just the Twelve but the larger circle of followers who had accompanied the journey. "Rejoice and praise God with a loud voice" (chairontes ainein ton Theon phōnē megalē — rejoicing, praising God with a great voice) "for all the mighty works that they had seen" (peri pasōn hōn eidon dunameōn — concerning all the works of power they had seen) — the praise is grounded in witnessed evidence.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, Blessed be the King that cometh in the name of the Lord: peace in heaven, and glory in the highest.
verses.original_text: λέγοντες, Εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος Βασιλεὺς ἐν ὀνόματι Κυρίου· εἰρήνη ἐν οὐρανῷ καὶ δόξα ἐν ὑψίστοις.
verses.transliteration: legontes, Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos Basileus en onomati Kuriou; eirēnē en ouranō kai doxa en hupsistois.
verses.study_note: "Blessed be the King that cometh in the name of the Lord" — a quotation of Psalm 118:26 (used at Tabernacles and Passover, a royal messianic psalm) with the insertion of "the King" (Basileus) — Luke's version explicitly identifies the incoming one as King. "Peace in heaven, and glory in the highest" — Luke's version differs from Mark and Matthew's "Hosanna in the highest": where the infancy narrative announced "peace on earth" (2:14), the entry hymn announces "peace in heaven" — the reconciliation of heaven and earth accomplished through the King's coming.

verses.kjv_text: And some of the Pharisees from among the multitude said unto him, Master, rebuke thy disciples.
verses.original_text: καί τινες τῶν Φαρισαίων ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄχλου εἶπαν πρὸς αὐτόν, Διδάσκαλε, ἐπιτίμησον τοῖς μαθηταῖς σου.
verses.transliteration: kai tines tōn Pharisaiōn apo tou ochlou eipan pros auton, Didaskale, epitimēson tois mathētais sou.
verses.study_note: "Rebuke thy disciples" (epitimēson tois mathētais sou — reprimand your disciples) — the Pharisees address Jesus respectfully as "Teacher" (Didaskale) but demand he suppress the messianic proclamation. Their concern may be both theological (objection to the royal messianic claim) and political (fear of Roman response to royal demonstrations). "Rebuke" (epitimēson) — the same word used of rebuking demons; the Pharisees want Jesus to silence what they perceive as a dangerous and unauthorized proclamation.

verses.kjv_text: And he answered and said unto them, I tell you that, if these should hold their peace, the stones would immediately cry out.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν, Λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐὰν οὗτοι σιωπήσωσιν, οἱ λίθοι κράξουσιν.
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis eipen, Legō humin hoti ean houtoi siōpēsōsin, hoi lithoi kraxousin.
verses.study_note: "The stones would immediately cry out" (hoi lithoi kraxousin — the stones will cry out, shout aloud; a future of certainty) — a hyperbolic declaration of inevitability: the entry of the King into his city is so cosmically significant that even inanimate matter would give testimony if the disciples were silenced. The saying may allude to Habakkuk 2:11 ("the stone shall cry out of the wall"). This is not a general principle about nature's praise but a specific statement: this moment — the Messiah's entry — is so significant that nothing can suppress its proclamation.

verses.kjv_text: And when he was come near, he beheld the city, and wept over it,
verses.original_text: Καὶ ὡς ἤγγισεν, ἰδὼν τὴν πόλιν ἔκλαυσεν ἐπ' αὐτήν,
verses.transliteration: Kai hōs ēngisen, idōn tēn polin eklausen ep' autēn,
verses.study_note: "He beheld the city, and wept over it" (idōn tēn polin eklausen ep' autēn — having seen the city he wept over it) — this is one of only three recorded instances of Jesus weeping in the Gospels (also John 11:35 and possibly John 12:27). "Wept" (eklausen — imperfect in the best manuscripts: he was weeping, he kept weeping) — not a single tear but sustained grief. The triumphal entry suddenly, shockingly, becomes a weeping entry. The crowd praises; Jesus weeps. The contrast is devastating: they celebrate a political triumph; he mourns an impending catastrophe they cannot see.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, If thou hadst known, even thou, at least in this thy day, the things which belong unto thy peace! but now they are hid from thine eyes.
verses.original_text: λέγων ὅτι Εἰ ἔγνως καὶ σύ, καὶ γε ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ σου ταύτῃ, τὰ πρὸς εἰρήνην σου· νῦν δὲ ἐκρύβη ἀπὸ ὀφθαλμῶν σου.
verses.transliteration: legōn hoti Ei egnōs kai su, kai ge en tē hēmera sou tautē, ta pros eirēnēn sou; nun de ekrubē apo ophthalmōn sou.
verses.study_note: "If thou hadst known" (Ei egnōs — if you had known, an unfulfilled condition) "the things which belong unto thy peace" (ta pros eirēnēn sou — the things toward your peace, what makes for your peace) — the city whose name includes "peace" (Yeru-shalom) does not recognize the Prince of Peace in its midst. "In this thy day" (en tē hēmera sou tautē — in this your day) — the day of visitation, the day of opportunity. "Now they are hid from thine eyes" (nun de ekrubē apo ophthalmōn sou — but now it has been hidden from your eyes, a divine passive) — the hiding, as in 18:34, is both judicial and tragic: Jerusalem has chosen blindness and blindness has been confirmed.

verses.kjv_text: For the days shall come upon thee, that thine enemies shall cast a trench about thee, and compass thee round, and keep thee in on every side,
verses.original_text: ὅτι ἥξουσιν ἡμέραι ἐπὶ σέ, καὶ παρεμβαλοῦσιν οἱ ἐχθροί σου χάρακά σοι καὶ περικυκλώσουσίν σε καὶ συνέξουσίν σε πάντοθεν,
verses.transliteration: hoti hēxousin hēmerai epi se, kai parembaloуsin hoi echthroi sou charaka soi kai perikuklōsousin se kai sunexousin se pantothen,
verses.study_note: "The days shall come upon thee" (hēxousin hēmerai epi se — days will come upon you) — prophetic language typical of OT judgment oracles. "Cast a trench about thee" (charakā soi — a palisade, a siege wall; charax = sharpened stake, military palisade) — a technical description of Roman siege warfare, literally fulfilled in the Roman siege of Jerusalem under Titus (AD 66-70) who built a wall around the city. "Compass thee round, and keep thee in on every side" (perikuklōsousin kai sunexousin se pantothen — surround and hem in from all sides) — the encirclement that preceded the Roman assault.

verses.kjv_text: And shall lay thee even with the ground, and thy children within thee; and they shall not leave in thee one stone upon another; because thou knewest not the time of thy visitation.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐδαφιοῦσίν σε καὶ τὰ τέκνα σου ἐν σοί, καὶ οὐκ ἀφήσουσιν ἐν σοὶ λίθον ἐπὶ λίθῳ, ἀνθ' ὧν οὐκ ἔγνως τὸν καιρὸν τῆς ἐπισκοπῆς σου.
verses.transliteration: kai edaphiousin se kai ta tekna sou en soi, kai ouk aphēsousin en soi lithon epi lithō, anth' hōn ouk egnōs ton kairon tēs episkopēs sou.
verses.study_note: "Lay thee even with the ground" (edaphiousin se — will dash you to the ground, raze you; edaphos = floor, ground) "thy children within thee" — a devastating picture of slaughter during the siege. "Not leave in thee one stone upon another" (ouk aphēsousin lithon epi lithō — will not leave stone upon stone) — fulfilled in the Roman destruction of 70 AD when Titus demolished the Temple and much of the city. "Because thou knewest not the time of thy visitation" (ton kairon tēs episkopēs sou — the season of your visitation/inspection; episkopē = visitation, pastoral oversight, inspection by a superior) — the root cause of the catastrophe: failure to recognize the Messiah's arrival.

verses.kjv_text: And he went into the temple, and began to cast out them that sold therein, and them that bought;
verses.original_text: Καὶ εἰσελθὼν εἰς τὸ ἱερὸν ἤρξατο ἐκβάλλειν τοὺς πωλοῦντας ἐν αὐτῷ καὶ τοὺς ἀγοράζοντας,
verses.transliteration: Kai eiselthōn eis to hieron ērxato ekballein tous pōlountas en autō kai tous agorazontas,
verses.study_note: "Went into the temple" (eiselthōn eis to hieron — having entered into the temple complex; hieron = the whole Temple precincts, including the Court of the Gentiles where the commercial activity occurred) — the Temple action immediately follows the entry. "Cast out them that sold therein, and them that bought" (ekballein tous pōlountas kai tous agorazontas — to throw out those selling and those buying) — Luke's account is the most compressed of the Synoptics, without the detail of overturned tables (Mark 11:15-16). The commercial exchange itself is the problem: the Temple had become a market.

verses.kjv_text: Saying unto them, It is written, My house is the house of prayer: but ye have made it a den of thieves.
verses.original_text: λέγων αὐτοῖς, Γέγραπται, Ὁ οἶκός μου οἶκος προσευχῆς ἐσται· ὑμεῖς δὲ αὐτὸν ἐποιήσατε σπήλαιον λῃστῶν.
verses.transliteration: legōn autois, Gegraptai, Ho oikos mou oikos proseuchēs estai; humeis de auton epoiēsate spēlaion lēstōn.
verses.study_note: "It is written" (Gegraptai — the perfect passive of graphō, the standard formula for citing authoritative Scripture) — two texts are combined: "My house is the house of prayer" from Isaiah 56:7 (where the full text includes "for all nations," which Mark preserves but Luke omits — significant in light of Luke's universal emphasis elsewhere) and "a den of thieves" (spēlaion lēstōn) from Jeremiah 7:11. Jeremiah's "den of robbers" occurs in a Temple sermon about the false security of Temple attendance combined with injustice. The combination of Isaiah and Jeremiah creates a prophetic indictment.

verses.kjv_text: And he taught daily in the temple. But the chief priests and the scribes and the chief of the people sought to destroy him,
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἦν διδάσκων τὸ καθ' ἡμέραν ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ· οἱ δὲ ἀρχιερεῖς καὶ οἱ γραμματεῖς ἐζήτουν αὐτὸν ἀπολέσαι καὶ οἱ πρῶτοι τοῦ λαοῦ,
verses.transliteration: Kai ēn didaskōn to kath' hēmeran en tō hierō; hoi de archiereis kai hoi grammateis ezētoun auton apolesai kai hoi prōtoi tou laou,
verses.study_note: "He taught daily in the temple" (ēn didaskōn to kath' hēmeran — he was teaching daily, a periphrastic imperfect indicating continuous daily activity) — Jesus establishes a teaching ministry in the Temple as his base of operations for chapters 20-21. "The chief priests and the scribes and the chief of the people sought to destroy him" (ezētoun auton apolesai — were seeking to destroy him; the imperfect: an ongoing sustained effort) — the same three groups who form the Sanhedrin coalition. The opposition is established as permanent and resolute; the passion is structurally inevitable from this point.

verses.kjv_text: And could not find what they might do: for all the people were very attentive to hear him.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐχ εὕρισκον τὸ τί ποιήσωσιν· ὁ λαὸς γὰρ ἅπας ἐξεκρέματο αὐτοῦ ἀκούων.
verses.transliteration: kai ouch hēuriskon to ti poiēsōsin; ho laos gar hapas exekrematō autou akouōn.
verses.study_note: "Could not find what they might do" (ouch heuriskon to ti poiēsōsin — were not finding what they should do; the deliberative subjunctive poiēsōsin expressing their frustrated deliberation) — the conspiracy is stalled by the people's response. "All the people were very attentive to hear him" (ho laos gar hapas exekrematō autou akouōn — for all the people was hanging from him listening; exekremato — a striking verb: to hang suspended from something, to be riveted, to hang on every word; found only here in the NT) — the people's riveted attention creates a protective barrier around Jesus that prevents the authorities from acting openly.

meta.overview: Luke 20 consists entirely of controversy dialogues in the Temple — a series of escalating confrontations between Jesus and the religious authorities (chief priests, scribes, Sadducees, and Pharisees) that constitute the public intellectual climax of his ministry before the passion. The chapter opens with the question of Jesus's authority (vv.1-8), which he answers with a counter-question about John's baptism that exposes the authorities' bad faith. The Parable of the Wicked Vinedressers (vv.9-19) then reframes the entire conflict as the culmination of a long history of Israel's rejection of God's messengers, ending with the Son himself.
meta.theme: The three subsequent controversies (vv.20-47) test Jesus from different ideological directions: the Herodians test him on the politically explosive question of Roman taxation (vv.20-26); the Sadducees test him with a riddle about the resurrection they do not believe in (vv.27-40); and Jesus tests the scribes with a question about Davidic sonship (vv.41-44) before issuing a public warning against the scribes' ostentatious piety (vv.45-47). Each controversy demonstrates Jesus's mastery: he does not merely avoid the traps but uses each encounter to advance positive theological claims about the nature of God, the resurrection, and his own identity as Lord.
meta.keyWordOriginal: The question about tribute to Caesar (vv.20-26) is perhaps Jesus's most famous political statement. The questioners have laid a dilemma: affirming taxation alienates the Jewish nationalists; denying it exposes Jesus to Roman prosecution for sedition. Jesus's answer — "Render therefore unto Caesar the things which be Caesar's, and unto God the things which be God's" (v.25) — is not a political theory but an eschatological reminder: what bears Caesar's image belongs to Caesar; what bears God's image (the human person, Gen 1:26-27) belongs entirely to God.
meta.keyWordMeaning: The Sadducees' question about levirate marriage and resurrection (vv.27-40) receives from Jesus an answer that both refutes their materialism and makes a positive theological claim: the resurrection is not the resuscitation of earthly existence but transformation into a qualitatively new mode of being, "equal unto the angels" (isangEloi), in which marriage is irrelevant because death is irrelevant. Jesus then turns to Exodus 3:6 to demonstrate resurrection from the Torah itself — the only Scripture the Sadducees accepted as fully authoritative. His argument — "God is not a God of the dead, but of the living" — establishes that covenant relationship implies ongoing life.
outline: ἐξουσία (exousia)

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that on one of those days, as he taught the people in the temple, and preached the gospel, the chief priests and the scribes came upon him with the elders,
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν μιᾷ τῶν ἡμερῶν ἐκείνων διδάσκοντος αὐτοῦ τὸν λαὸν ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ καὶ εὐαγγελιζομένου, ἐπέστησαν οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς καὶ οἱ γραμματεῖς σὺν τοῖς πρεσβυτέροις,
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en mia tōn hēmerōn ekeinōn didaskontos autou ton laon en tō hierō kai euangelizomenou, epestēsan hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis sun tois presbuterois,
verses.study_note: "On one of those days" — the daily Temple teaching ministry established in 19:47. "Taught...and preached the gospel" (didaskontos kai euangelizomenou — teaching and evangelizing; the two activities are linked: the gospel is proclaimed through teaching) — Jesus is in the midst of active ministry when interrupted. "Came upon him" (epestēsan — stood over him, came up to him; the word suggests a sudden confrontational arrival, used of armies and officials) — the three groups together (chief priests, scribes, elders) constitute the Sanhedrin's composition, making this an official delegation.

verses.kjv_text: Saying unto him, Tell us, by what authority doest thou these things? or who is he that gave thee this authority?
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπαν λέγοντες πρὸς αὐτόν, Εἰπὲ ἡμῖν ἐν ποίᾳ ἐξουσίᾳ ταῦτα ποιεῖς, ἢ τίς ἐστιν ὁ δοὺς σοί τὴν ἐξουσίαν ταύτην.
verses.transliteration: kai eipan legontes pros auton, Eipe hēmin en poia exousia tauta poieis, ē tis estin ho dous soi tēn exousian tautēn.
verses.study_note: "By what authority doest thou these things?" (en poia exousia — in what kind of authority; poia asks about the quality and source of the authority) — "these things" refers to the Temple cleansing (19:45-46) and the daily teaching. The question has a legal dimension: authorized teachers needed rabbinical ordination and appointment. Jesus has no such credentials. The question is formally respectful but designed to expose either a lack of authorization or a blasphemous claim of divine authorization.

verses.kjv_text: And he answered and said unto them, I will also ask you one thing; and answer me:
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς, Ἐρωτήσω ὑμᾶς κἀγὼ ἕνα λόγον, καὶ εἴπατέ μοι·
verses.transliteration: apokritheis de eipen pros autous, Erōtēsō humas kagō hena logon, kai eipate moi;
verses.study_note: "I will also ask you one thing" (Erōtēsō humas kagō hena logon — I also will ask you one word/question) — a counter-question is a recognized rabbinic method of responding to a question one judges to be in bad faith or that can best be answered by exposing the questioner's presuppositions. Jesus is not evading but engaging at the level of the questioners' actual motivation. "And answer me" (kai eipate moi — and tell me; the aorist imperative: give me an answer) — he expects and demands an answer, putting them in the same position they have put him.

verses.kjv_text: The baptism of John, was it from heaven, or of men?
verses.original_text: τὸ βάπτισμα Ἰωάννου ἐξ οὐρανοῦ ἦν ἢ ἐξ ἀνθρώπων;
verses.transliteration: to baptisma Iōannou ex ouranou ēn ē ex anthrōpōn?
verses.study_note: "The baptism of John, was it from heaven, or of men?" — a brilliantly constructed dilemma. The question is not merely about John but about the source of prophetic authority. "From heaven" (ex ouranou — from heaven, the Jewish circumlocution for "from God") implies divine authorization; "of men" (ex anthrōpōn — from humans, from human decision) implies human institution. John and Jesus shared a common prophetic mission and common opposition from the same authorities; the authorities' response to John logically implies their response to Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: And they reasoned with themselves, saying, If we shall say, From heaven; he will say, Why then believed ye him not?
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ συνελογίσαντο πρὸς ἑαυτοὺς λέγοντες ὅτι Ἐὰν εἴπωμεν, Ἐξ οὐρανοῦ· ἐρεῖ, Διατί οὐκ ἐπιστεύσατε αὐτῷ;
verses.transliteration: hoi de sunelogisanto pros heautous legontes hoti Ean eipōmen, Ex ouranou; erei, Diatī ouk episteusate autō?
verses.study_note: "They reasoned with themselves" (sunelogisanto pros heautous — they calculated together among themselves; the compound sunelogisanto suggests mutual deliberation, a committee decision) — they think through both horns of the dilemma before answering. "If we shall say, From heaven; he will say, Why then believed ye him not?" (Ean eipōmen...erei...Diatī ouk episteusate autō) — if John's authority was divine, their rejection of John was rebellion against God; their authority question against Jesus falls on themselves. The conditional syllogism is airtight.

verses.kjv_text: But and if we say, Of men; all the people will stone us: for they be persuaded that John was a prophet.
verses.original_text: ἐὰν δὲ εἴπωμεν, Ἐξ ἀνθρώπων· ὁ λαὸς ἅπας καταλιθάσει ἡμᾶς· πεπεισμένος γάρ ἐστιν Ἰωάννην προφήτην εἶναι.
verses.transliteration: ean de eipōmen, Ex anthrōpōn; ho laos hapas katalithasei hēmas; pepeismenos gar estin Iōannēn prophētēn einai.
verses.study_note: "All the people will stone us" (ho laos hapas katalithasei hēmas — all the people will stone us to death; katalithasei — a strong word, to stone thoroughly/completely; the prefix kata intensifies) — the crowd's protection of Jesus (19:48) extends to John's memory. Stoning was the prescribed punishment for blasphemy; denying John's prophetic status in the presence of the people would be politically suicidal. "Persuaded that John was a prophet" (pepeismenos — perfect passive: in a state of having been persuaded, settled conviction) — the people's conviction is firm and permanent.

verses.kjv_text: And they answered, that they could not tell whence it was.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπεκρίθησαν μὴ εἰδέναι πόθεν.
verses.transliteration: kai apekrithēsan mē eidenai pothen.
verses.study_note: "They answered, that they could not tell whence it was" (apekrithēsan mē eidenai pothen — they answered that they did not know from where) — a deliberate, strategic non-answer, driven by political calculation rather than genuine uncertainty. Their refusal to answer is not intellectual humility but calculated evasion. The irony is that their non-answer reveals their true stance: they will not commit to a position they know to be morally obligatory if true. Jesus's counter-question has exposed not their ignorance but their bad faith.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said unto them, Neither tell I you by what authority I do these things.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν αὐτοῖς, Οὐδὲ ἐγὼ λέγω ὑμῖν ἐν ποίᾳ ἐξουσίᾳ ταῦτα ποιῶ.
verses.transliteration: kai ho Iēsous eipen autois, Oude egō legō humin en poia exousia tauta poiō.
verses.study_note: "Neither tell I you by what authority I do these things" (Oude egō legō humin — nor do I tell you) — a perfectly symmetrical refusal matching theirs. This is not a mere rhetorical maneuver but a judgment: those who refuse to engage honestly with available evidence (John's ministry) are not entitled to further revelation. The pattern recurs throughout Luke: understanding is given to those who engage honestly, withheld from those who approach for entrapment (cf. 8:10). The silence of Jesus before those who have already decided is itself a form of judgment.

verses.kjv_text: Then began he to speak to the people this parable; A certain man planted a vineyard, and let it forth to husbandmen, and went into a far country for a long time.
verses.original_text: Ἤρξατο δὲ πρὸς τὸν λαὸν λέγειν τὴν παραβολὴν ταύτην· Ἄνθρωπός τις ἐφύτευσεν ἀμπελῶνα, καὶ ἐξέδετο αὐτὸν γεωργοῖς, καὶ ἀπεδήμησεν χρόνους ἱκανούς.
verses.transliteration: Ērxato de pros ton laon legein tēn parabolēn tautēn; Anthrōpos tis ephuteusen ampelōna, kai exEdeto auton geōrgois, kai apEdēmēsen chronous hikanous.
verses.study_note: "Began he to speak to the people" (ērxato pros ton laon — he began to the people) — the shift from the authorities (v.1-8) to the people is deliberate: the parable is public, exposing the authorities in the hearing of those they sought to maintain control over. "A certain man planted a vineyard" (Anthrōpos tis ephuteusen ampelōna) — the vineyard immediately evokes Isaiah 5:1-7, the Song of the Vineyard where the vineyard = Israel. "Went into a far country for a long time" (apEdēmēsen chronous hikanous — traveled away for considerable time) — the long absence mirrors Israel's experience of prophetic intervals.

verses.kjv_text: And at the season he sent a servant to the husbandmen, that they should give him of the fruit of the vineyard: but the husbandmen beat him, and sent him away empty.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐν καιρῷ ἀπέστειλεν πρὸς τοὺς γεωργοὺς δοῦλον, ἵνα ἀπὸ τοῦ καρποῦ τοῦ ἀμπελῶνος δώσουσιν αὐτῷ· οἱ δὲ γεωργοὶ δείραντες αὐτὸν ἐξαπέστειλαν κενόν.
verses.transliteration: kai en kairō apesteilen pros tous geōrgous doulon, hina apo tou karpou tou ampelōnos dōsousin autō; hoi de geōrgoi deirantes auton exapesteilan kenon.
verses.study_note: "At the season" (en kairō — at the appointed time, the harvest time) — the owner's request is legitimate and expected: the tenants are to render the fruit from the vineyard entrusted to them. "Beat him, and sent him away empty" (deirantes auton exapesteilan kenon — having beaten him they sent him away empty; deirō = to flay, to beat severely) — the rejection is violent and contemptuous. The servant represents the prophetic tradition: the OT prophets sent to call Israel to account for the fruit of covenant faithfulness.

verses.kjv_text: And again he sent another servant: and they beat him also, and entreated him shamefully, and sent him away empty.
verses.original_text: καὶ προσέθετο πέμψαι ἕτερον δοῦλον· οἱ δὲ κἀκεῖνον δείραντες καὶ ἀτιμάσαντες ἐξαπέστειλαν κενόν.
verses.transliteration: kai prosetheto pempsai heteron doulon; hoi de kakeionon deirantes kai atimasantes exapesteilan kenon.
verses.study_note: "He sent another servant" (prosetheto pempsai heteron — he added/proceeded to send another; a Hebraism for continuing a repeated action) — the second sending intensifies the pattern. "Beat him also, and entreated him shamefully" (deirantes kai atimasantes — having beaten him and having dishonored him; atimazō = to treat as without honor, to insult) — the violence escalates with dishonor added to beating. The accumulating servants represent the succession of prophets rejected by Israel, a theme explicit in Luke 11:49-51 and 13:34.

verses.kjv_text: And again he sent a third: and they wounded him also, and cast him out.
verses.original_text: καὶ προσέθετο πέμψαι τρίτον· οἱ δὲ καὶ τοῦτον τραυματίσαντες ἐξέβαλον.
verses.transliteration: kai prosetheto pempsai triton; hoi de kai touton traumatisantes exebalon.
verses.study_note: "They wounded him also, and cast him out" (traumatisantes exebalon — having wounded him they threw him out; traumatizō = to wound physically, the root of the English "trauma") — the third servant receives the most severe treatment: wounded and expelled rather than merely beaten and dismissed. The escalating series of three creates the narrative anticipation that the fourth action will either be the turn-around or the final catastrophe. In this parable, the fourth action brings the worst: the son.

verses.kjv_text: Then said the lord of the vineyard, What shall I do? I will send my beloved son: it may be they will reverence him when they see him.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ κύριος τοῦ ἀμπελῶνος, Τί ποιήσω; πέμψω τὸν υἱόν μου τὸν ἀγαπητόν· ἴσως αὐτὸν ἰδόντες ἐντραπήσονται.
verses.transliteration: eipen de ho kurios tou ampelōnos, Ti poiēsō? pempsō ton huion mou ton agapēton; isōs auton idontes entrapēsontai.
verses.study_note: "My beloved son" (ton huion mou ton agapēton — my son, the beloved one; agapētos — the same word used at Jesus's baptism, 3:22, and transfiguration, 9:35: "my beloved Son") — the parabolic identification of Jesus is transparent and deliberate. The audience would have recognized the allusion. "It may be they will reverence him" (isōs entrapēsontai — perhaps they will show respect; isōs = perhaps, perhaps the only time in the Gospels divine action is expressed with "perhaps") — the owner's hope, which proves unfounded, shows the depth of his patient mercy.

verses.kjv_text: But when the husbandmen saw him, they reasoned among themselves, saying, This is the heir: come, let us kill him, that the inheritance may be ours.
verses.original_text: ἰδόντες δὲ αὐτὸν οἱ γεωργοὶ διελογίζοντο πρὸς ἀλλήλους λέγοντες, Οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ κληρονόμος· δεῦτε ἀποκτείνωμεν αὐτόν, ἵνα ἡμῶν γένηται ἡ κληρονομία.
verses.transliteration: idontes de auton hoi geōrgoi dielogizonto pros allēlous legontes, Houtos estin ho klēronomos; deute apokteinōmen auton, hina hēmōn genētai hē klēronomia.
verses.study_note: "This is the heir: come, let us kill him, that the inheritance may be ours" (Houtos estin ho klēronomos; deute apokteinōmen auton — This one is the heir; come, let us kill him) — the tenants recognize the heir and deliberately choose murder to seize the inheritance. The logic: with the heir dead and no other successors, they can claim adverse possession of the vineyard. This reveals the nature of Israel's rejection of Jesus: not ignorance but calculated self-interest. The chief priests and scribes listening (v.19) would have recognized themselves in the deliberating tenants.

verses.kjv_text: So they cast him out of the vineyard, and killed him. What therefore shall the lord of the vineyard do unto them?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐκβαλόντες αὐτὸν ἔξω τοῦ ἀμπελῶνος ἀπέκτειναν. τί οὖν ποιήσει αὐτοῖς ὁ κύριος τοῦ ἀμπελῶνος;
verses.transliteration: kai ekbalontes auton exō tou ampelōnos apekteinan. ti oun poiēsei autois ho kurios tou ampelōnos?
verses.study_note: "Cast him out of the vineyard, and killed him" (ekbalontes exō tou ampelōnos apekteinan — having cast him outside the vineyard they killed him) — "outside the vineyard" may allude to Hebrews 13:12: "Jesus...suffered without the gate." The casting out precedes the killing, perhaps reflecting crucifixion outside the city walls. "What therefore shall the lord of the vineyard do unto them?" — Jesus turns the question to the listeners, inviting them to pronounce judgment on the tenants before they realize they are pronouncing judgment on themselves.

verses.kjv_text: He shall come and destroy these husbandmen, and shall give the vineyard to others. And when they heard it, they said, God forbid.
verses.original_text: ἐλεύσεται καὶ ἀπολέσει τοὺς γεωργοὺς τούτους, καὶ δώσει τὸν ἀμπελῶνα ἄλλοις. ἀκούσαντες δὲ εἶπαν, Μὴ γένοιτο.
verses.transliteration: eleusetai kai apolesei tous geōrgous toutous, kai dōsei ton ampelōna allois. akousantes de eipan, Mē genoito.
verses.study_note: "He shall come and destroy these husbandmen, and shall give the vineyard to others" — the verdict pronounced by the narrative itself, not by the listeners. "Shall give the vineyard to others" — the inheritance transferred: the kingdom given to a new stewardship (cf. Matt 21:43: "a nation bringing forth the fruits thereof"). "When they heard it, they said, God forbid" (Mē genoito — may it not be! let it not happen! the strongest negative expression in Greek, used by Paul in Romans for emphatic denial) — the audience recoils from the logical conclusion of their own narrative.

verses.kjv_text: And he beheld them, and said, What is this then that is written, The stone which the builders rejected, the same is become the head of the corner?
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἐμβλέψας αὐτοῖς εἶπεν, Τί οὖν ἐστιν τὸ γεγραμμένον τοῦτο, Λίθον ὃν ἀπεδοκίμασαν οἱ οἰκοδομοῦντες, οὗτος ἐγενήθη εἰς κεφαλὴν γωνίας;
verses.transliteration: ho de emblepsas autois eipen, Ti oun estin to gegrammenon touto, Lithon hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomountes, houtos egenēthē eis kephalēn gōnias?
verses.study_note: "He beheld them" (emblepsas — having looked intently at them; emblēpō = to look into, to gaze fixedly) — a penetrating look that precedes the Scriptural citation. "The stone which the builders rejected" (Lithon hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomountes) — Psalm 118:22, the same psalm cited at the Triumphal Entry (19:38). The "stone rejected by the builders" and "head of the corner" (kephalēn gōnias — cornerstone or capstone) applies to Jesus: the one declared unfit for the building by its official architects is nevertheless made its foundational stone by God.

verses.kjv_text: Whosoever shall fall upon that stone shall be broken; but on whomsoever it shall fall, it will grind him to powder.
verses.original_text: πᾶς ὁ πεσὼν ἐπ' ἐκεῖνον τὸν λίθον συνθλασθήσεται· ἐφ' ὃν δ' ἂν πέσῃ, λικμήσει αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: pas ho pesōn ep' ekeinon ton lithon sunthlasthēsetai; eph' hon d' an pesē, likmēsei auton.
